$ 710 [ 832 ] . If the defendant reside in the county in which the sum-
mons is issued , it shall be returned for trial in the district of his residence ,
unless he otherwise consent in writing , signed by him and indorsed on
the summons ; or , unless the justices in such district be interested or
refuse to act , in which case it shall be returned in an adjoining district .

before him, unless the defendant , or defendants all , reside in one district
and there be an acting justice of the peace and an acting constable in
such district .
$ 711 [ 833 ] . If there be two or more defendants residing in the coun-
ty, but in different districts , the summons shall be returnable in either
district .
$ 712 [834 ] . If a defendant transiently in a county be served with a
summons issuing in said county , it shall be returned in the district in
which he is served , unless he otherwise consent in writing signed by him.
713 [ 835 ] . The parties shall not be entitled to a trial by jury, unless
the matter in controversy exceed in value twenty dollars . Upon a jury
trial being claimed , if it be allowable , the presiding judge or justice shall
order a jury to be forthwith empanneled to try the action . The jurors
shall be qualified as is required in the circuit courts .
$ 714 [ 836] . A new trial may be granted in the quarterly courts or
courts of justices of the peace , upon motion made within ten days after
a judgment has been rendered , of which motion reasonable notice shall
be given to the adverse party .
$ 715 [ 837 ] . Each presiding judge and justice of the peace shall , in

$ 724 [ 847 ] . Appeals may be taken in the manner following :
1. The party appealing shall produce to the clerk of the court to
which the appeal is taken a certified copy of the judgment and
amount of costs , and cause to be executed before him , by one or
more sufficient sureties to be approved by him , a bond to the effect
that the appellant will satisfy and perform the judgment that shall
be rendered upon the appeal ; whereupon , the clerk shall issue an
order to the judge , mayor , or justice , rendering the judgment , to
stay proceedings thereon ; and to transmit to the office of said clerk
all the original papers in the case ; and the appellee shall be sum-
moned , actually or constructively , as is provided in chapter 2 of title
4, to appear and defend the appeal . The provisions of this section ,
in regard to clerks , apply to a judge who acts as clerk of his own
court .
2. If the amount in controversy be of the value of twenty dol-
lars or more , exclusive of interest and costs, either party may direct
the appeal to be taken to the circuit court , and the judge or justice
shall make a minute thereof as part of the record .
$ 725 [ 848 ] . The judge , mayor , or justice , on being served with said
order , shall , in ten days thereafter , transmit all the original papers in the
action to the clerk's office of the court to which the appeal is taken .
§ 726 [ 849 ] . Appeals shall be docketed and stand for trial as ordinary
actions , and shall be tried anew , as if no judgment had been rendered .

154 [TITLE XVIII.APPEALS TO THE COURT OF APPEALS .
§ 734 [ 876] . The mode of bringing the judgment of an inferior court .
to the Court of Appeals , for reversal or modification , shall be by an
appeal , which shall be granted , as matter of right , to a party or privy
against a party or privy , by the court rendering the judgment , on motion .
made during the term at which it is rendered , or thereafter by the clerk .
of the Court of Appeals , on application of either party or his privy ,
upon filing in the office of said clerk a copy of the judgment from which
he appeals .
$ 735 [877]. The party taking the appeal shall be called the appellant ;
and the adverse party , the appellee .
§ 736 [ 878 ] . 1. No summons of appellees shall be necessary , if the
appeal be granted by the inferior court ; or , if the appellees enter
their appearance in the Court of Appeals .
2. In all other cases , the appellees shall be summoned actually or
constructively , pursuant to the provisions of chapter 2 of Title IV ,
to appear and answer the appeal .
3. For an appellee who is constructively summoned in the Court
of Appeals , that court shall appoint an attorney , whose duty it
shall be to inform the appellee of the pendency of the appeal ; and
the court may allow him a reasonable compensation for his services ,
to be paid by the appellant and taxed as costs .
$ 737 [879]. Concerning transcripts of records to be used in the Court
of Appeals-
I. a . The clerk of the court which renders a judgment shall copy
the entire record , or parts thereof , according to the directions of the
judge of the court , or of the parties to the appeal ; and the filing of
a schedule , as hereinafter authorized , shall be deemed a direction to
copy the parts of the record therein mentioned .
b. The clerk shall be governed by the provisions of subsections 8
and 9 of this section , in making any transcript to which those pro-
visions apply.
2. In cases to which the provisions of chapter 3 of Title X apply ,
the appellant , whether the appeal be granted by the inferior court , or
by the clerk of the Court of Appeals , shall present to the judge
of the inferior court his assignment of errors ; the judge shall , by
directions indorsed on , or annexed to , the assignment , order the clerk-
of the inferior court to copy such specified parts of the record as , in
view of the alleged errors , may be material ; and the appellant shall
file said assignment and directions in the office of said last -mentioned
clerk .
3. In all other cases in which the appeal is granted by the inferior :
court , if the appellees shall have been defendants in the action , and

classification.title: Civil and Criminal Code 1883
classification.summary: This section of the Civil and Criminal Code of 1883 outlines the procedural rules for bringing a judgment from an inferior court to the Court of Appeals for review. It details how appeals are granted, the roles of appellant and appellee, the necessity of summons, and the process for preparing transcripts of records. It also specifies how attorneys are appointed for constructively summoned appellees and their compensation.

The document type is classified as 'codes' because it is part of a compiled code of laws from 1883.

a . The appellant , within ninety days after the granting of the

returned ; and , within twenty days after such service ; or , if such
notice be not served , within one hundred and twenty days after the
granting of the appeal ; or , whether such notice be served or not ,

pellant , and not afterward , the appellee may file in said office a

errors .
c. If the appellee file an assignment of cross -errors , the appellant ,

said ; or , if notice be not served , within one hundred and thirty days.
after the granting of the appeal ; or , whether such notice be served
or not , at any time before completion of the transcript ordered by
the appellee , and not afterward , may , with reference to the cross-
appeal , file an additional schedule similar to that above described .
5. Excepting the cases provided for by subsection 2 of this sec-
tion, if an appeal be granted by the clerk of the Court of Appeals ,
and if the appellees be actually summoned to appear to the appeal ;
or , if, being femes covert or free from disability , they enter their
appearance to the appeal , the appellant may order a transcript of
the entire record or of parts thereof .
6. In all other cases in which an appeal is granted by the clerk
of the Court of Appeals , the appellant shall order a transcript of
the entire record .
7. If the appellant , to whom an appeal is granted by the clerk of
the Court of Appeals , choose to file a transcript of a part only of

456 [TITLE XVIII .APPEALS TO THE COURT OF APPEALS .
a. He shall file in the office of the clerk of the inferior court his
assignment of errors , and a schedule similar to that above described ;
and shall cause notice of the filing thereof to be served on the ap-
pellees , and to be returned to said office , as a summons is directed
to be served and returned .
b. Within twenty days after service of such notice , or at any
subsequent time before completion of the transcript ordered by
the appellant , and not afterward , the appellee may file in said office
a schedule similar to that above described , and an assignment of
cross -errors , if he wish to take a cross -appeal .
c. If the appellee file cross -errors , the appellant , within forty days
after service of the notice mentioned in subdivision a of this sub-
section , or at any subsequent time before completion of the copy
ordered by the appellee , and not afterward , may file , with reference
to the cross -appeal , an additional schedule similar to that above
described .
8. The provisions of subsection 3 of section 335 shall apply to
all transcripts made by clerks .
9. If a subpœna duces tecum , issued pursuant to section 743 , sub-
section 2 , be delivered to the clerk of the inferior court before he
shall have copied the part of the record therein mentioned , he shall
transmit such part instead of a copy thereof , though a copy thereof
may have been ordered by the judge of the inferior court , or by a
party to the appeal .
10. The above mentioned directions of the judge , assignments
of error , schedules , and notices and returns thereon , shall , when
filed in the office of the clerk of the inferior court , constitute part
of the record ; but shall not be spread at large on the order -book ;
and shall be copied in the transcript .
II. A clerk who copies papers which do not constitute part of a
record , unless required to do so by a judge or a party ; and a party
who requires a clerk to copy such papers , or immaterial parts of a
record , shall pay the costs resulting therefrom , to be adjudged by the
Court of Appeals upon or without motion .
12. At the close of the transcript , the clerk shall certify , in sub-
stance , that it contains a true and complete copy of the record , or
of such parts thereof as he may have been required to copy , accord .
ing to the truth ; and shall append an index referring , consecutively ,
to the pages on which the copy of each pleading , or other separate
part of the record , commences ; and describing briefly the exhibits ,
if any.

158 [TITLE XVIII .APPEALS TO THE COURT OF APPEALS .
suant to subsections 3 , 4 , 5 , 6 , or 7 , of section 737 , may have ordered to
be copied — which parts of the record shall , in general terms , be described
in the statement , -the clerk of the Court of Appeals shall , upon the
application of such party , issue a writ of certiorari , describing , in general
terms , such omitted parts of the record , and commanding the clerk of
the inferior court , forthwith , to transmit copies thereof to the Court of
Appeals .
§ 743 [ 882 ] . 1. If the appellees , having been defendants in the orig-
inal action in the inferior court , be under any disability , other than
coverture , or infancy and coverture combined , the Court of Appeals
may , by writ of certiorari directed to the clerk of the inferior court
require him to transmit a transcript of such parts of the record as
the parties may have failed to require him to copy .
2. The Court of Appeals , if satisfied that a view of any part of a
record may be important to a correct decision of an appeal ; or that
the copying of any part of a record would cause great and unneces-
sary cost or delay , may , by a writ of subpana duces tecum directed
to the clerk of the inferior court cause him to transmit such part of
the record to the clerk of the Court of Appeals . It shall be identi-
fied by the clerk of the inferior court , by his certificate indorsed
thereon or appended thereto ; and , after the cause shall have been
decided by the Court of Appeals , shall be returned by its clerk to
the clerk of the inferior court , and be kept by him subject to the
order of the inferior court . The cost of such transmission and
return shall be paid by the party at whose instance the transmission
is made , and be taxed as part of the costs .
744 [ 883 ] . The appellant may be required to give security for costs ,
as plaintiffs in civil actions may be so required .
745 [ 884 ] . An appeal shall not be granted except within two years
next after the right to appeal first accrued , unless the party applying
therefor was then a defendant in . the action , and an infant not under
coverture ; or of unsound mind ; or a prisoner who did not appear by his
attorney - in which cases an appeal may be granted to such parties , or
their representatives , within one year next after their deaths or the
removal of their disabilities , whichever may first happen .
$ 746 [ 885 ] . The tax on an appeal shall be one dollar .

§ 747 [ 886] . An appeal shall not stay proceedings on the judgment ,
unless a supersedeas be issued .
$ 748 [ 887 ] . A supersedeas shall not be issued , until the appellant
cause to be executed before the clerk of the court which rendered the
judgment, or the clerk of the Court of Appeals, by one or more suffi-
cient sureties , to be approved by such clerk , a bond to the effect that the
appellant shall pay to appellee all costs and damages that shall be.ad-
judged against the appellant on the appeal ; also , that he will satisfy and
perform the judgment appealed from , if it should be affirmed , and any
judgment , or order , which the Court of Appeals may render , or order to
be rendered by the inferior court , not exceeding in amount , or value , the
original judgment and all rents , or hire , or damages to property , during
the pendency of the appeal , of which the appellee is kept out of pos-
session by reason of the appeal .
$ 749 [ 888 , 889 ] . 1. The bond must be executed before the clerk of
the court rendering the judgment , if the appeal be granted by that
court . In other cases , it must be executed before the clerk of the
Court of Appeals .
2. The clerk of the court rendering the judgment shall issue the
supersedeas , if the bond be executed before him before the expira-
tion of the time for filing a copy of the record in the clerk's office
of the Court of Appeals pursuant to section 738. In other cases ,
it must be issued by the clerk of the Court of Appeals .
$ 750 [ 890] . The appellee may move the Court of Appeals, if in
session ; or, on reasonable notice to the appellant , may move a judge
of said court in vacation , to discharge the supersedeas ; and , if the court
or judge shall consider the sureties insufficient , or the bond substantially
defective in securing the rights of the appellee , the court or judge shall
make an order discharging the supersedeas , unless a good bond with
sufficient sureties be forthwith executed . The order , if made by a judge ,
shall be in writing and signed by him ; upon the filing of which , or of a
certified copy of the order , if made by the court , in the clerk's office
of the inferior court , executions and other proceedings for enforcing the

classification.title: Supersedeas on Appeals
classification.summary: This statute outlines the process and requirements for obtaining a supersedeas to stay proceedings on a judgment during an appeal. It details the necessity of a supersedeas, the bond required, who can execute and approve it, and the procedures for issuing and discharging the supersedeas. The bond must cover costs, damages, satisfaction of the original judgment if affirmed, and any additional judgments or orders from the Court of Appeals, including rents or damages to property during the appeal.

The document type is classified as 'codes' because it appears to be part of a compiled legal code (Civil and Criminal Code 1883).

such assignment in the Court of Appeals, may obtain a cross -appeal ,

2. The failure of the appellee to take a cross -appeal after assign-
ing cross -errors pursuant to the provisions of subsections 4 or 7 of
section 737 , and not otherwise , shall bar his right to an appeal .
3. The failure of, an appellant to prosecute an appeal ; or his
dismissal of it after the appellee shall have assigned cross -errors
pursuant to the provisions of subsections 4 or 7 of section 737 , or
subsection I of this section , shall not prevent the appellee from
prosecuting a cross -appeal .
§ 756 [ 896 ] . 1. Every appellant or cross-appellant must , by his assign-
ment of errors , specify the particular errors on which he means to
rely ; and no others shall be alleged by the party or examined into
by the court ; nor shall a judgment be reversed or modified , except
for an error to the prejudice of the substantial rights of the party
complaining thereof .
2. But the Court of Appeals may allow a party , having been a
defendant in the original action in the inferior court , and being
under any disability , excepting coverture , or infancy and coverture
combined , to amend an assignment of errors or cross - errors , after
reasonable notice to the adverse party..
$ 757 [ 897 ] . If it appear from the record that an appeal was improp-
erly granted , or that the appellant's right to prosecute it further has
ceased , the appellee may , upon stating the grounds in writing, move the
court to dismiss the appeal . The motion shall not be heard , without the
appellant's consent , before the day on which the case is set for trial on
the docket .

§ 759 [ 899] . If an appeal with a supersedeas be taken for delay
merely , the appellee may at any time move the court to affirm the judg-
ment as a delay case ; before making which motion , he or his counsel
shall indorse on the record , in substance , that he has carefully examined
the record and believes the appeal is prosecuted for delay merely ; where-
upon , the court shall examine the record , and if they find no error in the
proceedings , and believe the appeal was prosecuted for delay merely ,
they shall affirm the judgment .
§ 760 [900 ] . 1. The Court of Appeals may make rules for the con-
venient dispatch of business , the preservation of order , the argument
of cases or motions , and the manner and time of presenting motions
or petitions for rehearing , therein ; and the time for issuing its man-
dates and decisions , and the mode of enforcing its mandates and
orders , and may change the same : Provided , that no mandate shall
issue , nor decision become final , until after thirty days , excluding
Sundays , from the day on which the decision is rendered , unless the
court , in delay cases , otherwise direct ; and , if said thirty days expire
during a vacation or recess of the court , a written order of one of
its judges , filed in its clerk's office , within said thirty days , shall
have the same effect to suspend the mandate , by allowing a petition.
for a rehearing to be filed , or by allowing time to file such petition ,
as if such order were made by the court .
2. The rules of said court in force when this Code takes effect , and
consistent with its provisions , shall continue in force until changed
by the court .
§ 761 [901 ] . 1. Upon the affirmance of, or the dismissal of an appeal
from , a judgment for the payment of money , or delivery of personal
property , the appellee may file in the clerk's office of the inferior
court a copy of the mandate of affirmance , or order of dismissal-
upon which the clerk shall indorse the time of its being filed ; and ,
thereupon , such writs of execution may be issued as if the mandate
or order had been entered in the court .
2. If a judgment be reversed , and the case remanded for a trial,
or other proceedings , it shall stand for trial , or for such other pro-
ceedings , in the court whence the appeal was taken , at the next
succeeding term thereof : Provided , that the mandate of the Court
of Appeals be filed in the clerk's office of the lower court , and
notice thereof given to the adverse party , if he be in the county ,
or, if he be absent from the county , to his attorney , ten days before
the commencement of such term . Such case shall have the same

CHAP . II .] 165LOUISVILLE CHANCERY COURT .
CHAPTER II .
Chancellor and Vice Chancellor .
2774. Term of office and election , of chancellor of.
8 775- vice chancellor of.
776. Chancellor and vice chancellor , compensation of.
8 777- powers and duties of.
2778. Special chancellor of, how elected .
§ 774. The office of chancellor is continued as heretofore , and the
present incumbent shall hold his office for the term for which he was
elected . Thereafter , a chancellor of said court shall be elected by the
qualified voters within its jurisdiction , when the judge of the Jefferson
circuit court shall be elected ; and vacancies in the office of chancellor
shall be filled in like manner as vacancies in the office of judge of the
Jefferson circuit court .
775. The office of vice chancellor shall continue as heretofore .
The present incumbent shall hold his office for the term for which he
was elected . A vice chancellor of said court shall be elected by the
qualified voters within its jurisdiction on the first Monday in August ,
1878, and every six years thereafter ; and shall have the same qualifica-
tions as the chancellor of said court , and hold his office for the term of
six years .
776. The compensation of the chancellor and vice chancellor shall
remain and be paid as is now provided by law.
§ 777. The chancellor shall be the presiding judge of said court ; and ,
during his absence or incapacity to sit , his duties shall be discharged by
the vice chancellor . It shall also be the duty of the vice chancellor
to hear and determine any causes or questions which may be assigned to
him by the chancellor . But it shall not be necessary for the record to
show that the vice chancellor has jurisdiction , and neither of said judges
shall vacate or modify any judgment rendered by the other . The vice
chancellor shall also have power to grant injunctions , restraining orders ,
and attachments .
§ 778. If the chancellor and vice chancellor be absent , or cannot
properly preside , a special chancellor may be elected , as is provided in
case of the absence of a circuit judge or of his incapacity to sit .
entries.citation: Civil and Criminal Codes of Practice of Kentucky, 1883, p. 186

§ 779 [ 804, 806 ] . The chancellor shall appoint a commissioner , who
shall hold his office during four years , unless removed , as he may be , at
the pleasure of the chancellor ; and the commissioner may , with the
approval of the chancellor , appoint deputies . He shall discharge the
duties , and have the powers appertaining to the office , of a master in
chancery , and as provided by law and the rules of the court .
§ 780 [ 805 , 806 ] . Before entering upon the discharge of his duties ,
the commissioner shall appear in open court , and take an oath that he
will honestly , impartially, and to the best of his judgment , discharge all
the duties of his office , without favor or respect of persons , and render
true and just reports of all his actings and doings as such ; and each of
his deputies shall take such oath before entering upon the discharge of
his duties .
§ 781. Conveyances of real property , which are authorized by law to .
be made by a commissioner appointed by the court , may be made by the
commissioner . In cases of the partition of land , there shall be but one
deed , unless otherwise ordered by the court .
§ 782. The commissioner shall be entitled to a fee of one dollar and
fifty cents for each deposition of not more than six pages , and twenty-
five cents for each additional page . One hundred and fifty words shall
be deemed a page . He shall be entitled to a fee of five dollars for each
conveyance executed by him, to be paid by the grantor ; and , in case of
a sale under a judgment for a specific sum , the officer making the sale
shall add a sum sufficient to pay for the execution of a conveyance to
each purchaser . The said commissioner shall be entitled , for reports and
other services rendered under order of court , to such fees as the court
may allow . His fees shall be taxed as costs , and be collectible as fees
of clerks .
$ 783 [ 807 ] . The commissioner may administer oaths and grant cer-
tificates thereof ; and may take depositions , and in the discharge of his
duties may compel the attendance of witnesses by subpoena and attach-
ment .

§ 784. The chancellor shall appoint a receiver , who shall hold his
office during four years , unless removed , as he may be, at the pleasure
of the chancellor .
§ 785. It shall be the duty of the receiver to receive and pay , under
order of court , all moneys paid into court , unless immediately with-
drawn . He shall deposit all funds received by him in a bank organized
under a charter granted by this Commonwealth , or by the United
States , and having a capital of not less than two hundred thousand.
dollars actually paid in , and which shall have given bond , with good
sureties , approved by the court , in the penalty of one hundred and fifty
thousand dollars , payable to the clerk of the court , and conditioned to
pay all moneys deposited by the receiver , or his successor , upon any
check drawn by him under order of the court . An account shall be
kept by the clerk of all moneys paid into the court , and immediately
withdrawn by the party entitled thereto ; and an account of all moneys
withdrawn by the receiver .
§ 786. Before entering upon the discharge of his duties , the receiver
shall take an oath , in open court , that he will faithfully and honestly dis-
charge the duties of his office ; and shall give bond , with good sureties ,
to be approved by the court , in the penalty of two hundred thousand
dollars , payable to the clerk of the court , and conditioned that he shall
correctly and faithfully discharge all the duties of his office , and account
for all moneys received by him, and pay the same in pursuance of the
orders of the court .
$ 787. It shall be the duty of the receiver to take , keep , and deliver ,
under order of court , any property committed to his keeping by order of
the court ; and he shall give a bond , with good sureties , to be approved
by the court , in a penalty to be fixed by a rule of the court , conditioned.
that he will discharge his duties required in this section . The court shall
designate a place of deposit for such articles as may be committed to the
charge of the receiver under this section .

entries.ocr_text:
168 [TITLE XIX .LOUISVILLE CHANCERY COURT .
§ 788. Any person interested may sue on any of the bonds mentioned
in sections 785 , 786, and 787 , for a breach thereof ; and the court may , by
rule and attachment against the obligors therein , enforce their perform-
ance .
$ 789 . The receiver shall , on the first Friday in January and July in
each year , report to the court the aggregate amount of funds in court ,
with the style of each cause in which there may be money , and the
amount thereof ; and what sums have been ordered to be paid which
remain unpaid , and the names of the persons entitled thereto . And on
the first day of August in each year , he shall cause to be published , in the
daily paper in the city of Louisville having the largest bona fide circula-
tion, a statement of such sums as have remained in court for more than
two years , in what causes said sums remain respectively , and how long
they have remained ; and , if ordered out , the names of the persons
respectively entitled thereto-for which , and to pay the expense of the
publication , the receiver shall be entitled to the sum of ten cents for
each publication of each and every sum and name so published , to be
deducted from the said respective sums .
§ 790. One year after each publication provided for in section 789 ,
the court shall order the receiver to pay into the Treasury of the Com-
monwealth all sums which shall have remained in court during ten years
prior to said publication ; and the receiver shall pay such sums , and fur-
nish to the Auditor of the State a statement thereof ; and the treasurer
of the State shall execute duplicate receipts therefor , one of which shall
be filed and preserved in the Auditor's office , and the other be kept by
the receiver . The moneys so paid into the Treasury shall be account-
ed for as a part of the ordinary revenues of the Commonwealth ; but,
whenever the person entitled thereto , or his personal representative ,
shall apply to the court and make due proof of the justice of his claim ,
the court shall certify it to the Auditor , who , upon said certificate , shall
draw his warrant on the Treasury, in favor of the said person , for the
said sum without interest .
$ 791 . Upon the appointment of a receiver for said court , the former
receiver , or his personal representative , shall be required by the court to
settle such receiver's accounts ; and shall , when ordered by the court ,
pay the funds to his successor , and deliver to him all property in his
hands as receiver , and also the books and papers pertaining to the office ;
and the bank in which the funds of the said court shall be deposited
shall honor the checks drawn by the new receiver , as if drawn by the old
receiver .
$ 792. For the services required of the receiver in section 786 , he
shall be entitled to such compensation in each case as the court shall allow .

§ 793. A clerk of said court shall be elected at the same time , and
in the same manner , and shall have the same qualifications , as the clerk
of the Jefferson circuit court ; and vacancies in his office shall be filled
by the chancellor , and a new election be ordered by him , in the same
manner as vacancies in the office of circuit court clerk are filled . He
shall also hold his office for the same term , and be removable in the
same manner , as such clerk . He shall appoint such deputies as he
deems proper , subject to the approval of the court .
$ 794 [ 788 ] . The clerk shall keep a rule -docket , in which he shall
enter the names of the parties in each case , the issuing and return of
process , and a memorandum showing the substance of each order of
court , or step taken in the clerk's office ; and shall receive five cents
for each order or step so entered , to be taxed as costs .
$ 795 [789] . He shall also keep a trial -docket .
§ 796. The Chancellor , when from personal inspection he deems it
necessary , shall order the clerk to copy any rule -docket or order -book
of said court ; and shall allow him a reasonable compensation therefor ,
which shall be certified to the Auditor of the State , who shall draw
his warrant therefor on the Treasurer , to be paid out of any money not
otherwise appropriated .
§ 797. The clerk shall record , in proper books , the statements of all
settled accounts of fiduciaries made in said court ; and shall be entitled
to the same fees as clerks of county courts , for similar services- to be
paid by the fiduciary and charged to the trust estate .

allother services the clerk shall be entitled

$ 799 . A marshal for said court shall be elected when the clerk is
elected , and shall hold his office for the same term , and be removable in
the same manner . He shall possess the qualifications of a sheriff , and
shall be ineligible for the succeeding term . Vacancies in his office shall
be filled as vacancies in the office of clerk are filled .
§ 800. The marshal may appoint , with the approval of the Chancellor ,
two or more deputies , who shall be removable at the pleasure of the
chancellor or of the marshal . Before the marshal or any deputy shall
enter upon the discharge of his duties , he shall take the oath required of
sheriffs ; and the marshal shall give bond , to be approved by the chan-
cellor , in the sum of twenty thousand dollars , conditioned for the faithful
performance of his duties . He may also require from each deputy bond ,.
with surety , for indemnification against the acts of such deputy .
§ 801. It shall be the duty of said marshal , by himself and his depu
ties , to attend the court and execute the commands and orders thereof ,
and to execute all lawful precepts directed to him ; and he or any of his
deputies may , by an indorsement on any writ or order , appoint a bailiff
specially to execute the same . The marshal and his deputies shall have
the same power in the execution of process as sheriffs have , and shall be
liable to the same penalties .
§ 802. The marshal shall make all sales ordered by the court , unless
he be interested in the action .
803. If the marshal be interested in any action in said court , any
process therein may be directed to the sheriff of Jefferson county ; or ,
if he be interested , to the coroner or jailer thereof ; and any process
directed either to the marshal , or to the sheriff, may be executed by the
other , unless he be interested .
§ 804. For sales under any order or judgment of court he shall receive
five per cent . on the first three hundred dollars , and one per cent . on all
sums over that amount , except that his compensation for one sale shall
not exceed one thousand dollars . He shall also be allowed his costs for
advertising , and for the care of any personal property ; but no fee shall

§ 809 [782 ] . The defense to an action shall be filed within twenty
days after the service of the summons in Jefferson county , or within
thirty days after such service elsewhere in the State ; or , if the defend-
ant be constructively summoned , the defense shall be filed in sixty days.
thereafter .
§ 810 [ 783 ] . Every pleading subsequent to the answer shall be filed
in fourteen days after the pleading is filed to which it responds , but the
court may extend the time for pleading .
811 [ 784] . The filing of a pleading in the clerk's office within the
proper time , and causing it to be noted upon the clerk's memorandum-
book and rule -docket , shall be equivalent to a filing in court .
812 [785 ] . Interrogatories annexed to a pleading shall be answered
when the responsive pleading is required to be filed .
§ 813 [ 808 ] . All depositions shall be taken upon interrogatories , unless
the court shall otherwise provide by rule , or by an order in the cause .
§ 814 [ 809 ] . Depositions shall be taken and certified in the manner ,
and by the officers , directed in this Code , subject to the following mod-
ifications :
1. Depositions in the city of Louisville shall be taken before the
commissioner of the court , and no commission shall be required
therefor .
2. Notice to the adverse party shall not be necessary ; but the
court shall , by rule , prescribe days for the filing of interrogatories ,
and the time they shall remain in the clerk's office .
3. If a deposition be taken upon interrogatories , neither party
nor his agent or attorney shall be present at the examination of the
witness , unless he have notified the adverse party of his intention to
be present ; and in such case , the party in whose behalf the deposi-
tion is to be taken shall notify the adverse party of the time and
place of the taking .

§ 815 [ 791 ] . On the trial -docket shall be entered the actions that are
ready for trial. Said docket shall be called at such times as the court
may deem proper-when the actions thereon shall be heard , or the trial.
be postponed to a future day , or the action be remanded .
§ 816. Any motion made and assigned to a future day for hearing
may be placed upon the trial-docket .
§ 817 [ 793 ]. When a party consents that the pleadings of the oppo
site party may be taken as true , the action may be at once placed on the
trial-docket .
§ 818 [ 794 ] . Any party may place the action on the trial -docket when
the time allowed to the opposite party to plead has expired without such
pleading being filed : Provided that , if any issue shall have been formed ,
thirty days shall have expired since it was formed .
§ 819 [796 ] . Any application for a new trial , except for the cause
mentioned in the 7th subdivision of section 340 , shall be made within
fifteen days after the judgment is rendered ; and , if the application be
for the cause mentioned in that subdivision , it shall be made within
sixty days after the judgment .
§ 820 [ 797 ] . If grounds for a new trial be discovered after the expi-
ration of sixty days from the judgment , the application may be made ,
as is provided in section 344 , within two months after the discovery .
§ 821 [ 801 ] . The proceedings authorized by section 518 , and the
succeeding sections of Title XII , may be had in reference to any order
or judgment of the court , after the expiration of sixty days from its
rendition . A motion to vacate a judgment because of its rendition .
before the action could regularly be placed upon the trial-docket shall
only be made within three months after its rendition .
$ 822 [ 802 ] . Within thirty days after the service of an order to revive
an action , as is provided in section 502 , the party upon whom it is made
may show cause against the revivor ; and , if sufficient cause be not shown .
within that time , the action shall stand revived .

APPENDIX .
CONTAINING AMENDMENTS OF THE CIVIL CODE OF PRACTICE,
ADOPTED BETWEEN JANUARY 1, 1877 , AND
APRIL 24 , 1882, INCLUSIVE .
$ 52 . Service of Summons when Infants are Parties .
An act approved January 16, 1882 ( 1 Session Acts , page 11 ) , enacts :
§ 1. That if any of the parties upon whom summons is directed to
be served by section 52 of the Civil Code is a plaintiff, then it shall be
served on the person who stands first in the order named in said section ,
and who is not a plaintiff; and if all such persons are plaintiffs , it shall ,
on the affidavit of one or more of them showing that fact , be the duty
of the clerk of the court to appoint a guardian ad litem for the infant,
and the summons shall be served on such guardian .
$ 53 . Service of Summons on Persons of Unsound Mind.
An act approved January 16 , 1882 ( 1 Session Acts , page 11 ) , enacts :
§ 2. That section 53 of the Civil Code of Practice be amended by
adding at the end of said section the following , to -wit : Service of a
summons , by delivering a copy of it to the physician having charge of
a person of unsound mind , shall have the same effect as a service on the
person of unsound mind , if such physician gives a certificate , attested
by the officer delivering him the copy , that a personal service would , in
his opinion , be injurious to such person of unsound mind .
$ 337, subsection 2. Time for Preparing a Bill of Exceptions .
An act approved February 27 , 1878 ( 1 Session Acts , page 24 ) ,
enacts :
§ 1. That subsection 2 of section 337 of the Civil Code of Practice
be, and the same is hereby , amended by striking out the words " at the
close of the trial , " and inserting in lieu thereof the words " during the
term at which the judgment becomes final ."

$ 435 . Refunding Bonds of Legatees and Distributees.
An act approved April 13 , 1880 ( 1 Session Acts , page 77 ) , enacts :
§ 1. That section 435 , of chapter 3 , title 10 , of the Civil Code of
Practice , be , and it is hereby , amended so as to read as follows : " The
court may , in its discretion , without motion , and shall , on motion of any
party in interest , require the distributee or legatee , before receiving his
distributable share or legacy , if received within five years after the grant
of administration , to execute bond , with good surety to the Common-
wealth , conditioned to pay his proportion , not exceeding the amount
received by him, of any debt which may appear against the estate of the
decedent within said period . '
Title X , chapter 14. Sales of Real Property of Persons under Disability ,
& c.
Acts approved April 15 , 1882 ( 1 Session Acts , page 90 ) , and April
24 , 1882 ( 1 Session Acts , page 128 ) , enact :
§ 1. That when lands are held in trust by one person for the life of
another , with remainder over to a class of persons , or to any person not
ascertained or to be ascertained until the death of the person upon
whose life such estate for life is made to depend , or with power on the
part of such person for whose life such life estate is held by the trustee ,
to dispose by last will and testament , or by an instrument in the nature
of a last will and testament , it shall be competent for the circuit courts or
courts of like jurisdiction in the county in which such land or a part there-
of is situate , in an action to which all persons having a present or vested
interest in such lands are parties , to direct the trustee to either sell or
mortgage such land ; but in all action it must be averred and proven to
the court that such sale or mortgage would be beneficial to all the parties
concerned , and facts showing such benefits must be alleged and proven .
Any deed or mortgage executed under authority , or in pursuance of any
judgment rendered in any such action , shall be held and construed and
have the same effect as if executed by every person having a vested or
contingent interest in , or ownership of, such land , and as if executed by
all persons and classes who could take under the limitations or provisions
of said deed , or as devisees under the exercise of such power to devise
or appoint , and as if every claimant , present or future , under such deed
or power, was under no disability whatever . The proceeds of sales
authorized by this section shall be paid into court , and shall be re -in-
vested by the court , after first having , by appropriate order , provided
for the payment of costs and taxes , if any , in other property to be con-
veyed and held subject to the same limitations and trusts as the land
sold was held . The proceeds of all mortgages of such lands as are

classification.title: AN ACT to amend the Code of Practice in civil cases
classification.summary: This act amends Section 756 of the Code of Practice in civil cases. It specifies that appellants or cross-appellants must assign their errors, and only those errors will be considered by the court. Judgments will not be reversed or modified except for errors prejudicial to the substantial rights of the complaining party. The Court of Appeals may, upon motion and notice, allow a party to file a transcript of an additional portion of the record. The act also amends Section 757, allowing the appellee to move to dismiss an appeal if it appears to have been improperly granted or if the appellant's right to prosecute has ceased, with specific notice requirements.
classification.reasoning: The provided text contains two amendments to the Code of Practice in civil cases. The first amendment (Section 756) deals with the procedure for appeals, specifically how errors must be assigned and how additional parts of the record can be filed. The second amendment (Section 757) outlines the process for moving to dismiss an appeal. Neither of these sections, nor the introductory text, contains any language that explicitly or implicitly refers to race, segregation, or any discriminatory practices. Therefore, this statute is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'civil_laws' as it amends the 'Code of Practice in civil cases'.

gentle harness -hor - e ; that the plaintiff said to the defendant , that said horse was a safe,
gentle harness -horse , although , as the plaintiff knew , said horse was not safe or gentle ;
that the defendant bought said horse , believing said statement to be true ; that , a few days
afterward , the said horse , by reason of his being timid and wild , ran off with the defendant's
buggy , to which said horse was harnessed , and broke it to pieces ; that , on [ the next day ]
the defendant took said horse to [the plaintiff's house ] , and offered to return him to the
plaintiff , stating the misconduct of the horse , as above stated , and requested the plaintiff to
return to the defendant the note in the petition mentioned ; that the plaintiff refused to
eceive the horse , or return the note ; and that the defendant has ever since been , and is
now , ready and willing to return said horse to the plaintiff, upon receiving a surrender of
said note .
2. That the defendant was damaged by the breaking of his buggy , as above stated , in the
sum of three hundred dollars , and since said breaking has been afraid to use, and has not
used , said horse , and that the keeping of him has cost the defendant fifty dollars .
Wherefore the defendant asks for judgment against the plaintiff for three hundred and
fifty dollars , and for costs, and other proper relief.
(Verification as on page 185. )
G. H. , Attorney.

75. PETITION IN ACTION ON A PENAL BOND .
Petition .
LEE CIRCUIT Court .
The plaintiff states that he holds a bond on the defendant , dated the first day of March ,
1872, for the payment of twelve hundred dollars , conditioned that if the defendant should
transfer to the plaintiff, on or before the first day of January , 1874, eight shares of Northern
Bank stock , and from the date of said bond pay to the plaintiff the dividends that should be
declared and paid by said bank on eight shares of its stock , until the transfer was made ,
then the bond should be void , but otherwise remain in full force , which bond is filed as
part hereof marked A ; that the defendant has not transferred to the plaintiff eight shares
of said stock , nor paid any part of the dividends that have been declared and paid by said
bank on eight shares of its stock , whereby the plaintiff has sustained damage in the sum of
eleven hundred dollars ; that the said bank declared and paid a dividend of four dollars and
fifty cents on each share of its stock on the first day of July , 1872 ; a dividend of four dollars
and fifty cents on each share on the first day of January , 1873 ; a dividend of five dollars on
each share on the first day of July, 1873, and a dividend of seven dollars on each share on
the first day of January , 1874 ; and that on the first day of January , 1874, each share of
Northern Bank stock was worth one hundred and ten dollars .
Wherefore , the plaintiff prays judgment for eleven hundred dollars damages , and for other
proper relief. E. F. , Attorney .
(Verification as on page 185. )

SONAL PROPERTY .

against
C. D. , Defendant .

LEE CIRCUIT COURT .
The plaintiff states that by a contract made by him with the defendant on the tenth day of
August , 1875, the defendant agreed to deliver to the plaintiff, at his factory in the city of
Frankfort , on or before the first day of March , 1876, five thousand pounds of merchantable
hemp , for which the plaintiff agreed to pay to him five dollars for every hundred pounds of
said hemp , on its delivery ; that the current market price of merchantable hemp on the 1st
of March, 1876, in Frankfort , was seven dollars per hundred pounds ; and that the defendant
has not delivered any part of the hemp according to his contract ; whereby the plaintiff has
been damaged in the sum of one hundred dollars .
Wherefore , he prays judgment for one hundred dollars , and for other proper relief.
(Verification as on page 185. )

and for interest and costs of suit . I. J. , Attorney .

83. SUMMONS IN ORDINARY ACTIONS . (Sections 40, 43 , 44. )
The Commonwealth of Kentucky to the Sheriff of Lee County :
You are commanded to summon C. D. to answer , on the first day of the next October
term of the Lee circuit court , a petition filed against him in said court by A. B. , and warn
him that , upon his failure to answer , the petition will be taken for confessed , or he will be
proceeded against for contempt ; and you will make due return of this summons on the first
day of the next October term of said court .
Witness , E. F. , clerk of said court , this 10th day of September , 1877.
84. SUMMONS IN EQUITABLE ACTIONS .
E. F.
The summons is the same , except that the words " in equity " should be inserted after the
word " petition ."
85. SUMMONS IN ACTION IN COURTS OF JUSTICES , & c .
The Commonwealth of Kentucky to any Constable of Christian County :
You are commanded to summon C. D. to answer in my court at its next September term ,
or in the court of any justice of the peace in said county to which he may have this sum-
mons returned , at the first term thereof beginning not less than five days after the service of
this summons , the claim of A. B. against him for twenty -five dollars , arising upon con-
tract for goods sold by said A. B. to said C. D. , which claim has been filed in my office ;
and warn him that upon his failure to answer , the claim will be taken for confessed , or he
will be proceeded against for contempt .
Given under my hand , as a justice of the peace , this July 10, 1877. G. H.
In counties to which the act of March the 20th , 1876 (Sess. Acts , 121), applies , this form
of summons is adapted to actions for fifty dollars or less , upon causes of action arising on
contract , or for injury to property , of which subjects alone said courts have civil jurisdiction
in said counties . When , in those counties , the amount in controversy is more than fifty ,
and not exceeding one hundred dollars , exclusive of interest and costs , the summons must
be served ten days before the beginning of the court , and the above form must be changed .
accordingly . In counties to which the provisions of this Code apply , the summons may be
issued on any claim arising upon contract , or for injury to person , property , or character not.
exceeding fifty dollars , exclusive of interest and costs , and may pursue the above form .

against
C. D. , Appellee .

To the Sheriff of Lee County :
97. Order of Arrest .
In an action pending in the circuit court of Lee county between A. B. , plaintiff, and C.
D. , defendant , the plaintiff has filed an affidavit to obtain an order of arrest against the
defendant , in which it is specified that the plaintiff's claim against the defendant in the
action is $ 100.00 . You are therefore commanded to arrest the defendant , C. D. , and hold
.him to bail in the sum of $ 125.00 , and will make return of this order on the first day of
the next September term of the said court , together with the bail -bond , if any be taken
by you.
Witness E. F. , clerk of said court , this 15th July, 1877. E. F.

ORDERS IN COURT.
JUDGMENTS IN ORDINARY ACTIONS .
125. By Defaull in an Action on a Promissory Note for the Payment of Money .
The defendant having been duly summoned and failing to answer , it is adjudged by the
court that the plaintiff recover of the defendant one hundred dollars , the amount claimed in
the petition , with interest thereon at the rate of six per cent . per annum from the fifteenth
of June , 1877, until´paid , and his costs herein expended .
126. Upon Issue found by Court , or Fury, for the Plaintiff.
The parties appeared by their attorneys , and the cause having been submitted to the court
[or-upon the trial of this cause , the following jurors (name them ) were sworn to try the
issue (or issues ), and having heard the evidence rendered the following verdict : " We , the
jury , find for the plaintiff three hundred dollars . A. B. , Foreman ." Therefore ] it is
adjudged by the court that the plaintiff recover of the defendant , & c .
"
127. Demurrer to Petition Adjudged Good, and Judgment Rendered in Bar of the Action .
This cause having been heard on a demurrer to the petition , it is the opinion of the court
that the petition is insufficient . And the plaintiff saying nothing further in the case, it is
adjudged that he take nothing by his petition ; and that the defendant recover of him his
costs herein expended .
128. Judgment for Plaintiff , in Action for Personal Property .
Upon the trial of this cause , the following jurors [name them ] were sworn to try the issue
[ or issues ], and having heard the evidence rendered the following verdict : " We , the jury ,
find for the plaintiff the horse in the petition mentioned , and find its value to be two hundred
dollars , and assess the damages for its detention at twenty dollars . A. B. , Foreman :" it
is therefore adjudged by the court that the plaintiff recover of the defendant twenty dollars
damages for the detention of the horse in the petition mentioned , and the plaintiff's costs
' herein expended , and the said horse if to be had ; or , if not to be had , two hundred dollars ,
its value .

appellees for the sum of four hundred dollars damages with costs [or for the conveyance of
a tract of land , or for surrendering the possession of a horse , &c . , reciting the substance of
the judgment , or the part thereof which the appellants desire to be superseded ] , and the
appellants desire to supersede the said judgment [or the part of said judgment above men-
tioned ] .
Now we , A. B. , C. D. , and E. F. , principals , and N. O. and P. Q. , sureties , hereby
covenant to and with the appellees , G. H. and L. M. , that the appellants will pay to appel-
lees all costs and damages that may be adjudged against the appellants on the appeal , and
also that they will satisfy and perform the said judgment [or the part of the judgment before
recited ] in case it shall be affirmed , and any judgment or order which the Court of Appeals
may render or order to be rendered by the inferior court , not exceeding in amount or value
the judgment aforesaid [or the part of the judgment aforesaid ], and also pay all rents , hire ,
or damage , which, during the pendency of the appeal , may accrue on any of the property of
which the appellee is kept out of possession by reason of the appeal .
Witness our hands this 5th of July, 1877 .
(The bond is only required to be signed by the sureties .)

137. Supersedeas on Appeals to Circuit and Quarterly Courts .
FRANKLIN QUARTERLY COURT .
To A. B. , Esq . , Justice of the Peace for Franklin County :
J. S. having taken an appeal to this court from a judgment rendered by you on the first
day of July , 1877 , in the action of T. J. , plaintiff , against said J. S. , defendant , you are,
therefore , ordered to stay all proceedings upon said judgment and to transmit to the clerk's
office of this court all the original papers in said action .
Given under my hand this 10th day of July , 1877 .
J. C., Fudge of Franklin County Court .

2 CIVIL CODE- NOTES TO SECS . 8, II.
28. ERROR AS TO FORM OF ACTION .
a. An error of the plaintiff , by describing , in the caption , a petition , which states
only a legal cause of action , as a " petition in equity , " is not ground for demur-
rer ; but only authorizes the defendant to move for a transfer of the action to the
ordinary docket . Trustees , &c., vs. Forest , & c., 15 B. M. , 171.
b. According to a dictum in Lansdale vs. Mitchel , 14 B. M. , 348 , a petition in equity,
which states only a legal cause of action , should not be dismissed , but the pro-
ceedings should be " changed by a transfer of the action to the proper docket ."
A decision , however , has gone further , and held that , in such a case , judgment
should be rendered for the plaintiff , without a transfer of the action , or an
amendment of the pleadings . Robinson , &c. , vs. West, & c., 14 B. M. , 3 .
But , in an ordinary action , claiming only a legal right , which the defendant con-
troverted , and which the plaintiff failed to sustain by proof, it was held that the
court, to which the law and facts were submitted by consent , erred in rendering
judgment for the plaintiff , upon proof showing that she was equitably entitled to
the property in controversy ; and it was said : " In such case , after answer filed ,
unless the plaintiff , by leave of court , amends his petition , so as therein to pre-
sent a case of equitable jurisdiction , and thus give the defendant notice of the
true nature and ground of the claim and action , the court should render judg-
ment in favor of the defendant . Garret vs. Gault, 13 B. M. , 378 ; citing 26 of
Code of 1851, which is substantially the same as 2 8 of this Code .
In a subsequent case , however , it was held that , if the plaintiff in an ordinary action
shared a right to equitable relief, and the case be tried by the court below as an
equity-case , without objection by either party , the Court of Appeals will so con-
sider and decide it . Bates vs. Culver , 17 B. M. , 167.
II note 1. TRANSFER OF ACTIONS .
Under 9 of the Code of 1851, and 10 of the Code of 1854, which are identical,
and for which II of the present Code is substituted , it was held-
(1) That , in an ordinary action-
a. The defendant can have no right to a transfer of the case , unless his answer
present a valid equitable defense . Jones vs. Letcher , 13 B. M. , 365 .
b. If the answer state an equitable defense , it must be disposed of before the plaint-
iff can have judgment ; and the defendant is entitled to a transfer to the equity-
docket. Bosley vs. Mattingly, 14 B. M. , 89 ; Hackett , vs. Schad , 3 Bush , 353 .
c. If the answer state an equitable and a legal defense , and the former be adjudged
insufficient, the latter should be tried by a jury , unless a jury -trial be waived .
Smith vs. Moberly , 15 B. M. , 70. Both issues must be disposed of, before the
plaintiff can have judgment . The equitable issue should be submitted to the
jury for a special verdict, if there be no motion to transfer it to the equity -docket.
Petty vs. Malier , 15 B. M. , 591.
But , in Bennet , &c., vs. Titherington , &c., 6 Bush , 192, the defendants , having
pleaded an equitable and a legal defense , moved to transfer , not merely the
equitable issue , but the case, to the equity -docket . The motion was overruled ;
and , after a jury -trial as to the legal issue , and a verdict for the plaintiff , a judg
ment was rendered against the defendants , which seemed to disregard their equi-
table defense . Upon their appeal , the judgment was affirmed , apparently upon
the grounds- 1. That the defendants did not request a postponement , for fur-
ther preparation as to their equitable defense . 2. That it must be presumed that
the court below passed upon the whole case presented by the pleadings and
evidence . 3. That , according to the evidence in the case , the judgment was
right .

See notes to sections 8 and II.
EQUITABLE DEFENSE TO ORDINARY ACTION .
a. See sections 378, 518, 520, and notes thereto .

entries.ocr_text:
4 CIVIL CODE -- NOTES TO SECS . 17, 18.
18.
law , because judgment cannot be rendered against judgment in a court of law .
Pearce vs. Hedrick, 3 Littell , 109 ; Thompson vs. Ware , 8 B. M. , 28. And it
has also been adjudged that usury paid upon a decree in equity cannot be re-
covered back in equity, because decree will not be rendered against decree .
Thompson vs. Ware , supra , and Moran vs. Woodyard , 8 B. M. , 538. But it
has never been held that usury paid upon a judgment at law cannot be recovered
back in equity."
In Scott vs. Shropshire , above cited , the usury was collected in an equitable action ,
which was brought upon judgments at law , after returns of nulla bona upon exe-
cutions thereon , and in which " the court rendered formal judgments again for
the debts , as well as for the sale of " certain land of the defendant . It was held ,
however, that the cases cited in Ross vs. Ross had no application to Scott vs.
Shropshire , because " the proceeding in equity was merely in aid of the common
law judgments , and for the sole purpose of having them satisfied ."
BY WHOM ACTION TO BE BROUGHT GENERALLY .
a . Actions by assignees of choses in action . It has been decided by the Court of
Appeals in several cases , that the rule of the common law , which allowed the
legal owner of a chose in action to sue upon it for the use of the beneficial
owner , was abolished by the Code . According to those decisions , if the owner
of a chose in action , which is not legally assignable - such as an unwritten
agreement to pay money (Lytle vs. Lytle , 2 Met ., 127) , or a covenant to pay rents
and make improvements (Hicks & Gill vs. Doty , 4 Bush, 420)-should make an
equitable assignment of it ; or, if the payee of a note , the legal title to which
he can assign by indorsement and delivery, should assign it by delivery only
(Carpenter vs. Miles, 17 B. M. , 598 ; Gill vs. Johnson's adm'r , 1 Met . , 649 ; Perry vs.
Seitz , 2 Duv . , 124 ,) — the assignor and assignee should join in the action ; or , if
the assignor refuse to do so , the assignee may sue in equity , making the assignor
a defendant .
And it was held that , if it appear that a plaintiff , who claims to be the assignee of
a note , has no right to it , legal or equitable , he cannot maintain an action upon
it , though consent thereto be given by a defendant who is entitled to the note .
Worthington vs. Greer, & c., 17 B. M. , 741 .
In an action by the assignee of a note , the payee is not a necessary party upon
a plea of usury , or of payment to the payee . True vs. Triplett, 4 Met . , 58.
b. Actions by one for the use of another . In Fenwick vs. Phillips , 3 Met ., 87, the court
said : " It having been stated in the original petition , that the suit was brought
for the benefit of Phillips , he was thereby virtually made a party to the action ,
and had a right , by an amended petition , to assume the attitude of the real
plaintiff in the action ; consequently , the defendant's demurrer to the petition as
amended was properly overruled ."
And in Neely vs. Merritt, &c., 9 Bush, 346 , which was an action by the Common-
wealth for the use of several persons , the Court , after referring to Fenwick vs.
Phillips , said : " The petition in this case not only states the names of those
for whose benefit the relief is asked , but these names are also fully set out in
the caption . They are virtually parties plaintiff , and the technical objection that
they do not so style themselves cannot prevail upon demurrer ."
And in Commonwealth for Harris vs. Teal, & c., 14 B. M. , 29 , it was held that ,
under 60 of the Code of 1851 (which is the same as 21 of the present Code ,
with reference to the question under consideration ) , an action upon a constable's
bond , for official defalcation , may be brought in the name of the Commonwealth
for the use of the person injured .

CIVIL CODE- NOTES TO SEC . 18 . 5
c. Action by one upon a contract with another . It was held , in Lane vs. Kasey , 1
Met. , 410 , that the owner of tobacco may bring an ordinary action , in his own
name , upon a warehouseman's bond to the Commonwealth ; because , according
to the opinion of the court : " Under the former system of pleading , the action
must have been brought in the name of the Commonwealth , for the use and
benefit of the plaintiff . But the Code of Practice has changed this rule of
pleading , by requiring every action to be prosecuted in the name of the real
party in interest . The plaintiff was the person injured , and the real party in
interest, and therefore had the right to bring the action in his own name .
In Humphreys vs. Pearce , 1 Duv ., 237, it was held that the beneficial owners of a
note sued on by the payee should be co -plaintiffs .
""
A may maintain an ordinary action upon B's receipt to C for money to be deliv-
ered to A. Allen vs. Thomas , 3 Met ., 198.
A, being indebted to B, made a contract with C , whereby C agreed to pay said
debt : held that B could maintain an equitable action for the debt against A and
C. Garvin , &c. , vs. Mobley , & c. , I Bush, 48 .
On a covenant to A, to provide for her children , by will , they may sue in equity .
Smith vs. Smith , & c. , 5 Bush, 625 .
A contracted with B for a machine for C , which C received , agreeing to pay the
price charged by B. C having failed to pay the price , A paid it . In an ordi-
nary action by A and B against C , for said price , it was held that they were
properly joined as plaintiffs ; and that A could not have sued without making B
a defendant , or uniting with him as plaintiff . Dean , & c. , vs. English , 18 B. M. ,
132.·
In an
A, to pay certain debts , left money with B , which he deposited with C , a banker.
On B's check , C , by mistake , paid $ 70 too much to D , a creditor of A.
action by A against D for the $70, it was held that , as the deposit was a general ,
and not a special one , the right of action was in C. Keene vs. Collier , & c., I
Met. 415.
d. In an action by one of several depositors in a bank , upon a guaranty to pay the
deposits in said bank , the plaintiff alone being entitled to the money claimed by
him , the other depositors are not necessary parties, though their acceptance of
the guaranty may have been necessary to make it obligatory in favor of the
plaintiff . Steadman vs. Guthrie , &c. , 4 Met . , 147.
e. A corporation may sue in its corporate name upon an obligation to its " presi-
dent, directors , and company " (Lackey vs, Richmond , & c., Turnpike Co. , 17 B.
M. , 48 ) ; or to its " president and directors ." Graves , & c. , vs. The Lebanon
National Bank , 10 Bush, 26 .
f. An action against officers of a corporation , for misconduct , should , generally , be
brought by the corporation ; but , if it be under their control , one or more of the
stockholders may sue them in equity. Jones, assignee, &c. , vs. Johnson , &c., 10
Bush, 660 .
g. A county may sue for the unlawful destruction of its court -house .
County Court vs. Rankin & Tharp, 2 Duv ., 502.
Christian
h. In Masterson vs. Hagan , & c. , 17 B. M. , 325 , it was held that an action for mesne
profits could not be maintained in the fictitious name of John Doe , though the
land was recovered in his name before the adoption of the Code .
i. An administrator having taken a note payable to himself, for a debt due to his
intestate , it was held that the administrator de bonis non could maintain an equi-
table action on the note , the administrator not having charged himself, nor having
been charged , therewith upon a settlement . Burrus , & c. , vs. Roulhac's adm'x ,
2 Bush, 42. In a similar case it was held that the administrator de bonis non

could maintain an ordinary action . Maraman vs. Trunnell , 3 Met ., 146. (But
quere .)
k. Upon a deed of trust , providing for payment of the debts of the cestui que trust ,
any creditor may sue without making other creditors parties , and without alleg-
ing that the plaintiff is the only creditor . Goldburg and wife vs. Drabelle , 4 Bush ,
426. (Quere .)
1. See 21.
ACTIONS BY ASSIGNEES .
a. As to the distinction between a discount and a set-off, see Trabue's ex'r vs. Harris , I
Met. , 597.
a . Parties to such actions generally . See note a to ? 18.
a. As to actions by assignees of bills of exchange, see Kelly , & c., vs. Smith & Shot-
well, I Met ., 313 ; Spencer vs. Biggs , 2 Id., 123.
PROSECUTION OF ACTION AFTER ASSIGNMENT PENDENTE LITE .
a. This section " gives a discretion to the court, as matter of convenience to the
parties , and to be exercised in the furtherance of justice , " with reference to
allowing a case to proceed in the name of the original party , or the substitution
of an assignee . It never was intended to authorize a member of a firm to trans-
fer his interest to his partners , so as to make him a competent witness for them .
Dougherty vs. Smith , Wilson , &c. , 4 Met . , 279.
b. And it gives to the plaintiff's assignee the right to prosecute the action , not-
withstanding a compromise between the plaintiff and defendant , made after the
defendant had notice of the assignment . Cantrell vs. Hewlett , & c. , 2 Bush, 311 .
c. An order that an assignee be substituted as plaintiff , " on condition that he be
responsible for all costs , past and future , " not objected to by the defendant ,
releases the original plaintiff . Warner vs. Turner , 18 B. M. , 758.
d. See note to ? 32.
ACTIONS BY FIDUCIARIES .
a. An executor , who is authorized by will to lease out a ferry , during his life , is a
trustee for life ; and may sue , in equity , for a quiet enjoyment of the franchise ,
without joining with the testator's heirs . Newport , &c., vs. Taylor's ex'rs , 16
B. M., 781.
b. A trustee , to whom property is conveyed for the separate use of a married
woman , may sue for it without making her a party. McClanahan vs. Beasly , 17
B. M. , 117.
c. An action for a ward's land , against a tenant holding over under a lease from the
guardian , must be in the name of the ward . Powell, & c. , vs. Gossum, 18 B. M. ,
192. (Quere : See Crane vs. Anderson , 3 Dana , 119.)
d. An action for property of a ward , which , without any contract with the guardian , is
unlawfully detained by the defendant , must be brought in the name of the infant ,
by his guardian or next friend . Anderson vs. Watson , 3 Met., 509 .
e. A trustee may sue at law for the possession of property to which he holds the
legal title ; and may sue in equity for property which he is entitled to receive
and hold for the benefit of the beneficiary , without making the latter a party .
But a trustee under a deed , made to secure payment of money to other persons ,
cannot sue for a sale of the property without making them parties . Bardstown
& Lou . R. R. Co. vs. Metcalfe , 4 Met . , 199.

CIVIL CODE- NOTES TO SECS . 23 , 24, 25 , 26 . 7
23 .
24.
$ 25.
26.
WHO MAY BE MADE DEFENDANTS , GENERALLY .
a. The court may cause any person having an interest adverse to the plaintiff to
be made a defendant . Gibson vs. Higdon , 15 B. M. , 209 .
b. A trustee should be party to an action brought by cestui que trust against a person
to whom the trustee is alleged to have conveyed the property in violation of the
trust. Covington & Lex . R. R. Co. vs. Bowler's heirs , &c., 9 Bush, 484 .
It was held that the representative of a deceased defendant might be made
defendant by amended petition , notwithstanding 560 of the Code of 1854 (which
is substantially the same as subsection 3 of 500 of this Code ) . Greer vs. Powell,
&c., I Bush, 495 , 496 .
d. An action against the Auditor of the State , in his official capacity , may pro-
ceed against his successor , the change being suggested of record by proper
orders . Lindsey vs. Auditor of Ken ., 3 Bush , 231. See 18 B. M. , 13.
e. It was held that , if heirs make oath that an administrator is defending a suit
"in bad faith ," they have a right to be made parties , and to defend the action ,
under an act of 1846 (1 Sess. Acts, 53), which was not, in the opinion of the
court, repealed by the Code of the Revised Statutes . Lusk vs. Anderson's adm'r ,
I Met. , 428. See 15 B. M. , 208, which goes still further .
PARTIES UNITED IN INTEREST TO JOIN , OR BE JOINED .
a. Several distributees , though they may join in an action against an administrator
for a settlement of the decedent's estate , cannot join in an action on his bond
for their distributive shares . Pelly vs. Bowyer , & c., 7 Bush, 513 .
b. The trustees of each school district in a county have a separate cause of action
against a defaulting school commissioner and his sureties , for the benefit of the
teacher in each district ; and such action cannot be maintained by the commis-
sioner's successor in the name of the Commonwealth. Hammond vs. Crawford ,
&c., 9 Bush, 75.
c. The owner of a tract of land may sue , in one action , several persons having pos-
session of different parts of it . Woolfolk , & c., vs. Ashby , & c., 2 Met ., 288. But
see dictum in Sale vs. Crutchfield , 8 Bush, 646 .
ACTION OR DEFENSE WHEN PARTIES ARE NUMEROUS .
a. If one sue for himself and others , the presumption is that the others concur ,
Flint vs. Spurr, & c., 17 B. M. , 513.
b. An action in behalf of about fifty descendants of a decedent , to set aside deeds
made by him , may be brought by some for all . Hendrix , & c. , vs. Money , & c., I
Bush, 308. And see Louisville and Oldham Turnpike Road Co. vs. Ballard , 2 Met.,
165.
c. It was held , under the Code of 1854, that a creditor who sued to subject prop-
erty, which he alleged was conveyed by the debtor with the intent to prefer a
creditor , could not dismiss the action to the prejudice of other creditors . Saw-
yers , &c., vs. Langford, &c. , 5 Bush, 539. (See sections 428 , 438. )
d. For opinions as to actions by trustees , under mortgages or deeds of trust , made
to secure payment of debts , see Bardstown & Lou . R. R. Co. vs. Metcalfe , 4 Met.,
204 , 205 ; Robinson vs. Robinson's trustee, 11 Bush, 177.
JOINT ACTION AGAINST PERSONS SEVERALLY LIABLE .
a. One action lies against 27 guarantors who agreed to pay $ 100 each for goods
sold . Wild & Co. vs. Haycraft , & c., 2 Duv ., 309.
b. In an action for the value of street improvements ordered by a city to be
made at the cost of property -owners , they and the city may be joined as defend-

8 CIVIL CODE- NOTES TO SECS . 26 , 27 , 28 .
827.
ants (the city being contingently liable ) . City of Louisville vs. Henderson , &c., 5
Bush, 515.
c. A lessee may sue his sub -lessee , and the assignee of the sub -lessee , upon the cov
enant of the latter to pay rent to the plaintiff . Trabue vs. Me Adams , 8 Bush, 74.
SEVERAL ACTIONS AGAINST PERSONS JOINTLY LIABLE .
a . Upon the note of Q and D to A and the said D , A can sue both or either of the
payors . Quisenberry vs. Artis, 1 Duv . , 30 .
b. In Nichols , &c., vs. Burton, & c., 5 Bush, 320 , it was said that a judgment
against one of two partners bars a subsequent action against the partners ; and
that " our Code does not authorize separate suits at different times against the
partners ." Quere : According to the common law , the plaintiff could not, if
objection were made , maintain an action against A upon a joint contract of A
and B. But doubts have been expressed whether or not, even according to the
common law , a judgment against A , rendered without objection on account of
the non -joinder of B, would bar an action against B. Sayre vs. Coleman , 9
Dana , 174. It seems clear that the Code authorizes several actions against each
of several joint obligors . The Code of 1854 may not have authorized an action
against two joint debtors , after obtaining judgment against one of them . Clearly ,
this Code does not, as a general rule , except at the plaintiff's cost . (2687. )
But it seems that, according to the Code of 1854, and this Code , the objection is
not available, except upon the motion of the joint debtor against whom judg-
ment has been rendered (Dean, & c. , vs. English , 18 B. M. , 136) ; and that an
order sustaining his motion would not prevent judgment against his co -defend-
ant . (See note to ? 28.)
28. POWER AND DUTY OF COURT , AS TO MAKING PROPER PARTIES .
a. If the answer show a defect of parties , the court should require the plaintiff to
bring before it the necessary parties ; and , if he fail to do so, the action should
be dismissed without prejudice . Carpenter vs. Miles , 17 B. M. , 598 .
b. If a garnishee file an affidavit that the debt has been assigned , the alleged as
signee should be made a party . Forepaugh , Bishop , & c., vs. Appold & Sons, 17
B. M. , 625 .
c. In an attachment -suit , a prior lien -holder on the attached property is an indis-
pensable party . Mills vs. Brown , &c. , 2 Met ., 406 .
d. In an action , upon a return of " no property found , " to subject an equitable
title to land to the payment of the plaintiff's demand , the holder of the legal
title is a necessary party ; but the court should not dismiss the petition , for
failure to make him a party , without notice to the plaintiff , and reasonable time
to supply the defect . Ogle vs. Clough , 2 Duv . , 125.
e. Heirs of the vendor of an equitable title to land are necessary parties to an
action by his administrator , to enforce a lien for the purchase -money . Anderson
vs. Sutton , &c. , 2 Duv . , 486 .
f. An administrator , against whom a judgment has been rendered for a debt of
the decedent , is a necessary party to an action by a creditor of the decedent to
subject property alleged to have been fraudulently conveyed by the decedent .
Alexander and Lancashire vs. Quigley's ex'rs , 2 Duv ., 399.
g. In an equitable action by A , upon a return of nulla bona, against his debtor , B ,
and against C , the surety of D, who was the executor of a person who owed a
certain sum of money to B, the petition alleged that the executor , D , had com-
mitted a devastavit as to said money , and was insolvent ; and prayed for judg-
ment against the surety , C , for the amount thereof : dictum — the executor was an
indispensable party. Lee vs. Waller, & c. , 3 Met ., 61. (Quere .)

enforce the lien . Leacock vs. Hall, 13 B. M. , 210.
i. In an action by bond -holders against a city council , for a mandamus to compel
the levy of a tax for payment of interest on bonds of the city held by the plaint-
iffs , neither the tax -payers , nor the railroad company that assigned the bonds ,
nor other bond -holders , are necessary parties . Maddox , & c., vs. Graham &
Knox, 2 Met ., 65 .
j. Sureties upon a guardian's bond in the county court are not necessary parties to
an action on a bond given by the guardian , in the circuit court , in a proceeding
for the sale of the ward's land . Johnson's heirs vs. Chandler's heirs , 15 B. M. ,
584.
k. In an action by A on a note to him for the price of cattle which , according to
the evidence , belonged to A and B, it was held that the defendant's failure to
object by answer to the non -joinder was a waiver of the objection . Justice vs.
Phillips , 3 Bush, 200 .
7. In an action against one partner, for a firm debt , defendant's failure to object
by answer or demurrer , for want of necessary parties , is a waiver ; and a judg-
ment against him will not be reversed for want of proper parties . Albro vs.
Lawson , 17 B. M. , 642. (Quere , whether a partner has a right to object to the non-
joinder , as co -defendant , of a co -partner . Each partner being liable for the debt ,
has not the creditor a right to sue either of them , singly ? See 27, and note
thereto .)
m. A deed of trust for a married woman having declared that the property should
be liable for her debts , it was held that any creditor could sue to subject the
property , without alleging that he was the only creditor . Goldberg and wife vs.
Drabelle , 4 Bush , 426. (Quere .)
n. Can an undivided interest in property be attached without making the joint
owners parties ? Talbot vs. Pierte , 14 B. M. , 202 , 203.

o. A judgment against the plaintiff will not be reversed , at his instance , for the
want of proper parties whom he should have brought before the court .
dever, &c., vs. Vandever , & c., 3 Met ., 137.
INTERVENTION IN AN ACTION BY A CLAIMANT .
a. This section embraces ? 41 , and part of 257, of the Code of 1854 ; the latter
being made to conform to the former , as to a claimant of property in litigation being
made a party to the action in an attachment-case, as well as any other case.
This change should be considered , while examining the somewhat inconsistent de-
cisions in Schwein vs. Simms , 2 Met . , 209, and Taylor vs. Taylor, 3 Bush , 118 ;
which are , perhaps , the only reported decisions concerning sections 41 and 257
of the Code of 1854, except the decision in Miller vs. Desha and wife , 3 Bush,
212 ; in which it was held that the execution of a forthcoming bond , pursuant to
235 of the Code of 1854, estopped the obligor from suing the plaintiff in the
attachment -case , in which the bond was executed , after a sale of the attached
property and payment of the proceeds to the plaintiff .
INTERPLEADING .
a. The affidavit required by this section was designed to take the place of the bill of
interpleader under the old practice . Starling vs. Brown , 7 Bush , 164.
a. As to deposit in court in cases of interpleader . See sections 303, 304 .
