made a defendant , claimed the money : held that the court erred in instructing
the jury to find for A. Davis vs. Watkins , 2 Bush , 224 .
SUBSTITUTION OF PARTY FOR OFFICER SUED .
If the order of substitution be made without security , the plaintiff cannot after-
ward object , unless on the ground of surprise . Gunn vs. Gudehus , 15 B. M. ,
449 .
834. ACTIONS BETWEEN , OR BY , OR AGAINST , MARRIED PERSONS .
a . Inherited land is not separate property of a wife . Her separate property is that
alone over which an exclusive control is given to her by the instrument which
invests her with the title to it . Petty vs. Malier , 14 B. M. , 247.
Moore vs. Moore ,b. A wife may sue in her own name for an equitable settlement .
14 B. M. , 259 ; Wright , & c. , vs. Arnold, & c., Id., 642 .
c. Though the Code of 1854 authorized married women to sue without the aid of a
next friend (Matson vs. Matson , 4 Met . , 262 ; Hardin vs. Gerard , & c., 10 Bush,
259 ), it did not confer upon them any new right of action ; and it was held in
Matson vs. Matson that a wife could not sue her husband for the possession of
slaves devised to her for her separate use , which he refused to deliver to her, no
other ground for relief, legal or equitable , being alleged .
d. Pending an action by a wife for divorce and alimony, she may make a person
who has won money from her husband a defendant , and have judgment against
him . Cain vs. McHarry , 2 Bush , 263.
e. A husband having conveyed his wife's land (not her separate property ) , with a
warranty of title- which made his interest antagonistic to hers -it was held that
she had a right , under 49 of the Code of 1854, to sue him and his vendee for
the land . Hardin vs. Gerard , & c. , 10 Bush, 259.
f. The husband is a necessary party to an action against his wife , upon her con-
tract made before marriage . Beaumont vs. Miller , & c., 1 Met., 68 .
g. As to the husband's liability in such action , see 9 B. M. , 502 ; 1 Met ., 68 .
h. A woman , holding a note by gift causa mortis , married , and subsequently assigned
the note to a man , who brought an . action upon it , making her å co-plaintiff .
But the petition failed to allege that her husband consented to the assignment :
held that the petition should be dismissed without prejudice . Turpin vs. Thomp-
son, 2 Met., 420 .
i . If a husband desert his wife , she may sue in his name without his consent . If
they join in the action , and it be dismissed as to the husband , on the defend-
ant's motion, the wife may prosecute it in her own name . Stith vs. Patterson ,
& c., 3 Bush, 132.
j. Husband's insolvency , failure to provide for his family , and habitual drunken-
ness , constitute desertion according to the Code . Berry vs. Norris , 1 Duv ., 302 .
k. The mere suggestion of the marriage of female parties to an action is all that is
necessary to enable the court to make their husbands parties . Dickinson , & c.,
vs. Trout , &c., 8 Bush, 443 .
7. With the exception named in 2 51 of the Code [of 1854 modified by 34, subsec-
tion 4, of this Code ] , a wife cannot sue alone for an injury to her person or char-
acter . Anderson vs. Anderson , 11 Bush , 327. In the same case it was held that
the next friend of a female infant , who commenced such action for her , dum sola,
could not prosecute it against the wishes of her husband , whom she married pen-
dente lite , though he was also an infant .
m . It was held , under the Code of 1854. that an infant female plaintiff , having
married an'infant , pendente lite, the court , properly , entered him upon the record

ACTIONS BY INFANTS .
a. An infant suing , by his next friend , to rescind a contract , upon the ground of
fraud , and failing to prove it, cannot have relief upon the ground that he was
an infant . Bryant , &c. , vs. Pottinger , &c., 6 Bush , 473 .
b. As to actions which guardians may bring in their own names , see notes c, d, to
? 21.
c. An alleged written agreement of [infant] plaintiffs , to dismiss an action , cannot
be enforced upon a motion of the defendant , but must be pleaded. Hopkins vs.
Virgin , 11 Bush.
ACTIONS AGAINST INFANTS .
a. In the case of Smith vs. Ferguson , & c., 3 Met ., 426 , it was said that a judgment
against an infant -plaintiff , upon a counter -claim of the defendant , before defense
by the plaintiff's guardian ad litem would be erroneous . That opinion was founded
on 55 of the Code of 1854, which declares that " no judgment can be rendered
against an infant , until after a defense by a guardian ." But this Code places
infant-plaintiffs upon the same footing as adults . See sections 116, sub. 2 ; 126,
subs. 1 and 2 ; 391 ; 574 ; 743, sub. 1 ; 745 ; 756, sub. 2.
b. Judgment against infant -defendant , constructively summoned , before answer by
a guardian ad litem , though erroneous , is not void . Simmons , &c., vs. McKay ,
& c., 5 Bush, 25.
c. In an action against an infant, constructively summoned, it was , perhaps , necessary ,
under the Code of 1854, that both an attorney and a guardian ad litem should be
appointed to defend him . Covington and Lexington Railroad Co. vs. Bowler's
heirs , & c., 9 Bush, 468. This Code requires the appointment of an attorney ,
only, in such cases : 59.
38. (1)
a.
WHEN GUARDIAN AD LITEM MAY BE APPOINTED .
56 of the Code of 1854 declared that the appointment of a guardian ad litem
cannot be made until after the service of summons in the action . It was held ,
however, that an infant , who filed a petition to be made a defendant , need not
be summoned . Burch and wife vs Breckinridge , & c., 16 B. M. , 491 .
b. But an order of court , by consent of counsel , that infants be made parties , is
insufficient to authorize proceedings against them . Pond vs. Doneghy , &c. , 18 B.
M. , 558.
c. As to service of summons upon infants , see notes to ? 52.
(2) FEE OF GUARDIAN AD LITEM .
d. See the case of Williamson vs. Williamson , & c., 1 Met . , 303.
39. a. Limitation runs until the summons is issued (Butts vs. Turner & Lacy , 5 Bush,
435 ) ; even under the act of 1856, which required petition to be filed within six
months after conveyance by debtor , without saying anything about the sum
Cecil vs. Sowards , & c., 10 Bush, 96 .

667 , and notes thereto .
RETURN OF SUMMONS .
a. As to the officers to whom a summons may be directed , see ? 667 .
b. An officer's return , " executed on defendant ," is sufficient , as the court will pre-
sume that it was done properly ( Webber vs. Webber, 1 Met . , 18 ; Davidge , & c. , vs.
Colston , Id. , 145) ; but a return , " executed by reading the within to " the
· defendant , is insufficient . Davidge , & c., vs. Colston, supra .

e. The presumption in favor of a sworn officer's return does not , perhaps , arise as to
acts of his bailiff. Lloyd's adm'r vs. McCauley's adm'r , 14 B. M. , 535 ; Webber vs.
Webber, Met. , 18.
As to mode of making service, see sections 47 to 56, and notes to sections 47 and 52.
SERVICE UPON INFANTS .
a. In an action against an infant , an officer's return , that the summons was executed
upon the defendant and his mother, was held to be presumptively sufficient ,
under 81 of the Code of 1854. Webber, & c. , vs. Webber, & c., 1 Met ., 18.
b. But a bailiff's affidavit that he had served a summons on a mother and her infant
children was held to be defective , because it did not show that the father, or
guardian , could not be found . Lloyd's adm'r vs. McCauley's adm'r , 14 B. M. , 543-
c. The return of an officer , upon a summons against infant defendants , that he exe--
cuted it by delivering a copy to each person named in it , negatives the presump-
tion that he delivered a copy to any one else ; and such of the infants as were
not over 14 years old are not legally before the court. Beverley vs. Perkins , 1
Duv ., 251.
d. Observe, that this Code does not, as the Code of 1854 did , require service of sum--
mons upon defendants under 14 years of age .
SERVICE OUT OF THE STATE .
a. 419 forbids a personal judgment against the defendant , upon such service of
summons .
CONSTRUCTIVE SERVICE .
a . If a warning order be made upon a defective affidavit , is not the judgment void ?
Jackson vs. Speed, 2 Duv ., 428.
b. Can a defendant in a divorce -case be constructively summoned ? Meyar vs. Meyar ,
3 Met., 302.
c. If defendant be warned to appear at a term commencing within sixty days , the
proceedings are void . Brownfield , & c., vs. Dyer, &c. , 7 Bush , 505 .
Note to subsection 1.
ATTORNEYS FOR ABSENT DEFENDANTS .
a . It was held , under the Code of 1854, that a judgment against a defendant con--
structively summoned , before an attorney was appointed to defend , though
erroneous (Payne vs. Witherspoon , 14 B. M. , 270), was not void . Thomas vs.
Mahone , 9 Bush, 125. But quere whether such judgment would not be void
under this Code : compare 60 with Brownfield , & c., vs. Dyer, & c., 7 Bush, 505 .
Note to subsection 2.
b. It was held, under the Code of 1854, that the failure of an attorney for a
defendant constructively summoned to make defense or report correspondence ,
was not a reversible error. Brown vs. Early , 2 Duv . , 372 ; Thomas vs. Mahone ,
9 Bush, 125. And such seems to be the law under the present Code , except as
to persons under disability ; see subsection 7 of 59.
Note to subsection 3.
c. It was held , under the Code of 1854, that the consent of an attorney appointed :
for a defendant constructively summoned , and not actually employed by him,
did not authorize judgment . Anderson vs. Sutton , &c. , 2 Duv . , 489-90 .

WHEN CONSTRUCTIVE SERVICE TAKES EFFECT .
a. See sections 57, 102, 363, 364.
b. Observe that 91 of the Code of 1854 has been changed by inserting the words ,

c. It was held that , under ? 91 of the Code of 1854, the plaintiff , after thirty days .

? 59, sub . 5.
VENUE OF ACTIONS CONCERNING LAND .

b. In an action against partners , for a firm debt , the real property of the firm, in

VENUE OF ACTIONS , & C . , CONCERNING WILLS .
a. See 17 B. M. , 640 .
VENUE OF ACTIONS FOR DISTRIBUTION , & C .
a . This section applies to set -offs as well as to original actions .
Crocklin , 3 Met ., 322. (But see Millet vs. Watson , 4 Bush , 642. )

b. And it applies to an action for the sale of land , directed by will to be sold , and

VENUE OF ACTIONS BY WARDS AGAINST GUARDIANS .
a . This section was held to apply to an action by the administrator of a deceased
ward against the guardian , for money and property alleged to have been re-
ceived by him ; although the plaintiff did not ask for a settlement of the defend-
ant's accounts , but alleged that a settlement had been demanded , and that the
defendant had refused either to settle or pay . Stone's adm'r vs. Powell , 13 B.
M., 342.
b. Action on guardian's bond must be brought in the county in which he was quali-
fied , though he may have settled his accounts in a different county . Greenly vs.
Daniels , 6 Bush , 41 .
VENUE OF ACTIONS ON RETURN OF " NULLÀ BONA . ”
a. Observe, that the words , " the county in which the judgment is rendered , " are
used in 70, instead of the words , " the court from which the execution issued ,"
which were used in 474.
b. Under 474 it was held-
(1) . That an action might be maintained in the court from which the execution
issued , or in which the defendant resides or is summoned , for the sale of land in
any county . Nixon vs. Jack , 16 B. M. , 181.
(2) . That , after a return of nulia bona upon an execution issued by a justice of the
peace of M. county , an action to subject personal property of the defendant may
be brought in the circuit court of McC . county, the defendant being summoned
there . Austin vs. Payne , 7 Bush , 480 .
(3) . But that , after such return upon an execution issued by the quarterly court of
L. county, an action cannot be maintained in the court of common pleas of said
county , but must be brought in the quarterly court . Burrus vs. Cade, 10 Bush,
251.
(4) . That an action against a railroad company must be brought in the court from
.which the execution issued ; or in the county in which the defendant has its
principal office , or place of business , or in which its chief officer , or a depot
agent , resides . McDormant vs. Lou ., Cin . & Lex . R. R. Co. , 11 Bush , 386 .
871. VENUE OF ACTIONS AGAINST BANKS AND INSURANCE COMPANIES .
a. This section was held to apply to an insurance company , the charter of which ,
granted in 1839, required all suits against the company to be brought in the
Jefferson circuit court . Howard vs. Ky . and Louisville Mutual Ins . Co. , 13 B.
M. , 282 .
875 .
278.
b. Answer to the merits of an action waives objection to jurisdiction of the court
as to locality of a corporation or its officers . Baker vs. L. & N. R. R. Co. ,
4 Bush , 619 .
VENUE OF ACTIONS AGAINST ABSENT DEFENDANTS .
a. If all of several defendants be sued as non -residents , the action must be brought
in a county where one of them has property . But if one or more reside in this
State , the action should be brought in a county in which one of them resides or
is summoned . Nixon vs. Jack, 16 B. M. , 180.
TRANSITORY ACTIONS .
a . For exceptions to the provisions of this section , see sections 81, 542.
b . The Code authorizes an attachment to issue from the county in which the defend-
ant resides , or is summoned , upon his land in any county . Nixon vs. Jack, 16
B. M., 174

c. Jurisdiction cannot be given over defendants in another county , by improperly
uniting with them a defendant who resides or is summoned in the county in
which the action is commenced . Randall vs. Shropshire , 4 Met., 327.
d. And though the defendant , summoned in the county , be a necessary party-for
instance , the assignor of an account - if the petition fail to state , or if the evi-
dence fail to sustain , a cause of action against him , no judgment should be ren-
dered against defendants summoned out of the county . Fernold vs. Speer, 3
Met. , 459 ; Meguiar vs. Rudy, 7 Bush , 432 .
e. The filing of an answer , without objection to the jurisdiction of the court , by
a defendant who resides and is summoned out of the county in which the action
is brought , does not give jurisdiction as to other defendants who are summoned
out of it . Kennedy vs. Davenport , 13 B. M. , 167.
f. And , though the plaintiff's petition , filed in the J. circuit court , state a cause of
action against A and B, judgment should not be rendered against B, upon
service of summons in D. county , before service upon A in J. county , unless it
be shown that A or B resided in J. county when the action was commenced .
Pottinger is. Mayfield , 14 B. M. , 647 .
g. See notes to 2 517, as to rendering judgments against defendants summoned out
of the county.
TRANSITORY ACTION AGAINST ONE DEFENDANT .
a . In an action against one defendant-
( 1) . He cannot be lawfully summoned out of the county in which it is brought ,
unless he remove therefrom after its commencement ; and going to another
county, as a witness , is not a removal . Raymon vs. Reed, 16 B. M. , 345 .
(2) . The record must show his removal . Ruby vs. Grace, 2 Duv ., 540 ; Dyas vs.
Lindsey , 4 Bush , 349 .
(3) . His appeal to the Court of Appeals is not such an appearance as authorizes
judgment against him on return of the cause . Dyas vs. Lindsey , 4 Bush, 349 .
(4) . The defendant was sued in G. county and summoned in H. county . Evidence
that he lived in G. county when the action was commenced , and still lived there ,
did not authorize a judgment by default against him in G. county. Dyas vs.
Lindsey , 5 Bush, 506 .
TRANSITORY ACTION AGAINST SEVERAL DEFENDANTS .
a . For decisions concerning ? 108 of the Code of 1854, which has been materially
changed by ? 80 , see I Bush , 357 ; 10 Id. , 51. Also see notes to 3 78 .
283. (1) JOINDER OF CAUSES OF ACTION GENERALLY .
a . It was held that ? III , which is substantially the same as 83, only applied to
causes of action existing when the action was commenced ; and , consequently ,
that , in an action for slander , the plaintiff could not complain , by an amended
petition of words spoken pendente lite . Taylor vs. Moran , 4 Met ., 127.
But , as to lien -debts maturing pendente lite, see ? 135.
b. A sued B, C , and D , 1, to recover a debt due from B to A ; 2, to enforce a
mortgage made by B to A, to secure the debt ; 3, to obtain immediate possession
of the mortgaged property , which B had subsequently mortgaged to C and D :
held to be a misjoinder . McKee vs. Pope, 18 B. M. , 555.
c. A, the owner of a female slave , hired her , for a year , to B and C , who hired her
to D. D , with E as surety , gave a written agreement to B for payment of the
hire , and a return of the slave . D killed her and absconded . Before the end
of the year, A, B, and C joined in an action against E for her hire and value ;
and for the purpose of attaching the property of E , who , as the petition alleged ,

3. That , as to the value of the slave , the action must be regarded as an action

e. Petition against T and B alleged - 1st, that the note sued on , which was executed
by T, in his own name , was given for property purchased by T as agent for B ,
whereby B became liable to the plaintiff ; 2d , that T fraudulently concealed his
agency , &c. , and thereby became liable to the plaintiff : held to be a misjoinder .

directly connected with the contract , may be joined . Jones vs. Johnson , 10 Bush,

JOINDER OF CAUSES OF ACTION CONCERNING REAL PROP-
ERTY .
a. The owner of a tract of land may bring one action for the possession against
several persons occupying separate parts of it . Woolfolk vs. Ashby , 2 Met ., 288 .
This seems to be sound law , notwithstanding the dictum in Sale vs. Crutchfield ,
8 Bush, 646 .
b. Plaintiff , in an action for land , has the option to sue for rents or not ; and , if he
do not, may recover them in a separate action . Burr et al. vs. Woodrow , I Bush,
602 ; Walker vs. Mitchell, 18 B. M. , 541.
c. An action for land , and for damages for its detention , was held to be a bar
against a subsequent action for rents and profits , although the plaintiff had
recovered only one cent damage . Walker vs. Mitchell, 18 B. M. , 541.
d. But , in action for land , and for damages for being kept out of possession , a
judgment for the land , and for one cent damage , does not bar an action for cut-
ting timber on the land , it being proved that this question of damage was not
tried in the former action . Burr et al. vs. Woodrow , 1 Bush , 602.
months ,e. A petition alleging that defendant had been in possession of land
and praying for judgment for the land , and $ 500 damages for rent of the same ,
was held not to apply to rents accruing pendente lite . Shean vs. Cunningham , 6
Bush, 123.
f. In such cases , a judgment for rent may be reversed , without affecting a judgment
for the land . Shean vs. Cunningham , 6 Bush , 123.
CAUSE OF ACTION MAY BE STRICKEN FROM PETITION .
a. A petition having alleged a breach of contract , the plaintiff filed an amended
petition alleging fraud in obtaining the contract : held, that , by his amended
petition , he abandoned the original cause of action ; and that , as a plaintiff may

strike from his petition any cause of action before a final submission of the case ,
this plaintiff had a right , by thus virtually striking out his original cause of
action, to present a different one , though it could not have been joined with the
original one . Hord vs. Chandler , 13 B. M. , 403. But quere ; and see Hancock vs.
Johnson, 1 Met. , 242. And see, now , ? 113, sub . 4 .
85, 86. a. Objection to misjoinder of causes of action must be made in the court below
(McKee vs. Pope , 18 B. M. , 555) , by motion to require plaintiff to elect . Sale
89 .
vs. Crutchfield , 8 Bush , 646 ; Dragoo vs. Levi, 2 Duv . , 520 ; Hord vs. Chandler , 13
B. M. , 403 .
b. Filing an answer is a waiver of the objection ; and the waiver remains , though
the answer be withdrawn . Chiles vs. Drake , 2 Met . , 154.
c. If the plaintiff offer an amended petition , presenting a new cause of action , the
defendant may object to the filing of it ; or may let it be filed , and move to
strike out one of the causes of action . Hancock vs. Johnson , 1 Met., 242 .
d. If there be a misjoinder of causes of action , the plaintiff should be required to
elect which of them he will prosecute ; and , if he fail to do so , the court should
strike out one of them ; and , if the plaintiff then refuse to proceed , should dis-
miss the action . Dragoo vs. Levi, 2 Duv ., 520 .
WHAT PLEADINGS ARE ALLOWED .
a. The Code of 1854 did not require, but forbade parties to form an issue , by plead-
ings , unless it could be formed by petition and answer ; or by an answer , stating
a set -off or counter -claim , and a reply thereto . This Code not only permits, but
requires , parties to " form a material issue concerning each cause of controversy ,"
before trial. 114.
Sections 89 and 114 apply to equitable as well as ordinary actions . Observe , in
connection with them , sections 90, 95 , 98, and 99, which require a statement of
the facts relied on by the plaintiff or defendant ; and 2 119, which forbids the
statement in a pleading of " the evidence relied on by a party ; ” and ₹ 685,
which forbids an action for a discovery , excepting certain actions mentioned in
sections 439 and 685 ; and sections 140 and 143, which authorize a party to annex
to his pleading interrogatories to his adversary ; and subsection 10 of 606 ,
which declares that " a party may compel an adverse party to testify as any other
witness ."
90 . PETITIONS .
1. Forms , not substance, dispensed with by the Code. Page 18.
2. Facts must be stated, not inferentially , nor argumentatively , but positively . Page 18.
3. Statements of legal conclusions are immaterial , except in certain cases after a trial .
Page 19.
4. Facts constituting a cause of action , and not evidence of the facts, must be stated.
Page 21.
5. Statement of a prima facie cause of action is generally sufficient . Page 21.
6. Allegations concerning notice, demand , readiness, and tender. Page 22.
7. STATEMENT OF CAUSE OF ACTION :
( 1.) In actions for injury to character . Page 23.
(2.) In actions for injury to theperson . Page 23 .
(3.) In other actions for tort . Page 23.
(4.) In actions on return of nulla bona. Page 23.
(5.) In actions by vendor of land to enforce lien for purchase -money. Page 23.
(6. ) In actions to rescind contract concerning sale of land. Page 23 .

entries.ocr_text:
18 CIVIL CODE- NOTES TO SEC . 90 .
(7.) In actions upon implied contracts. Page 24.
(8. ) In actions upon contracts generally . Page 24 .
(9.) In actions under statutory provisions . Page 25.
(10.) Miscellaneous cases. Page 26.
8. Petitions in actions upon writings . Page 26.
9. Proof and allegations must correspond. Page 26.
10. Effect of unnecessary allegations .
11. Prayerfor relief. Page 27.
Page 27.
1. FORMS , NOT SUBSTANCE , DISPENSED WITH BY THE CODE .
a . The Code made no change of the law concerning facts which constitute a cause
of action . Hill vs. Barrett , 14 B. M. , 85 ; Richmond , & c., vs. Rogers , 7 Bush,
535.
b. "A petition which goes for a forcible injury should state such facts as would be
equivalent to an action of trespass at common law . If the trespass be waived ,
and the petition go for negligence or want of skill , it should state facts which
are equivalent to an action in case according to common law principles ." Kountz
vs. Brown , 16 B. M. , 584 .
c. In an action against a constable , for the value of a slave who escaped from his
custody , it was held that the plaintiff might recover for the failure to use ordi-
nary diligence , though the petition alleged gross negligence . Tudor vs. Lewis , 3
Met. , 378 .
d. A demurrer will not be sustained , if the petition state a cause of action in any
form (Hill vs. Barrett , 14 B. M. , 85) ; to any extent , legal or equitable (Foster
vs. Watson , 16 B. M. , 387 ; Robinson vs. West , 14 B. M. , 3 ; Lansdale vs. Mitchell,
14 B. M. , 348) ; though the style of the action be improperly stated in the peti
tion . Trustees , &c., vs. Forrest , 15 B. M. , 168.
e. But it was held that a plaintiff claiming a legal right , and failing to sustain it by
proof , could not have relief , though , according to the evidence , he was entitled
to equitable relief . Garrett vs. Gault, 13 B. M. , 380 .
2. FACTS MUST BE STATED , NOT INFERENTIALLY , NOR ARGUMENTATIVELY , BUT
POSITIVELY .
a. An allegation that the plaintiff " believes ," or " learns , " that a fact exists ; or
that the defendant " has admitted " the existence of a fact , is not sufficient .
Patterson vs. Caldwell , 1 Met ., 491 ; Walters vs. Chinn , Id., 502 ; Garnett vs. Fin
nell, 2 Duv ., 166 ; 2 Bibb , 608 ; 3 Dana , 74.
b. In an action against the surety on a bail -bond , the petition must allege that the
judgment is unsatisfied . It is not sufficient to allege that the principal had not
surrendered himself, nor been surrendered by his bail , and that a capias had been
returned not found . Jones vs. Baum, 2 Met . , 490. And see 9 Bush, 745 .
c. The charter of Louisville made it the duty of the city council to license as many
taverns as might be necessary for public accommodation , and such license gave
the right to retail liquor . In an action by a tavern -keeper to compel the city
council , by mandamus , to give him a license, he alleged that the defendants
claimed the right to prohibit the retailing of liquor , and had refused a license
to the plaintiff on that ground . The petition was held to be defective , because
it did not allege that the plaintiff's tavern was necessary for public accommoda-
tion . City of Louisville vs. Kean , 18 B. M. , 16.
d. In an action against a constable , under a provision of the Revised Statutes which
made him liable , when a claim was placed in his hands , " for any injury in fail-
ing to collect the same , when , by proper diligence , it might have been collected ,"
an allegation that , " if he did not collect the same , it was his own neglect and
fault ," was held to be insufficient. Walters vs. Chinn, x Met ., 503.

CIVIL CODE- NOTES TO SEC . 90 . 19
e. An allegation that a horse was branded " U. S. " is not equivalent to an allega
tion that the horse was the property of the United States . Plummer vs. Newdi-
gate, 2 Duv ., 1.
f. But , in an action against an election -judge , an allegation that he " knowingly
and willfully , with the intention unlawfully to deprive the plaintiff of his right
to vote , refused to receive his vote , and thereby unlawfully prevented him from
voting ," was held to be sufficient . An express averment that the defendant
knew that the plaintiff was legally entitled to a vote was held to be unnecessary .
Morgan vs. Dudley, 18 B. M. , 713.
g. And in an action against a city for paving done under a contract by which the
plaintiff agreed with the city to look to property -holders for his pay , though it
was held that the petition must state facts showing that the city had failed to
take the necessary steps to make the property -holders liable to the plaintiff ; yet ,
allegations , that , in a suit of the property -holders against the plaintiff and the
city , it was adjudged that the property -holders were not liable for said paving ,
and that the plaintiff was enjoined from collecting pay therefor, which judgment
is in full force and unreversed , were held to be sufficient . Kearny vs. City of
Covington , I Met., 339 .
h. And it was held that an allegation , that the plaintiff was , by appointment of the
Federal Court , made the assignee of H. , authorizes the inference that H. had
been declared a bankrupt , and that his estate had regularly been assigned to the
plaintiff , and is prima facie sufficient . Boone vs Hall, 7 Bush , 66 .
3. STATEMENTS OF LEGAL CONCLUSIONS ARE IMMATERIAL , EXCEPT IN CERTAIN CASES
AFTER A TRIAL .
a. An allegation , in an action against guarantors , that , by the terms of the guar-
anty, its publication and delivery , the defendants waived notice of its acceptance ,
is immaterial , being an allegation , not of any fact , but of a legal conclusion .
Steadman vs. Guthrie , 4 Met ., 159.
b. An allegation , in an action for the breach of a covenant to furnish a slave winter
and summer clothing , that the defendant " did not clothe said slave properly , ”
is an allegation of a legal conclusion , and immaterial . Skillman vs. Muir, 4
Met., 282 .
c. An allegation , in an action by a city to enforce a lien for taxes , that the defend-
ant " was and is indebted for city taxes , " & c., is immaterial : the petition should
have stated facts showing indebtedness , viz : that the taxes were assessed , &c.
City of Louisville vs. Bank of Ky., 3 Met . , 148.
d. Allegations , in an action against an election -judge , that he willfully , & c., “ re-
fused to receive the plaintiff's vote , or to permit it to be recorded , whereby he
could not and did not vote ," were held to be immaterial : the petition should
have stated the concurrence with the defendant of the other judge , or of the
sheriff, such concurrence being necessary to prevent the plaintiff from voting .
Caulfield vs. Bullock , 18 B. M. , 494. (Quere .)
e. An allegation , that the plaintiff is one of the heirs of A, was held to be a state-
ment of a legal conclusion which need not be denied . Larue vs. Hays, 7 Bush, 53.

classification.summary: This section of the Civil Code provides notes and case law interpretations related to Section 90, focusing on the sufficiency of legal allegations in various civil actions. It clarifies what constitutes a sufficient statement of facts versus a legal conclusion in pleadings, citing several court cases.

entries.ocr_text:
20 CIVIL CODE- NOTES TO SEC . 90 .
law presumes a fact, it need not be stated in pleading, " says : " So , if a person
plead that he is heir to A , he need not say either that A is dead , or that he had no
son." I Chitty's Pl. , side page , 253 ; American edition , 1837. And Mr. Chitty
gives several illustrations , showing that the common law was liberal in allowing
latitude to avoid prolixity . i Ch . Pi., side pages 262 , 333 , 567. There seems to
be reason for doubt, whether the Code has so changed the common law as to
require a lawyer to state in a pleading "the genealogy of the patriarchs . ”
f. In an action against the indorser of a note , payable in Illinois , an allegation that,
by the law of Illinois , the defendant was indebted to the plaintiff in the amount
of the note, was held to be inmaterial : the plaintiff should have stated the law
of Illinois , as a fact . Roots vs. Merriwether , 8 Bush, 397 .
*
g. Our Constitution declares that sheriffs shall be elected in August , and “ enter
upon the duties of their respective offices on the first Monday in
January next succeeding their election ." ( Art . 6, sec. 4. ) And the Revised
Statutes declared that , if the official bond of a sheriff " is not given within a
month of the time when his appointment ought to take effect , the
office shall be considered vacant ." ( Ch . 71, 12.) Sureties of a sheriff, who
executed bond , February 11, 1867, claiming that the bond was void , because it
was not executed " within a month of the time " required by law , alleged that
the sheriff's election "6.Fought to have gone into effect on the first Monday of
January , 1867," &c.: held, that the allegation was immaterial ; and that the
plaintiffs should have alleged that the election was held in August , 1866, if they
relied on that fact. Commonwealth vs. Cook, 8 Bush , 220.
h. In an action on a note by one of two payees , his allegation , that he was the
legal owner of the note , was held to be immaterial : he should have alleged an
assignment from his co -payee . Quisenberry vs. Artis, 1 Duv ., 30 .
i. Allegations that the plaintiff purchased an entry of land , and holds the equitable
title , without alleging a deed or assignment to him , are immaterial . Craig vs.
Horine , 1 Bibb , 113.
j. It was said , however , in Fritz vs. Tudor, 2 Duv . , 174 : “ But , as adjudged in New
York , in Corwin vs. Corwin , 9 Barb., 219 , under a Code just like ours in this
particular , a simple allegation of title , untraversed by the answer , may be con-
sidered an admitted fact ."
k. A person having signed a note , as trustee for another , it was held that the ques-
tion , whether he meant to bind himself, or the trust property , was one of law ,
to be decided by the court, in view of the language of the note , the circumstances
under which it was executed , and the situation of the parties ; and that an alle-
gation , as to the intention with which it was executed , was an allegation of a
legal implication , of which no denial was necessary . Wells vs. Lewis, 4 Met. , 355.
1. A petition was held to state a cause of action , although it erroneously described
a writing , on which the action was founded, as a note . Bradley vs. Mason , 6
Bush , 602 .
m . And , even under the old practice , a party was held to be entitled to the benefit
of the facts , notwithstanding his erroneous statement , against himself , of a legal
conclusion . Trimbles vs. Harrison , 1 B. M. , 142 ; Oldhams vs. Jones, 5 Id., 462 .
n . But , in an action under a statute , giving damages in certain cases for causing the
death of a person " by willful neglect , " it was held to be sufficient for the plaint-
iff to allege that the death was caused by the defendant's " willful neglect , "
without stating facts showing the neglect . Chiles vs. Drake , 2 Met ., 146.
. If, however , a pleading , though it do not state facts constituting a cause of action ,
or a defense , state legal conclusions embracing such facts , the defect may be
cured by a trial , and a verdict for the party so pleading ; nor should he , if the

classification.title: Civil and Criminal Code 1883, Section 90 Notes
classification.summary: This section of the Civil Code provides notes and case law interpretations related to Section 90, focusing on the requirements and sufficiency of allegations in legal pleadings. It discusses common law principles regarding pleading facts presumed by law, the materiality of certain allegations in specific legal actions (like those involving notes, sheriff bonds, land entries, and wrongful death), and how the code might have altered common law practices. It also touches on how courts interpret allegations of title and legal conclusions.

entries.ocr_text:
CIVIL CODE- NOTES TO SEC . 90. . 21
judgment be against him , be precluded from reversing it for erroneous instruc-
tions . Bently vs. Bustard, 16 B. M. , 690–91 ; Skillman vs. Muir , 4 Met ., 283 ;
Barlow vs. Wiley, 3 A. K. Mar. , 460 .
4. FACTS CONSTITUTING A CAUSE OF ACTION , AND NOT EVIDENCE OF THE FACTS ,
MUST BE STATED .
a . Before the adoption of the Code , it was allowable , and usual , for plaintiffs , in
bills in chancery , though not in declarations at law, to state the evidence on which
they relied , as well as the facts constituting their causes of action . The Code
of 1854 did not , as ? 119 of this Code does , forbid the statement in a pleading of
" the evidence relied on by a party ."
! But it never was sufficient for a party to state in his pleading merely the evidence
relied on to maintain his action or defense (Plummer vs. Newdigate , 2 Duv .,
1 ; Haggard vs. Hays ' adm'r , 13 B. M. , 176 ; Jones vs. Bunn , 2 Met ., 492 ; Muh-
ling vs. Sattler , 3 Met ., 285 ; Alexander & Lancashire vs. Quigley , 2 Duv . , 405 ) ;
unless , at any rate , the evidence stated raise a conclusive presumption of a fact
entitling the party to relief . Gregory vs. McFarland , Duv . , 59 ; Williams vs.
Gordon , 11 Bush ; see, however , Lewis vs. Quinker , 2 Met ., 284.
5. STATEMENT OF A PRIMA FACIE CAUSE OF ACTION IS GENERALLY SUFFICIENT .
a. In an action for the value of property converted by the defendant , it is sufficient
to allege that the property was forcibly taken from the plaintiff , without alleging
that he owned it, as possession implies title prima facie. Eversole vs. Moore , 3
Bush, 51.
b. In an action by a surety against the payee of a note for relief on account of
indulgence to the principal , the defendant's knowledge of the suretyship must be
presumed , and need not be alleged . Champion vs. Robertson , 4 Bush , 17.
c. In an action for an injury caused by the defendant's negligence , the petition need
not allege that the plaintiff was not negligent . Louisville and Portland Canal Co.
vs. Murphy , 9 Bush, 529.
-d. In an action for dower, allegations that the plaintiff's husband was legally seized ,
and sold the land without her consent , and that plaintiff is entitled to one third
for life , & c . , were held to be sufficient , though she failed to allege that she was
the vendor's wife at the time of the sale . Fritz vs. Tudor , 2 Duv . , 174. Quere ,
and see Yancy vs. Smith , 2 Met . , 411.
e. In an action by a husband's creditor , to subject a wife's chose in action , the
plaintiff need not allege that the wife is not entitled to an equitable settlement ,
that being matter of defense . Tobin vs. Dixon and wife , 2 Met. , 424 . But see
Elliott vs. Waring , 5 Mon. , 340 , 341 .
f. In an action against the trustees of a town , upon their subscription of stock ,
which the Legislature had authorized them to make , with the consent of the citi-
zens , an allegation that the citizens had given such consent was held to be
unnecessary . Shelbyville Trustees vs. Shelbyville , & c,, Turnpike Co. , 1 Met ., 54.
g. Though the payor and payee of a note have the same name , the plaintiff , being
an assignee of the note , need not allege that they are different persons . The
legal presumption is in favor of the validity of the contract. The fact that they
are the same person is matter of defense . Cooper vs. Poston , 1 Duv ., 92 .
-h. A petition in the name of A. J. Morrison & Co. , upon a bill of exchange payable
to A. J. Morrison & Co. , was held to be good on demurrer . If the firm em-
braced more than one person , the defendant should have shown it ; and might then
have compelled an amendment by motion . Morrison & Co. vs. Tate, 1 Met ., 569 .
. It is settled , unless as to actions founded upon statutes , that limitation is matter
of defense ; and that the plaintiff need not show, in his petition , that his action
is not barred .

entries.ocr_text:
22 CIVIL CODE- NOTES TO SEC . 90 .
A demurrer to a petition , upon the ground that it discloses a bar to the action , by .
limitation , cannot be sustained , unless the petition show not only a sufficient
lapse of time , but , also , " the non -existence of any ground of avoidance , which
a plaintiff is not apt ever to do ." Rankin vs. Turney, 2 Bush, 555 .
And in an action for damages , caused by the killing of a person , brought under a
statute which declared that " the actions under this act shall be commenced .
within one year after such death , " it was held that the petition need not show
that the killing was within one year before the commencement of the action .
Chiles vs. Drake , 2 Met ., 148.
But , in an action brought under the act of 1856, concerning fraudulent preferences
by debtors , the court, after stating that the act required such actions to be
brought " within six months after the recording of such transfer , or the delivery
of the property or effects transferred , ” said : “ This fact , being essential to the
existence to the right [ of action ], must be made to appear , either by a direct
allegation on the subject in the petition , or by stating therein the time the trans-
fer was recorded , or the sale made and the property delivered ; so that , by refer
ence to that statement , and the time the petition was filed , it shall appear to
have been filed within the time limited ." Wintersmith & Young vs. Poynter&
Conway , 2 Met ., 460. This , however , as the court said in Story vs. Graham , 4
Met. , 322 , was a dictum ; " because the petition not only failed to show that the
conveyance was made within six months , but distinctly showed that it was made
more than six months before the commencement of the action ; and , conse
quently, that the plaintiff had no right to maintain the action ." See subdi-
vision (9) of note 7 to 90 , page 23 .
6. ALLEGATIONS CONCERNING NOTICE , DEMAND , READINESS , AND TENder .
(1) Notice .
a. A petition against guarantors alleged that the plaintiff accepted and acted upon
the guaranty , " of all which the defendants had due notice : " held , that the
averment of notice was insufficient , and that the allegation should be specific ,
and such as would enable the court to determine from the statements of the
petition whether or not such notice was given as the law requires . Steadman vs.
Guthrie , 4 Met. , 159. See 14 B. M. , 191 ; 13 B. M. , 383 .
b. In an action against the creator of a nuisance , the plaintiff need not allege notice
to desist from the wrong , nor a request to repair the injury : aliter , as to a con-
tinuer of the nuisance . Ray vs. Sellers, 1 Duv ., 254.
(2) Demand .
c. It was held that an action could not be maintained against a sheriff or constable ,
for money collected , without a previous demand . Huston vs. Hagar , Duv .,
24. But an allegation that he had collected the money , and refused to pay it to
66the plaintiff , although the same had been demanded of him , " is sufficient .
Harris vs. Perry , 2 Bush, 101.
d. Petition against an attorney , for money collected , must aver a demand . Roberts
vs. Armstrong's adm'r, 1 Bush, 263 .
e. It was held that an executor could , without averring a demand , maintain an
action against the testator's widow for the value of slaves of the testator , the
possession of which she had held after the testator's death , and parted with
before the commencement of the action . Easley's ex'r vs. Easley, 18 B. M. , 92 .
And see Divine vs. Bullock, 3 Met., 418 .
(3) Readiness .
ƒ. In an action upon a contract which contains mutual and dependent agreements ,
the petition must state the plaintiff's readiness to perform his part of the con--
tract at the required time and place . Sousely vs. Burns ' adm'r , 10 Bush, 87.
entries.citation: Civil and Criminal Codes of Practice of Kentucky, 1883, p. 347

classification.summary: This text consists of notes and case law interpretations related to Section 90 of the Civil Code of 1883, specifically concerning legal procedures for filing lawsuits, including statutes of limitations, allegations of notice, demand, readiness, and tender. entries.ocr_text:
CIVIL CODE- NOTES TO SEC . 90 . 23
*
(4) Tender.
g. In an action by a woman for an alleged breach of a contract to marry her, the
petition was held to be defective because it did not aver that she had “ proposed
and made the offer to fix the time and place and fulfill the engagement ." Fible
vs. Caplinger , 13 B. M. , 464. And see note 3c to 2 96 , subs . 2.
7. STATEMENT OF CAUSE OF ACTION .
(1) In actions for injury to character .
a. In an action for slander , the specific words in which the slander is conveyed must
be stated in the petition . It is not sufficient to allege merely the effect of the
words, or that the defendant charged the plaintiff with the commission of a
particular crime . Taylor vs. Moran , 4 Met ., 137.
b. The words , " he , the defendant , had had sexual intercourse with the plaintiff at
divers different times ," are actionable ; and the plaintiff need not allege or prove
special damage . Adams vs. Rankin , 1 Duv ., 58.
aa. The petition must allege malice . It is not sufficient to allege that the defendant
falsely charged the plaintiff with being " a horse -thief . " The words may have
been spoken in jest , or upon an occasion which would rebut the presumption of
malice . Williams vs. Gordon , 11 Bush.
bb. A judgment , upon a verdict based on presumed loss of character and time , was
affirmed , though there was neither allegation nor proof of special damage result
ing from either cause . Hill and Bergen vs. Harris , 4 Bush , 450. [ Quere .]
(2) In actions for injury to the person .
c. It is not sufficient to allege that an injury complained of was caused by the
defendant's willful neglect : some fact must be stated showing the neglect . Lou-
isville & Portland Canal Co. vs. Murphy's adm'r , 9 Bush , 522. But evidence of the
facts need not [and now must not , Code ? 119] , be stated . It is sufficient to allege
that plaintiff's intestate was killed by the defendant's employes , in carelessly and
negligently causing a train of cars to be run over him . Louisville , Cin . & Lex.
R. R. Co. vs. Case's adm'r, 9 Bush, 731.
(3) In other actions for tort .
d. Allegations that defendant extorted and obtained from plaintiff $ 500, without
any consideration , and is therefore indebted to plaintiff in that sum , do not state
a cause of action. Murphy vs. Estes , 6 Bush , 532 .
(4) In actions on return of nulla bona .
e. In an action against A , B , and C , upon a return of nulla bona as to A , the peti
tion , after stating the amount of the plaintiff's judgment against A , alleged that
B and C were indebted to A " in an amount sufficient to satisfy said judgment ;
and praying that they might be required to discover any debt due to said A by
them severally," and for judgment against them " severally :" held , that the
petition did not authorize a judgment by default against B for the amount of the
judgment against A , nor , it seems , for any amount . Seaton vs. Warner , 9 Bush,
217. See 5 J. J. Mar. , 641 .
· (5) In actions by vendor of land to enforce lien for purchase -money.
f. In an action to enforce a vendor's lien for the purchase -money of land , the peti-
tion must state that the vendor has complied with his contract , or that he is able
and willing to do so . Williams vs. Abrahams , 3 Bush, 186.
(6) In actions to rescind contract concerning sale of land.
g. It is not sufficient to allege , in a petition for the rescission of a contract for the
sale of land , that the defendant never had a good and perfect title , and that the
title is defective . Davis vs. Dycus , 7 Bush, 4.

entries.ocr_text:
24 CIVIL CODE- NOTES TO SEC . 90 .
(7) In actions upon implied contracts.
h . "Under the old practice , in an action of debt for the price of goods sold and
delivered , it was not necessary to aver a promise to pay the sum claimed (1 Chit-
ty's Pl. , 362) ; and , although , in an action of indebitatus assumpsit for the price of
goods sold and delivered , it was necessary to aver a promise to pay, it was not
necessary to prove such promise ; because proof of the sale and delivery of
the goods , and of their value , raised a legal implication of a promise to pay the
value , though no price had been agreed upon ." Snodgrass vs. Broadwell, 2 Litt.,
353 ; Jenkins vs. Richardson , 6J. J. Mar. , 441 .
" Under the Code of Practice , which requires only a statement of the facts .
constituting the cause of action , what the law implies need not be averred (secs .
115 , 144) ; and a petition , alleging a sale and delivery to the defendant of goods
worth a certain sum , or the rendition of services worth a certain sum , or contain-
ing equivalent allegations , is sufficient to authorize a judgment by default for
that sum , because a promise to pay it is implied by the law . In such a case , the
value of the goods , or services , need not be proved , because , in legal contempla
tion , the action is founded upon the defendant's promise to pay a certain sum
of money ," Skillman vs. Muir's adm'r , 4 Met ., 284. Acc ., Woods vs. Wells , 2-
Bush, 198.
(8) In actions upon contracts , generally .
i. In an action for money which the defendant promised to pay to the plaintiff " on
sight, " the allegation , " which sum of money the defendant did not pay to him .
on sight , " was held to be a sufficient statement of a breach of the contract.
Dehoney vs. Sanford , 2 Bush , 170.
j. In an action for a certain sum of money alleged to be due to the plaintiff , for
goods , wares , and merchandise sold and delivered to the defendant , at his special
instance and request , the allegation " which sum is just , due , and unpaid ," was
held to be a sufficient statement of a breach of the contract . Wood vs. Wells , &c.,
2 Bush, 198.
k. A petition alleged that , on the
-
-
-
- day of — , 1851, the defendant's testator pro-
posed to the plaintiff that , if he would quit his trade as a carpenter , and go and :
live on the testator's farm , the testator would do better for him than he was -
doing , or could do , by his trade ; that the plaintiff , confiding in said promises ,
did , on the day of- , 1851, quit his trade , and go to the farm of the testa-
tor , and live with and aid him in his business from the day of , 1851, until
his death in 1855 ; and that his trade was worth $ 1,200 per annum : yet neither the
testator , nor the defendants , his administrators with the will annexed , “ hath or
have paid to the plaintiff anypart of said sum of money, " although often requested ,
& c. held bad on demurrer , I , because it did not show an acceptance of the
testator's proposition within a reasonable time ; 2, because it did not show a .
breach . Moxley's adm'rs vs. Moxley , 2 Met ., 309 .
:
1. In an action against the directors of a bank for the value of special deposits of
the plaintiff , alleged to have been converted by officers of the bank to its use,
the plaintiff need not state by what officers the conversion was made . United
Society of Shakers vs. Underwood , & c. , 9 Bush, 609 .
m. A corporation , suing upon a contract, need not allege that it has been organized
as required by its charter. Hughes vs. Bank of Somerset, 5 Litt ., 46 ; Wright
vs. Shelby R. R. Co. , 16 B. M. , 7 ; Henderson & Nashville R. R. Co. vs. Leavell ,
Id. 363. Contra , Fry vs. Lexington & Big Sandy R. R. Co. , 2 Met ., 323.
n. In an action on a note , the petition failed to state its date ; but stated its amount ,
and the time when it was payable ; and the note was filed as part of the petition ::
held, that it was sufficiently described , Totten vs. Cooke, 2 Met ., 275:

CIVIL CODE- NOTES TO SEC . 90. 25
o. In an action on a bail -bond , the petition must show that it was executed before
the proper officer . Jones vs. Bunn , 2 Met ., 490 .
p. To enforce a lien on baggage of a guest , for the price of board , the petition must
allege that the plaintiff was a tavern -keeper . It is not sufficient to allege that
he was " a landlord , proprietor of the Myers House . " Southwood vs. Myers , 3
Bush, 681.
9. In an action for one third of the profits arising out of the sale of certain goods
by the defendants , the petition was held to be defective , because , though it
alleged that the profits amounted to a certain sum , it did not allege that they
had been collected by the defendants . Gould vs. Bonds , 1 Bush, 191.
r. In an action on an injunction -bond , conditioned to pay the judgment enjoined ,
if the injunction , be dissolved , the petition must allege that the judgment is
unpaid . Crawford vs. Woodworth , 9 Bush, 745. And see 2 Met . , 490 .
s. The plaintiff having agreed to deliver 100,000 bushels of charcoal for 3½ cents
per bushel, sued on the contract , alleging that he had delivered 126,463 bushels ;
and claiming 32½ cents per bushel for the excess . The defendants denied that
the plaintiff had delivered the alleged quantity of coal , and stated that they had
no knowledge , nor information to form a belief, as to the quantity he had deliv-
ered held , after a verdict and judgment for the plaintiff , for the excess , that , if
the defendants received more coal than they were entitled to , they should pay
for the excess what it was worth , whether they had knowledge of the excess or
not, when the coal was delivered ; but that the petition laid no foundation for
such recovery , because it failed to state the value of the coal . Caldwell , Hunter
& Co. vs. Dawson , 4 Met ., 122.
t. Allegation that the defendant owes " about $400 , " does not authorize judgment
for that or any other sum . Garnett vs. Finnell , 2 Duv ., 166 .
It was held , however , under the old practice , that a complainant who alleged , in a
bill in chancery , that he could not state the true amount which he was entitled
to recover , but that it could not be less than a certain sum , stated in the bill, was
entitled to recover the sum to which the evidence showed he was entitled , though
it exceeded the sum stated in the bill ; because " he was not bound to swear to
too much, for the purpose of obtaining enough ." Moore vs. Simpson , 5 Litt ., 52.
u . In an action surcharging a guardian's settlement , made in the county court, the
petition must specify the items objected to . Tanner vs. Skinner , 11 Bush , 129,
130.
7. As to actions by assignors against assignees of notes , see 16 B. M. , 343 ; 1 Duv .,
301 ; General Statutes, ch. 22, 87 .
(9) In actions under statutory provisions .
w . In an action against a defendant , for killing the plaintiff's father, the petition
must follow the language of the statute , and must allege that the killing was
done not in self-defense (Becker vs. Crow , 7 Bush , 202) ; and was caused by
defendant's " willful neglect❞ —an allegation of " gross neglect " being insuffi-
cient . Hansford's adm'x vs. Payne , 11 Bush , 382 .
x. The Revised Statutes having made abandonment a cause for divorce to the party
not in fault , it was held that the husband's petition for a divorce , on account of
alleged abandonment by his wife , must state that it was without his fault . Ep-
ling vs. Epling , I Bush, 74.
y. The Revised Statutes having declared that bills of exchange , payable in bank
notes or currency , shall be deemed negotiable, and treated in all respects as if
drawn for money , except as to the value of currency in which they are payable ;
in an action on a bill payable " in current exchange ," a petition , which described
the bill as payable in money , was held to be good on demurrer . Morrison & Co.
vs. Tate , 1 Met., 569 .

(10) Miscellaneous cases.
aa . A defective petition cannot be cured by a statement of facts contained in a paper
filed with the petition . Murphy vs. Estes , 6 Bush, 532. And see notes to sections
120 and 128.
bb. In an action for a divorce in the Campbell circuit court , an allegation that the
plaintiff is a resident of said county , omitting the words " in Kentucky , " is
sufficient . Strode vs. Strode, 3 Bush , 227.
8. PETITIONS IN ACTIONS UPON WRITINGS .
a. In an action on a contract , if the petition do not state that it is in writing , nor
refer to any writing , the court must presume that it is a verbal contract (Smith
vs. Fah , 15 B. M. , 443 ; Hocker vs. Gentry , 3 Met ., 474) ; though the record may
contain what purports to be a copy of a written contract between the parties .
Byassee vs. Reese, 4 Met ., 372. But in Haly vs. Auditor , 4 Bush , 493 , the court
said : " It does not appear from the petition in this case , whether the work was
done under a contract , made with the directions and advice of the Governor or
not ; and , as the petition alleges that the plaintiff was employed by the super-
intendent to do the work , and does not disclose that the contract was not in
writing , it must be presumed that the officers did their duty , in the absence of
facts to sustain a contrary conclusion ."
b. Upon the question , whether or not a defective petition can be aided by a writing
filed therewith , there is a conflict of opinions .
A petition , in an action upon a note , which was filed therewith , failed to allege
when the note became due . The court, after referring only to the case of Dodd
vs. King, Met ., 430 ; and after stating that " there is no essential analogy be
tween these cases ," said : " Here , the omission to allege when the note was pay .
able was supplied by the note itself, as effectually as if it had been literally copied

itsallegations

CIVIL CODE- NOTES TO SECS . 90 , 92 . 27
10. EFFECT OF UNNECESSARY ALLEGATIONS .
a . Generally , a party is bound by his statement of any material fact pleaded by
him, though he need not have stated it.
Thus , in an action on a policy of insurance , which the defendant seeks to avoid ,
because prohibited articles were kept by the plaintiff , the defendant is not bound
to specify the prohibited articles so kept , not being presumed to know them
[quere] ; but if he do so , specifying some , without alleging that others are kept
by the plaintiff , the jury cannot consider any except those specified . Phanix
Insurance Co. vs. Lawrence , 4 Met.. 18.
b. So , in an action upon a contract , if the plaintiff state for what consideration it
was made , the legal presumption of a sufficient consideration ceases to exist ; and
the alleged consideration , if controverted , must be proved by the plaintiff . Bul-
litt vs. Raiston , 1 A. K. Mar. , 331 ; Steadman vs. Guthrie , 4 Met . , 152.
c. But in an action for a reward offered by the defendant for the capture and con-
viction of certain persons , who had been captured by the plaintiffs , the petition
alleged that the defendant had procured the dismissal of the indictment against
those persons , after they had confessed their guilt in court ; and that the defend .
ant did so with the fraudulent intent to evade payment of said reward : held ,
that the last named allegation was immaterial , and need not be proved . Louis-
ville and Nashville R. R. Co. vs. Goodnight , 10 Bush , 554.
d. And in an action which a married woman had a right to bring , without joining
with her husband , her allegation that he refused to join with her was held to be
not traversable . Hardin vs. Gerard , 10 Bush , 259 .
11. PRAYER FOR RELIEF .
a. Observe that 90 of this Code forbids judgment by default except for relief
specifically demanded .
b. And it was held , under the Code of 1854, that though , perhaps , in an action
upon an unliquidated account , a count for interest or damages is unnecessary ;
yet , in such an action , unless interest or damages be claimed , no judgment should
be rendered for either. Adams Express Co. vs. Milton , 11 Bush , 49 .
c. A petition having stated several claims against the defendant , and the amount of
each , prayed for judgment for the amount of said claims : held to be sufficient .
2 Bush, 102-3 .
d. In Williams vs. Abrahams , 3 Bush , 187, it was held , in an action by a vendor , to
enforce a lien for purchase -money , that the plaintiff was entitled to a judgment
for the money , though he failed to show a right to enforce the lien .
e. A plaintiff having got possession , under bond , of a horse for which he sued , the
defendant answered avowing title in himself, and praying to be hence dismissed with
costs: held, that the defendant , having proved title in himself , was entitled to a
return of the horse , or to its value , and to damages for the detention . Bates vs.
Buchanan , 2 Bush, 117.
f. See 3 Monroe , 50 ; 5 J. J. Mar. , 125 ; 5 Dana , 338 .
SPECIAL DEMURRERS .
1. When objection to be made by motion instead of demurrer .
2. Objection to jurisdiction of the court .
3. Objection to plaintiff's capacity to sue.
4. Pendency of another action .
5. Defect of parties .
1. WHEN OBJECTION TO BE MADE BY MOTION INSTEAD OF DEMURRER .
a. Neither a general , nor a special , demurrer lies , because the plaintiff has failed to
swear to his petition (Harris vs. Ray, 15 B. M. , 630) ; nor because he has brought
92.

28 CIVIL CODE- NOTES TO SECS . 92 , 93 .
€ 93 .
an equitable , instead of an ordinary , action ; nor vice versa (Trustees , &c., vs.
Forest , 15 B. M. , 172 ; Foster vs. Watson , 16 Id., 387 ) ; nor for a misjoinder of
parties , plaintiffs or defendants (Dean vs. English , 18 B. M. , 136) ; nor because
an amended petition sets up a new cause of action . Hord vs. Chandler , 13 B. M. ,
403 .
Such objections must be made by motion . See cases supra .
2. OBJECTION TO JURISDICTION OF THE COURT .
b. Objection to jurisdiction of the person of the defendant is waived , unless it be
made by a special demurrer , or by answer ; aliter , as to jurisdiction of the subject:
of the action . See 118, and notes thereto .
3. OBJECTION TO PLAINTIFF'S CAPACITY TO SUE .
c. See ? 118, and notes thereto .
4. PENDENCY OF ANOTHER ACTION .
d. Observe that 92 applies only to another action pending in " this State ." Such
was the law before the adoption of the Code ; and such was declared to be the
law under the Code of 1854, though it did not literally make such a limitation .
Davis vs. Morton , Galt & Co., 4 Bush , 442 .
e. An action to enforce a lien for purchase -money does not prevent an action on the
note therefor . Julian vs. Pilcher, 2 Duv ., 254 .
f. An action having been brought by A in W. county against B and others , for the
recovery of slaves , was moved by change of venue to C. county . Subsequently ,
A sued the same parties in W. county for an attachment of said slaves ; and
the petition showed that the first named action was still pending in C. county :
held , that a demurrer to the petition should have been sustained . Moore vs. Shep
pard , Met., 97.
g. Under the old practice , it was decided that an action should not abate on account :
of a prior one , pending when the second was commenced , the former having been
dismissed before the filing of the plea . Adams vs. Gardiner, 13 B. M. , 197.
5. DEFECT OF PARTIES .
h . Though the defendant cannot object , by demurrer , to a misjoinder of parties
(Dean vs. English , 18 B. M. , 136) , a defect of parties , appearing in the petition ,
is ground for a special demurrer . If it do not so appear , it may be stated in the
answer . If the objection be not made by demurrer or answer , it is waived (Albro
rs. Lawson , 17 B. M. , 645) ; and the court may decide the case between those
before it , if the rights of others can be saved . (Code , 40. ) If there be other
indispensable parties , the court should require them to be brought before it .
Johnson vs. Chandler , 15 B. M. , 589 .
i. An objection , that the plaintiff is but the equitable owner of the note sued on , is
waived , unless made by demurrer or answer . Levi vs. Mendell , Duv ., 77.
j. But the objection to an action by one payee , upon a note to two , is not merely
an objection for the want of proper parties . A general demurrer lies , on the
ground that the petition fails to state a cause of action . Quisenberry vs. Artis , I
Duv ., 30.
k. See notes to ? 118..
GENERAL DEMURRERS .
a. A demurrer lies to a defense which is insufficiently pleaded in a paragraph with
one which is sufficiently pleaded . Williams vs. Langford , 15 B. M. , 569 .
b. A demurrer to a pleading presents all previous pleadings for the consideration .
of the court, which should decide against the party committing the first fault .
Martin vs. McDonald , 14 B. M. , 547 ; Young vs. Duhme , 4 Met ., 242.
But , as the defendant , by answering to the merits , waives all objections to the peti
tion , except the objections , that the court has no jurisdiction of the subject of

classification.title: Civil and Criminal Code 1883 - Notes to Sections 93, 94, 95
classification.summary: This section of the Civil Code from 1883 provides notes and case law interpretations related to demurrer and answer procedures in civil actions. It discusses how a defendant can object to a petition's sufficiency, the implications of answering after a demurrer is overruled, and how an answer can sometimes cure defects in a petition. It also touches on the amendment of petitions after a demurrer has been sustained.

30 CIVIL CODE - NOTES TO SEC . 95 .
tions that it was indorsed by the Curlew Coal Company , and that the defendants
were members of the company , are sufficiently controverted by denying that the
defendants were members of the company . Curlew Coal Co. vs. Grief [two cases] ,
2 Duv ., 527, 528.
c. Matters of defense averred in an answer inconsistent with facts alleged in the
petition , which the defendant has impliedly admitted by failing to deny them ,
are unavailing . Corbin vs. Commonwealth , 2 Met ., 381-2 .
d . In an action against a city to recover taxes paid , the plaintiff alleged that he
paid them in ignorance of his rights , and of the fact that the assessment was
illegal : the answer , alleging that the plaintiff paid the taxes " with full knowl-
edge of the law and facts ," was held to be insufficient on demurrer . City of
Covington vs. Powell, 2 Met ., 226 .
e. A petition alleged that the defendant owed the plaintiff $ 140 for the hire of a
slave for the year 1859 ; that the defendant had hired said slave , and had him
in possession during said term , and received the product of his services to the
amount or worth of $ 140 : an answer , denying that the defendant had any slave
of plaintiff hired as charged , or that the defendant agreed to pay either clothing
or hire ; and therefore denying that the defendant owed the plaintiff $ 140, or
any other sum , was held to be insufficient . Baum vs. Winston , 3 Met ., 127.
f. In an action to subject property , under the act of 1856 concerning fraudulent
preferences , the petition alleged that the debts secured by the mortgage , which
was alleged to have been made in contemplation of insolvency, " were not
created simultaneously " therewith : an answer , averring that said debts were
simultaneous with the mortgage , was held to be evasive . Terrell vs. Jennings ,
I Met. , 457 .
g. A petition alleged that the defendant was indebted to the plaintiff in the sum
of $620 for " merchandise ," a long account of which was filed with the peti-
tion , without any specification of the articles sold : an answer denying that the
defendant was indebted to the plaintiff more than $ 500 on the account presented
by the petition was held to be sufficiently responsive ; and held, that , upon the
pleadings, the plaintiff was only entitled to judgment for $ 500. Webb vs. Jef
fries, 2 Bush, 222.
h. Under the old practice it was held that an allegation of a bill , which was denied
by answer , must be proved , though it was repeated in an amended bill to which
there was no answer . Ditto vs. Young, 3 J. J. Mar. , 189 ; Greenwade vs. Green-
wade , 3 Dana , 495. Acc . , Robinson vs. Williamson , 7 Bush, 604 .
i. As to the plea of non est factum , see note to Form 12.
j. As to plea of no consideration , see note to Form 21 .
2. STATEMENT OF NEW MATTER OF defense .
a. Averment in an answer that a certain fact " did in fact , as defendant believes, exist
and was true," is not a sufficient averment of the fact . Mitchel vs. Mattingly, I
Met., 241.
b. In an action by an administrator for a debt , an answer , that the defendant's wife
is a distributee of the estate , and that no distribution has been made , does not
present an equitable defense : it should show that the plaintiff has a sufficient sum
for distribution to entitle each distributee to a sum equal to the amount of the
note sued on . Haggard vs. Hay, 13 B. M. , 176 .
c. In an action upon a policy , which the defendant seeks to avoid , on the ground
that prohibited articles were kept by the plaintiff , it was held that the defendant ,
not being presumed to know what prohibited articles were kept by the plaintiff ,
was not bound to specify them in his answer ; but that , having done so , specify .
ing some , without alleging that any others were kept by the plaintiff , the jury

entries.ocr_text:
CIVIL CODE - NOTES TO SEC . 95 . 3D
should not be permitted to consider any except those specified . Phænix Insur-
ance Co. vs. Lawrence , 4 Met ., 18.
d. To a petition on a note for the purchase -money of a slave , an answer that the ven-
dors had no title , and had not , and could not convey any title to the defendant ,
presents no defense , as it does not aver a warranty of title . Patrick vs. Swinney ,
5 Bush, 424.
e. Limitation must be pleaded , though the action be apparently barred (Chiles vs.
Drake, 2 Met ., 146 ; Rankin vs. Turney, 2 Bush , 555 ; Board vs. Jolly , 5 Bush, 86);
unless the petition shows the non-existence of any ground of avoidance of the
plea (Rankin vs. Turney, supra ) ; or , possibly, unless the action be founded upon
a statute which requires it to be brought within a certain time . See note i to
90, pages 21, 22.
f. An answer , pleading limitation , presents a valid defense though it admits that
the debt has not been paid . Gray vs. McDowell, 6 Bush , 475 .
3. PROOF AND ALLEGATIONS MUST CORRESPOND .
a . Evidence that a contract was illegal cannot be considered , unless the defense be
pleaded . Denton vs. Logan 3 Met., 434. But quere: if the petition shows that
the contract was illegal , will the court enforce it , though the illegality be not
pleaded by the defendant ? See 15 B. M. , 443 ; 3 Met ., 464–5 , 469 , 474 ; and
Alfriend vs. Hughes , 4 Bush , 40 ; which was an action for money alleged to have
been " paid , advanced , and loaned " by the plaintiff to the defendant . The
answer denied the allegations of the petition , but did not state any new matter of
defense . The evidence showed that the advance was made , as the plaintiff knew ,
to enable the defendant to pay money lost on a horse -race ; and it was held that
the plaintiff was not entitled to recover .
b. In an action for an assault , the defendant denied the allegations of the petition :
proof of threats made by the plaintiff and of facts occurring some months before ,
were held to be inadmissible ; though the defendant might have proved any part
of the res gesta , in mitigation of damages . Slater vs. Sherman , 5 Bush, 212.
c. Upon pleas of no consideration , and of failure of consideration , to a petition on
a note , defendant cannot prove that the note was given in blank , and filled up
with a larger sum than was authorized . Sanford vs. Smith, 5 Bush, 471 .
4. STATEMENTS OF LEGAL CONCLUSIONS .
a. It is not sufficient for the defendant to deny that he is indebted to the plaintiff ;
or to deny that he owes anything to the plaintiff ; or to deny " the correctness
of each and every item " of the plaintiff's account against him . Haggard vs.
Hays' adm'r, 13 B. M. , 175 ; Clark vs. Finnell , 16 Id. , 335 ; Francis vs. Francis ,
18 Id. , 60 ; Baum vs. Winston , 3 Met ., 127 ; McElfatrick vs. Taft , 10 Bush , 160 ;
Whitaker vs. Sandifer , 1 Duv . , 262 .
b. As to the plea of non est factum , see note 1 to Form 12.
c. In an action on a bond , it was held that the defendant , in order to obtain the
benefit of the presumption of payment , resulting from lapse of time , should
have pleaded payment ; and that it was not sufficient , for that purpose , to plead
that he was not indebted to the plaintiff . Haggard vs. Hays' adm'r, 13 B. M.,
175.
d. In an action against a common carrier, for a loss caused by the jettison of goods,
an answer alleging that the loss was occasioned by dangers of the river , and that
the accident was unforeseen and unavoidable , was held to present no defense ,
because it merely stated legal conclusions : it should have stated , with reasonable
precision and certainty, what the danger was , and the facts which prevented it
from being foreseen , and which rendered it unavoidable . Bently vs. Bustard, 16
B. M. , 686.

entries.ocr_text:
332 CIVIL CODE- NOTES TO SEC . 95 .
e. A statement in an answer , that the defendant was under the age of twenty -one
years when he executed the note sued on , is a sufficient plea of infancy . The
defendant need not state that the note was voidable, nor otherwise state the
law of the case. Stern vs. Freeman , 4 Met ., 311 .
f. An allegation " that the writing sued on was obtained from the defendants by
the fraud , covin , and misrepresentation of the plaintiff , " was not a mere con-
clusion of law ; but was " the averment of a substantive and traversable fact ."
Whitehead vs. Root , 2 Met . , 588. But quere as to an allegation of fraud which
does not even show whether the alleged fraud related to the exccution , or the
consideration of the contract . See Barlow vs. Wiley, 3 A. K. Mar. , 460 ; and
Forms 23 and 30.
g. In an action concerning land , a defendant's allegation , that he was an innocent
purchaser , was held to be immaterial : he should have alleged payment of the
purchase -money without notice , & c. Wing vs. Hayden , 10 Bush , 280.
h . See note 3 to 90, page 19.
5. LEGAL PRESUMPTIONS MUST BE REBUTTED : ONUS PROBANDI .
a. A's widow , suing for land which had belonged to him , alleged that the defend-
ants , who claimed the land as his heirs , and all of his relations , were foreigners
by birth , and aliens at the time of his death . The answer denied that the de-
fendants were aliens at the time of A's death , but did not deny that they were
foreigners by birth : held, that, having impliedly admitted that they were for-
eigners by birth , it must be presumed that they were aliens at the time of A's
death ; and that the onus was on them to allege and prove their naturalization .
White vs. White , 2 Met., 185.
The payors of two notes , having given to the payee a writing in which , after re-
ferring to the notes , they said : " We expect to pay them when they fall due .
We have no offsets , nor will have , on those notes ;" their denial that they exe-
cuted the writing to enable the payee to sell the notes was held to be insufficient
to throw on the assignee the burthen of proof. The payors should have shown
for what purpose the writing was executed . Wells vs. Lewis , 4 Met ., 269 .
c. " A denial by a married woman that she signed a note with the intention to
bind her separate estate was held to be insufficient to throw upon the plaintiff
the burthen of proving the intention . " Wells vs. Lewis , 4 Met ., 273 , citing Eastin
vs. Fulwiller , MS. opin .
d. A bill alleged a sale of a horse by the plaintiff to the defendant for $ 70, to be
credited on the plaintiff's note ; the answer admitted the purchase of the horse
at that price , but alleged that it was to be credited on another demand : held, in
the absence of proof, that the plaintiff was entitled to the credit . Greenwade vs.
Greenwade , 3 Dana , 498 .
e. It seems probable that, in the cases of Wells vs. Lewis and Eastin vs. Fulwiller ,
above cited, the court might have gone farther, and have held that the parties
were estopped from denying their intention . See Lewis vs. Harris , 4 Met ., 353 ,
in which it was held that the question concerning the intention with which a
party executed a note - whether to bind himself, or as trustee for another- is a
question of law , to be decided by the court , in view of the language of the
note , the circumstances under which it was executed , and the situation of the
parties.
6. ANSWERS CURING DEFECTIVE PETITIONS .
a. In an action for trespass , the petition alleged that the defendant broke into
the plaintiff's house , and demanded her watch , without alleging that he took
the watch the defect was cured by the answer , which denied that the defend-
ant took the watch . Jewell vs. Mills , 3 Bush , 66 .

CIVIL CODE- NOTES TO SECS . 95 , 96. 33
"b. So , in an action for an injury caused by a defective bridge , the plaintiff's failure
to allege that it was a public bridge , &c ., was cured by the defendant's denial
that it was a public bridge , & c. Louisville and Portland Canal Co. vs. Murphy ,
9 Bush, 529.
c. " It is a sound rule , that a bill ought to contain such charges and allegations
as will support the jurisdiction of a court of equity , and warrant the decree of
the chancellor upon its allegations . But it is a sound exception to that rule ,
that many of such charges and allegations may be dispensed with , where the
complainant is ignorant of his rights ; and , if the answer or proof aids such a
case , relief may be granted without regard to the defective , and sometimes incor-
rect , statements of the bill ." Deatly vs. Murphy, 3 A. K. Mar. , 474. [In that
case , all of the plaintiffs , except one , were under disability .]
d. In Neilson vs. Churchill, 5 Dana , 338 , relief , which was not designated either by
the allegations of the bill , or the prayer for relief, was given under a prayer for
general relief , upon the ground that " the defects and omissions , being of mat-
ters which cannot be presumed to be correctly known to the complainants , are
aided by the statements in the answer ."
e. See 2 Met ., 88 ; 2 A. K. Mar. , 317 ; 1 Mon. , 237 ; 7 Dana , 173 ; 8 Dana , 184 ; 4
B. M. , 199 ; 367 .
96, subs . I. COUNTER -CLAIMS .
Slone
a. In an action for an assault , the defendant may , by counter -claim , recover dam-
ages for an assault committed on him , at the same time , by the plaintiff .
vs. Slone, 2 Met ., 339.
b. In an action by a mortgagor against a mortgagee , for wrongfully taking possession
of mortgaged property , before a forfeiture of the condition , the defendant may
plead the mortgage debt as a counter -claim . Brown vs. Phillips , 3 Bush , 656.
c. The only restriction made by the Code , as to a counter -claim , is that it shall be a
cause of action arising out of the contract or transaction set forth in the petition ,
as the foundation of the plaintiff's claim. It is not necessary that it be founded
on contract , or arise out of the contract set forth in the petition . It is sufficient ,
if it arise out of the transaction set forth in the petition , or be connected with
the subject of the action . Tinsley vs. Tinsley, 15 B. M. , 454 .
d. In an action for trespass by A against B, the purchaser of A's land , sold by a
sheriff under a void execution , the defendant's demand for the purchase -money
paid by him is not a valid counter -claim ; though it would be an equitable set-
off if the plaintiff were insolvent . Geoghegan vs. Ditto , 2 Met ., 433 .
e. In an action for the contract -price for building a bridge , the defendants may , by
counter -claim , demand damages for the plaintiff's failure to build the bridge
according to contract . Moore vs. Caruthers and Applegate , 17 B. M. , 669 .
f. In an action by a railroad company for money due upon a subscription of stock ,
the defendant alleged that he had sustained damages by the plaintiff's taking of
a way through his farm ; and that , at the time of the subscription , the plaintiff
agreed that said damages should go in discharge of his subscription : held, on
demurrer , to be a valid counter -claim , being connected with the subject of the
action by the agreement . Lou . and Nash. R. R. Co. vs. Thompson , 18 B. M. , 735°
g. In an action on an injunction -bond , given in a proceeding to enjoin a judgment
recovered on a writ of forcible entry and detainer , the defendant may demand ,
by way of counter -claim , the value of a crop of corn , which was planted by him
whilst the injunction was pending , and which was growing when it was taken by
the plaintiff , and when the injunction was dissolved . Tinsley vs. Tinsley, 15 B.
M. , 460 .
