34 CIVIL CODE- NOTES TO SEC . 96 .
h. In an action by A against B on a note which B had signed as surety for C , B may
claim damages for A's failure to procure D's signature as co -surety , according to
A's promise . Murphy vs. Hubbie , 2 Duv ., 247 .
i. In an action by A against B for the price of a mill and of accounts due , which
A had sold to B, including an account for $ 500 against one C ; B was allowed to
plead , as a counter-claim , $ 190, which C claimed as a deduction from the assigned
account . Cook vs. Gray, 2 Bush, 121.
j. In an action by A's two administrators against B, for the price of wood sold by
them as the decedent's property , it was held that the defendant's demand against
one of the plaintiffs , in his individual capacity , for the value of part of said
wood , claimed and converted by him as his individual property , was not a valid
counter -claim , though the value might be recovered by a cross-petition . Phillips
vs. Keifer , &c., 2 Met. , 478 .
k. In a proceeding by mandamus , to compel a county court to levy a tax , and pay
the plaintiff for building a bridge , the defendant cannot maintain a counter-
claim for money paid to the plaintiff for building the bridge . Anderson County
Court vs. Stone & Son . 18 B. M. , 848.
1. A and B became sureties for C , upon B's promise to indemnify A. A , having
paid the debt , sued B for indemnity . B answered that C had executed a mort.
gage to A , as indemnity , and prayed for its foreclosure : held, that the answer did
not present a defense , or a counter -claim . Jones vs. Letcher , 13 B. M. , 365.
m . Preston , an infant , sued Boyd on a note . Boyd pleaded that it was given for a
jack , which he had purchased from Preston , and which he and one Wells war .
ranted to be a good foal -getter ; and that it was not, & c .; and asked for a judg-
ment againt Preston and Wells for $ 250 damages . Preston pleaded non estfactum ,
and Wells demurred . There was a verdict and judgment for Preston against
Boyd , and for Boyd against Wells . The latter judgment was reversed , upon the
ground that the warranty did not constitute ground for either a counter -claim or
cross -petition . Wells vs. Boyd , 1 Duv . , 367 .
n . In an action for dower, by a widow against her husband's vendee , he cannot sue-
the heirs, by counter-claim , on the vendor's covenant of warranty . Hill vs. Gol-
den, 16 B. M. , 551.
That case was decided in 1855. It seems probable that , under the act of 1857,
authorizing cross -petitions , which has been substantially re -enacted in this Code
(2 96 , subs . 3), the defendants could maintain a cross -petition in such a case .
o. In Rankin vs. Barnes , 5 Bush, 20, the facts are not fully stated ; but the court
seems to have held that the defendant might maintain a counter -claim upon a
cause of action which did not arise out of the contract or transaction stated in
the petition , and which was not connected with the subject of the action ; be.
cause the plaintiff , being a non -resident , was not " suable here by original pro-
See note 2 to subs . 2 of 96 .cess .
p. If a fact alleged in the answer be merely a denial of a fact alleged in the pe-
tition , or the affirmance of a fact which is denied in the petition , no reply is necessary ,
though it be stated and relied on as a counter -claim . Davis vs. Dycus , 7 Bush , 9 .
96, subsection 2.
1. Legal set-offs. Page 34.
2. Equitable set- offs. Page 36 .
SET -OFFS .
3. Invalid claims to set-offs. Page 37.
4. General rules concerning pleas of set-offs and counter-claims . Page 38.
1. LEGAL SET -OFFS .
a . An administrator cannot set off a debt to himself against a debt of the decedent .
Commonwealth vs. Bosley , 5 Bush , 223.

CIVIL CODE- NOTES TO SEC . 96 . 35
b. In an action by A and wife , on a note executed to her as administratrix of B ,
before her marriage to A , it was held that the defendant might set off a debt
due from A's wife to him ; the effect of which defense could only be avoided by
allegation and proof , on the part of the plaintiffs , that the note sued on still con-
tinued to be legal assets of B's estate . Jones vs. Everman and wife , 15 B. M. ,
633 .
c. A being indebted to B $ 100, and C being indebted to A $300 , C died ; and his
estate , worth over $300, was converted by B to his own use , as executor de son
tort ; after which B died : in an action by B's administrator against A , for the
$ 100, it was held that A could set off his demand against B, as executor de son
tort for the $300 , because said demand was against B personally, and not as the
lawful representative of C. McKenzie vs. Pendleton's adm'x , 1 Bush, 164.
d. In an action against an administratrix , on notes executed by the decedent , she
may , waiving the trespass , claim , as a set -off, the value of property of the intes-
tate wrongfully converted by the plaintiff to his own use . Eversole vs. Moore , 3
Bush, 49 .
e. In an action by A against B, on a note executed by B, and by C deceased , for
whom B was surety , it was held that B could set off the value of C's property
which had been converted by A , as executor de son tort . Finnell vs. Meaux, 3
Bush, 449 .
f. Though no action can be maintained against the Commonwealth , except by
express permission of the Legislature , in an action brought by the Common-
wealth , the defendant may set off a demand against it . Commonwealth vs. Todd ,
9 Bush, 708 .
g. Though a sheriff's claim on his deputy is a chosein action , upon which the Com-
monwealth , having obtained a judgment against the sheriff, has a lien ; yet , as
the deputy was surety of the sheriff , and had been compelled , as such , to pay
money for him , before the Commonwealth's lien attached , the sum so paid was
held to be a valid set-off against the Commonwealth, or its assignee . Harlan vs.
Lumsden , I Duv ., 86.
In such cases , the sum due is that which remains after deducting the set -off. Id.
h . In an action against the debtor of an insolvent bank , which was brought by
commissioners to whom its effects were assigned under an act of the Legislature ,
which provided for a pro rata payment to

entries.ocr_text:
36 CIVIL CODE- NOTES TO SEC . 96.
due to him from B, to satisfy which the attachment -suit was brought . Jackson,
Cox & Co. vs. Holloway , 14 B. M. , 133.
m . A defendant may set off , against the plaintiff , a joint demand against him and
another person . Harrison vs. Slone, 4 Bush, 577.
n. In Millet & Co. vs. Watkins ' adm'r , 4 Bush, 642 , it was held that the provision
of 473 of the Code , which declared that " no suit shall be brought against a
personal representative until after a demand is made of him , " does not apply to
a demand for a set-off ; because "it is neither in the common nor legal accepta-
tion of the word a ' suit , ' or action ." Quere , even as to actions under the Code
of 1854. See Bennett vs. Mc Crocklin , 3 Met . , 322 .
This Code declares that " the word ' action ' embraces a set -off or counter-claim "
( 732 , subsection 34) ; and that " the word ' sue ' refers to an action or special pro-
ceeding ." 732, subsection 37.
o. In an action by the assignee against the payor of a note which was payable in
Tennessee , and placed by the law of that State upon the footing of a bill of
exchange , it was held the defendant had a right to set off a demand against the
payee . Davis vs. Morton , Galt & Co. , 5 Bush, 160.
•This would have been conformable to the decisions in Carlisle vs. Chambers , 4 Bush,
268 ; and Hyatt vs. Bank of Kentucky , 8 Bush, 193, if it had appeared that the
assignment was made in Kentucky ; aliter , it seems , if it had appeared that the
assignment was made in Tennessee . According to these cases , the rights of
assignees of notes are governed by the law of the State in which the assignment is
made.
In Davis vs. Morton , Galt & Co. , it did not appear where the assignment was made .
The decision might , it seems , have been placed on safe ground , by holding that
the burden was on the plaintiff to show that the assignment was made in Ten-
nessee , if such had been the fact ; and that , having failed to do so , the court
must assume that it was made in Kentucky . The decision was , however , placed
upon the ground that " a set -off is but a part of the remedy ;" and , consequently ,
that the right of set -off depended upon the law of Kentucky , even if the note
had been assigned in Tennessee . Quere .
p. Though the plaintiff cannot , in his reply , plead a set -off against a set-off stated
in the answer , he may plead that goods , mentioned in the reply , were sold and
delivered to the defendant in satisfaction of the defendant's account , pleaded
as a set -off against the plaintiff . Williams & Davis vs. Jones and wife , 1 Bush , 628 .
2. EQUITABLE Set -offs .
a. " The only grounds of equitable set-off are : 1. That the demands are connected ,
as that one is a consideration of the other ; or , 2. That there has been an agree-
ment to set them off ; or , 3. That they have been completely liquidated at law ;
or, 4. That there is some obstacle to the legal coercion of the demand sought to
be set -off, as non -residence , insolvency , or the like ." Graham vs. Tilford &
Barkley, Met., 115.
b. The right of set -off, arising from unconnected demands is not of itself an equity.
It becomes an equity only in consequence of some additional fact , such as insol-
vency ; and , if that fact do not exist before notice of assignment , its subsequent
existence cannot create an available equity against the assignee of a note , in
favor of the payor, having a demand against the payee , who became insolvent
after notice of the assignment ; though it would prevail against the payee , if
he sued on the note . Graham vs. Tilford & Barkley, 1 Met ., 112.
<. In an action by an insolvent debtor , for trespass , against a purchaser of his land ,
sold by a sheriff under a void execution , the defendant may claim , as an equita-
ble set -off, the purchase -money paid by him . Geoghegan vs. Ditto , 2 Met ., 433 .

CIVIL CODE- NOTES TO SEC . 96 . 37
d. The right of set -off exists , in equity , even for unliquidated damages , and even
against an assignee , when the demands are connected , or the one sought to be
set off formed the consideration of the other , if the assignor be insolvent or a
non -resident . Taylor & Son vs. Stowel et al. , 4 Met ., 175.
. A judgment -creditor, having collected money for his debtor , under an agreement
to apply it to the judgment , the Chancellor may , at the instance of the debtor ,
enjoin the judgment , and set off the money collected . Lansdale vs. Mitchell , 14
B. M. , 350.
f. A demand for unliquidated damages for the breach of a warranty of the quality
of a commodity , for which the note sued on was given , constitutes an equitable
set -off against the assignee of the note , if the payee be insolvent or a non-
resident . Taylor & Son vs. Stowel et al ., 4 Met ., 175.
g. In an action by the indorser of a bill of exchange , who had taken it up , against
the acceptor , the defendant alleged that he and the plaintiff were in fact only
sureties of the drawer , the plaintiff having indorsed and the defendant having
accepted the bill, for the accommodation of the drawer ; and that the plaintiff
owed to the drawer , who had died insolvent, more than the amount of the bill :
held , to be a valid equitable set-off. Bowman vs. Wright , 7 Bush, 375 .
3. INVALID CLAIMS TO SET -offs .
a. A set-off, to be available , either at law or in equity , against an assignee , must
have existed before notice of the assignment . The fact that the payor of a note ,
on which he is sued , is the payee's surety on another note , constitutes no defense ,
and gives no right of set -off, in law or equity, against either the payee or his
assignee , though the payee be insolvent , the surety -debt not having been paid :
to constitute a defense against the action , the surety -debt should have been
paid before notice of the assignment . Walker vs. McKay , 2 Met ., 294.
b. See notes 2 a and b to ? 96 subs . 2.
c. A borrowed the notes of a bank , giving his own notes therefor ; the bank after-
ward failed ; its assignees sued A on his note ; he alleged that the bank was
broken when he borrowed its notes , and that the fact was known to its officers ,
but unknown to the defendant ; that he tendered its notes to its officers , but they
refused to receive them , and he then sold them for the best price he could obtain ;
and he claimed , as a set -off , the loss he had thus sustained : held, that he was not
entitled to a set -off , because , if for no other reason , he not only failed to tender
the notes in court , but showed that he had deprived himself of the power to do
Clark, & c., vs. Finnell , &c. , 16 B. M. , 337.So.
d. A and B , mutually indebted , executed notes to each other for the amounts of the
respective debts , agreeing that each might use the other's notes to raise money ,
or pay debts . In an action by C , to whom A , in order to pay a debt to C , had
assigned one of these notes , it was held that B could not set off A's note to him .
Barbaroux vs. Barker, 4 Met., 47.
e. A claim for unliquidated damages upon a contract is not a valid [legal ] set -off.
Shropshire vs. Conrad , 2 Met ., 143.
ƒ. Upon a rule against a receiver , to pay money collected by him under an order of
court , he cannot set off his claim against a creditor who is entitled to a part of
the money. Johnson vs. Gunter , 6 Bush, 534 .
g. A, having taken out letters of administration in Nelson county , brought an ac-
tion against B, in Spencer county , for a debt due to the estate : held, that under
297 of the Code of 1854, the defendant could not set off his claim as distributee
of the estate . Bennett vs. Mc Crocklin , 3 Met., 322 .
h. An administrator cannot set off a debt due to himself against a debt of the dece-
dent . Commonwealth vs. Bosley , 5 Bush, 223.

38 CIVIL CODE- NOTES TO SEC . 96 .
4. GENERAL RULES CONCERNING PLEAS OF SET -OFFS AND COUNTER -CLAIMS .
a. It was settled , that an answer which stated facts constituting a set -off or coun--
ter-claim , with a proper prayer for relief , must be treated as a set -off or counter--
claim, though not so denominated . Hutchings vs. Moore , 4 Met . , 113; True vs.
Triplett, Id., 57.
b. But it was held that an answer , which presented matter , constituting a counter-
claim , merely as matter of defense , could not be treated as a counter -claim .
Thus , A, being sued for the purchase -money of land , pleaded that the plaintiff
had not conveyed the land according to contract ; but asserted no claim founded
on the alleged non -performance , and asked for no relief in consequence of it ;
but relied upon it for no other purpose , and ascribed to it no other effect , than
that of defeating the relief sought by the plaintiff ; and it was held that the
answer was not a counter -claim . Hutchings vs. Moore , 4 Met . , 110.
c. An answer , which presents merely matter of defense , cannot be treated as a
counter -claim or set -off, though it be so denominated in the answer . Thus, in
an action by the assignee of a note , it was held that a plea of usury embraced
therein , or of payment to the payee , presented matter of defense , and not of
counter-claim or set -off, and that no reply was necessary . True vs. Triplett, 4
Met. , 57.
d. But an answer claiming usury , paid before the note sued on was given , presents
matter of set -off, and must be taken as true unless denied by a reply . Hays vs.
Goodwin , 4 Met. , 83 .
e. It was held that a plea setting up a counter -claim , though signed and verified by-
a person not a party to the action , having been filed as the answer and petition .
of the defendants , " must be regarded as the counter -claim of the defendants ;"
and in the absence of a reply its allegations must be taken as true . Lyle & -
Eubank vs. Poynter , 1 Duv . , 357 .
f. If the answer state a set -off against the plaintiff , which is also a demand against
others , against whom the answer is made cross -petition , the plaintiff is not
bound to reply to the set -off until the defendants to the cross -petition are
brought before the court, or until the cross -petition is discontinued as to them .
Scott vs. Wilson , 2 Bush, 603 .
g. If the defendant's answer present no defense , but presents a counter -claim for as
large a sum as that claimed by the plaintiff , the plaintiff is not entitled to judg--
ment until after trial of the issue upon the counter -claim ; and , if the counter-
claim exceed the plaintiff's demand , the defendant is entitled to a judgment for
the excess . Moore vs. Caruthers , 17 B. M. , 681.
h. To a plea of set -off, the plaintiff may reply the statute of limitation .
Coyle, 2 A. K. Mar. , 564.
Banks vs.
But limitation against the set -off ceases at the commencement of the action . Hayes
vs. Goodwin , 4 Met., 83 .
i. If an answer merely deny facts alleged in the petition , or merely allege facts deniea
in the petition , no reply is necessary , though the answer constitute a counter -claim .
Davis vs. Dycus , 7 Bush, 9.
j. Observe that 97, subs . 1, forbids a judgment by default , upon a set -off or counter-
claim , except for relief which is specifically demanded ; and that subsection 4 of
97 forbids judgment upon a set -off or counter -claim , presented in an answer ,
unless its caption contain the words " answer and set -off , " or the words " answer
and counter -claim ;" or upon a counter -claim , presented in a reply, unless its-
caption contain the words " answer and counter -claim ." This does not seem to
make it necessary to describe the pleading , in the body of it, as a set -off or coun-
ter-claim .

CIVIL CODE- NOTES TO SEC . 96. 39
96 , subsection 3. CROSS -PETITIONS .
a. The Code of 1854 substituted counter -claims for cross -petitions, as between
plaintiffs and defendants in equitable actions ; and authorized counter -claims
in ordinary actions ; but did not authorize either counter -claims or cross -peti-
tions, as between co -defendants or co -plaintiffs . Subsection 3 of 96 is , substan-
tially , a re -enactment of an act of 1857, as to cross -petitions by defendants ; and
of an act of 1859, as to cross -petitions by plaintiffs .
b. Under the aforesaid act of 1857, it was held , that, in an action brought by A , as
administrator of B , against C , the latter could maintain a cross-petition against A,
for a wrong done by him ; because the bringing of the action by A , in his fiduciary
capacity , did not make him individually a party to the action . Phillips vs. Keifer,
2 Met. , 478 .
c. In an action for debt , by a testator's creditor against his executor and devisees ,
the executor cannot maintain a cross -petition against the devisees , for an alleged
balance due to him from the estate ; nor can they maintain a cross -petition against
him for a devastavit . Crabtree vs. Banks , 1 Met ., 482 .
d. A lessee , sued for rent, may , by cross -petition , sue his assignee for payment
thereof. Trabue vs. Mc Adams , 8 Bush, 80 .
e. A sued B to enjoin the enforcement of a judgment , because A was a surety , and
had been released by lapse of time . B made his answer a cross -petition against
C , a sheriff , claiming damages for his failure to return an execution upon said
judgment , and his failure to sell property levied on : held, that a demurrer to the
cross -petition should have been sustained , because the cause of action therein
stated did not affect the subject -matter of the action . Royse vs. Reynolds , 10
Bush, 286.
f. In an action brought by A against his debtor , B, the defendant was constructively
summoned , and a tract of land belonging to him was attached . B mortgaged
the land , pendente lite, to C ; who , having been made a defendant , filed an answer
contesting the attachment , and praying for an enforcement of the mortgage . It
was held that the attachment was void , and that , therefore , the court had no
jurisdiction as to the cross -petition . Peters vs. Conway , Bush, 571.
But , quere ; because , though the attachment was void, it seems that the court had
jurisdiction of the action of A ; and might , on final hearing , have ordered a sale
of the land for the payment of A's demand , after satisfying the mortgage -debt .
The court seems to have believed that an attachment is necessary to give juris-
diction of an action against a defendant constructively summoned . This seems
to be erroneous . An attachment is not an action ; nor is a ground for an attach-
ment a cause of action . Such ground need not be stated in the petition ; and ,
until the decision was rendered in Scott vs. Doneghy , 17 B. M. , 321, there seemed
to be good reason for believing that such ground could be stated only in a sepa-
rate affidavit . " This provisional remedy is merely ancillary and incidental to
the action in which it is sought . It may be had either at or after the commence-
Iment of the action ." Moore vs. Sheppard , 1 Met ., 97. To make the jurisdiction
of the court, as to an action , depend upon the validity of an attachment obtained
in it , would reverse the rule , that " the incident follows the principal ." Section
449 of the Code of 1854, of which 418 of this Code is substantially a copy ,
declared : " No lien on the property of a defendant constructively summoned
shall be created otherwise than by an attachment , as provided in chapter 3 of
title 8, or by judgment ." It seems clear that the Legislature contemplated that
an action against a defendant constructively summoned might proceed to judg
› ment against him ; and that the judgment might create a lien upon his property ,
though the plaintiff had failed to attach it . It seems difficult to perceive upon

98.
what principle it can be held that an abortive effort to obtain an attachment-
lien can affect the jurisdiction of the court , or the rights of the plaintiff , as to the
action .
g. Lien -notes , for the purchase -money of land , having been assigned to four per-
sons , severally ; in an action by one of the assignees against the others , and
against the vendee , to enforce the lien , it was held that the assignees who were
defendants were entitled to relief upon their answers , without cross -petitions ,
their demands having been stated both in the petition and in their answers .
Jenkins vs. Smith , 4 Met ., 380 .
In Cavin & Elliott vs. Williams & Ray, 8 Bush , 346 , the court undertook , expressly ,
to overrule Jenkins vs. Smith , by a dictum —a dictum , because the parties had failed
to show that the vendor had tendered , or was " able to make , a good deed for
the land ." This dictum was founded upon the act of 1857, above referred to
(note a), which , amending ? 125 of the Civil Code , authorized cross -petitions , as
between defendants , and declared that “ the defendant to such cross -petition may
be actually or constructively summoned ." The court said that , "if a literal
meaning be given to said amendment , a summons on such cross -pleading and ser-
vice thereof, either actual or constructive , is indispensable to authorize any relief ;
and , until that amendment was made , cross -pleadings were not allowed by the
Civil Code ." This seems to be a begging of the question . Clearly , service of a
summons is necessary upon what is , necessarily , a cross -petition . The question
is, whether or not a cross -petition is necessary in cases similar to that of Jenkins
vs. Smith .
In Sale , & c., vs. Crutchfield , & c., 8 Bush , 647 , which was an action by A against B
and others , for the recovery of land which was alleged to belong to A and B, and
which was claimed adversely by the defendants , other than B ; and for partition
of the land between A and B ; it was held that B was entitled to relief against
his co -defendants , though his answer was not made a cross -petition against them .
And see Blakey vs. Blakey, & c. , 3 J. J. Mar. , 674 .
h. A having agreed with B to assume his liabilities as a member of the firm of B &
C , it was held that , though a creditor of the firm of B & C , suing them and A,
was entitled , by substitution , to a personal judgment against A ; other creditors .
of the firm , who were made defendants , and who answered , asserting their claims
against A, but who failed to have him summoned , were not entitled to judgments
against him ; and a distinction was taken between a right to a personal judgment
against one agreeing to pay firm debts , and a right to subject a common fund .
Francis vs. Smith , Duv ., 125.
i. Observe 692 as to enforcement of liens.
REPLIES .
a. Under the Code of 1854, which forbade a reply except to a set -off or counter-
claim , it was held that a plaintiff could, without amending his petition , avoid a
plea of infancy , by proving a ratification (Stern vs. Freeman , 4 Met ., 309 ) ; or, a..
surety's plea of limitation , by proving that the plaintiff had sued the principal
debtor within seven years (Harris vs. Moberly , 5 Bush, 556) ; or, a plea of pay-
ment of money , for which the defendant filed the plaintiff's receipt , by proving
that the receipt was obtained by fraud . Robinson vs. Williamson , 7 Bush , 604 .
See sections 114 and 126, in connecThis Code required replies in all such cases .
tion with 98.
b. It was held that , if a fact , stated in the answer , be merely a denial of a fact :
stated in the petition , or an affirmance of a fact which is denied in the petition , no-
reply is necessary , though the answer constitutes a counter -claim. Davis vs.

CIVIL CODE- NOTES TO SECS . 98 , 102 , 107 , III , 113 . 41
Dycus , 7 Bush , 9. And see 11 Bush , 233 ; and note to Form 2, as to the neces-
sity of replying to a plea of payment ; and note to Form 22, as to the necessity of
replying to a plea of no consideration .
c. It was held that matters of defense , stated in a petition to be made a party- such
petition being filed as an answer - need not be controverted by a reply . Graves
vs. Ward et al., 2 Duv ., 301.
d. In an action for damages , for failure to convey land and deliver possession , the
defendant , by counter -claim , sought a specific performance of the contract ; and
it was held that the plaintiff could not , in his reply , demand a rescission of the
contract , the reply being restricted to matters of defense . Spalding vs. Alexan-
der, 6 Bush, 164.
e. Though the plaintiff cannot plead a set -off against a set -off, he may state , in a
reply , that goods therein mentioned were sold and delivered to the defendant in
satisfaction of the set -off . Williams & Davis vs. Jones and wife , 1 Bush , 628 .
? 102. a . As to time offiling defense, and time of trial , in actions against defendants con-
structively summoned , see sections 60, 102, 104, and 364 ; and Harris vs. Adams ,
2 Duvall , 141.
107. a. Affidavit for a continuance , stating that the defendant has a good defense , but
without stating the nature of it , is insufficient. Engleman vs. National Bank of
Lancaster , 2 Bush , 165.
111. a. Though the plaintiff cannot plead a set- off against a set- off, he may plead a delivery
of goods to the defendant , in satisfaction of the set -off . Williams & Davis vs.
Jones and wife , 1 Bush , 628 .
? 113, sub . 3. PARAGRAPHING OF PLEADINGS .
a. A petition against husband and wife , for tuition , & c. , furnished her , alleged the
value thereof, the receipt of property by him from her, and his promise to pay
plaintiff's demand in consideration of forbearance to sue : held , that the petition
did not state two causes of action , though it sought satisfaction of one claim on
different grounds . Bonney vs. Reardin , 6 Bush , 38 .
b, The objection , that several causes of action are contained in one paragraph , must
be made , not by demurrer , but by a motion to strike out some of them , or to
have them stated in distinct paragraphs (Williams vs. Langford, 15 B. M. , 569) ;
and the objection is waived by answering Noel vs. Hudson , 13 B. M. , 205 .
113, sub. 4. INCONSISTENT PLEADINGS .
a. It was held that , under 125 of the Code of 1854, an answer might present
inconsistent defenses — such as not guilty and justification in an action for slander .
Horton vs. Bauner, 6 Bush, 596.
And it was held that , in an action upon a contract , the plaintiff had a right to file
an amended petition , alleging that the defendant had procured the contract fraud-
ulently , and claiming damages for the fraud ; the court resting its decision upon
the grounds that , under 138 of the Code of 1851, which is substantially the
same as 84 of this Code , " the plaintiff may strike from his petition any cause
of action , at any time before the final submission of the case ;" and that the
filing of the amended petition was virtually a striking out of the original cause
of action . Hord vs. Chandler , 13 B. M. , 404 .
But in Hancock , &c. , vs. Johnson, 1 Met ., 242 , it was held that the plaintiff had no
right to file an amended petition stating a cause of action which could not be
joined with the cause of action stated in the petition .
b. The Code of 1854 (2138 ) authorized the plaintiff to allege , in reply to a counter-
claim or set -off , " any new matter not inconsistent with the petition ." In an

Sureties of a trustee , who was directed by an order of court to invest funds , must

That question was answered affirmatively by a dictum in Enders vs. Williams, I
Met. , 354-5 - a dictum , because the court held that , in view of all the evidence
in the case , the testimony of the plaintiff's single witness was insufficient to
overcome the defendant's denial . And in Crutchfield vs. Thurman , 4 Bush, 498 ,
the court, whilst impliedly conceding the sufficiency of one witness , refused
relief on his testimony .
In Worley vs. Tuggle , 4 Bush , 182, the court said : " In Albro vs. Lawson , 17 B. M. ,
644 , this court held , that the rule which required two witnesses , or one witness
with strong corroborating circumstances , to overcome a denial in chancery , has
been changed by said section [ 142] of the Code ; which was subsequently affirmed
by this court in Enders vs. Williams, 1 Met ., 354-5 . So it may now be regarded
that the oath of the plaintiff and the defendant , making an issue , are equiva-
lents , and the issue [must be ] adjudicated upon the evidence ."
The court erred in supposing that this question was decided in Albro vs. Lawson , as
that was an ordinary action . Moreover , the opinion in Worley vs. Tuggle is a
dictum , because , as the opinion states , the testimony of the witness was " sig-
nally confirmed " by other evidence .
118. GROUNDS FOR SPECIAL DEMURRER , NOT APPEARING IN PETI-
TION , MAY BE STATED IN ANSWER .
a. The provisions of this section are substantially the same as provisions contained
in 123 of the Code of 1854.
b. A petition which shows that the court has not jurisdiction of the subject of the
action should be dismissed , even though the parties agree to submit to the juris-
diction . Hughey , & c., vs. Sidwell's heirs , 18 B. M. , 259 ; Fidler vs. Hall, 2 Met.,
461 .
c. But objection to jurisdiction of the person of the defendant is waived by failure
to present it by special demurrer , or by answer . Thus , an objection by a corpo-
ration , that an action against it was not brought in the county in which its prin-
cipal office is situated , is waived by its answering to the merits . Baker vs. L. &
N. R. R. Co. , 4 Bush, 619 .
d. The Code does not authorize a plea in abatement . But , upon a demurrer to an
answer which showed that the plaintiff had not [temporarily ] capacity to sue , it
was held that the petition should be dismissed without prejudice . Norris vs.
Doniphan , 4 Met ., 402 .
e. A pleading , which showed that the court had no jurisdiction of the person of the
defendant , was held to be a valid answer , although it was denominated a plea in
abatement . Stone's adm'r vs. Powell, 13 B. M. , 343-
f. If the fact that the plaintiff has not legal capacity to sue be not shown by the
petition , but be shown by the answer and evidence , a judgment for the plaintiff
cannot be sustained . In such cases , no motion by the defendant is necessary .
Petty vs. Maleir , 14 B. M. , 246 .
g. An answer , that the note sued on belongs to a person other than the plaintiff is
good, on demurrer . Carpenter vs. Miles , 17 B. M. , 602 .
If there be an issue as to the plaintiff's ownership , it is triable by the court , and
not by a jury ; and if the issue be found for the defendant , the court should not
dismiss the action peremptorily , but should order the owner to be made a party,
on pain of dismissal without prejudice . Carpenter vs. Miles, supra ; Vanbuskirk
vs. Levy, 3 Met., 133.
h. The waiver mentioned in ? 118 [ ? 123] applies to defendants constructively sum-
moned . Gill vs. Johnson's adm'rs , 1 Met . , 649 .
i. A garnishee may object , by affidavit , that there is a defect of parties . Forepaugh ,
Bishop , &c., vs. Appold & Sons, 17 B. M. , 625.

44 CIVIL CODE - NOTES TO SECS . 119 , 120 , 124 .
119. 1. a. As to stating evidence in a pleading , see note 4 to 90 , page 21.
2. b. As to stating legal presumptions in a pleading , see Gregory vs. McFarland , 1 Duv .,
59; Williams vs. Gordon , 11 Bush.
3. As to pleading private statuies .
c. Local or private acts or resolutions of the General Assembly may be given in
evidence without being specially pleaded . (R. S. , ch. 35, § 1 ; Gen. S. , ch. 37 ,
1.) Section 119 of this Code does not require , but permits, the pleading of
private statutes . This permission was given for fear that the prohibition , as to
evidence , might be regarded as applying to private statutes .
d. Though the bill of exceptions fail to show that a private statute was read in the
court below , the Court of Appeals will take notice of it , if the pleadings refer to
it. Lackey vs. R. and L. T. P. Co. , 17 B. M. , 47- And see Bixler's adm'x vs..
Parker, & c., 3 Bush , 166 ; H. and N. R. R. Co. vs. Leavell , 16 B. M. , 361 .
120. 1. Filing of writings on which action is founded.
124.
2. Effect of such writings .
I. FILING OF WRITINGS ON WHICH ACTION IS Founded .
a. Section 120 is substantially the same as ? 145 of the Code of 1854, except that
120 does not require the plaintiff to file with his petition a copy of an account
upon which he sues . The writer of these notes is unable to state the reason for
that omission , or what will be its effect . It seems , however , at any rate , that the
plaintiff can be compelled to file the account by a rule founded upon the defend-
ant's affidavit showing that it is necessary to the substantial ends of justice .
Ruggles vs. Moore , 18 B. M. , 824. And see 2 Bush , 222 ( Webb vs. Jeffries) , cited
in note 19 to 95.
b. A writing , upon which an action is founded , must not only be filed with , but must
be referred to in , the petition (Hill vs. Barrett , 14 B. M. , 86 ; Dodd vs. King, 2´
Met. , 430 ) ; in which latter case it was held that , though a note which was
referred to in , and filed with , the petition , was part of the record , an assign-
ment of the note to the plaintiff , which was not mentioned in the petition , was
not part of the record .
But a petition for a debt due " by note herewith filed " makes a sufficient reference ,
if the note filed be substantially the same as that described in the petition . Tot-
ten vs. Cooke, 2 Met. , 275.
c. It was held , under the Code of 1854, that a writing , upon which an action was
founded , and which was filed with the petition , and referred to therein , formed a
part of the record , without being made so by a bill of exceptions (Dodd vs. King,
1 Met., 430) ; and , in this respect , differed from writings [exhibits] filed under
2155 of that Code . Vaughan vs. Mills , 18 B. M. , 633 ; Dodd vs. King, supra ;
Haney vs. Tempest , 3 Met ., 95.
d. It was held that a bill of particulars which was filed in court, but not referred
to in the petition , was not part of the record , and need not be noticed in the
answer . Webb vs. Jeffries , 2 Bush , 221.
e. In an action for the value of lost baggage , it was said that " the schedule filed .
with the petition , and made part thereof, is essentially a part of the case ; and
the jury had the right to regard the statement of facts therein as admissions on
the part of the plaintiff ." L., C. & L. R. R. Co. vs. Mahan , 8 Bush , 187.
2. EFFECT OF WRITING ON WHICH ACTION IS Founded .
f. See note 8 to 90, page 26 .
ACTIONS FOR LIBEL OR SLANDER .
a . Sections 123 and 124 of this Code are substantially the same as sections 150 and
151 of the Code of 1854.

CIVIL CODE- NOTES TO SECS . 124 , 125 , 126 . 45.
8 125.
126.
b. Under the old practice it was held that the defendant , in an action for libel ,
might , upon a plea of not guilty (Hart vs. Reed, 1 B. M. , 166) ; or upon a plea .
of justification (Morehead vs. Jones , 2 B. M. , 210), prove mitigating circumstances ,
not amounting to a justification , to reduce the damages . And see 18 B. M.,
791 .
c. " When the only answer is a denial of the speaking of the words , nothing is
admissible in evidence which tends either to prove the truth of the charge , or to
establish a defense on the ground that the words were spoken on a justifiable
occasion ." Harper vs. Harper, 10 Bush, 458 .
d. It was said in Harper vs. Harper, 10 Bush , 458 , that ? 151 of the Code of 1854 .
" abrogates the rule that an unsustained plea of justification is evidence of malice ,
and will enhance the damages ."
e. It was also said in Harper vs. Harper, 10 Bush , 458 , that the defendant , in an
action for slander , " may , if he chooses , deny the speaking of the defamatory
matter charged ; and in a second paragraph he may admit the publication and
state its truth ; and in still another he may admit the words , and , without alleg-
ing them to be true , or admitting that they were false , justify by alleging such
facts as are relied on to excuse their publication .”
But see now 113, subs . 4, which forbids inconsistent pleadings.
f. In Harper vs. Harper, 10 Bush , 459 , the court said : " Whether , if the special
facts set forth [ in the answer ] had been accompanied by an admission of the
publication of the words charged , they would have constituted a defense , we
need not decide . A plea of justification must admit the words spoken .”
See 113, subs . 6, which declares that an express admission of a fact is not neces-
sary to render valid a plea of avoidance or estoppel .
ACTIONS FOR THE RECOVERY OR SUBJECTION OF LAND .
a . The case of Farrow vs. Farrow , 2 J. J. Mar. , 388, aptly illustrates the loose prac-
tice which prevailed , and which the Code attempts to reform , concerning the
description of land claimed by the plaintiff , and of that claimed by the defend .
ant, in actions of ejectment .
b. The common law rule , that a judgment against the plaintiff , in an action of eject-
ment , cannot be pleaded in bar of another action for the same land , has been
abolished , by statutes , in Kentucky . Troutman vs. Vernon , I Bush, 482 .
JUDGMENT PRO CONFESSO .
a . A fact not alleged in the petition cannot be taken as true because the defendant
fails to answer an interrogatory contained in the petition . Burnett & Miller vs.
Garnett & Thompson , 18 B. M. , 71.
b. If, in an action against two persons , one file an answer - such as a plea of pay-
ment -showing that neither is liable , there can be no judgment pro confesso-
against the other. Rouse vs. Howard, 1 Duv ., 31.
c. It was said that a judgment pro confessoshould not be rendered against one defend-
ant before process is served upon others concerned with him in interest . Alex-
ander & Lancashire vs. Quigley's ex'rs , 2 Duv ., 402 .
d. A judgment , merely , that allegations of a petition be taken for confessed , was
held to be interlocutory . Alexander & Lancashire vs. Quigley's ex'rs , 2 Duv ., 402 .
But see Kendrick vs. Fields , 2 Bush , 153, which was an action for damages for
the conversion of personal property . Judgment was rendered pro confesso except-
as to the allegation of value , and the case was continued for an inquiry as to the
value : held , that the judgment was final as to the defendant's guilt , and as to the
articles alleged to have been taken , leaving nothing for litigation except the
value of the property.

46 CIVIL CODE- NOTES TO SEC . 126 .
e. The Code of 1854 (2153 ) declared that " allegations of value , or of amount of
damage , shall not be considered as true , by the failure to controvert them ."
It was held that an allegation of value is not essential , as it cannot be taken for
confessed . Wintersmith vs. Tabor, 5 Bush , 110.
It was held that allegations of value , or of amount of damage , cannot be taken for
confessed in either equitable or ordinary actions . Clark vs. Seaton , 18 B. M. ,
229. But the court may hear proof and assess the value , or the damages , with-
out the aid of a jury . Daniel vs. Judy, 14 B. M. , 393 ; Lambert vs. Ingram's
adm'r, 15 Id. , 270 .
And when proof of value or damage is necessary to authorize a judgment on fail-
ure to answer , the record must show that there has been an inquest by the court ,
or a jury , or a commissioner . Daniel vs. Judy, 14 B. M. , 395 ; Clark vs. Seaton,
18 B. M. , 226 ; Marr's adm'r vs. Prather, 3 Met . , 197 ; Gould vs. Bonds , I Bush,
191 ; Beam vs. Hayden , 5 Bush , 426 .
"But a recital in a judgment , that the cause was heard , authorizes the inference that
evidence was introduced on the trial . Dehoney vs. Sandford , 2 Bush , 169.
In an action for the hire of a slave , it was held that the jury , or a judge to whom
the law and facts are submitted , may assess the value , from their general knowl-
edge . Baum vs. Winston , 3 Met . , 127. But see Pharis vs. Carver , 13 B. M. , 238.
It was held that no assessment of value is necessary in an action for the value of
medical services and medicine (Harris vs. Ray , 15 B. M. , 628) ; nor in an action
for goods sold and delivered to the defendant ( Wood vs. Wells , 2 Bush , 197) ; but
the court may give judgment by default for the debt , with interest from the
commencement of the action (Francis vs. Francis , 18 B. M. , 57) ; because , in
legal contemplation , the action is founded upon the defendant's promise to pay
a certain sum of money , since the law implies a promise to pay the value . Skill-
man vs. Muir's adm'r , 4 Met ., 284 ; Beam vs. Hayden , 5 Bush , 429 .
But an assessmeut of value or damage was held to be necessary in an action for the
value of coal taken by the defendant from the plaintiff (Daniel vs. Judy, 14 B.
M. , 393) ; in an action for trespass on the plaintiff's land (Clarke vs. Seaton, 18 B.
M. , 226) ; in an action for the value of goods which a common carrier failed
to deliver according to contract (Huston et al. vs. Peters et al. , 1 Met ., 558) ; for
the value of cash notes which the defendant failed to assign according to his
covenant (Marr's adm'r vs. Prather , 3 Met ., 196) ; for the value of negro clothing
which the defendant failed to furnish according to his contract (Skillman vs.
Muir's adm'r, 4 Met . , 282) ; and in an action for the value of whisky which the
defendant failed to deliver according to his covenant . Beam vs. Hayden , 5 Bush,
426 .
In an action upon a contract, for the conventional price of stipulated services , part
of which the defendant refused to let the plaintiff perform , a judgment by de
fault , without an assessment by a jury , upon proof, was held to be erroneous .
Wood vs. Morgan , 6 Bush , 507.
Rogers vs. Aulick, 2
In an action for assault and battery , it was held that , upon a failure to answer , the
jury might find more than nominal damages , without proof .
Duv ., 419 .•
In an action for cash-advances , the plaintiff is entitled to interest.
3 Bush, 519 .
Field vs. Burnam ,
f. See 379, as to judgment and the assessment of damages , upon failure to plead ;
and sections 312 and 331 as to the defendant's right to a trial by jury.

entries.ocr_text:
CIVIL CODE- NOTES TO SECS . 128 , 129 . 47
128. EXHIBITS .
129.
a . This section is a substitute for , and has , perhaps , materially modified , 2 155 of
the Code of 1854.
b. It was held that said ? 155 did not apply to writings on which actions are founded,
such as a note for money , or a covenant to perform an act ; that it only applied
to exhibits , such as a deed relied on by the plaintiff to prove his title to land , in
an action against an adverse possessor ; or an execution , and return thereon ,
relied on by the defendant to justify the seizure of the plaintiff's property ; that
such exhibits might be used as evidence , though not relied upon in pleading, and
need not be so used , though so relied upon ; that they did not constitute part of
the record , though filed and referred to in pleadings ; that they could be made
part of the record only by a bill of exceptions ; and that no benefit could result
from relying on them in pleadings except that given by 2 527 [588] . 3 Met., 95 ;
18 B. M. , 633 ; Id. , 824 ; 15 Id. , 451.
It was said , however , in Harman vs. Wilson , &c., 1 Duv ., 327 , that an exhibit filed
with a petition in equity constituted part of the record , without being made so
by a bill of exceptions . It does not seem , however , that the Code of 1854 made
any distinction between ordinary and equitable actions , with reference to ex-
hibits .
c. Section 552 of this Code requires , as a general rule , that the proof in equitable
actions shall be made by depositions or exhibits ; with a proviso " that the gen
uineness of exhibits may be proved orally in court , as heretofore allowed by law."
Of course , exhibits may be proved , by depositions , in an equitable action ; or,
viva voce, upon the trial of an ordinary action . The only question is as to prov-
ing exhibits , viva voce, in equitable actions .
In Christ vs. Brashears , 3 A. K. Mar. , 170, it was held that papers which were not
referred to in the pleadings , though they were filed with a deposition in the case,
could not be proved ore tenus, upon the trial of a suit in chancery .
" The practice in England , when a party desires to prove exhibits viva voce in court,
on the hearing , is to procure an order for leave to do so . But to entitle the
party to make the proof, a copy of the order must be served on the clerk of the
opposite party , at least two days before the hearing . (1 Smith's Practice , 413- )
The practice here has been to prove exhibits either at or before the hearing ; and
there can be no great objection to indulging the proof , as well before as at the
hearing, as witnesses may , with most facility , be procured to attend . But we
think notice of the day when the proof will be made , should be given to the op
posite party or his counsel ." Cosby's heirs vs. Wickliffe , 7 B. M. , 121.
d. It was held , under the old practice , that the execution of a deed , which was
made an exhibit by the complainants , must be considered as admitted , when it
is offered in evidence by the defendant , although the authentication of it is not
sufficient . Borah vs. Archers , 7 Dana , 176.
e. It was held , under the old practice , that, if a bill in equity refer to a pending
action , as an exhibit , the court must consider subsequent, as well as previous, pro
ceedings therein . Daniel and Breck vs. Smythe, 5 B. M. , 347 .
VARIANCE BETWEEN PLEADINGS AND PROOF .
a. In an action on a note against A , as administrator of B, it was held that there
could be no judgment against him as administrator , because he executed the
note individually ; and that there could be no judgment against him individually ,
because the petition stated . that he executed the note in his " fiducial capacity,”
and asked for judgment against him in that capacity . Lusk vs. Anderson's adm'r,
1 Met. , 426 .

entries.ocr_text:
48 CIVIL CODE- NOTES TO SECS . 129 , 131 .
131.
b . In an action on a note payable to A , and indorsed to the plaintiff , the petition
alleged that the note was payable to the plaintiff , and referred to it , but made
no reference to the assignment . The note was filed with the petition . A judg-
ment by default was reversed on account of the variance between the petition
and note . Dodd vs. King, 1 Met., 430 .
c. In an action on a contract, made June 2d , to sell certain slaves to the plaintiff as
soon as they should have passed through the measles , and to be paid for on
delivery ; the defendant pleaded that on July 6th he saw the plaintiff , and it was
then agreed that the slaves should be delivered on July 18th , on which day the
plaintiff came and said he had no money , and did not pay for or take the slaves ;
that, on the same day , the defendant notified him to come and take them on
July 24th , but he failed to come , or send any word to the defendant , and never
paid or tendered any money to the defendant ; and that the defendant , suppos-
ing that the plaintiff had disaffirmed the contract , did , on the 17th of August ,
and afterward , sell said slaves . Evidence was admitted to show that on the
18th of July the slaves had passed through the measles , and that the plaintiff
was so notified ; and the jury was instructed to find for the defendant , if, after
the slaves had passed through the measles , and the plaintiff had been notified
of that fact, he did not, within a reasonable time , take and pay for them , but
failed and refused to do so . After a verdict and judgment for the defendant , it
was held that there was not such a variance between the allegations and proof
as to authorize a reversal . Woodcock vs. Farrell , 1 Met., 437 .
d. A bill for " current exchange ," which , under the R. S. , ch . 22, § 11, is to be
" treated in all respects as if drawn for money , except as to the value of the
currency in which it is payable " -was declared on as a bill for money . On de-
murrer, the variance was held to be immaterial. Morrison & Co. vs. Tate , 1 Met.,
569 .
e. In an action upon a writing which purported to be an unconditional guaranty ,
but which did not express the consideration on which it was executed , the peti-
tion alleged that the contract was conditional , depending for its validity upon
the performance of certain acts by the promisee : held , that there was no vari-
ance between the contract contained in the writing , and that stated in the peti-
tion . Steadman vs. Guthrie et al., 4 Met., 153, 154.
Gen-
f. In an action for the hire of slaves for the year 1863, the proof was that they
were hired for the year 1864 ; and the variance was held to be immaterial .
try vs. Doolin, 1 Bush, 1.
g. In an action on a note alleged to have been executed and delivered to the plaintiffs ,
there being no denial by answer of that allegation , a variance between the peti-
tion and the note , as to the name of one of the plaintiffs , was held to be imma-
terial . Anderson vs. Rogers & Clark, I Bush, 200 .
h. The allegation , in a petition , of willful negligence , necessarily includes all
inferior grades of negligence . L., C. & L. R. R. Co. vs. Case's adm'r, 9
Bush, 733 .
i. If, in an action .upon a bill of exchange , the petition alleges due presentment for
payment ; and that allegation is denied by the defendant's answer ; and , on the
trial , the plaintiff introduces evidence , without objection , to prove that present-
ment was unnecessary ,-can the court consider the evidence ? Barbaroux vs.
Waters , 3 Met., 304 .
FAILURE OF PROOF .
a. In an action against two defendants , upon a contract alleged to have been made
by them , a judgment against one , upon proof that the contract was made by
him only , was held to be erroneous . Gossom vs. Badgett , 6 Bush , 97 .

CIVIL CODE- NOTES TO SECS . 131 , 132 , 134 . 49
But, in an action against A, upon a contract alleged to have been made by him ,
a judgment against him was sustained , though the evidence showed that the
contract was made by him and others . Waits vs. McClure, 10 Bush , 763 .
AMENDED PETITIONS .8132.
a . Summons must be served on an amended petition which states a new cause of
action (Cecil vs. Sowards , 10 Bush, 96) ; although it be filed before the filing of an
answer (Rutledge vs. Vanmeter , 8 Bush , 354 ) : aliter , if the amended petition do not
state a new cause of action . Thus , in an action against husband and wife for
her debt , created before marriage , it was held that no summons was necessary
upon an amended petition stating that they had been divorced , and asking for a
judgment against her . Joyes vs. Hamilton , 10 Bush , 544. And , in an action by
A's administrator for damages , the petition alleged that A's death was caused by
the defendant's negligence ; an amended petition alleged that the death was
caused by the defendant's " willful negligence :" held , that no summons was nec-
essary . L. , C. & L. R. R. Co. vs. Case's adm'r , 9 Bush, 728 .
But , in an action against the defendant , as indorser of a bill of exchange , an
amended petition , alleging that he had wrongfully converted the bill to his own
use , was held to be a statement of a new cause of action .
Kentucky , 8 Bush, 193.
134. 1. Amendments in attachment -cases.
2. Amendments in other cases.
3. Substantial and unsubstantial errors and defects.
1. AMENDMENTS IN ATTACHMENT -CASES.
See 268 , subs . 2, and note 2 to ? 196.
2. AMENDMENTS IN OTHER CASES.
Hyatt vs. Bank of
a. As to stating , in an amended pleading , facts which are inconsistent with those
stated in a previous pleading ; and as to stating in an amended petition a cause of
action which could not have been joined with that stated in the original petition ,
see ? 113, subs . 4, and notes thereto .
b. In an action against a city for work done at its instance , the petition alleged the
performance of the work ; the proof was that the work was done under a special
contract that the plaintiff was to look to the lot-owners for payment ; and the
court instructed the jury to find for the defendant ; after which , but before the
verdict was rendered , the plaintiff offered an amended petition , alleging facts
sufficient to render the city liable under the special contract . Held, that the
amended petition should have been permitted to be filed . Kearney vs. City of
Cov ., 1 Met . , 339 .
c. The defendant pleaded that the note sued on contained $ 160 of usury . The
plaintiff , in reply , admitted that it contained $ 36.92 of usury . Upon the
statement of a witness tending to show there was no usury , the plaintiff
was allowed to amend his reply and deny that the note contained usury .
Held, to be error. Hubble vs. Murphy, 1 Duv ., 278.
d. In Downing vs. Bacon , 7 Bush , 680 , the defendant was allowed to file an amended
answer correcting an alleged mistake in a former answer , with reference to a fact
which was presumptively within his knowledge.
e. The plaintiff in an action for slander cannot , by an amended petition , declare
upon words spoken by the defendant pendente lite . Taylor vs. Moran , 4 Met.,
127.
3. SUBSTANTIAL AND UNSUBSTANTIAL ERRORS AND DEFECTS .
a. A party cannot object for the first time in the Court of Appeals because the
record fails to show that the court below disposed of a demurrer , or of a refer-

entries.ocr_text:
50 CIVIL CODE- NOTES TO SEC . 134 .
ence to arbitrators : after trial it must be presumed that the demurrer was waived
or overruled , and the order of reference set aside or abandoned by the parties .
Danville, Lancaster and Nicholasville Turnpike Road Co. vs. Stewart , 2 Met . , 119.
b . An error of the court in refusing to strike irrelevant or redundant matter from
a pleading cannot avail for a reversal . Buckles vs. Lambert , 4 Met ., 330 .
c. The plaintiff sued for services alleged to have been rendered in 1863, and proved
that they were rendered in 1864 : the variance was held to be immaterial . Gen-
try vs. Doolin , 1 Bush, 1.
d. The Code requires arbitrators to make an oath " to decide the controversy to
them submitted according to law and evidence and the equity of the case , to
the best of their judgment without favor or affection ." An oath that they
" would faithfully discharge the duties devolved on them as arbitrators " was
held to be sufficient . Snyder vs. Rouse , 1 Met ., 625 .
e. In an action for a breach of the plaintiff's close , and an assault and battery upon
him , in which there was no evidence of a breach of the close , the jury found
the defendant " guilty as charged in the petition , " and assessed damages , and
judgment was rendered accordingly : it was held , that , though the verdict may
have been informal , it must be assumed that the jury did not assess any part of
the damages on account of the alleged breach of the close , and that the judg-
ment must be sustained . Noel vs. Hudson , 13 B. M. , 204 .
f. The objection that there are blanks in a petition , or redundant matter , cannot .
avail after verdict . E. & P. R. R. Co. vs. Pottinger & Bro ., 10 Bush , 187.
g. In a case in which the petition failed to show a cause of action , by a statement
of either facts or legal conclusions , it was held, that the defect was cured by a
verdict and judgment for the plaintiff . In that case , A's execution against B,
having been levied on tobacco , and A having given a bond to indemnify any
claimant of it, C , claiming it , sued A on the bond , but did not state its terms ,
nor allege a breach . A answered that the tobacco was B's property ; C obtained
a verdict and judgment ; A made no motion to arrest the judgment , and in his
motion for a new trial did not suggest the defects of the petition : on an appeal
by A, the judgment was affirmed . Riggs vs. Maltby & Co. , 2 Met ., 88.
h. But in a similar case to that in 2 Met ., 88, it was held that the defect was not
cured . A woman brought an action for dower against a purchaser who bought
the land from her husband in 1855 ; she alleged that her husband owned the
land in 1855, and that she was his wife when he died in 1858 ; the defendant
denied her right to dower on the ground that it was barred by jointure : a judg
ment in her favor was reversed because the petition did not allege that she was
the vendor's wife at the time of the sale in 1855. Yancy vs. Smith , 2 Met ., 408 .
i. It was said , that an affidavit that the plaintiff believes, that the defendant has
concealed himself , & c., is perhaps sufficient to authorize an attachment ; but if
not, yet if the defendant , instead of moving to discharge the attachment , files a
response , denying that he has concealed himself, & c., the Court of Appeals will
not reverse a judgment against him because the affidavit was defective . Ryon
vs. Bean's adm'r , 2 Met . , 137.
j. In an attachment -suit , A filed a petition claiming the attached property, and the
court ordered him to be made a defendant , which was not done ; but the case
proceeded as if it had been done ; and A obtained a verdict and judgment ,
which the court refused to disturb . Schwein vs. Sims et al. , 2 Met ., 209 .
k. In an action for specific personal property , the plaintiff made affidavit that it
was worth $ 200 ; and obtained possession , pursuant to provisions of the Code
concerning the " claim and delivery of personal property ;" the defendant con-
troverted the plaintiff's right to the property , which was proved to be worth

CIVIL CODE - NOTES TO SECS . 134 , 135 . 51
8135.
$90 ; and the jury found for the defendant : a judgment in the defendant's favor
for the return of the property, or for $ 90, was reversed , because the jury did
not find what was the value of the property . Young vs. Parsons , 2 Met ., 499 .
7. Parties, having appeared to a motion , having agreed on a day to try it , and
having tried it on the merits , were held to have waived objections to all pre-
vious irregularities . Commonwealth vs. Marks, 2 Duv ., 387 .
m . It was held that the plaintiff , by replying to a counter -claim , waived the objec-
tion that the counter -claim had no connection with the subject of the action.
Boyd vs. Day & Gorrell , 3 Bush, 617 .
n . An objection to an answer , because it was not properly verified , was waived by
the plaintiff's offering to reply to it, by his filing an amended petition , and by his
expressly waiving any objections to a subsequent answer . Either of those acts
would have been a waiver. Mason vs. Mason , 5 Bush , 187.
o. If it were erroneous to permit the filing of a cross -petition of an infant , by his
guardian ad litem , because the infant was not summoned , the plaintiffs have no
right to object after issue , and judgment for the infant . Mason vs. Mason , 5
Bush , 187.
p. In an action for the price of extra work , though the petition fail to show that the
plaintiff is entitled to pay therefor , yet , if the answer deny that any extra work
was done , the defect of the petition may be cured by a verdict for the plaintiff .
Escott & Son vs. White , 10 Bush, 169.
q. In an action upon a contract , the petition failed to state a cause of action ; the
answer denied the making of the contract ; the plaintiff obtained a verdict and
judgment for the sum claimed in the petition : the judgment was reversed be-
cause the petition did not state a cause of action. Sousely vs. Burns's adm'r,
10 Bush, 87.
r. In an action for a breach of an agreement to marry , the petition was defective in
failing to aver that the plaintiff ever offered to perform the engagement , or pro-
posed to the defendant to fix time and place , and execute the contract . The
answer relied , as matter of defense , upon the alleged fact that the plaintiff was
an infant , and that her father had refused consent to the marriage ; and , sec-
ondly , that by mutual consent the marriage contract was rescinded . A judg
ment for the plaintiff , upon a verdict of a jury , was reversed because the petition
did not state a cause of action . Fible vs. Caplinger , 13 B. M. , 464 .
s. If the plaintiff's right to recover depend on the performance of a condition pre-
cedent , his failure to aver performance is cause for reversal , though the action
was defended , not upon that , but upon other grounds. Fry's ex'r vs: Lex . & B.
S. R. R. Co. , 2 Met ., 324.
That doctrine may be right , but the application of it , in that case , seems question-
able . See note m to 90, page 24 .
t. As to the effect of a trial upon pleadings which form an issue of legal conclu-
sions , instead of an issue of fact , see note 3 o to ? 90 , page 20 ; and note 4 to ? 95,
page 31.
SUPPLEMENTAL PLEADINGS .
a . A petition by a mortgagee for the possession of mortgaged property , having
been filed before a forfeiture , may be supplemented by an amended petition ,
alleging the forfeiture , pendente lite . Brookover vs. Hurst , 1 Met . , 665 .
b. But the plaintiff in an action for slander cannot , by an amended petition , com-
plain of words spoken pendente lite . Taylor vs. Moran , 4 Met ., 127.

52 CIVIL CODE- NOTES TO SECS . 138 , 140 , 141 , 152 , 153 , 162 .
138. WAIVER OF OBJECTION AS TO VERIFICATION .
a. The words , " the commencement of the trial , " in 2 138, were substituted for " the
judgment ," in ? 165.
It was held that a motion made by the defendant , after a verdict against him , but
before judgment , to dismiss the petition because it was not verified , was properly
overruled ; upon the ground that the defendant waived the objection by answer-
ing and going into trial . Meador vs. Turpin , 4 Met ., 93 .
And , the plaintiff having moved , after the jury was sworn , to strike from the record
the defendant's amended answer , because it had not been verified ; the court held
that the defendant should have been allowed to verify it. Wheeler vs. Wales , 3
Bush, 225.
140, 141. INTERROGATORIES TO PARTIES
a. The defendant is not bound to answer interrogatories contained in the body of
the petition (Burnett & Miller vs. Garnett & Thompson , 18 B. M. , 68) ; nor to
make a statement which might subject him to a criminal or penal prosecution .
Cole vs. Wilson , 18 Id. , 216.
b. Defendant's answers to plaintiff's interrogatories have the effect of a deposition .
Short vs. Tinsley, I Met . , 397 ..
c. Under the Code of 1854, a party could not testify for himself except concerning
new matters stated by the party interrogated . Musick vs. Ray , 3 Met., 431 .
d. Under the Code of 1854, a party could not compel the adverse party to give a
deposition . But a plaintiff voluntarily gave his deposition at the request of the
defendant ; and it was held that this did not give the defendant the right to tes-
tify even as to new matters . Musick vs. Ray, 3 Met ., 427 .
e. As to the rights and duties of parties with reference to testifying , under this
Code, see sections 606 , 608 , 609 .
8 152, 153. ORDERS OF ARREST .
a. See sections 237 to 248, as to arresting debtors in actions brought before maturity
of the debt .
b. An order of court, sustaining an order of arrest made by the clerk before the
filing of the requisite affidavit , is a judicial error, and not a clerical misprision .
Pauer vs. Simon , 6 Bush , 514.
c. The defendant was arrested upon an affidavit that he was " about to depart from
this State , without leaving property therein sufficient to satisfy the plaintiff's
claim "-viz : $ 500 . It was proved that the defendant , though about to leave
the State , had on deposit , in a Kentucky bank, good cash notes amounting to
at least $ 20,000 , and stock , of the value of $ 1,300 in a turnpike company .
The court said : " Whether the evidences of debt deposited in the bank should
be regarded as property within the meaning of the Code [or not ], we are satisfied
that the turnpike road stock should be ." Fields vs. Montmollin , 5 Bush, 455 .
d. The intention of a resident of this State to leave it , temporarily, on business ,
with the intention to return , does not subject him to arrest . Myall vs. Wright, 2
Bush , 130.
162 a. Liability of sheriff's sureties, for money deposited in lieu of bail, is , I , that the sher-
iff shall account for the money to the court ; 2, that he shall keep it , if directed
to do so by the court . They are not responsible for his conduct under an order
of court directing him to lend the money . Sanders vs. Parrott , 1 Duv ., 292.

CIVIL CODE- NOTES TO SECS . 163 , 171 , 181 , 184 . 53
-2.163 .
8.171.
BAIL -BOND .
a. A bail -bond , stipulating that the defendant " will perform the judgment in the
action," was held to be void . Lex . and Danville R. R. Co. vs. Barbee , 1 Met. ,
384 .
b. But a bond, that the defendant will " render himself amenable to the court there-
upon❞ —omitting the words " process of ” —is valid . Abbott vs. Daniel , 3 Met.,
339 .
c. The sheriff's failure to return the bond to the clerk's office does not affect the
rights of the obligee . Jones vs. Bunn , 2 Met . , 490 .
d. As to some requisite allegations , in an action on a bail -bond , see Jones vs. Bunn ,
2 Met. , 490 .
FIXING LIABILITY OF BAIL .
a . To fix the liability of bail , the Code requires that an execution against the body
of the defendant shall be placed in the hands of the sheriff within twenty days
after such execution may be issued ; but does not require a return of the execu-
tion within twenty days after the judgment , nor within twenty days after the
execution is issued . Allcorn vs. Tuggle , 3 Met ., 537 .
b. Nor does a return of "not found ," made before the return -day of the execution ,
release the bail . Jones vs. Bunn , 2 Met., 490 .
. An execution commanding the sheriff to take the body of the defendant , to sat-
isfy and pay the Commonwealth of Kentucky the sum of $ 125.00 , adjudged , not in
favor of the Commonwealth , BUT IN FAVOR OF A ; and a return of " not found "
upon the execution , did not fix the liability of bail . Abbott vs. Daniel, 3 Met.,
339 .
d. The liability of bail , when fixed in the mode prescribed by the Code , is for
the judgment and costs , without regard to the solvency or insolvency of the
principal . Abbott vs. Daniel, 3 Met., 342 .
181, 184. ACTION FOR THE IMMEDIATE DELIVERY OF PERSONAL PROP .
ERTY .
a . If the plaintiff has obtained possession of the property , pursuant to this sec-
tion , a verdict for the defendant must state the value of the property . Young
vs. Parsons , 2 Met., 499 .
b. A defendant from whose possession a horse was taken , filed an answer , pursuant
to this section , claiming title , and praying to be hence dismissed with costs: held,
that he , having proved his title , was entitled to a return of the horse , or to its
value , and to damages for the detention . Bates vs. Buchanan , 2 Bush , 117 ; and
see 8 Bush , 163.
c. A plaintiff , having obtained possession of property , pursuant to 181, cannot
dismiss his action to the prejudice of the defendant . Rogers vs. Bradford , 8
Bush , 163.
d. In such cases , if the jury find for the defendant , the judgment should be for the
return of the property , or for its value , &c .; and a judgment- not for a return
of the property , or its value - but for its value , is erroneous . Rogers vs. Brad-
ford, 8 Bush, 163.
e. A, in an action against B, obtained possession of a horse , pursuant to 181. C ,
on his application , was made a defendant , and filed an answer avowing title in
B, under a sale , with warranty, made by him , C , to B. A judgment was erro-
neously rendered in favor of C , instead of B, for the value of the horse . It was
held , however , that C was entitled to recover the value of the horse , in an action
upon the bond of A and his surety . Mc Glasson vs. Bradford, 7 Bush , 250 .

54 CIVIL CODE- NOTES TO SECS . 184 , 188 , 194 .
? 188 .
Quere : that decision was avowedly based upon a provision of 2 874 of the Code of
1854, which is not in this Code-viz : that " the provisions of this Code , and alli
proceedings under it , shall be liberally construed , with a view to promote its
object, and assist the parties in obtaining justice ."
BOND TO OBTAIN RESTORATION OF PROPERTY .
a. A sued B, C, and D, Ist for a debt of $ 500 due from B ; 2d to foreclose a
mortgage on a slave made by B to secure that debt ; and 3d for the immediate
possession of said slave : C and D held a junior mortgage on the slave , and were
therefore made defendants : the defendants , with E as surety , gave bond to per-
form the judgment of the court in the action : there was a judgment for $ 500
against B, and for a sale of the slave to pay it : the slave was surrendered and
sold for $400 : A sued on the bond for the residue of the debt , and it was held,
that he was not entitled to recover . McKee vs. Pope , 18 B. M. , 553.
b. Surety in such bond cannot be proceeded against by motion .
Bush, 606 .
Gay vs. Morgan, 4
c. Obligors in the bond are liable for costs of the action , as well as for the property .
Galloway vs. Bethune , 6 Bush , 113.
194. GENERAL ATTACHMENTS .
a. In what county attachment to be obtained.
An attachment is ancillary to the action , and cannot be obtained in a county dif
ferent from that in which the action is pending . Moore's adm'r vs. Sheppard , I
Met., 97.
i. Property liable to attachment .
Money due to a teacher of a common school cannot be attached in the hands of
school -commissioners . Tracy & Loyd vs. Hornbuckle and wife , 8 Bush , 336. Nor
can pension -money be attached . Eckert vs. McKee , 9 Bush , 355 .
c. An act of the Legislature , adopted before the Code , having , in effect , exempted
property of the Mobile and Ohio R. R. Company from attachment , its property
was not made liable to attachment by provisions of the Code. Martin & Merri-
wether vs. Mobile and Ohio R. R. Co. , 7 Bush , 116.
d. Land may be attached though occupied by a military encampment .
Mahone , 9 Bush, 120.
Thomas vs.
e. Indebtedness upon negotiable paper is not , before its maturity , subject to attach-
ment . Greer vs. Powell, 1 Bush, 489 .
f. Effect of attachment .
An attachment gives only an equitable lien , which must yield to a prior equity ;
such as a title under an unrecorded assignment ( Ward vs. Crotty , 4 Met ., 61) ; or
the right of a vendor to goods which have been purchased from him with the
fraudulent intent not to pay for them (Lane & Bartlett vs. Robinson , 18 B M.,
623 ) ; or the right of partnership -creditors to subject partnership -property (Oban-
non & Bashaw vs. Miller & Hopkins , 4 Bush , 25 ; Howell, Gano & Co. vs. Com-
mercial Bank of Ky ., 5 Bush , 93) ; or the right of a creditor who has advanced
money on property pledged by the delivery of the bill of lading (Pettit & Co. vs.
First National Bank of Memphis , 4 Bush, 334) ; or the right of a principal to
money deposited in bank by his agent to the credit of the agent , the money be--
ing the proceeds of stock belonging to the principal which had been sold by the
agent . Skilman vs. Miller , 7 Bush , 428 .
It was held, however , that an attachment of personal property in favor of a Kentucky
creditor of a citizen of Ohio gave a lien superior to the claim of an assignee for
the payment of debts , under an assignment made in Ohio , though the assignee-

CIVIL CODE- NOTES TO SECS . 194 , 196. 55
had accepted the deed , and it conformed to the laws of Kentucky and Ohio .
Johnson vs. Parker, & c., 4 Bush, 149.
An attaching creditor occupies no better position than that of his debtor , as to a
claim against agarnishee . Greer vs. Powell , I Bush , 497 .
An attachment of land cannot be affected by the subsequent levy of an execution .
Husbands vs. Jones , 9 Bush, 223 .
g. Grounds of attachment .
Under the Code of 1854 the existence of a ground of attachment against a single
defendant authorized an attachment of the property of other defendants jointly
liable with him for the plaintiff's demand . 2 Met ., 440 : aliter under this Code .
An affidavit stating , in the alternative , two grounds of attachment is sufficient , if
each of the grounds would authorize an attachment . Wood vs. Wells , 2 Bush,
197 ; Hardy , & c., vs. Trabue , Davis & Co. , 4 Bush , 644 .
h. Non -residence is ground for an attachment , though the defendant be in this
State . Jackson vs. Perry, 13 B. M. , 231 .
i. Four months ' absence. The defendant left his residence , intending to go out
of the State , and did go . It was held that four months ' absence from his resi-
dence authorized an attachment , though an unexpected casualty prevented him ,
for a few days , from leaving the State . Spalding vs. Simms , 4 Met ., 285 .
j. Concealment . An attachment is authorized , if the defendant , to avoid the ser-
vice of a summons , conceal himself at home , or secretly depart therefrom to
some other place in or out of the county . Dunn vs. Salter , 1 Duv . , 342 .
k. Removal of property . The insolvency of a debtor, or his attempt to remove
from the State and not return , is not ground for an attachment . Clarke vs. Sea-
ton, 18 B. M. , 230.
1. Fraudulent conveyance. Is an allegation that property was conveyed with the
intent to defraud creditors sustained by proof of constructive fraud ? See I
B. M. , 155 ; 3 Id . , 201 ; 9 Id. , 431 .
m. Intent to defraud . An affidavit that the defendant is " about to waste or dis-
pose of his property , " with fraudulent intent , is sufficient . Lane vs. Robinson ,
18 B. M. , 632 .
196 1. Affidavit for attachment.
2. Amendments in attachment-cases.
1. AFFIDAVIT FOR ATTACHMENT .
a. If the petition state the facts , and be sworn to , a separate affidavit is unnecessary .
Scott vs. Doneghy , 17 B. M. , 321 ; Burnam vs. Romans , 2 Bush , 191.
b. An affidavit that the plaintiff " is informed and believes " that the defendant is
about to sell , & c. , with a verification stating that the plaintiff " believes " that
the said affidavit is true , is not sufficient to authorize an attachment . Williams
vs. Martin , I Met ., 42 .
But such an affidavit , with a verification stating that it is true , is sufficient . Ryon
vs. Bean's adm'r , 2 Met ., 137.
c. Failure of the affidavit to state that the claim is just is fatal to an attachment
(Worthington vs. Carey , 1 Met . , 470) ; though the demand be not controverted by
the defendant's answer . Taylor vs. Smith , 17 B. M. , 542 .
d. An affidavit that the plaintiff " will recover "" a certain sum is not equivalent to
an affidavit that the claim " is just , " and that he " ought to recover , " and is not
sufficient . Pool vs. Webster, 3 Met . , 281.
.e. An agent's affidavit that he believes the statements of the petition are true is
sufficient (Franklin Savings Institution vs. M. M. Bank of Wheeling , 1 Met ., 156) ;
but he must state that the principal is out of the county . Pool vs. Webster, 3
Met., 278.
