56 CIVIL CODE- NOTES TO SECS . 196 , 198 .
198.
f. An answer , denying the statements of the plaintiff's affidavit , makes an issue , and
casts the burden of proof on the plaintiff ; and the issue should be tried by the-
court, even in an ordinary action . Talbot vs. Pierce , 14 B. M. , 195.
g. It was held , under the Code of 1854, that the plaintiff's statement , in his peti-
tion , or in his separate affidavit , that the defendant had been absent from the
State four months , or is a non-resident , must be proved , although not contro-
verted by the answer or affidavit of the defendant . Buckner vs. Bush, 1 Duv .,
394 ; Jackson vs. McElroy , 2 Bush , 132.
But see, now, 258 , subs . 6. See , also , Garland vs. Denny & Colston , 3 B. M. , 127.
2. AMENDMENTS IN ATTACHMENT -CASES.
h . It was held , under the Code of 1854, that the plaintiff might amend a defective
petition on which he had obtained an attachment under 2 476 (Lewis vs. Quinker ,
2 Duv . , 284) ; or under 2 221 (Clarke vs. Seaton, 18 B. M. , 230) ; or might amend
a defective affidavit on which an attachment was obtained under 2 221. Allen vs.
Brown , 4 Met . , 342 (overruling Poole vs. Webster, 3 Met ., 282) ; Bailey, vs. Beadles
& Bolinger, 7 Bush , 383 .
Those cases refer merely to defective statements of grounds of attachment existing
when it was obtained , or defective statements as to the nature , justness , and
amount of the plaintiff's claim . An act of March 15, 1862, declared that "in
any proceeding by attachment , now pending or hereafter commenced , the affida-
vit or ground of attachment may be amended , so as to embrace any grounds of
attachment that may exist up to and until the final judgment upon the same .
If the amendments embrace only grounds existing at the commencement of the
proceeding , the lien created by suing out or levying the original attachment shall
be held good ; but , if the amendments embrace new grounds , not existing at the
time of suing out the original attachment , the lien shall exist on the property
levied on from the filing of the same ." Sess. Acts , 92 .
That act relates only to the grounds of the attachment , and authorizes amendments
merely for the purpose of stating grounds which existed when the attachment
was obtained , and which were not stated in the original affidavit ; or new grounds
arising subsequently . Allen vs. Brown , 4 Met ., 342 .
It was held that an amendment of a defective attachment could not override ar
intermediate attachment . Bell, Berkley & Co. vs. Hall's ex'rs , 2 Duv . , 288 ;
Peters vs. Conway , 4 Bush , 571.
See 268.
LIABILITY FOR WRONGFUL ATTACHMENT .
A person , whose property has been wrongfully attached , may obtain redress-
1. By an action on the attachment-bond.
In this action , the plaintiff need not allege malice , or want of probable cause ; but
must allege that the attachment was " wrongfully obtained " —of which , as a
general rule, a judgment discharging the attachment is conclusive evidence. (See cases
cited in the next following paragraph .) For the only exception to that rule ,
known by the writer of this note , see Cooper vs. Hill, 3 Bush , 219.
In this action, the plaintiff may recover actual damages ; as for injury sustained by
being deprived of the use of his property, or by its loss , or deterioration (Kaye
vs. Kean, 18 B. M. , 839) ; or loss sustained by his prevention from performing a
contract (Carpenter vs. Stevenson, 6 Bush , 259) ; and he may recover the costs of
the attachment , including a reasonable attorney's fee (Trapnall vs. McAfee , 3 Met .,
34) ; but it must appear that he has paid , or agreed to pay , to his attorney as
certain sum . Shultz vs. Morrison, & c., 3 Met ., 98.

c. Bond given pursuant to 214 does not discharge the attachment . A person

doing so by executing a forthcoming bond . The plaintiff cannot maintain an
action on the bond , unless the attachment be sustained . If the attachment be
sustained , the obligors are estopped by 216 from re -litigating the question
whether or not the property was subject to the attachment . Schwein vs. Sims , 2
Met., 212 ; Miller vs. Desha and wife , 3 Bush , 212 .
b. See notes to ? 29.
DISPOSITION OF ATTACHED PROPERTY .
a. It appeared that a garnishee was indebted to the defendant , but it did not appear
how much the garnishee owed , or when the debt would fall due : held, that the
court could only make such interlocutory order , in the form of an injunction , or
otherwise , as will protect the rights of the plaintiff , retaining control of the case
for such further and final judgment as the facts afterward to be developed , and
shown by additional pleadings , may authorize . Roberts vs. Drinkard , 3 Met . , 309 .
b. The court may order the sale of attached property upon parol proof , and the
correctness of the order will be presumed , nothing appearing to the contrary .
Dunn vs. Salter , 1 Duv . , 246-7 .
c. It was held in Greer vs. Powell, 1 Met . , 124, that the sale of attached property
under an order of the presiding judge of the county court must be confirmed by
the court in which the action was pending .
d. In an action under the act of 1856 concerning fraudulent preferences , it was held
that the court might order a sale of the property , before final hearing , though
the petition did not allege that the property would be illegally disposed of, or
would not be forthcoming at the rendition of the judgment . Terrill vs. Jen-
nings , I Met., 459 .
EFFECT OF BOND TO DISCHARGE ATTACHMENT .
a . Observe the distinction between a bond to retain possession , under 2 214, which
does not discharge the attachment , and a bond under ? 221, which does discharge
the attachment ; and see 2 Met ., 209 , and 3 Met. , 558 .
b. In an action on a bond conforming to ? 221, neither the sufficiency of the grounds
of the attachment , nor the liability of the property to the attachment , nor the
right of an obligor in the bond to the property , can be considered . Hazelrigg
vs. Donaldson , 2 Met ., 445 ; Taylor vs. Taylor, 3 Bush , 118 ; Inman & Carr vs.
Stratten & Snodgrass , 4 Bush , 445 .
In Hazelrigg vs. Donaldson the claimant had a right to give, and offered to give, a
forthcoming bond , pursuant to 2 214 ; but the sheriff refused to let him retain
possession , unless he would execute the bond pursuant to ? 221. The court held
that the unlawful withholding of a man's property by a sheriff does not consti-
tute duress ; and , consequently , that the claimant was estopped by his bond , ex-
ecuted pursuant to 221, from asserting his right to the property .
c. Although , if an attachment of specific property be discharged by giving the
bond prescribed in 221, the bond may be enforced by rule pursuant to ? 232
(see 258) , the obligors are entitled to notice . Taylor vs. Taylor , 3 Bush , 118.
d. Surety in a bond given pursuant to 221 is released by a bond replevying an
execution issued on the judgment . Ray vs. Merrill , 11 Bush, 633.
e. See notes to sections 29 and 214.
223 to 227. PROCEEDINGS AGAINST GARNISHEES .
a. Where the garnishee in a general attachment has been summoned , and appears ,
and discloses the amount of his indebtedness to the defendant ; or, where he
makes default by failing to appear , and the court, as it may do , hears proof as

court , or to a receiver, or may be allowed to retain it on the terms fixed by law .
The court may make either of these orders , at its discretion , and as the circum-
stances of the case may render most appropriate , and may , by its process of rule
and attachment , compel the garnishee to obey and perform its mandate , as in
like cases . If, at the time the garnishee so discloses , or the amount of his indebt-
edness is so proved , there is a judgment against the defendant to which the in-
debtedness can be immediately applied , the appropriate order would be to require
the garnishee to pay the fund into court ; and when paid , the proceeds should
be applied as directed in 229. Smith vs. Gower , 3 Met ., 171.
b. But it is erroneous to render a personal judgment , enforceable by execution , against
a garnishee , until he has been made a defendant to the action , pursuant to 2 227.
Smith vs. Gower , 3 Met ., 171 ; Griswold vs. Popham , ɩ Duv . , 170. Aliter if he be
made a defendant to the action . Bowen vs. Emmerson , 4 Bush , 345.
c. If the payor of a note , having notice of its assignment , be summoned as gar-
nishee , and fail to make the assignee a party, by cross -petition or otherwise , he is
liable to the assignee , notwithstanding a judgment in favor of the attaching
creditor, and although the payor stated in his answer that he had been informed
that his note had been assigned . Bibb vs. Tomberlin , 1 Duv ., 186.
229. ORDER IN WHICH ATTACHED PROPERTY SHALL BE SUBJECTED .
a . Under 250 of the Code of 1854, it appearing that garnishees owed a sufficient
amount to pay the plaintiff's demand , a judgment for the sale of land was erro-
neous : the debts garnisheed should have been first applied to the satisfaction
of the judgment . Anderson vs. Sutton , 2 Duv ., 489 .
230 . AFFIDAVIT BEFORE SALE OF REAL PROPERTY .
a. An order for the sale of real property attached , before the filing of an affidavit .
as to the defendant's personal property , is an error of the court, and not a cler--
ical misprision . Payne vs. Witherspoon , 14 B. M. , 270 .
239. STATEMENT IN ORDER OF ATTACHMENT OF THE SUM FOR WHICH
IT IS ALLOWED .
8252 .
8263 .
a. The petition having stated the amount of the plaintiff's demand , and having
prayed judgment therefor , it was held that an order of attachment , granted by
the clerk , " according to the prayer of the petition , " was valid . Burnam vs.
Ronans , 2 Bush , 191.
BOND FOR SPECIFIC ATTACHMENT .
a. In an action of A against B, to enforce a mortgage on a boat , C and D executed
a bond to the effect that they would pay to the plaintiffs such sums as might be
adjudged in the action , or would have the boat " forthcoming for the satisfaction
of such judgment , whichever shall be ordered by the court." The court gave
judgment against B for the debt due the plaintiff , and ordered C and D to pay
it ; and issued a rule against them , to which they responded , offering to surrender
the boat , and saying that it was in as good condition as when they gave the
bond : held, that the response was sufficient . Halbert vs. McCulloch , 3 Met ., 456 .
And see 18 B. M., 553.
AFFIDAVITS IN ATTACHMENT -CASES .

shall have no other effect , " than to constitute pleadings concerning the attach-
ment , was designed merely to prevent them from constituting evidence ; and that

controverting the attachment . Duncan vs. Wickliffe , 4 Met ., 118.
b. Grounds stated in the plaintiff's affidavit for an attachment are sufficiently con-
troverted by a denial of them in the defendant's answer . Talbot vs. Pierce , 14-
B. M. , 201. And see 261 , which expressly makes such denial sufficient .
264. EVIDENCE ON , AND TRIAL OF , MOTION TO DISCHARGE ATTACH-
MENT .
a. Under the Code of 1851 ( 8 307) , affidavits were admissible as evidence on motion
to discharge attachments . Talbot vs. Pierce , 14 B. M. , 197. Aliter now . See 3.
Met. , 27.
b. Though the Code of 1851 did not expressly declare , as this Code does , that trials-
of attachments shall be by the court , it was held that such a trial might be by
the court, and without a transfer to the equity-docket , though the attachment
had been obtained in an ordinary action . Talbot vs. Pierce , 14 B. M. , 201-2 .
265 , 266. EFFECT OF ORDER , ON MOTION TO DISCHARGE ATTACHMENT .
a. An order , overruling a motion to discharge an attachment , is not final ; and,
consequently , an attachment may be discharged on final hearing, though a motion
to discharge it may have been overruled ; and though no additional evidence may
have been introduced . Talbot vs. Pierce , 14 B. M. , 195.
b . Nor is a preliminary order , sustaining an attachment , a final order : the court
may change the order before , or upon , the final hearing of the case ; and , conse-
quently , no appeal can be taken from such an order . Hanson vs. Bowyer , 4 Met.,
108.
c. Nor is a preliminary order , discharging an attachment , a final order ; and , con,
sequently , the plaintiff cannot appeal therefrom : he should apply for a re -instate-
ment of his attachment to a judge of the Court of Appeals . Leet vs. Lockett , 4-
Met. , 55.
d. Though an order , on a motion to discharge an attachment is not final , it may,
after a final judgment in the action , be reviewed by the Court of Appeals . Tal
bot vs. Pierce , 14 B. M. , 195.
e. The defendant may appeal from so much of a judgment as sustains an attach-
ment of his property , without appealing from so much of it as orders him to pay
the debt . Such appeal [with a supersedeas] stays a sale of the attached property ,
but does not prevent the plaintiff from enforcing his judgment in personam , by
execution . If the judgment sustaining the attachment be affirmed , the court
will order a sale of the attached property , to satisfy the personal judgment , un-
less it has been satisfied . Hawkins vs. Baldauf & Bro . , 10 Bush , 624 .
268 , subs . 2. As to amendments in attachment cases, see note 2 to ? 196.
8271 . EFFECT OF INJUNCTION .
a. The Code has not changed the effect of an injunction , though the mode of pro-
curing it has been changed . An injunction releases property from the lien , or
the levy , of an execution . Keith vs. Wilson , 3 Met ., 201 .
272 to 277. INJUNCTIONS .
a. The Code has not enlarged the jurisdiction of the chancellor concerning injunc …
tions . Musselman vs. Marquis, 1 Bush , 464 .
b. Though , as a general rule , the chancellor will not enjoin the commission of a
trespass , he may enjoin the commission of repeated and successive trespasses by
irresponsible persons (Musselman vs. Marquis , 1 Bush, 463) ; or the cutting of
timber , or any intrusion , on land claimed by both parties (Peak vs. Hayden , 3-

Bush, 125) ; or a nuisance , such as the damming up of water on the plaintiff's
land (Hahn & Harris vs. Thornberry , & c., 7 Bush, 403) ; but the case must be
clearly made out , to authorize the exercise of this extraordinary power : a mere
possibility that the erection of a powder-house may result in injury to neighbors
will not authorize the chancellor to suppress it . Dumesnil vs. Dupont , 18 B.
M. , 800 .
And , the plaintiffs having erected a wharf in the town of Catlettsburg , as author-
ized by a special act of the Legislature , and the authorities of the town having
passed an ordinance requiring all boats arriving there to land at the public land-
ing ; the plaintiffs sought , by injunction , to restrain the enforcement of the ordi-
nance , claiming that it was void : held , that , though the action should have been
transferred to the ordinary docket , if the plaintiffs had sued for damages , as well
as for an injunction ; as they sued for an injunction only, and had failed to estab-
lish the illegality of the ordinance by a judgment at law- as they might have
done in an action for damages , if the ordinance was illegal- their petition was
properly dismissed . Brown , & c., vs. Trustees of Catlettsburg , 11 Bush, 435 .
c. Proceedings for the sale of devised property , and distribution of the proceeds ,
may be enjoined , until an action to set aside the will can be heard . Sanders vs.
Sanders , 17 B. M. , 10.
d. As to enjoining a judgment , see notes to ? 17.
INJUNCTION -BOND .
a. No action can be maintained upon an injunction -bond before a final judgment in
the case in which the bond is given . Wood vs. Laycock , 3 Met., 192.
JURISDICTION AS TO INJUNCTIONS .
a . A justice of the peace may enjoin a judgment rendered by him , though , by rea-
son of interest and costs , it exceed the amount of his jurisdiction . Davis vs.
Davis, 10 Bush, 274 .
b. Section 25 of the Code of 1854 declared that " the title of real property " should
not be affected by any judgment of a quarterly court . It was held, that the
judgment of a quarterly court , affecting title to land , must be enjoined by said
court ; but that the case must be transferred to the circuit court , pursuant to 2 25.
Kelly vs. Kelly , 2 Duv ., 363 .
For said 25, and nearly all of the provisions of the Code of 1854, concerning the
jurisdiction of courts , see the General Statutes .
8293. a . Order dissolving an injunction is not final . An action for a malicious procurement
of it cannot be maintained , until the action , in which it is obtained , is disposed
of. Wood vs. Laycock , 3 Met ., 192.
295 . DAMAGES UPON DISSOLUTION OF INJUNCTION .
a . Special damages cannot be recovered upon an injunction -bond , unless they be
assessed when the injunction is dissolved . Crawford vs. Woodworth , 9 Bush , 745 .
298 to 302.

Maysville , &c., vs. Punnett , 15 B. M. , 48 .
Observe the change made by ? 298 of this Code .
b. A receiver who had given bond and collected funds , subsequently , to obtain
indulgence , gave another bond - in which no obligee was named -with sureties ,
for the performance of his duties : held, that the sureties in the latter bond were
liable for said funds . Rowlet vs. Eubank , 1 Bush , 477 .

CIVIL CODE- NOTES TO SECS . 302 , 303 TO 308 , 312 , 313 . 63.
�
c. " Though no statute authorizes the appointment of a general receiver for the
courts , * if, for convenience , the receiver , by the consent and approval .
of the court, shall execute a general bond , we know of no law nor public policy
which would forbid or make it illegal ; and , therefore , it should be upheld as a
common law obligation , though not as a statutory bond ." Ellis vs. Carr, 1 Bush,
527.
303 to 308.
? 312 .
2313 .
DEPOSIT IN COURT .
a. Clerks of courts have no right , ex officio, to receive money upon a judgment , or
replevy -bond , or execution ; nor even to receive money paid into court ; and their
sureties are not liable for money so received by them . Durant vs. Gabby, 2 Met.,
91 ; Chinn vs. Mitchell, Id. , 92 ; Hardin vs. Carrico , 3 Met ., 289.
HOW ISSUES SHALL BE TRIED .
a. Observe the difference between this section and section 342 of the Code of 1854,
which declared that " issues of fact , arising in [ an ] action by ordinary proceed-
ings , for the recovery of money , or of specific real or personal property , shall be
tried by a jury , unless a jury -trial is waived ." Section 312 of this Code declares
that , in such cases , excepting actions for injury to person or character , issues of
fact shall be tried by the court , " unless a jury-trial be demanded by a party.”
And see 331 and notes thereto .
VERDICTS UPON EQUITABLE ISSUES .
a . In Moore's heirs vs. Shepherd, & c. , 2 Duv ., 132, it was held that , under the Code
of 1854, a verdict upon an issue in chancery is entitled to as much effect as a
verdict in a common law action . Contra , Hendrix vs. Money , 1 Bush , 309 ; and
see 9 Bush, 164.
b. It was also said in Moore's heirs vs. Shepherd, 2 Duv . , 132, that , even if a verdict
upon an issue in chancery be not, in all cases , entitled to the same effect as a
verdict in a common law action , " we cannot doubt that , when, as in this case ,
the parties prefer a jury and elect a trial of an issue of fact by the jury rather
than the court , the verdict is entitled to as much effect as if either party had
demanded a jury as a legal right ."
c. In Crabbe vs. Larkin , 9 Bush , 164, it was held that , in order to have an equitable
issue tried by a jury , it is not necessary that the issue should be formed by the
pleadings . The issue may be granted on exceptions to a master's report , just
as was done in this case .
But in Horner vs. Harris ' ex'r, 10 Bush , 360 , the court said : "The chancellor may
and frequently does submit questions of fact to a jury, but the issues are always .
made up from the pleadings in the action . We are aware of no state of case in
which the chancellor can compel a petitioner to establish his cause of action, or
any fact essential thereto , to the satisfaction of a jury , until he has first required
the defendant to answer , and has thereby ascertained whether or not the exist-
ence of the fact is denied ."
""
d. In an equitable action , in which the only issue was as to the defendant's indebt-
edness to the plaintiff , an order , that " the issue raised by the pleadings was
to be tried by a jury , was held to be sufficiently explicit . Savings Bank of Cin-
cinnati vs. Benham , 2 Met ., 243. But see 1 Met ., 570 ; 2 Id. , 287.
e. In an equitable action , the court may determine the value of property wrong-
fully converted by the defendant , without a jury . Greer vs. Powell, 1 Bush , 489 .
f. It does not seem to the writer of these notes that the Code has changed the
former practice , either as to the mode of proceeding , or the effect of the verdict
of the jury , upon the trial of an equitable issue . As to the old practice , see

Griffith vs. Depew , 3 A. K. M. , 183 ; Moore vs. Simpson , 5 Litt., 51 ; Reading vs.
Ford's heirs , 1 Bibb , 340 ; Owens vs. Owens , Hardin , 460 ; Goodloe vs. McLanathan,
6 Mon., 310.
GROUNDS FOR CONTINUANCE .
a . The absence of a witness , residing out of the county , furnishes no ground for a
continuance , unless his traveling expenses have been paid or tendered , or the
right thereto waived . Thurman vs. Virgin and wife , 18 B. M. , 790.
2317 , subs . 3 . BURTHEN OF PROOF .
a. The petition alleged that the plaintiffs were the owners of the slave in contest ,
and that she had been unlawfully taken from their possession by the defendant .
The answer admitted that the defendant had taken the slave from the plaintiffs ,
but denied that they were the owners of her , and alleged that she belonged to
his wife held , that the burthen of proof was on the defendant , and that he had
the right to conclude the argument to the jury . Vance vs. Vance , 2 Met ., 581.
b. Further , as to burthen of proof , see note 5 to 2 95 , page 32 .
-Subs . 5. INSTRUCTIONS TO JURY .
c. The failure of the court to reduce its instructions to writing , when required to
do so by a party , is ground for reversal . But , the court having given oral instruc.
tions, told the jury , that they could retire , and that the instructions would be
reduced to writing , and that if they had any difficulty , they could send for them ;
and the plaintiff's counsel said he hoped that the court would do so : held , that
this was not a requirement of written instructions . Ferguson vs. Fox's adm'r, I
Met. , 83 .
d. The court should decide all questions of law, by instructions , and submit only
controverted facts to the jury. Maltus vs. Shields, 2 Met ., 553.`
Consequently, the court has the right to instruct the jury that they must regard as
true , for the purposes of the action , a fact which is not controverted by the plead-
ings . Hartly vs. Hartly , 3 Met . , 56 .
Consequently , also , an instruction which submits a question of law to the jury—
for instance , an instruction that every material allegation of a pleading , which
is not controverted by an adverse pleading , must be taken as true - is erroneous .
Tipton vs. Triplett, 1 Met ., 570 ; Alard vs. Smith , 2 Met ., 287. See , however , 2
Met. , 243.
Subs . 6.
321.
OPENING AND CONCLUSION OF ARGUMENT .
f. Upon a plea of probable cause , in an action for a malicious prosecution , the
defendant has the conclusion . Brown vs. Morris , 3 Bush , 81.
g. See notes a and b to 317.
EXAMINATION OF WITNESS BY JURY , AFTER RETIRING .
a . A jury may be allowed , after retiring , to return into court, and ask a witness
what he had stated ; and cross -examination of the witness , thereupon , would
be improper . Thompson vs. Poston , 1 Duv . , 389 .
b. "In some rare cases , after retirement , a jury might be permitted to re -examine a
witness respecting a fact not testified to in the first examination . But then ,
cross -examination would always be a clear and equal right , and this inconven-
ience , and the danger of fraud and surprise resulting from such irregularity of
post -examination on a new question , require that , before it should be allowed ,
the sound discretion of the court should be addressed by a peculiar reason indi-
cating that, without it , probable injustice would be unreasonably done ." Thomp
son vs. Poston , 1 Duv ., 391.

CIVIL CODE- NOTES TO SEC . 326. 65
326. 1. General verdicts.
2. Separate-general verdicts.
3. Special verdicts.
1. GENERAL VERDICTS .
a . In an action upon a note , a verdict finding " for the plaintiff the debt in the
petition mentioned " authorizes the clerk to enter judgment for the debt with
interest . Brannan & Smith vs. Foree's adm'rs , 12 B. M. , 506 .
b. In an action on a note , the defendant pleaded usury , and the verdict was : " We
of the jury find for the plaintiff . " Held, that judgment was properly rendered
in favor of the plaintiff for the amount of the note , with interest . Cooper vs.
Poston , Duv ., 94 .
c. Petition alleged that the defendant broke the plaintiff's close , and assaulted and
beat the plaintiff . The defendant denied the allegations . The evidence proved
the assault upon the plaintiff , but did not conduce to prove the breaking of the
close held , that a verdict finding the defendant guilty " as charged in the peti-
tion " was sufficient . Noel vs. Hudson , 13 B. M. , 204 .
d. In an action for the value of work and labor , the jury have a right , in the
absence of all proof of the value of such labor, and from their own knowledge
of the ordinary transactions and business of society , and especially from their
presumed knowledge of the value of labor , to find a verdict for the price of the
work done , and for which the action was brought . Baum vs. Winston , 3 Met .,
129.
e. If the law and facts be submitted to the court , by consent , the court has all the
rights and powers of a jury in determining facts , and assessing damages (Baum
vs. Winston , 3 Met ., 129) ; and its finding has the same effect as the verdict of a
jury would have had . Mulholland & Bros. vs. Samuels , 8 Bush , 66.
2. SEPARATE -GENERAL VERDICTS .
f. The provisions of this Code , concerning separate general verdicts , are contained
in subsection 5 of 317, and in sections 326 and 327. Those provisions are , so
far as the writer knows , without precedent . He believes that their object is to
enable parties , and the court, in a case involving several issues , to know upon
what grounds the verdict is based , without requiring the details of a special ver-
dict . For example , suppose the issues to be : 1. Whether or not the defendant
was an infant when he purchased goods from the plaintiff ; 2. Whether or not,
if he was then an infant , he ratified the contract after he became of age ; and
3. Whether or not , though he was an infant when he purchased the goods , they
were necessaries , suitable to his estate and condition . A general verdict for the
plaintiff would leave it uncertain , whether the jury went upon the ground that
the defendant was of age when he purchased the goods , or upon the ground that
he ratified the contract after becoming of age , or upon the ground that the goods
were necessaries ; and , consequently , the court could not grant a new trial , if
there were evidence conducing to sustain the plaintiff's allegations as to any one
of the issues ; though there may have been a preponderance of evidence against
him upon that issue , and though he may have failed to sustain his allegations
upon the other issues . For example , the plaintiff may have failed to sustain the
allegation that the defendant was of age , or the allegation that the goods were
necessaries ; but may have introduced evidence conducing to prove a ratification ,
with a preponderance of evidence against him upon that issue ; and the jury
might find a general verdict for the plaintiff , believing , or professing to each
other the belief, that the goods were necessaries , notwithstanding the expressed
opinion of the court to the contrary ; and the court could not set aside the ver-
: NOTES-

dict , because , so far as the court can know , it may have been based upon the
ground that the defendant had ratified the contract.
In the case supposed , the court must , on the motion of either party , direct the jury
to return , with their general verdict , separate verdicts upon the questions , I.
Whether or not the defendant was of age when he purchased the goods ; 2.
Whether or not, if he was an infant when he purchased the goods , he ratified
the contract after becoming of age ; 3. Whether or not the goods were necessa .
ries ; and , if the jury should find for the defendant upon the first and second
issues , and for the plaintiff upon the third issue , it would seem that the court
may set aside the finding upon the third issue ; and then , the general verdict
being inconsistent with the findings upon the other issues , may render judgment
for the defendant , notwithstanding the verdict .
3. SPECIAL VERDICT .
g. It was held, under the Code of 1854, that the court was not bound , on a motion
of a party, to direct the finding of a special verdict . L. , C. & L. R. R. Com-
pany vs. Case's adm'r , 9 Bush , 734 But see now ? 327, subsection 2.
h . If legal as well as equitable issues be submitted, in an ordinary action , the
legal issues should be tried by a jury ; and the court may submit matters of
fact , arising upon the equitable issues , to the jury , if such issues have not
been transferred to the equitable docket ; and the finding of the jury upon them
should be special , to enable the court to decide the case . Petty vs. Malier , 15
B. M. , 604 .
ASSESSMENT OF VALUE OR DAMAGE BY JURY .
a. To authorize a jury to assess damages for property illegally taken and detained ,
there must be some evidence of the value ; or some description to enable the
jury to determine the value from their own knowledge . Pharis , etc., vs. Carver ,
13 B. Mon. , 238.
b. A defendant from whom the plaintiff had taken a horse , avowed title in himself,
and prayed to be dismissed , with a judgment for costs : held, that , having proved
title in himself, he was entitled to a return of the horse , or to its value , and to
damages for the detention . Bates vs. Buchanan , 2 Bush , 117.
c. See 388, and notes to 184 and 331.
a. See 312, subsection 2.
TRIAL BY COURT .
b. It was held , under the Code of 1854, that assent to the waiver of a trial by jury
should appear in the entry submitting the facts to the court ; or may be inferred
from an entry showing that the court had heard testimony , and pronounced
judgment thereon ; but cannot be inferred from a judgment by default . Gos-
som vs. Donaldson , 18 B. M. , 230 .
c. It was held , under the Code of 1854, that, even in an equitable action for dam-
ages , they must be assessed by a jury , unless a jury be waived (Gossom vs. Don.
aldson , 18 B. M. , 229) ; except upon the defendant's failure to answer , in an
action upon a contract . Daniel vs. Judy, 14 B. M. , 393 ; Lambert vs. Ingram's
adm'r, 15 B. M. , 270 ; Wilson vs. Barnes , 13 B. M. , 331 .
Burgen vs. Jacobs ,
d. It was held that the filing of an insufficient answer , and the defendant's failure
to plead further , was not a waiver of his right to a jury -trial .
14 B. M. , 521.
e. It was held that a transfer of the case , and not merely of an equitable issue , to
the chancery docket , by consent , was a waiver of a jury -trial . Smith vs. Moberly
and wife, 15 B. M. , 70 .
f. See notes to ? 126.

It also explains the role of a bill of exceptions and the importance of timely objections.

68 CIVIL CODE- NOTES TO SECS . 334 , 335 .
335 .
and wife , 4 Bush , 409) ; though an order'of court may have been made allowing
it (Allard vs. Smith , 2 Met ., 298) ; unless the order was made by consent of par-
ties entered of record . Kelsoe vs. Ellis , 10 B. M. , 38.
3. TIME OF FILING BILL OF EXCEPTIONS .
<. Myers ' Code , page 481 , note , cites the case of Singleton's adm'rs vs. Kennedy , 9
B. M., 222 , and a MS. opinion , in support of the proposition that if an order
allow exceptions to be filed on or before a particular day of the term , the filing
of them at a subsequent day of that term is sufficient , unless excepted to at the
time . Contra , Freeman vs. Brenham , 17 B. M. , 603 ; Tweedy vs. Commonwealth , 2
Met. , 378 ; Bailey vs. Villier , 6 Bush , 27 ; Meadows vs. Campbell , 1 Bush , 104. In
the latter case it was held that , if time be given until the first day of the next
term to file a bill of exceptions , it must be filed on that day , or time must then
be given to file it on a subsequent day ; and that an order made on a subsequent
day, allowing the exceptions to be filed nunc pro tunc , was unavailing .
If time be given to prepare and file exceptions , the record must show the order .
In the absence of such an order , made at the trial -term , exceptions cannot be
filed at the next term nunc pro tune . Vandever vs. Griffiths , 2 Met ., 425 .
A bill of exceptions cannot be filed nunc pro tune, upon the judge's statement that
he had made an order allowing it to be filed . Lynch vs. Reynolds , 6 Rush , 547 .
Time was given until the third day of the next term to file a bill of exceptions ;
on that day further time was given ; without designating a day : held , that the
latter order was void , and that the bill of exceptions filed was no part of the
record . Smith vs. Blakeman , 8 Bush , 479 .
A motion for a new trial , made at the March term , 1873, was continued until the
September term , when it was overruled , and time given until the fifth day of the
March term , 1874, to file a bill of exceptions . On the second day after the
making of the order overruling the motion for a new trial , that order was set
aside , and the cause was continued to the March term , 1874, without taking any
notice of that part of the former order which gave time until the third day of
the March term , 1874, to file a bill of exceptions . At said March term , the
motion for a new trial was again overruled , and on the sixth day of that term a
bill of exceptions was filed : held to be in time. Harper vs. Harper, 10 Bush , 451 .
The Jefferson court of common pleas has control over its judgments during sixty
days . Can the court extend the time for filing exceptions beyond sixty days ?
Bailey vs. Villier , 6 Bush, 27. At any rate , if time be extended beyond sixty
days , the exceptions must be filed on the day fixed , and not afterward . Id.
But if time be extended by that court beyond sixty days , and the bill of excep
tions be filed within the time allowed, without objection , it will be regarded as
properly in the record . Downing vs. Bacon , 7 Bush, 684 .
The right of a party to object to a bill of exceptions , because it is not tendered in
proper time , is not waived by reason of assistance given by his counsel out of
court in the preparation of said bill . Smith vs. Blakeman , 8 Bush, 480 .
WHAT THE BILL OF EXCEPTIONS MUST SHOW .
1. As to evidence.
2. As to instructions .
1. AS TO EVIDENCE .
a . According to the general practice , under the Code of 1854, a party relying , for a
reversal , only upon errors as to instructions , stated all of the evidence in the bill
of exceptions ; and this was necessary , according to a dictum in Vandever vs.
Griffith , 2 Met. , 425 .
This is forbidden by subsection 1 of 2 335, which requires parties , in such cases , to
state merely "the material facts which the evidence conduced to prove ."

entries.ocr_text:
CIVIL CODE - NOTES TO SEC . 335 . 69
b. If, however, the judge refuse to certify that the evidence conduced to prove
facts , as stated in the appellant's bill of exceptions ; or , if the appellant wish to
appeal upon the ground that the verdict is not sustained by the evidence , the
bill of exceptions must state the evidence " in full " ( 335, subs . 2) ; and , of
course , the bill must show , in some manner , that it states the evidence " in full ."
It is usual , and best , to close the statement of the bill , concerning evidence , with
the words , " and this was all of the evidence ." But this is not necessary , if the
bill otherwise show that it states all of the evidence . In the case of Bracken
County Court vs. Robertson County Court , 6 Bush, 74, the court said : " Although
the bill of exceptions is inartificial , yet it does show the plaintiff's evidence , the
agreed facts , and defendant's evidence , and then concludes , ' the court, having
heard the evidence , adjudged ;' from all of which it is most apparent that it con-
tains all the evidence ."
c. In the case of Walrath vs. Viley , 1 Bush, 268 (which was an ordinary action ) , it
was held that , though rejected depositions, which the defendant had offered to read ,
were copied in the record , and were noted as such by the clerk ; and though the
bill of exceptions showed that the defendant had excepted to the decision of the
court , rejecting them , the Court of Appeals would not consider them ; because
neither they, nor the exceptions to them , were incorporated in the bill of excep-
tions , or so referred to as to be made part thereof.
d. The testimony of a witness having been erroneously excluded , upon the ground
that it was incompetent upon account of his interest , as shown by his statements
upon voir dire , which were contained in the bill of exceptions , the judgment was
reversed , though the bill of exceptions did not contain all of the evidence .
Easley vs. Easley , 18 B. M. , 94.
e. It was held , under the Code of 1854, that exhibits copied in the record , but not
made part of the bill of exceptions , could not be noticed by the Court of
Appeals . Vaughn vs. Mills , 18 B. M. , 634 ; Haney vs. Tempest , 3 Met., 95. Con-
tra, as to exhibits in equitable actions . Harman vs. Wilson , I Duv . , 327. See
128 and notes thereto .
ƒ. A paper copied in the record , and certified by the clerk as an amendment offered ,
without any reference to it in an order rejecting it ; or any other order recogniz-
ing it as the paper offered to be filed ; and which is not embodied in a bill of
exceptions , making it part of the record , cannot be noticed by the Court of
Appeals . Hortsman vs. Lex . & Cov . R. R. , 18 B. M. , 223.
g. In the case of Young, McDowell & Co. vs. Bennett , & c. , 7 Bush, 476 , the clerk
having copied in the transcript a paper which he designated an amended peti-
tion , the court said : "It is true , the clerk has copied a paper which he desig-
nates as such amendment ; but it is not identified by a bill of exceptions , nor by
any order of the court. The record shows that the amended petition was not
allowed to be filed : it remained , therefore , merely a private paper in the hands
of appellants."
h . Although the reason for excluding the evidence of a witness , by the court below,
be insufficient ; yet , if there be no statement in the bill of exceptions of what
the witness would have proved , or what the party offering him believed he would
prove , the Court of Appeals cannot interpose . Bowler vs. Lane , 3 Met ., 311.
i. Exceptions to depositions , for incompetency , which do not appear to have been
acted on by the court below, are regarded by the Court of Appeals as waived .
Corn vs. Sims , 3 Met . , 397 ; Lewis vs. Wright , 3 Bush, 311 .
2. AS TO INSTRUCTIONS .
j. Subsection 2 of 337 declares that, if a party except to a decision of the court,
in granting or refusing an instruction , all of the instructions given or refused shall

entries.ocr_text:
CIVIL CODE- NOTES TO SEC . 340 . 71
f. It is such misconduct for one juryman to state to the other jurors his knowledge
of the subject -matter of the case to be decided , as will authorize a new trial ; but
this cannot be established by the affidavit of a part of the jury . 3 Monroe , 415 ,
and cases cited .
Subs . 3. Accident or surprise .
g. "It does not appear that a party was prevented from defending the action by
any unavoidable casualty or misfortune , although one of his counsel was pre-
vented from attending the court by sickness , when there was a manifest lack of
diligence on the part of such party in not attending the court or providing other
counsel ." Landrum vs. Farmer, 7 Bush, 47 .
Subs . 4. Excessive damages.
h. Upon a motion for a new trial , on account of excessive damages and erroneous
instrctions , if the plaintiff seek to defeat the motion by a remittitur , it should be
such as will show clearly that the damages retained are sustained by the proof .
Masterson vs. Hagan, 17 B. M. , 332.
i. Upon a verdict for $ 4,000 , in an action for malicious prosecution , the judge
announced that he would grant a new trial unless the plaintiff would accept
a verdict for $ 1,000 , which the plaintiff did under protest . The defendant
appealed , and the plaintiff took a cross -appeal : held, that “ the judgment is cer-
tainly anomalous . Courts have often granted new trials on equitable conditions
applied to the applicant ; but we know of no precedent for overruling a motion
for a new trial on terms required of the party opposing it . In this instance the
court itself virtually assessed the damages , and thereby deprived each party of
his right to have an assessment by a jury . This seems to be error to the preju
dice of the defendant , if he was entitled to a new trial , and to the plaintiff's
prejudice , if the defendant was not entitled to a new trial ." Brown vs. Morris,
3 Bush , 81.
j. In an action for damage caused by taking of plaintiff's slave to Ohio , whereby
he escaped , the court said that the value of the slave was the best criterion of
damage ; and that , " although we might not concur with the jury as to the cor-
rectness of their estimate , we do not feel authorized to disturb the verdict for
that reason . McClain vs. Esham , 17 B. M. , 157.
The court refused to set aside a verdict for $4,000 , in an action for slander ; and
said that, in such cases , a new trial should not be granted , " unless the damages
be so great as to strike the mind at first blush as having been superinduced by
Letton , &c., vs. Young, & c., 2 Met . , 560 .passion or prejudice ."
In L. & N. R. R. Co. vs. Fox , 11 Bush , 495 , a verdict and judgment for the
plaintiff for $ 25,000 , damages for a personal injury , were set aside by the Court
of Appeals , upon the ground that the damages were excessive . See that case
for a discussion of the question .
Subs . 6. That the verdict or decision is not sustained by the evidence, or is contrary to law.
k. A verdict and judgment for a plaintiff who has failed to state a cause of action
(Fible vs. Caplinger , 13 B. M. , 466) ; or who is shown by answer and evidence
not to have a right to sue in her own name alone (Petty vs. Malier, 14 B. M. ,
246) , must be set aside .
Subs . 7. Newly discovered evidence.
1. An affidavit of the plaintiff's attorney that he was not aware of the existence of
a city -ordinance until after the trial was held to be insufficient to authorize a new
trial . Babbitt vs. Woolley , & c., 3 Bush, 703 .
m. Cumulative parol evidence is not ground for a new trial . Mason vs. Mason , 5
Bush, 187.

entries.ocr_text:
72 CIVIL CODE- NOTES TO SECS . 340, 342 , 343 , 344 , 359.
n. A defense , of which the defendants must have had knowledge , if true , and
which was not relied upon on the trial , and the alleged discovery of evidence to-
sustain such defense , do not authorize a new trial . Mason vs. Mason , 5 Bush,
187.
o. To entitle a party to a new trial , " the discovered evidence must be of such per-
manent and unerring character as to preponderate greatly, or have a decisive
influence upon the evidence to be overturned by it ." Allen vs. Perry, 6 Bush , 91.
p. "It is a general rule that a new trial should not be granted upon the sole ground
of the discovery , after verdict , of parol testimony concerning a point litigated , or
a fact known to a party ; because the converse of this rule would open a wide field
for unfairness and subornation , and would tend to protract litigation , and render it not
only uncertain but almost interminable ." Leonhart vs. Stalyenberger and wife , 7 Bush,
210 .
Subs . 8. Error of law, exceptedto.
8342 .
q . Upon the question , when an exception is necessary, see note 1 to 2 333.
TIME OF APPLYING FOR NEW TRIAL .
a . The three days allowed by 342 , for an application for a new trial , are three-
juridical days , including the day on which the judgment was rendered . Long vs.
Hughes , Duvall , 387 ; White vs. Crutcher , Bush, 472 .
343. GROUNDS FOR NEW TRIAL MUST BE STATED IN WRITING , & C .
a. It was held , under the Code of 1854, that the Court of Appeals would not notice
an alleged error, which the appellant had failed to specify in his grounds for a
new trial , although the record showed that he had excepted thereto , during the
trial . Hopkins vs. Commonwealth , 3 Bush , 480 ; Slater vs. Sherman , 5 Busk , 211 ;
L., C. & L. R. R. Co. vs. Mahony's adm'r , 7 Bush, 238 .
This Code has not changed the law in that respect ; but it has imposed on the
appellant an additional duty , viz : to specify the grounds upon which he relies for :
a reversal , by an assignment of errors . Sections 737, 739.
8344. GROUNDS FOR NEW TRIAL DISCOVERED AFTER TRIAL -TERM..
a. After the term when a judgment is rendered , it cannot be vacated , except in-
the mode prescribed by sections 344 [373] , 519 [ 580], or 520 [581] , nor for any
cause except one of those mentioned in section 340 [369] or 518 [ 579] . Hocker
vs. Gentry , 3 Met ., 469 .
8 359 . JUDGMENT BY DEFAULT .
a. If the defendant admit the plaintiff's demand , or present an immaterial issue-
concerning it, but plead a counter -claim or set -off for an equal amount , the
plaintiff can have no judgment until the plea is disposed of. Moore vs. Caruth-
ers, & c. , 17 B. M. , 681 ; Geoghegan vs. Ditto , 2 Met . , 438 .
b. There is no provision of the Code , or rule of practice , which authorizes the
court, before an action is ready for final trial , to hear and try it , and render
judgment in favor of one of the parties (whose right to recover anything on the
final hearing is not admitted ) , on the mere probability that he will be then
entitled to the sum adjudged in his favor . Mattingly vs. Bosley , 2 Met., 443 .
c. In an action against two of three joint and several obligors , one of the defendants
pleaded payment by the obligor who was not sued ; and , without disposing of
that issue , judgment was rendered against the other defendant by default . Held
that the judgment was erroneous . Rouse vs. Howard, 1 Duv ., 31 .

entries.ocr_text:
CIVIL CODE- NOTES TO SECS . 363 , 364 TO 366 , 368 , 369 , 370. 73
363. TIME OF TRIAL OF ORDINARY ACTIONS WHEN PART ONLY OFTHE DEFENDANTS ARE SUMMONED .
a. In actions ex delicto against several persons , some of whom have , and some of
whom have not , been summoned , it is erroneous to render judgment against the
former , without discontinuing the case as to the latter on the first day of the
term . Hedger vs. Downs , 2 Met ., 160 ; Buckles vs. Lambert , 4 Id ., 330 ; overruling
Waller vs. Martin , 17 B. M. , 188.
But a different rule prevails if all of the defendants have been summoned . See
2373 , and notes .
364 to 366.
368 .
TIME OF TRIAL OF EQUITABLE ACTIONS .
a. An equitable action does not stand for trial at the term at which an answer is
filed , unless the plaintiff consents that its statements may be taken as true. /
Gruel vs. Smalley , 1 Duv ., 358 .
b. Infant plaintiffs in an action in equity have no right to a trial at the same term
at which their reply, by guardian ad litem , is filed to the defendant's counter-
claim . Smith vs. Ferguson , 3 Met . , 424 .
c. Upon a change of venue , if the papers are filed ten days before the term of the
court , to which the case is removed , it stands for trial at that term , if it would
have stood for trial at the next term of the court from which it was moved .
Dale vs. Hays , 14 B. M. , 316 ; Gen. St. , chap . 12, art . 1, 7.
d. As to time of trial in actions against defendants constructively summoned , see
Harris vs. Adams , 2 Duv . , 141.
JUDGMENTS .
a . A decree is a judgment within the meaning of the Code. Hughes vs. Shreve, 3
Met., 547.
b. "It is clearly no part of the judgment of the court to state whether the process
has been executed or not ;" and a statement in the judgment that process has
been served does not prove that fact. Robinson vs. Mobley , 1 Bush , 196 ; Long vs.
Montgomery , 6 Bush , 394.
c. As to what orders are final , see notes to ? 514.
369, 370 . JUDGMENTS IN ACTIONS BY OR AGAINST SEVERAL PARTIES.
a . A judgment against one defendant , though replevied , does not bar proceedings
against another. Burrus vs. Anderson , 3 Met ., 500.
b. A judgment may properly be rendered against the surety before the principal is
summoned . Patton vs. Shanklin , 14 B. M. , 17.
c. As to judgments in actions ex delicto against several defendants , only part of whom
have been summoned , see note a to 363 .
d. In an action against several defendants , one of them being a nominal and unnec-
essary party, between whom and the plaintiff there is no litigation , a judgment
against "the defendants " without discrimination , although erroneous , is a clerical
misprision and not correctable by the Court of Appeals [no motion to correct it
having been made in the court below]. Cin . , Ham., and Dayton Railroad Co.
vs. Spratt , 2 Duv . , 4.
e. If some of the defendants have been , and others have not been , summoned , a
judgment against the " defendants " applies only to those summoned . Clark vs.
Finnell , 16 B. M. , 334 ; and see Kountz vs. Brown , &c. , Id. , 585.
f. In an action against two defendants , on their joint note , it was held that a
judgment by confession against " the defendant " was a judgment against both .
McCouns vs. Holmes and Chinn , 4 Litt ., 389 .

entries.ocr_text:
74 CIVIL CODE - NOTES TO SECS . 370, 371 , 373 , 374.
But in Blight's heirs vs. Banks , & c. , 6 Mon. , 225 , a decree by consent was held to
be a decree only against the defendants who had been summoned or appeared .
? 371 , subs . 2.
8373-
8 374.
DISMISSAL OF ACTION FOR WANT OF PARTIES .
a. See discussion in Cov . and Lex . R. R. Co. vs. Bowler's heirs , 9 Bush , 498 , as to dis-
missal of action for want of proper parties , or for failure to have guardian ad
litem appointed for infant defendants .
b. As to separate actions , and separate judgments against several wrong -doers , see
United Society of Shakers vs. Underwood , 11 Bush, 265 ; and Buckles vs. Lambert ,
4 Met., 330.
c. If a defect of parties be shown by the defendant's answer , the court should
require the necessary parties to be brought before it , on pain of dismissal with-
out prejudice . Carpenter vs. Miles , 17 B. M. , 602 .
JUDGMENTS IN ACTIONS AGAINST SEVERAL DEFENDANTS .
a . Section 373 [402] of the Code " authorizes several separate or joint judgments
against one or more of the defendants , as the case may require . Buckles vs.
Lambert , 4 Met., 333 ; and see 11 Bush , 265 .
b. If the jury find a joint verdict against several defendants , in an action for tort,
the court may set aside the verdict as to one , and render judgment for the
amount of it against the others . Shelton vs. Harlow , 15 B. M. , 547 ; Buckles vs.
Lambert , 4 Met . , 333.
c. A release of one joint trespasser does not bar an action against the others . Sellard
vs. Zomes, 5 Bush, 90 .
If the Code authorizes several actions against co-contractors , as the writer believes
it does (see ? 27, and note 6 thereto ) , there seems to be no good reason for hold-
ing that the release of one joint contractor bars an action against the others .
d. In an action against co -contractors , the plaintiff may dismiss as to one , and take
judgment against the other . Quisenberry vs. Artis, 1 Duv ., 30 .
e. Upon a plea of infancy , or other individual defense , by one of several defend-
ants , judgment may be rendered against the others , without disposing of that
issue ; but a judgment by default cannot be rendered against one defendant ,
pending an issue presented by another which extinguishes the plaintiff's demand ;
as, a plea of payment . Rouse vs. Howard, I Duv ., 31 .
SALE OF PROPERTY UNDER MORTGAGE OR LIEN .
a. It was held , under the Code of 1854-
1. That , in an action by the holder of several lien -notes , some of which were due ,
and some not due , the plaintiff's allegation that the sale of an entire tract of
land was necessary , to prevent loss to him , did not authorize a judgment for a
sale for money not due . Burton vs. McKinney , 6 Bush , 428 .
2. That , the plaintiff holding three lien -notes , it was erroneous to order a sale of
so much of the land as might be necessary to pay two of said notes , which were
due , subject to the plaintiff's lien for the third note , which was not due ; and
that the court should have ordered a sale of so much of the land as might be
necessary to pay the notes due , leaving the residue of it subject to the lien for
the note that was not due . Emison vs. Risque , 9 Bush, 24.
b. The plaintiff having obtained an attachment of land , an order for the sale of it ,
subject to the vendor's lien for purchase money - without making the vendor a
defendant , or ascertaining the sum due to him- was held to be erroneous . Mills
vs. Brown , 2 Met ., 405.

d. Concerning the sale of property to satisfy lien -debts due to a defendant , see ? 692 ,
and note g, page 40.
FORECLOSURE OF A MORTGAGE IS FORBIDDEN .
a . The Code of 1854 declared that , " in the foreclosure of a mortgage , a sale of
the mortgaged property shall , in all cases, be ordered ." 404 .
The foreclosure of a mortgage is an order which bars the mortgagor's right to
redeem , and is inconsistent with an order for the sale of the property to pay the
mortgage -debt . Hence , for the sake of consistency , this Code , whilst it author-
izes a sale of the property , as did the Code of 1854, forbids a foreclosure .
MORTGAGEE'S RIGHTS OF ACTION .

76 CIVIL CODE- NOTES TO SECS . 391 , 394 , TO 400, 402 , 405 TO 407 .
2391 .
1
SHOWING CAUSE AGAINST JUDGMENT BY INFANT .
a . 421 of the Code of 1854 authorized an infant to show cause in any case in
which , according to the former practice , a reservation of his right to do so would
have been proper. As to the former practice , see 4 Bibb , 11 ; 4 Mon. , 416 ; 10
Bush, 61.
b. An infant may show cause against a judgment before he becomes of age . New
land vs. Gentry , 18 B. M. , 671 ; Allen vs. Troutman's heirs , 10 Bush, 61.
c. But an infant cannot take advantage , at the same time , of his right to show
cause against the judgment , and of an appeal to the Court of Appeals ; and if
the infant appeal , an affirmance of the judgment is a good plea in bar as to any
matter which could have been heard on the appeal . Speak vs. Mattingly, 4 Bush,
310 .
d. The rights and interests of infants are under the special protection of courts of
equity , and they may , at any time within the period prescribed by section 421
of the Civil Code , apply to such courts to vacate any judgment or final order by
which injustice has been done them (Newland vs. Gentry , 18 B. M. , 670 ) ; and
the fact that the action to set aside a judgment against infants was not brought
within twelve months after one of the infants arrived at age does not bar the
rights of the others who sued within the prescribed time . Allen vs. Troutman's
heirs , 10 Bush, 61 .
394 to 400 .
#
402 .
CONVEYANCE BY COMMISSIONER .
a. The old practice required , as does the Code , an approval by the court of a deed
made by a commissioner . Dickerson's heirs vs. Talbot's ex'rs , 14 B. M. , 72.
b. An order confirming a sale is not equivalent to an order directing a conveyance
of the property . Gill vs. Hewitt, 7 Bush, 15.
c. It seems that if a purchaser die after an order of conveyance , but before it is
made , it should be made to him , and not to his devisee .
d. In an action to subject land , held by a wife , for a debt of her husband , judg
ment was rendered accordingly , and the land was sold , and purchased by the-
plaintiff , who assigned his bid to a person who was not a party to the action .
Upon an appeal , taken before conveyance to the assignee was approved , the
judgment ordering the sale was reversed : held, that the wife was entitled to
restitution of the land , subject to the purchaser's lien for purchase -money paid .
Miller , &c. , vs. Hall and wife , 1 Busk , 229 .
e. See notes to ? 694 .
EXECUTION UPON JUDGMENT AFTER PLAINTIFF'S DEATH .
a . The death of the plaintiff , though it does not " abate either the judgment or the
execution , " suspends proceedings until administration be granted , and until the
clerk be authorized to make the proper indorsements upon the execution ; and a
replevin -bond taken after the plaintiff's death , and before such indorsement , is
void ( Venable vs. Smith's ex'r , 1 Duv . , 195 ; Morgan vs. Winn's adm'r , 17 B. M.,
244 ) ; and no order of the court is necessary to enable the clerk to issue an execu-
tion on the judgment . Morgan vs. Winn's adm'r , 17 B. M. , 244 .
b. The lien of an execution which has been levied is not lost by the death of the
plaintiff , if a venditioni exponas be issued within reasonable time. Morgan vs..
Winn's adm'r, 17 B. M. , 233.
405 to 407. EFFECT OF DEFENDANT'S DEATH AFTER JUDGMENT .
a. The provisions of section 405 apply to lost records as well as other cases . Fleece
vs. Goodrum , & c., I Duv . , 306 .

CIVIL CODE- NOTES TO SECS . 405 TO 407 , 409 , 410. 77
b. If the defendant die after a levy upon his land , but before a sale of it, the title
passes to his heirs ; and a sale of it by the sheriff , without a revivor against the
heirs , is void . Huston vs. Duncan , 1 Bush, 205 ; Holeman's ex'r vs. Holeman's
heirs , 2 Bush, 514 ; Burge's adm'r vs. Brown , 5 Id. , 535 .
c. If the defendant become of unsound mind after a judgment against him , it may
be revived by an action against him and his committee . McNees and wife vs.
Thompson , & c., 5 Bush, 686 .
d. The provisions of 407 apply to replevin -bonds . Chinn's adm'r vs. Harrodsburg
Savings Institution , 8 Bush , 290.
e. The death of one of several defendants , against whom judgment has been ren-
dered , does not prevent the issuing of execution against the survivors . Fleece
vs. Goodrum , I Duv ., 306 .
f. As to the meaning and effect of 407 , see 11 Bush, 400 .
g. The provisions of the Code of 1854, concerning the revivor of judgments , were
held to apply to judgments rendered before the Code was enacted (Hughes vs.
Shreve, 3 Met ., 547) ; and those provisions do not authorize a scire facias. Id.
h. Observe that 2 405 has so amended ? 437 as to authorize the revivor of judgment
by motion instead of action.
2409. WHAT STATEMENTS MAY BE TAKEN AS TRUE AGAINST DEFEND-
ANTS CONSTRUCTIVELY SUMMONED .
a. In an action against a defendant who is constructively summoned , the plaintiff
need not prove the execution of the note sued on ; nor its transfer to the plaint-
iff, without indorsement -the possession of it being prima facie evidence of own-
ership (Gill vs. Johnson's adm'rs , 1 Met ., 649) ; nor the execution of a conveyance
alleged to have been made by the defendant . Barret vs. Coburn , 3 Met ., 516.
b. The affidavit mentioned in ? 409 may be supplied by the allegations of a verified
petition . Tipton vs. Wright , 7 Bush , 448 .
c. In Jackson vs. McElroy , 2 Bush , 132, it was held that the non -residence of the
defendant , whether stated in the petition , or in a separate affidavit , must be
proved . But see ? 58, subsection 6, and Garland vs. Denny & Colston , 3 B. M. ,
127.
d. In an action against a non -resident to enforce a lien on land for the purchase .
money , the plaintiff alleged that the first installment had been paid, and filed a
deed (which had never been delivered ) reciting such payment . In an amended
petition it was alleged that the acknowledgment of payment in the original peti-
tion was a mistake , and that no part of the first installment had been paid . Held,
that the allegations of the amended petition did not overcome the recital in the
deed and the admission made in the original petition . Jackson
vs.Speed
,
2Duv
.,
426
.
410. APPEARANCE OF AND BOND TO DEFENDANT CONSTRUCTIVELY
SUMMONED

a. The filing
ofan affidavit by
adefendant constructively summoned
, controverting
the ground upon which an attachment was issued
,

tothe action
(Duncan vs. Wickliffe

);
so
isthe filing
ofan answer by him
, though
he state therein that
it
ismade without entering his personal appearance
, which
he distinctly refuses
todo
(Tipton vs. Wright
,
7Bush

):
so
ishis prosecution
of an appeal
; and upon reversal
, and the return of the cause
tothe court below
no warning order
, nor appointment
ofan attorney
,
isnecessary
; but the defend-
ant may demur
oranswer
(Gill vs. Johnson's adm'rs
,
1Met

; Allen vs. Brown
,
4Met

); and
,
ifno bond had been given
, the attachment should be dis-
charged
. Anderson vs. Sutton

78 CIVIL CODE- NOTES TO SECS . 410 , 414 , 417 .
414 .
13417.
But an appeal in the name of the defendant , without his knowledge or consent , is
not an appearance . Parks' ex'r vs. Cooke, 3 Bush, 171.
b . Bond to the defendant must be taken and approved by the court before judgment .
It is error to authorize the plaintiff to receive money upon giving bond in the
clerk's office . Gill vs. Johnson's adm'rs , 1 Met ., 649 .
Such bond does not secure to a purchaser of the defendant's property a return
of purchase -money paid by him , if the sale should be set aside ; but the court
will require the plaintiff who received the money to return it ; and upon a re -sale ,
the purchaser is entitled to priority over all the parties . Salter vs. Dunn , 1 Bush ,
311 .
Such bond need not be given by a plaintiff before enjoining a judgment obtained
against him by a non -resident . Fellows & Co. vs. Day , 5 Bush, 666 .
c. For the provisions of subsection 1 of 440 , and of
sections 58, 59.
I 441 , of the Code of 1854, see
RE-TRIAL TO DEFENDANT CONSTRUCTIVELY SUMMONED .
a . It was held that 445 of the Code of 1854 applied to divorce -cases . Meyar vs.
Meyar, 3 Met ., 298. But this was changed by an act of 1869, which was re -en-
acted by 414.
b . It was held , under the Code of 1854, that the defendant might take an appeal ,
within three years , for errors apparent in the record (Payne vs. Witherspoon , 14 B.
M. , 370) ; although the court below had power to vacate the judgment , within
five years , for such errors (Jackson vs. Speed, 2 Duv ., 426) ; and that an appeal ,
and judgment thereon , barred his right to move for a re -trial , on account of errors
apparent in the record . Salter vs. Dunn , 1 Bush , 317 .
But 763 requires a motion before an appeal .
c. A motion to set aside a sale , if made within five years after an order confirming
it , is in time ; and with the motion should be tendered exceptions to the report
of sale . Williams vs. Taylor , 11 Bush , 375 .
d. An order stating that the defendants appeared by their attorney , Y , and that on
his motion it is " ordered that he be entered as security for costs for said defend-
ants ,"," is a sufficient compliance with the provision of 414 as to costs . Kinney
vs. O'Bannon's ex'x , 6 Bush, 692 .
e. In an action against a defendant constructively summoned , the court erroneously
rendered a judgment , under which his property was sold . Upon an appeal , pros-
ecuted by an attorney employed by the defendant's wife , the judgment was
reversed . On the day the mandate of the Court of Appeals was entered in the
court below , the sale was confirmed , and an appeal from the order of confirma-
tion was prosecuted by the wife in the name of the defendant , who was still
absent . That order was affirmed by the Court of Appeals ; but , a few days there-
tofore , the defendant had appeared in person in the court below , filed an affida-
vit , and obtained leave to file an answer within sixty days . At the next term of
the court below , but after the expiration of the sixty days , the defendant offered to
file an answer presenting a valid defense to the action , leave to file which was
refused : held, that the answer ought to have been allowed . Beazley vs. Maret,
& c., 1 Bush, 466 .
PURCHASER'S TITLE TO ABSENT DEFENDANT'S PROPERTY .
a. The plaintiff's attorney , purchasing the property of a defendant constructively
summoned , holds it in the same manner as the plaintiff would have done ; and
his conveyance of it, as a gift to his children , does not defeat the defendant's
right to restoration. Lusk vs. Salter , 1 Bush, 311.

CIVIL CODE — NOTES TO SECS . 418, 419 , 420 , 422 , 424 , 425 . 79>
8418 .
419 .
420 .
8422 .
8424.
€ 425 .
LIEN ON PROPERTY OF ABSENT DEFENDANT.
a. No lien can be created on property of a defendant constructively summoned ,
except by attachment or judgment . Bank of Louisville vs. Barrick , 1 Duv ., 51.
b. The Court of Appeals seems to have held that an attachment is necessary , in
order to give jurisdiction of an action against a defendant constructively sum- .
moned (Peters vs. Conway , 4 Bush , 571) ; for comments upon which see note ƒ to
96 , p. 39.
PERSONAL JUDGMENT AGAINST ABSENT DEFENDANT .
a . In Griswold vs. Peckenpaugh , I Bush , 220 , the court , overlooking this section ,
held that under the provisions of 56 [86] the plaintiff was entitled to a per- -
sonal judgment against the defendant upon whom a copy of the petition was
served out of this State . See 14 B. M. , 270 ; 1 Duv ., 170.
b. As to what is an appearance , see notes to ? 410.
VENUE OF ACTION FOR ALIMONY OR DIVORCE .
a. In Rhyms vs. Rhyms , 7 Bush, 316, it was held that , the husband having left this
State , his wife had a right to sue him for a divorce in the county of her resi
dence .
But can a husband proceed against his wife as a non -resident , and, upon construc-
tive service , obtain a divorce ? Meyar vs. Meyar, 3 Met ., 298 .
See 76 of this Code .
PROOF IN DIVORCE CASES .
a. " Two witnesses , or one and strong corroborating circumstances , shall be neces
sary to sustain the charge of adultery or lewdnesss ." R. S. , chap . 47 , art . 3, § 3 ;
G. S. , chap . 52, art. 3 , 3. Any other ground for a divorce may be proved by
one witness . Stibbins vs. Stibbins , 1 Met ., 476 .
MAINTENANCE OF WIFE , AND COSTS OF ACTION .
a . Though "the law , with due consideration for the frailty of human nature , does
not require the conduct of a wife to be entirely blameless , " she cannot recover
alimony if she be decidedly in fault (Lee vs. Lee, 1 Duv . , 196) ; though the hus-
band may also have been decidedly in fault . Griffin vs. Griffin , 8 B. M. , 120 ;
Finley vs. Finley , 9 Dana , 52.
b. As to the husband's liability for costs , and for the fee of the wife's attorney , see
Gen. St. , chap . 26, 28 ; re -enacting 32 of chap . 25 of the Revised Statutes ;
and see 18 B. M. , 518 ; 2 Met . , 412 ; 3 Id. , 298 ; 432 ; 1 Duv . , 287 ; 289 .
RESTORATION OF PROPERTY UPON DIVORCE .
a . See , upon this subject , ? 6 of article 3 of chapter 47 of the Revised Statutes ; ? 462
of the Code of 1854 ; Williams vs. Gooch, 3 Met ., 486 ; Phillips vs. Phillips , 9 Bush,
183 ; and 6 of article 3 of chapter 52 of the General Statutes ; and observe the
change which 425 of this Code makes , by declaring that any property obtained
by one married person from the other , during marriage , without valuable consid-
eration , shall be deemed to have been obtained by reason of the marriage .
b. Property conveyed by a husband to a trustec for his wife , in lieu of alimony, to
compromise a suit by her for divorce and alimony , cannot be regarded as having
been obtained by her in consideration , or by reason , of marriage ; and the hus-
band is not entitled to a restoration of the property , upon obtaining a divorce .
Flood vs. Flood, 5 Bush, 167.
Disagreements between husband and wife constitute a consideration [as to them]
for a conveyance made by him , in trust for her, for the sake of peace . Gaines
adm'x vs. Poor, 3 Met . , 503.

:80 CIVIL CODE- NOTES TO SECS . 425 , 428 ro 438.
€ 426 .
c. A judgment of divorce , in favor of a wife , entitles her to immediate possession
of her land, which had been previously conveyed by her husband , only . Hays
vs. Sanderson and wife , 7 Bush , 489 .
ANNULLING JUDGMENT OF DIVORCE .
a . It was held , under the Code of 1854, that a new trial might be granted , after the
term , in divorce -cases , as in other cases , though one of the parties may have
married in the meantime . Meyar vs. Meyar, 3 Met . , 298. But see now sections
344 and 414 .
428 to 438. SETTLEMENT OF DECEDENT'S ESTATES AND TRUST ESTATES .
1. Settlement of decedent's estates.
2. Settlement of trust -estates.
1. SETTLEMENT OF DECEDENT'S ESTATES .
a. Jurisdiction . The statute which regulates the administration of the estate of any
person who was an inhabitant of any other State or country , as well as that which
provides for the settlement and distribution of insolvent estates , by an action to
be brought for that purpose by the administrator , contemplates the existence of
personal estate , and its due and proper administration ; and has no application to
a case where the estate of the decedent does not pass into the hands of an admin-
istrator , and where no duties or liabilities can devolve upon him . Thumb vs.
Gresham , & c., 2 Met . , 306.
Section 429 authorizes the sale of land descended or devised to infants as well as
adults ; but , as to the former , according to the decision in Gill vs. Givin's adm'r,
4 Met., 197, a sale , under this proceeding, of more land than is necessary to pay
the debts of the decedent , is void . That decision conformed to numerous decis-
ions of the Court of Appeals , concerning the effect of chapter 86 of the R. S.
It was held , however , in Thornton vs. McGrath , I Duv . , 349 , that a failure to com-
ply with the provisions of said chapter 86 rendered sale of infant's land only
voidable . See upon this subject sections 489 to 498 of this Code , and especially
2493 , subsection 3.
A creditor may impeach a settlement , made before commissioners of the county
court , of the debtor's accounts as guardian ; and may recover compensation to
which he was entitled , but which was not claimed by him or his administrator .
Alsop's creditors, & c., vs Barbee and wife , 14 B. M. , 522.
b. Parties . It was said in Francis vs. Smith , 1 Duv ., 125 : " Where there are a num-
ber of creditors who are parties to , or beneficiaries under, a deed of trust for
payment of debts , the uniform practice is not to require all the creditors to be
brought before the court as parties , but to allow them to prove their claims before
the commissioners , and to distribute the proceeds of the trust estate according to
the rights of all concerned ." And such was the practice under the Code of 1854,
as to creditors of decedent's estates . Horner vs. Harris ' ex'r, 10 Bush , 359. But
428 of this Code , following an act of 1839 (Loughborough's Dig . , 240), declares
that the creditors of the decedent , so far as known to the plaintiff , must be made
parties 438 makes that provision applicable to trust-estates .
c. Order of reference to commissioner is a ministerial and not a judicial act . Smith
vs. Cochran , 7 Bush , 548.
d. Presentment of creditor's claim against the estate of principal debtor , though at
the request of the surety , does not prevent limitation from running in favor of
the latter . Harris vs. Moberly , 5 Bush, 556 .
The usual practice is for creditors to present their claims verified and proved , as
required by the statute . Upon exceptions to the master's report, the sufficiency

entries.ocr_text:
CIVIL CODE- NOTES TO SECS . 428 TO 438. 81
of the verification and proof can always be inquired into , and in such cases it
seems allowable to investigate and pass upon the merits of the claim . But a
creditor may file a petition presenting his claim ; and , if he do so, the representa-
tive of the decedent must state his defense by a verified answer or exception .
Horner vs. Harris ' ex'r , 10 Bush , 357.
e. An infant ward does not lose his claim against decedent's estate by failing to
appear and prove his claim before distribution between other creditors ; but may
compel them to refund pro rata : aliter , perhaps , as to persons not under disabil
ity. Roberts vs. Phillips , 11 Bush , 11.
f. Refunding bond must be given by legatees or distributees before even a judg
ment by default can be rendered in their favor against an executor or adminis-
trator . Montjoy's adm'r vs. Pearce , 4 Met., 97.
But a refunding bond cannot be required after an action against the personal
representative has been barred by limitation . Grigsby's ex'r vs. Wilkinson, 9
Bush, 92.
5. Affidavit and demand .
To an account for moneys paid by plaintiff as surety for defendant , was appended
an affidavit of one J that the affiant was teller of the Northern Bank of Ken-
tucky while the said payments and assumptions were made by the plaintiff , and
that he believed the same to be correct : held to be prima facie sufficient .
vs. Powell, Bush, 489 .
Greer
An affidavit of the claimant to verify a demand against the estate of a decedent ,
stating that the demand " was just and due him , and wholly unpaid , and there
was no just offset against the same known to him , and there was no usury con-
tained therein ," is insufficient ; it should , in addition , state that there is " no dis-
count against the demand ." Trabue's ex'r vs. Harris , 1 Met., 597.
Plaintiff's petition charged that the defendant's testator had , as agent , sold lands
for the benefit of the plaintiff , for which he never accounted , and the plaintiffs
have no knowledge of the fiducial transactions of the employé ; that the defend-
ant (his executor ) holds the only evidences thereof , and not only withholds them ,
but declines to make a settlement , and therefore praying for a discovery and set-
tlement . Held, that the affidavit and demand , before filing the petition in this case,
were not required by the statutes. " But after discovery , and before a coercive set-
tlement , the spirit of the amendatory enactment of February 28, 1865 (Myer's
Supplement , 224) , might require , for the executor's reciprocal security , an affidavit
of the amount paid by the testator , or of the non -payment of any thing ." Fox ,
&c., vs. Apperson's ex'r, 6 Bush, 653 .
No demand upon a decedent's personal representative is necessary before a suit
against his heirs (Johnson vs. Belt, 4 Bush , 405) ; nor upon a plea of set -off in an
action by an executor (Millet & Co. vs. Watkin's adm'r , 4 Bush , 642) ; nor is a
demand upon a decedent's executor necessary before a suit against his sureties .
Rogers vs. Mitchell's ex'r, 1 Met ., 22.
But affidavit and demand were held to be necessary in an action against the admin-
istrator of a defendant who died pendente lite ( Mathews vs. Jones' adm'r, 2 Met.,
254) ; and in an action to revive a judgment against a deceased defendant . Cur-
ry's adm'r vs. Brant's adm'r , 7 Bush, 301 .
It was held in several cases that in an action against a personal representative , the
petition need not allege that the plaintiff had made affidavit and demand , as
required by 437 [473 ] ; and that the defendant , in order to avail himself of the
plaintiff's failure to make affidavit and demand , must make an affidavit and take
a rule on the plaintiff before answer ( Thomas ' ex'r vs. Thomas , 15 B. M. , 178 ;

82 CIVIL CODE- NOTES TO SECS . 428 TO 438 , 439 TO 443 .
Rogers vs. Mitchell's ex'r, 1 Met ., 22) ; and that , after the defendant had taken
such a rule , the plaintiff could not cure the defect by making affidavit and
demand ; and that his action should be dismissed without prejudice . Rogers vs.
Mitchell's ex'r, 1 Met., 22. It was held , however, in Nutall's adm'r vs. Bran-
nin's ex'rs , 5 Bush, 11, that such rule might be obtained by the defendant after
answer .
If the plaintiff's claim be upon a note , his affidavit is sufficient ; if his claim be
upon an account , his affidavit must be accompanied by other proof , unless the
decedent's personal representative alone can prove the account , and refuses to
give an affidavit . Trabue's ex'r vs. Harris , 1 Met., 597.
2. SETTLEMENT OF TRUST -ESTATES .
a. Section 238 makes the provisions concerning the settlement of decedent's estates
applicable to suits the sale of property held in trust by virtue of the provisions
of article 2, chapter 14, of the General Statutes [act of 1856 concerning prefer-
ences by insolvent debtors ] .
It was held that a suit by one creditor , under said act , enures to the benefit of all
of the grantor's creditors (Roberts vs. Phillips , 11 Bush , 11) ; and that the bringing
of such suits in the State courts is not prohibited by the bankrupt law of the
United States . Linthicum vs. Fenley , 11 Lush , 131.
439 to 443 . ENFORCING SATISFACTION OF JUDGMENTS .
a. As to the court or county in which an action should be brought upon a return of " no
property found , ” see ? 70 and notes thereto , and 11 Bush , 32 ; 636 .
b. As to proceedings against garnishees , after a return of " no property found ," see
notes to sections 223 to 227 ; and Coburn vs. Currens & Owens , 1 Bush , 242, in
which it was held that a judgment against a garnishee is equivalent to a payment
to his creditor , and , if rendered before notice to the garnishee of an assignment
by his creditor of the claim on him , protects him against the assignee ; and Joyce
vs. O'Toole , 6 Bush , 31, in which it was held that the plaintiff is entitled to judg-
ment by default against garnishees , who are made defendants and summoned .
c. It was held that the judgment and return of " no property found " are prima
facie evidence that the debt is unpaid , and that that fact need not be alleged (Lewis
vs. Quinker, 2 Met ., 285) ; but if such an allegation were necessary , it might be
made by an amended petition ; and would , it seems , give a lien, by relation ,
upon property described in the petition , from the commencement of the action.
Id. Quere : see Jones vs. Lusk, 2 Met . , 359 ; Allen vs. Brown , 4 Met ., 342 .
d. Excepting actions against defendants who are constructively summoned (? 418) ,
a suit in rem , upon a return " no property found , " to subject property which .
is described in the petition creates a lien without an attachment (Parsons vs.
Meyburg , 1 Duv . , 206 ; Huffman vs. Thomas , 2 Duv ., 105) ; and that lien is supe-
rior to an inchoate lien acquired by a suit for a discovery previously brought by
another creditor . Ward's adm'rs vs. Robinson , & c. , 1 Bush , 294 .
e. In Weatherford vs. Myers , 2 Duv . , 91, it was held that the plaintiff cannot main-
tain an equitable action , upon a return of “ no property found , ” to subject land
the legal title to which was held by the defendant ; and in Davidson vs. Simmons ,
11 Bush , 333 ; and in Smith vs. Belmont and Nelson Iron Co. , 11 Bush , 392 , it was
held that the action authorized by ? 439 [474] is the only kind of action which
can be brought upon a judgment of a court of this State .
f. Sufficiency of officer's return .
A return by a deputy sheriff of " no property found , " without adding " in my
bailiwick ," was held to be sufficient . Evens & Love vs. Wait , 5 J. J. Mar. , 110 .
