A return of " no property found , " made two days after the test of the execution ,
was held to be sufficient . Dana vs. Banks , 6 J. J. Mar. , 219.
But an allegation that the plaintiff " had an execution issued and returned no
property found ' by the sheriff " is insufficient . The petition should have shown
that the execution was directed to the sheriff of the county in which the judg
ment was rendered , or in which the defendant resided or was summoned . Mad
dox vs. Fox , 8 Bush, 402 .
g. Property which plaintiff may subject upon return of “ no property found .”
See notes to 194 ; and Webb vs. McCauley , 4 Bush , 8, in which it was held that a
jailer's claim on county , for fees , & c., cannot be attached ; and see, as to the
interest of a beneficiary of a trust which may be attached , or otherwise subjected
by his creditor (Eastland vs. Jordon , 3 Bitb, 186 ; Costyrs . Ferguson , 3J. J. Mar.
264 ; Pope's ex'rs vs. Elliott , & c. , 8 B. M. , 56 ; Samuel & Johnson vs. Ellis , &l.,
12 B. M. , 479 ; Samuel vs. Salter , 3 Met . , 259) ; and , as to the right of the cred-
itors to subject property fraudulently purchased by a debtor in the name of his
wife and children , see Doyle vs. Sleeper, 1 Dana , 531, and Hanby vs. Logan , 1,
Duv . , 242.
444 to 449 . SUMMARY PROCEEDINGS .
a. The provisions of ? 444 [479] do not prevent a surety , who has paid a judgment ,
from having it transferred to him, pursuant to the R. S. , ch . 97, 28. Alexander
vs. Lewis , I Met ., 407. See G. S. , ch. 104, 29 .
b. Surety in bond given pursuant to 188 cannot be proceeded against by motion .
Gay vs. Morgan, 4 Bush , 606 .
c. The provisions of ? 446 [481], requiring a statement , in the notice of a motion ,
of the nature and grounds of the motion , do not apply to a motion made pursu
ant to 648 [ 716], upon a bond executed pursuant to 645 [713] . Smith vs. Wells'
adm'x , 4 Bush , 92 .
d. In a proceeding , by motion , against a sheriff and his sureties for the recovery of
money collected by him on execution , if the motion be not made in court , nor
entered on the motion docket , on the day specified in the notice , the motion shall
be considered as abandoned . Civil Code, 447 [482] . And a judgment subse
quently rendered is a nullity . Foster vs. Wade , 4 Met ., 252. Notice of a motion
against a sheriff was given for the fourth day of the next term . On the third day

84 CIVIL CODE- NOTES TO SECS . 451 , 452 To 469 .
Suit brought to settle a partnership . Before answer filed , agreement made to arbi-
trate , and suit dismissed . That no answer had been filed did not prevent the
defendant from relying , before the arbitrators , upon the same matters of defense
and counter -claim which he might have done had the action proceeded , in order
to a settlement of all the matters of controversy between the parties . Newton
vs. West , 3 Met ., 24.
Subs. 2. a . Qualifications of arbitrators . A certificate that they made oath according to law,
or made oath that " they would faithfully discharge the duty devolved on them
as arbitrators and umpire , " is sufficient . Snyder vs. Rouse , 1 Met . , 625 .
If there is no proof to the contrary , it will be presumed that the arbitrators were
sworn and gave notice , from their statements of those facts . Carson vs. Carson ,
I Met., 436 .
Subs. 4. a . Submission by fiduciaries . An executor may submit a controversy with the
same effect , and the same freedom from liability , without as with the interven-
tion of the court. Overly's ex'r vs. Overly's devisees, 1 Met . , 121.
Subs . 7. a . Rendering judgment on award , made within ten days before the term , is a cler-
ical misprision . See sections 516, 517. Carson vs. Carson , I Met ., 436 .
Subs . 8. Form and effect of award .
a. Merely formal objections to awards should be disregarded . The settlement of
controversies by arbitration is favored by law , and should be encouraged by
sustaining awards , notwithstanding they may be liable to technical and formal
objections which do not affect the substantial rights of the parties . Snyder vs.
Rouse, Met ., 625 .
b. A mere error of arbitrators , either as to law or facts , is no ground for setting
aside their award (Rudd, etc., vs. Jones, etc., 4 Dana , 230) ; hence it is inadmissi-
ble to permit evidence to show such error . Overly's ex'r us. Overly's devisees, I
Met. , 117.
c. The parties to an arbitration having been present and heard before the arbitra-
tors , it is no objection to the award that it does not state that the parties had
notice of the time and place the arbitrators met and proceeded to act . Newton
vs. West , 3 Met ., 24 .
d. That the umpire was chosen by the arbitrators before they proceeded to act under
the reference , forms no objection to the award . Newton vs. West, 3 Met., 24.
? 452 to 469 . FORCIBLE ENTRY AND DETAINER .
a. See notes to several of those sections , repeated below.
b . The provisions of the Code of 1854, concerning forcible entry and detainer , were
said to be substantially the same as those of the act of 1810 ( M. & B. , 725) .
Belcher vs. Barrett , 4 Met ., 307 .
That act " was framed to accomplish speedy and cheap country justice ," and should
be liberally and beneficially enforced . Swartswelder vs. U. S. Bank , 1J. J. M. ,
45 .
§ 452 , subs . 2. WHEN AND FOR WHAT THE WRIT OF FORCIBLE ENTRY
LIES .
a. The writ of forcible entry does not lie for a ferry, that being incorporeal prop-
erty, nor for the shores and banks which are used as necessary incidents to the
ferry . Rees vs. Lawless , Litt . Sel. C. , 184.
b. Residence upon part of a tract , claiming the whole , though part be not inclosed ,
may give possession of the whole tract within the meaning of the statute con-
cerning forcible entry (Henry vs. Clarke , 4 Bibb , 426 ; Smith vs. Morrow , 5 Litt.,
210) ; and deeds may be introduced to show the extent of the possession , but

classification.summary: This section of the Civil Code provides notes and case law interpretations related to Section 452, specifically concerning writs of forcible entry and detainer. It clarifies the conditions under which these writs can be maintained, including definitions of possession, the rights of executors, landlords, tenants, and disseizors. It also outlines when a tenant's continued possession after the expiration of a term constitutes a forcible detainer.

entries.ocr_text:
86 CIVIL CODE- NOTES TO SECS . 452 , 454 , 455 .
@454 .
$ 455 .
g. The writ may be maintained though the lease be void with regard to the land .
lord's obligations to let the tenant remain in possession . Haynes vs. Adams , 3
Mar. , 149.
h. An acquittal upon a charge of forcible detainer does not bar a writ for a subse-
quent forcible detainer . Shepherd vs. Thompson , 2 Bush , 176.
i. Though the lease purport to have been signed by the landlord and tenant , its
execution by the landlord need not be proved . Gray vs. Nesbet, 2 Mar. , 35 .
WARRANT FOR FORCIBLE ENTRY OR DETAINER .
a. In a warrant for forcible detainer , the relation of landlord and tenant must be
alleged , or in some form appear on its face . Powers vs. Sutherland , I Duv . , 151 ;
Taylor & Speed vs. Monohan , 8 Bush, 238.
b. Though a forcible entry and a forcible detainer are distinct offenses , they may be
charged in the same warrant ; and the jury may find the defendant guilty of
either (Swartzwelder vs. U. S. Bank , 1J. J. Mar. , 44 ; Cammack vs. Macy, 3 Mar. ,
296) ; and a charge of a forcible entry or detainer is sufficient to support a verdict
for either (Carpenter vs. Shepherd, 4 Bibb , 501) ; or for both of those offenses
(McBrayer et al. vs. Wash , 6 J. J. Mar. , 464) ; and , upon a warrant charging a
forcible entry , a verdict that the defendant is guilty of the forcible entry com-
plained of is sufficient , without finding him guilty of a detainer (Brumfield vs.
Reynolds , 4 Bibb , 388) ; but a verdict for a forcible detainer is not supported by a
charge of a forcible entry ( Gayle vs. Overton , 1 J. J. M. , 550) ; nor by a charge
that the defendant forcibly entered on the land , and that he detains it , without
any allegation applying the force to the detention . Lewis vs. Stith , 2 Litt., 294 .
c. Omission to state the name of a county in which the land lies cannot first be
taken advantage of by objection in the Court of Appeals . Rowe , & c., vs.
Powell's heirs , 4 J. J. Mar. , 153.
d. The writ need not state the boundaries of the land . A general description of it
is sufficient . Moore vs. Massey , 3 Litt., 296 .
e. Pendency of another writ cannot be pleaded in abatement , but the court may
direct an election . Boucher vs. Williamson , 1 Dana , 227.
f. A writ is not barred by a judgment dismissing a defective writ . Hanel vs. Law .
rence, 1 Mar. , 330 .
g. Parties . It was held under the act of 1810 that , though coparceners might and
should join in a warrant for forcible detainer , the warrant should not be abated
for their failure to do so (Jones vs. Overton , 4 Bibb , 334) ; that tenants in common
cannot join in a warrant for forcible detainer (Rogers vs. Turley , 4 Bibb , 355) ; and
that a joint warrant in favor of three persons , only two of whom had a cause of
action, could not be maintained . Thomas vs. Jones , 2 Mar. , 356 ; Haynes vs.
Adams , 3 Id., 149.
NOTICE OF WRIT .
a. A defendant , having appeared on the trial in the country , cannot , upon a tra-
verse , make any objection concerning notice of the writ . Philips vs. Harmon ,
Dana , 468 .
b. R sued out a warrant of forcible entry and detainer against M. C appeared and
consented to become a defendant , but the warrant was not amended so as to
include him . The inquest found M and C both guilty , whereupon C alone
entered a traverse . The circuit court, on motion of R , dismissed the traverse ,
and awarded restitution against C. Held that the proceedings were irregular .
The award against C was improper , as he was no party to the warrant , and the
inquest as to him was void . The proceedings should have been remanded to the
magistrate , that restitution might have been had against M. Crawford vs. Roch-
ester, 4 Bibb , 203 .

CIVIL CODE- NOTES TO SECS . 459 , 460 , 463 , 465 . 87
459 , 460 .
463.
465 .
INQUEST AND JUDGMENT .
a. The issue upon a writ of forcible entry or detainer relates merely to the right of
possession . Title papers showing that the prosecutor , or the defendant , has the
title , legal or equitable , or the right of entry , are irrelevant , unless for the pur-
pose of showing extent of possession . Mansfield vs. Dun , 2 Bibb , 582 ; Smith ,
&c., vs. Dedman , 4 Id . , 192 ; Beauchamp vs. Morris , 4 Id. , 312 ; Mattox vs. Helm ,
5 Litt., 185.
b. That one , against whom the prosecutor is not entitled to recover , is joined in the
writ , is no ground for non -suit . Alexander vs. Reed & Myers , 3 Mon. , 246.
c. Verdict of the jury , " the defendants not guilty , " is sufficiently explicit . Belcher
vs. Barrett, 4 Met., 307.
d. See further , as to inquest , notes to 454 .
TRAVERSE .
a. The absence of a judgment upon a verdict in the country , on a writ of forcible
entry and detainer , is not ground for dismissing a traverse . Belcher vs. Barrett,
4 Met., 307.
b. In Wayman vs. Taylor , 1 Dana , 527, it was held that a recital in a traverse -bond ,
that an obligor had traversed the inquisition , estopped him from denying that
fact ; and that , the bond having been duly executed , and the papers returned to
the circuit court , the proceedings may be presumed , nothing appearing to the
contrary, to have been regular ; and that , if no traverse be found in the papers ,
one may be filed nunc pro tunc , on motion of the traverser . But see, contra , Bur-
chett vs. Blackburn , 4 Bush , 553 .
c. The traverser need not sign the traverse . Jones vs. Skiles , 1 Mar. , 54.
d. A traverse -bond executed by a surety , though not by the traverser , is sufficient .
Smith vs. Turley , 3 Bibb , 188.
e. The substitution of a new traverse -bond , with other surety , does not release the
surety in the first bond . Ashby vs. Sharp , 1 Litt ., 156.
f. If, upon a traverse , the justice stay proceedings , and return a bond , it must be
presumed that the bond was executed before and approved by him , though he
omit so to certify . Smith vs. Dedman , 2 Bibb , 575 ; Stillwell vs. Harris , 1 Mar. ,
342 .
g. The requisition as to return of the proceedings to the circuit court, within ten
days , is merely directory to the justice ; and his failure to comply should not
prejudice the rights of the parties . Wayman vs. Taylor, 1 Dana , 527 .
h . If the original writ be returned to the circuit court and lost , the justice may
supply it by a sworn or certified copy . Logan vs. Smith , 2 Mar. , 53.
TRIAL AND JUDGMENT UPON TRAVERSE .
The circuit court may quash a defective writ at any stage of the trial . Powers
vs. Sutherland , 1 Duv . , 151.
b. Upon an appeal from a justice of the peace , the same action may be tried anew ,
but no new action can be tried . Burbage vs. Squires , 3 Met ., 79. The same rule
applies to a traverse . The proceedings cannot be amended by introducing new
plaintiffs . Powers vs. Sutherland , 1 Duv ., 151.
c. If the traverser fail to appear on the calling of the cause , judgment may be ren-
dered against him by default . Dibble vs. Porter, 1 Duv ., 190.
d. Upon the traverse , though by the defendant , the burthen of proof is on the
prosecutor ( Beauchamp vs. Morris , 4 Bibb , 312) ; and he shall have restitution
according to the proof, though it be for less than he demands , and less than the
jury in the country gave him . Atchley vs. Lathan , 3 Mar. , 164.

88 CIVIL CODE - NOTES TO SECS . 465 , 466 , 468 , 470 TO 472 , 474 TO 479 .
466 .
e. Upon a traverse , the defendant cannot be found guilty of a different offense from
that of which he was found guilty by the jury in the country (Cammack vs. Macy,
3 Mar. , 297 ; St. Clair vs. Sanders , 3 J. J. Mar. , 303) ; nor can the defendant be
found guilty of any offense , upon the traverse of an inquest which failed to find
him guilty of any offense . Thus , upon a warrant for a forcible detainer , the jury
in the country , instead of finding the defendant guilty of the detainer , found “ the
right of possession in the " prosecutor . A verdict , upon the traverse , that the
defendant was guilty of the forcible detainer complained of in the warrant was
held to be void . Todd vs. Bates , 3 Bibb , 100.
f. A traverse takes issue on the truth of the inquest , and is a virtual waiver of
objections to its form, to informalities of the judgment of the justice , and irreg-
ularities in the proceedings before him , not affecting the merits of the case . I
Mar. , 54 ; 2 Bibb , 434 ; 1 J. J. Mar. , 45 ; 3 Id ., 168 ; 1 Dana , 468 .
g. Judgment of restitution may be given against persons engaged in making the
unlawful entry , but who did not detain the premises afterward , as well as against
those who continued in the occupation of them . Young vs. Ringo , 1 Litt. , 225 .
COSTS AND RESTITUTION .
a. An order restraining restitution should not be made because the defendant has
recovered a judgment in ejectment against the prosecutor . Dedman vs. Smith , 2-
Mar. , 260.
468. LIMITATION TO WRIT OF FORCIBLE ENTRY OR DETAINER .
a. If the warrant be invalid , and the proceedings upon it be sustained only by con-
struing the defendant's appearing and traversing the finding of the jury , into a
waiver of irregularity , the limitation should be counted back from the appear-
ance . Humphrey vs. Jones , 3 Mon. , 263 .
470 to 472. SETTLEMENT OF ACCOUNTS OF FIDUCIARIES BY COUNTY
COURT .
a. A guardian may maintain an action in equity against a former guardian for settle-
ment of his accounts , notwithstanding a reference of them to a commissioner , by
the county court. Commonwealth for Hooper vs. Henshaw , 2 Bush, 286 .
b. The clerk having certified that a copy of a settlement was a true copy of settle-
ment made " with the Mercer county court, which is on file in this office ," the
copy was held to be competent evidence , though the settlement purported to
have been made with the presiding judge of said court . McAfee vs. Balden , 6
Bush, 537.
474 to 479 .
1. Mandamus .
WRITS OF MANDAMUS AND PROHIBITION .
a. Nature of the writ . " Mandamus is not now , as formerly , a high prerogative writ ,
emanating from the grace or favor of any one ; but it is a statutory writ , or order
of a court of competent jurisdiction , and , in a proper case , is to be granted on
the motion of any party aggrieved ." Maddox vs. Graham & Knox , 2 Met ., 70.
And it is a peremptory , and not an alternative , writ . Cumberland & Ohio R. R.
Co. vs. The Judge of the Washington County Court , 10 Bush, 564 .
b. When mandamus is the proper remedy.
A quarterly judge , having dismissed an appeal on the ground that he had no juris-
diction , it was held that mandamus did not lie against him , 1, because the dis--
missal was a judicial determination ; 2, because the plaintiff could appeal to the
circuit court . Goheen vs. Myers , 18 B. M. , 423 .

90 CIVIL CODE — NOTES TO SECS . 480 TO 488 , 489 TO 498 , 490 TO 500 .
480 to 488. REPEALING OR VACATING CHARTERS , AND PREVENTING
THE USURPATION OF AN OFFCE OR FRANCHISE .
a. A fine for usurpation of office cannot be recovered in an action , under the provis-
ions of these sections . Commonwealth vs. Adams , 3 Met . , 6.
b. The provisions of the Code authorizing an action against the usurper of an office ,
for the usurpation , do not preclude an inquiry into the legality of his title to the
office , in an action against him , for a wrong done under color of such title . Pat-
terson vs. Miller , 2 Met ., 403 .
489 to 498. SALES OF REAL PROPERTY OF PERSONS UNDER DISABILITY ,
&C .
490 .
1. See notes to several of those sections , repeated below .
2. Those sections were substituted for sections 539 to 545, inclusive , of the Code of
1854 ; and for articles 2 to 6, inclusive , of chapter 63 of the General Statutes-
those articles of the General Statutes having been substituted for chapter 86 of
the Revised Statutes . For decisions concerning chapter 86, see Myers' Codes,
page 527 to 530, and General Statutes, page 591.
3. As to sale of infant's land for debt , see note to ? 429 .
SALE OF JOINT PROPERTY .
a . For decisions concerning sections 542 and 543, for which 490 was substituted ,
see I Met . , 260 ; Id. , 281 ; 2 Duv . , 82 ; 2 Bush , 439 ; 6 Id. , 8 and 369 .
492. PROCEEDINGS FOR SALE OF LAND FOR PERSONS UNDER DISA-
BILITY .
8493 .
a. Observe , the Code does not require the appointment of commissioners to report
as to value , & c. ; but it requires an action to be brought against the person under
disability ( 489) , in which facts must be alleged and proved as in any other
action . (Subsection 4 of ? 492. ) As to defenses in such cases , see sections 36
and 38, concerning infants and persons of unsound mind ; and 34, and subsec-
tion 4 of 493 , as to married women .
BOND TO BE GIVEN , OR SALE VOID .
a. The Code does not attempt to render void sales of lands of persons under disa-
bility , except for failure to give bond for the application of the proceeds . The
Court of Appeals having ( Thornton vs. McGrath , 1 Duv ., 349) declared , contrary
to numerous previous decisions of that court , that the word void , as used in chap-
ter 86 of the Revised Statutes , meant voidable ; the framers of the Code found it
Withdifficult to use language which might not be overridden in like manner .
the hope of doing so , they resorted to tautology , and used the words " absolutely
void and of no effect ."
498. JOINDER OF GUARDIANS , & C . , IN ACTION FOR SALE OF LAND .
a. See form of petition , page 193.
""499 , subs . 13. COSTS OF DIVISION OF LAND IN COUNTY COURT .
a. This subsection was substituted for 555 of the Code of 1854, the word " costs
in which was held not to embrace an attorney's fee allowed to a guardian aa
litem . Williamson vs. Williamson, I Met., 303.
8 500. REVIVOR OF ACTIONS .
a . It was held that the word " survives ," in ? 557 of the Code of 1854, refers to a
technical survivorship , and not to a descent from one plaintiff to another. Smith
vs. Ferguson , 3 Met . , 424 .

resentatives , but was not served , and the record did not show on whose motion
the order was made . A judgment against the defendant was held to be void ,
because there was no service of the order on him, and it could not be presumed ,
in order to sustain the judgment , that the order was made on his motion . Amyx
vs. Smith's adm'x , 1 Met ., 529.
c. The plaintiff suggested the defendant's death , and continued the case for revivor
against C , as his executrix . At the next term , without formal revivor , she
answered , without objection . Upon the case having been taken a second time
to the Court of Appeals , it was held to be too late to question her right to defend
as executrix . Moss vs. Rowland, 3 Bush , 505.
d. It was held under the Code of 1854 that an action might be revived against the
representatives of a deceased defendant in the mode prescribed by ? 560, or by
an amended petition and summons against them . Greer vs. Powell, 1 Bush, 496 .
Subsection 3 of 500 is substantially the same as said ? 560.
505. REVIVOR OF PERSONAL ACTION AGAINST REAL REPRESENTATIVES .
a. If there be no personal representative , a suit in equity may be maintained against
the heirs or devisees alone ; and where they are infants it should be alleged and
proved either that personal estate has been distributed , or that real estate has
descended , to them ; and if the claim can be satisfied in no other way than by a
sale of their real estate , the sale should be made under the supervision of the
chancellor, and not by the sheriff under execution . Hagan vs. Patterson , 10
Bush, 441.
b. If a defendant die pending an action , and no personal representative qualify , it
may , under 505 [566], of the Civil Code , be revived against the heirs alone ; but
the remedy is exclusively equitable , and the action , if at law, should be trans-
ferred to equity, and the proper amendments made to the pleadings . Hagan vs.
Patterson , 10 Bush , 441 .

It details procedures for continuing lawsuits, handling cases with deceased defendants or plaintiffs, and distinguishing between clerical errors and judicial errors. The text includes references to various Kentucky court cases and their rulings on these procedural matters.

92 CIVIL CODE — NOTES TO SECS . 516 , 517 , 519 .
note to B ( Cooper vs. Poston , 1 Duv . , 92) ; or to render a judgment against "the
defendants ,""
two of whom were nominal defendants (Cin ., Ham . and Dayton R.
R. Co. and Dayton and Mich . R. R. Co. vs. Spratt , 2 Duv ., 4) ; or to render judg
ment upon an award returned to the court within ten days before the commence-
ment of the term (Carson vs. Carson , I Met . , 434) ; or to render judgment ,
prematurely , against a defendant constructively summoned (Buckner vs. Bush,
1 Duv . , 394 ; Harris vs. Adams , 2 Id., 141) ; or to render judgment against part
of the defendants , upon trial , before all of them are summoned . Breeding vs.
Stamper , 18 B. M. , 175.
In an action brought in the county in which the defendant resided , he was sum-
moned in that county , but not in time , and out of that county in time , for judg
ment at the next term ; and the court said that a judgment by default would
have been a clerical misprision . Raymon vs. Reed, 16 B. M. , 350.
AS TO INFANTS : It was held , under the Code of 1854, that rendering judgment
against an infant , before an answer by his guardian ad litem , was a judicial error .
Pond vs. Doneghy , 18 B. M. , 558. Consequently , the infant could not obtain .
relief against a judgment so rendered , except by an appeal . See subdivision 2:
of 517-the object of which is to enable infants to obtain relief, in such cases ,
by motion in the court below , without requiring then to make the motion within ,
the first three days of the next term . See ? 519.
2. WHAT ARE JUDICIAL ERRORS .
It is an error of the court to render a judgment after the plaintiff's death , and '
before an order of revivor is served on the defendant (Amyx vs. Smith's adm'x ,
I Met., 529) ; or to render a personal judgment against a defendant constructively
summoned (Payne vs. Witherspoon , 14 B. M. , 270) ; or to render a judgment sus-
taining an attachment against a defendant constructively summoned , before the
filing of an affidavit by or for the plaintiff , as is required by the Code (Payne vs..
Witherspoon , 14 B. M. , 270) ; or to sustain an order of arrest issued by the clerk .
before the filing of an affidavit by or for the plaintiff , as is required by the Code
(Pauer vs. Simon , 6 Bush , 514) ; or to render judgment against a defendant who
has not been summoned (Long vs. Montgomery , 6 Bush , 394) ; overruling Robin-
son vs. Mobley , 1 Bush, 196 ; or to render judgment against a defendant who is
summoned out of, and who did not reside in , the county in which the action is
brought . Ruby vs Grace , Duv ., 540 ; Dyas vs. Lindsey , 4 Bush, 349 .
In Pottinger vs. Mayfield , 14 B. M. , 647 , which was an action against two defendants ,
it was held to be a clerical misprision to render judgment against a defendant
who did not reside in , and who was summoned out of, the county in which the
action was brought . See contra , Ruby vs. Grace , 2 Duv . , 540 .
In an action for personal property , possession of which the plaintiff has obtained
pursuant to the provisions of the Code , a judgment for the defendant for the
value of the property , without allowing the plaintiff to discharge the judgment
by returning the property , was held to be judicially erroneous . Rogers vs. Brad-
ford, 8 Bush , 163.
If a party move for a continuance , or object to a trial , before the case is ready for
trial , it is an error of the court , and not a clerical misprision , to overrule the
objection or motion . Raymon vs. Reed, 16 B. M. , 344 ; Hedger vs. Downs , 2 Met . ,
162 ; Mattingly vs. Bosley , 2 Met . , 444 ; Smith vs. Ferguson , 3 Met ., 424 .
If a question be presented to , and decided by, the court, the judgment is not a
clerical misprision , but a judicial error. Dodd vs. Combs, 3 Met . , 29, 30 ; and see
cases cited in last paragraph .
In Deshong vs. Cain , 1 Duv ., 309 , it was held that a judgment in favor of A against
B , for $300, upon confirmation of the report of a commissioner to whom the

case had been referred , and whose report showed that B owed A $300, was an
error of the court, and not a clerical misprision . Quere .
3. PROCEEDINGS TO CORRECT CLERICAL MISPRISIONS .
a. An action is not an available remedy to correct a clerical misprision . It cannot
be corrected except upon motion in the court below (McCown vs. Macklin's ex'r, 7 .
Bush, 308) ; or upon an appeal if the motion be overruled . Dodds vs. Combs, 3 .
Met., 29, 30.
b. Petition by A and B against C , on his note to A , alleged that it had been assigned
to B. A joint judgment was rendered in favor of A and B, which B moved to
correct : held , that he must give notice to both A and C. Oldham vs. Brannon , 2.
Met., 305.
c. There is no limitation of time for correcting a clerical misprision except the pre-
mature rendition of judgment . Smith vs. Mullens , 3 Met . , 182.
518. VACATING JUDGMENT BY COURT BELOW , AFTER EXPIRATION OF
TERM .
a. See notes to several subsections cited below.
b. A final order cannot be vacated on motion after the
there was neither proof nor allegation to support it .

1
c. Before the Code was adopted , any substantial error to the prejudice of the party
complaining , and apparent in the record , was sufficient to authorize a bill of
review ; but now a final order cannot be vacated , after the term , by the court
which rendered it , except upon the grounds , and in the mode , mentioned in sec-
tions 518 [579] and 520 [581] . Anderson vs. Anderson , 18 B. M. , 95 ; McLean vs.
Nixon , 18 B. M. , 775 ; Hocker vs. Gentry , 3 Met . , 469 ; Scott vs. Scott's ex'r , 9 Bush,
174. See contra , Peak vs. Percifull , 3 Bush , 218 , in which the court said : “ This
suit, being brought to correct errors on the face of the decree , and to perfect the
title which it purported to pass , may be considered of the nature of a bill of
review . The record shows two such reviewable errors - 1st . In rendering a decree
against infants without legal notice or defense ; and , 2d . In rendering judgment
in favor of Percifull's administrator without service of notice on , or appearance
to , his cross -petition as an unsued intervenor , and without any order making him
a party ." And see also Bush vs. Madeira's heirs , 14 B. M. , 212 .
d. Regularly , a petition to vacate or modify a judgment should not be filed until..
the mandate of the Court of Appeals has been carried into effect ; but if it be
filed before , and present sufficient cause for vacating or modifying the judgment :
of the circuit court after the expiration of the term , it may be sustained . McLean
vs. Nixon , 18 B. M. , 775 ; Scott vs. Scott's ex'r, 9 Bush, 177. See note to 2 762 .
e. A judgment can be vacated after an affirmance by the Court of Appeals only for
errors which that court could not notice , and upon grounds prescribed in 2 518
[ 579] . Mc Lean vs. Nixon , 18 B. M. , 775 ; Speak vs. Mattingly, 4 Bush , 310 .
f. It was held , under the Code of 1854, that judgment granting a divorce might be
set aside after the term , though the successful party had married again . Meyar »
vs. Meyar, 3 Met ., 298. But see now ? 344.
SUBSECTIONS OF 2 518 :
1. Grounds for new trial generally . See notes to sections 340 , 342 , 343 , and 344 .
2. New trial to defendants constructively summoned . See notes to ? 414 .
3. For misprisions of the clerk. See notes to sections 516, 517, 519.
4. For fraud in obtaining judgment .
a . The fact that there was neither allegation nor proof to sustain the judgment :
does not show that the plaintiff's conduct was fraudulent . Anderson vs. Ander
son, 18 B. M. , 95 .

94 CIVIL CODE- NOTES TO SEC . 518 .
b. A consent-judgment was set aside , because the attorney who gave the consent had
no authority to do so ; though , in the opinion , no fraud was imputed to the par-
ties in whose favor it was rendered . Smith's heirs vs. Dixon , &c., 3 Met., 438 .
c. Money collected under a judgment fraudulently obtained , in an ordinary action ,
may be recovered in equity without awarding a new trial or setting aside the
judgment . Ellis vs. Kelly , 8 Bush , 621 .
5. Erroneous proceedings against persons under disability .
a . An infant may sue to vacate a judgment before or after he becomes of age . New-
land vs. Gentry , 18 B. M. , 671. And see, as to infants , 391 and notes thereto ;
and Richards vs. Richards ' adm'r , 10 Bush , 617.
b. In an action for land , against several defendants , it was held that they were entitled
to a new trial " on the ground of the infancy of one of them , unrepresented by
guardian , and not shown by the record of that action to be an infant .” Jamison
vs. Petit, 6 Bush, 669 .
c. In an action to subject property mortgaged by A and B , the fact that the clerk's
certificate of acknowledgment of the mortgage -deed , stating that B was a mar-
ried woman , was held to be insufficient to show that she was married when the
judgment was rendered , there being no allegation in the pleadings to that effect ;
and , consequently , she , having shown that fact , in an action for a new trial , was
entitled to that relief , under subsection 5 of 579, of the Code of 1854. Adams
vs. Jett's adm'r, 6 Bush, 585 .
Observe that coverture is not a disability according to subsection 5 of 518 of this
Code .
6. Death of a party beforejudgment .
a. A judgment of the Court of Appeals , reversing a judgment , cannot be disre
garded by the circuit court , for the reason that the person in whose name the
appeal was prosecuted was dead when it was taken . Spalding vs. Wathen , 7 Bush,
659. The appellant's death , if known , should have been taken advantage of in
the Court of Appeals by a plea ( 758) . Id . Quere , " whether or not the Court
of Appeals could have vacated its judgment of reversal , upon an application
made after the expiration of the time within which a petition for a rehearing
could have been filed , even though it might have been made to appear that the
death of the appellant did not come to a knowledge of the appellee until after
the expiration of that time ." Id.
7. Unavoidable casualty or misfortune .
a. A citizen's danger of arrest by Federal military authority , in Kentucky , in the
year 1862, was held to be an unavoidable misfortune which entitled him to relief
against a judgment . Yowell vs. Gaines , 2 Bush , 211 .
b . In Landrum vs. Farmer, 7 Bush , 47 , a new trial was refused to a party , “ although
one of his counsel was prevented from attending the court, by sickness , when
there was a manifest lack of diligence on the part of such party in not attending
the court or providing other counsel ." But in Triplett vs. Scott, 5 Bush , 81, a
new trial was granted to the defendant on account of the unavoidable non -at-
tendance of his attorney ; the defendant residing in a remote county , and having
a good defense , and no knowledge of the non -attendance of his attorney .
c. A non -resident , temporarily in Kentucky , was unexpectedly sued by several per-
sons , and in a moment of exasperation destroyed the summonses delivered to him
by the sheriff; but he promptly employed an attorney , who went with him to the
clerk's office , and asked the clerk for the papers in all the suits against the defend-
ant . The clerk produced the papers in a number of suits , but failed to produce
those in the suit of McCall , whose attorney was present , and assisted the clerk in
finding some of the papers . A judgment by default having been rendered in

McCall vs. Hitchcock , 9 Bush , 66 .
8. Error in judgment against infant.
a . See notes to 391 ; and Richards vs. Richards ' adm'r , 10 Bush , 617, in which it
was held that an infant " may waive mere errors or irregularities in the prepara-
tion of the cause , even when they are patent , and proceed at once to have the
judgment vacated or modified upon the merits of the controversy ;" and that , if
he do so , " an investigation into the regularity of the original proceedings is
neither necessary nor proper . The judgment is prima facie correct , and the onus
is upon the infant to show , not that it ought not to have been rendered , in view
of the defective preparation of the cause , but that it is unjust and unauthorized
by the law applicable to the facts of the particular case .
" The errors contemplated by subsection 8, 518 [579] , are such as effect the substan-
tial rights of the infants , and to obtain relief they must show that actual injus
tice has been done them ." Richards , guardian , &c. , vs. Richards ' adm'r , & c.,
10 Bush, 617 .
PROCEEDINGS TO VAĆATE JUDGMENT AFTER TERM .
a . A judgment granting a new trial , in an action brought pursuant to this section ,
is final, and may be appealed from . McCall vs. Hitchcock , 7 Bush , 615 ; and 9
Bush , 66 .
b. A defendant , after getting a new trial , cannot prosecute an appeal from the orig-
inal judgment . McCall vs. Hitchcock , 9 Bush, 66 .
523. INJUNCTION SUSPENDING PROCEEDINGS IN ACTION FOR NEW
TRIAL .
2527.
? 547.
a . If the plaintiff in an action to set aside a judgment do not obtain an injunction
suspending proceedings upon the judgment , he cannot recover damages from the
plaintiff , or a commissioner , for selling land pursuant to the judgment . Brown
vs. Hudson , 3 Bush , 60.
WRITINGS OF ADVERSE PARTIES CONSTITUTE EVIDENCE .
a. Observe that 527 speaks of writings " made " by a party ; and not of writings
" executed " by a party , as did the Code of 1854.
b. 588 applies not only to writings upon which actions are brought pursuant to
120, but also to exhibits which are relied upon as evidence pursuant to 128.
Haney vs. Tempest , 3 Met . , 95 ; Barret vs. Coburn , 3 Id ., 510 ; Wells vs. Lewis , 4
Id. , 269 .
But a paper referred to in a pleading, but not filed before the commencement of the
trial , cannot be read as evidence without proof of its execution . Gentry vs.
Doolin, Bush, 1.
c. The provisions of ? 527 apply to defendants constructively summoned . Buckner
vs. Bush, I Duv ., 394 ; and see notes to 409 .
FOR WHAT PURPOSES AFFIDAVITS MAY BE READ .
a. A master in chancery has inherent power to take and report testimony concern-
ing matters of fact referred to him by his court . " In such inquiries , formal dep-
ositions before an examiner are neither necessary nor ever certified otherwise than
by the master in his report." Taylor vs. Young's adm'rs , 2 Bush, 428 .
2550. a. The affidavit of an agent or attorney, that he believes the facts , is sufficient . Frank-
lin Savings Institution vs. M. M. Bank of Wheeling , 1 Met., 156.

PROOF IN EQUITABLE ACTIONS .
a. As to proving exhibits , see note c to 128.
b. As to testimony upon a jury trial of an issue in a chancery case: In Owens vs. Owens ,
Hardin , 160, it was said that , in such cases , everything should , and usually does ,
appear in the record ; and in Hammon and wife vs. Pearl, 6 Mon. , 413 , it was said
that the practice of referring matters of account , in equity , to a jury , to be tried
upon parol testimony , and therefore not subject to the revision of the appellate
tribunal , cannot be approved .
It.may be doubted whether that objection was well taken , because it seems that
the parol testimony may be exhibited to the Court of Appeals by a bill of excep-
tions ; as appears to have been done in the case of Savings Bank , & c. , vs. Benton ,
2 Met. , 240 ; and see Reading vs. Ford's heirs , 1 Bibb , 340 ; Moore vs. Simpson , 5
Litt., 51 ; and Griffith vs. Depew , 3 Mar. , 183, in which it was said that " it does
not appear what kind of evidence was used on the inquiry- whether the deposi-
tions which were filed , or that of witnesses ore tenus- when it would have been
proper to have pointed out the nature of the evidence in the decree which
directed the inquiry ."
In Savings Bank , &c. , ds. Benton , above cited , it was held that , upon a trial by
jury of an issue formed in an equitable action , depositions , as well as parol evi-
dence , may be used , unless the court direct otherwise as to theformer. It seems that ,
under this Code (? 552, subs . 2) , depositions only can be used in such cases, unless
the court direct otherwise .
WHEN PARTIES MAY COMMENCE TAKING DEPOSITIONS .
a . As to the time when a plaintiff may commence taking depositions against an
infant defendant , who is constructively summoned , see Covington and Lexington
R. R. Co. vs. Bowler's heirs , 9 Bush, 468 .

ADJOURNMENT OF TAKING DEPOSITIONS .
a. A deposition having been commenced , in presence of both parties , according to
notice, but not concluded on that day , the witness was necessarily called off for
the next day ; a notice was then given that the deposition of another witness
would be taken on the next day , so as to preserve the right then to adjourn over to
the third day , when the deposition of the first named witness would be concluded :
held , that the deposition should be read , and not quashed for the reason that it

b. As to affidavits concerning cause of adjournment of taking depositions under
635 of the Code of 1854, see Price vs. Caperton , 1 Duv ., 207 .
OF TAKING OF DEPOSITION .

though it was sealed when delivered to the clerk . Breeding vs. Stamper, 18 B.

CIVIL CODE - NOTES TO SECS . 587 , 588 , 596 To 598 . 97
587.
588.
EXCEPTIONS TO DEPOSITIONS .
a. Exceptions to depositions are regarded as waived , unless it appear that the court
below decided upon them , or refused to do so . Bronson vs. Green , 2 Duv ., 234.
b. Exceptions to depositions , for any other cause than the incompetency of a wit
ness , or the irrelevancy or incompetency of the testimony , must be filed and noted
of record before the commencement of trial . Eastham vs. Curd , 15 B. M. , 105.
c. It is too late to object in this court, for the first time , that depositions were taken
two days before the appointment of a guardian ad litem for infant defendants .
Webber vs. Webber, 1 Met ., 18.
d. Erroneous decisions upon exceptions to depositions must be excepted to when
made . Corn vs. Sims , 2 Met . , 391.
e. Depositions taken by A , in his suit against B, are incompetent against C ; and
exceptions by him are unnecessary , and would be improper ; but , if A should
offer to read the depositions against C , he should object , and , if the court should
overrule his objection , he should except . Kerr vs. Gibson , 8 Bush, 129.
AMENDING CERTIFICATES OF DEPOSITIONS .
a. As to amending depositions under the Code of 1854, the provisions of which are
materially different from those of 588, see Hall vs. Renfro , 3 Met . , 51.
? 596 to 598. IMPEACHING AND CONTRADICTING A WITNESS .
a . The Code of 1854 declared , as does ? 596 of this Code , that a party may contra-
dict his own witness " by other evidence , and by showing that he has made state-
ments different from his present testimony ." It was held that a party might
contradict his witness in either of those modes . Champ vs. Commonwealth , 2 Met.,
22, 23.
But where a witness stated a fact unfavorable to the party who called him , and
denied the existence of a fact favorable to that party ; it was held that he could
not be contradicted by evidence that he had previously admitted the existence
of the latter fact . Champ vs. Commonwealth , 2 Met. , 24.
b. A deposition taken to impeach the credit of a witness cannot be read by the
adverse party , if the party taking it declines to read it , and fails to assail the
character of the witness intended to be impeached by the deposition . If the
character of the witness had been assailed by the party taking the deposition ,
and he had declined to read it , then the adverse party would have had the right
to use the deposition for the purpose of sustaining his witness . Sullivan vs. Nor
ris, 8 Bush, 519.
c. A party who reads a deposition taken by the adverse party , as, ordinarily , a party
has a right to do , makes him his own witness , and cannot impeach him other-
wise than any other of his witnesses . Musick vs. Ray, 3 Met ., 427.
d. The extent of the inquiry allowable, on the examination in chief, is as to the
general character of the witness , whether good or bad ; his or her general char-
acter for truth or veracity , and whether worthy of credit or not . The general
moral character of the witness is also a fair subject of inquiry in chief ; but not
general character as to any particular acts of ignominy or turpitude . The par-
ticular facts can be inquired into on the other side , and such inquiry , when begun
on cross -examination , may be pursued by the party impeaching , but he has no
right to do so in chief. Thurman vs. Virgin and wife , 18 B. M. , 792.
e. Before other evidence can be offered of the witness having made at another time
a different statement , he must be inquired of concerning the same , with the
circumstances of time , place , and person present as correctly as the examining

party can present them ; and , if it is in writing , it must be shown to the witness ,
and he be allowed to explain it . Murphy vs. May , 9 Bush, 38.
f. The regular mode of inquiring into the reputation of a witness is to ask of those
introduced for that purpose whether they know the general reputation of the
person in question among his neighbors ; and what that reputation is . The
inquiry must be as to his general reputation where he is best known , or with
those among whom he dwells . And when the impeaching witness is asked for
what others say respecting such person , he must be able to state what is gen-
erally said by those among whom he dwells , or with whom he is conversant .
Henderson vs. Hayne , 2 Met ., 342.
g. But it is not essential to the admissibility of such statements that they should
come from those who are both neighbors and personal acquaintances of the party
impeached . They are admissible , if made either by those who are acquainted
with him , or those who dwell in his immediate neighborhood ; and the weight
to be attached to either class is for the jury to consider and determine . Ib . , 342.
h . An instruction which confines the jury , in considering the evidence relating to
the character of an impeached witness , to such statements respecting his general
moral character and principles as have been made by those " among who he
dwells and to whom he is known ," is misleading . Ib ., 342 .
.
EVIDENCE OF GOOD CHARACTER OF WITNESS .
a. Evidence of the good character of a witness is not admissible where he is only
impeached by proof of his having made a different statement on another occa-
sion , or by proof of facts which contradict those stated by him in his testi-
mony. Vance vs. Vance , 2 Met., 581.
SEPARATING WITNESSES .
a. The provisions of this section apply to a motion to disbar an attorney . Walker
vs. Commonwealth , 8 Bush , 86.
605 to 607. COMPETENCY OF WITNESSES .
1. For decisions concerning competency of witnesses under the Code of 1854, see
14 B. M. , 320 ; 15 Id . , 641 ; 16 Id. , 182 ; 357 ; 564 ; 697 ; 18 Id . , 94 ; 125 ; 631 ;
734 ; 758 ; 1 Met ., 575 ; 2 Id. , 517 ; 608 .
2. Husband and wife .
a . Before the act of 1872, chap . 139, re -enacted in the G. S. , chap . 37, a husband
was held to be incompetent to testify for his wife (Kaye vs. Tydings, 3 Met . ,
527) ; and it was held in several cases , that the wife could not testify for or
against her husband during his life .
It was held , however , that , after the death of the husband , the testimony of his
widow to facts within her knowledge , not derived from the husband , was com-
petent to show that a conveyance by the husband was made to defraud his
creditors, but not to diminish the liability of the husband's estate to the creditor
to whom the conveyance was made . Short vs. Tinsley, & c., I Met., 397.
b . The aforesaid act of 1872 is substantially the same as subsection 1 of 2 606 of this
Code , with reference to husband and wife , except with reference to lost baggage .
Under said act of 1872 it was held-
I. A witness is not incompetent by reason of his wife's interest in the matter in
litigation .
2. A married woman is in no state of case a competent witness for or against her
husband .
3. When the wife is the real party in interest her husband shall not be called to tes-
tify against her interest , and shall not be allowed to testify in her behalf in case

4. A widow having no interest in the result of the suit between her husband's
executor and another is a competent witness for the executor . Booth's ex'r vs.
Vanarsdale , 9 Bush, 717 .
c. For decisions concerning competency of a wife for accomplices of husband in
criminal cases , see 1 Met ., 13 ; 3 Id. , 481 .
5. TESTIMONY OF PARTY AFTER DEATH OF ADVERSE PARTY .
a. The defendant cannot testify for himself as to what took place between him and
the plaintiff's deceased partner , in the absence of the plaintiff . Lawhorn vs. Car-
ter, 11 Bush, 10.
6. TESTIMONY OF PARTY AGAINST DEFENDANT CONSTRUCTIVELY SUMMONED .
a . One of two partners mortgaged his interest in the partnership property and left
the State , and the mortgagee brought suit to foreclose against him by construct-
ive service , and his partner by actual service of process . The latter answered ,
alleging an indebtedness to himself by the firm on account of advances , for
which he claimed a prior lien on the partnership property . Held, that as between
the mortgagee and this remaining partner the latter was a competent witness to
prove the nature and extent of his claim ; but neither he nor the mortgagee was
a competent witness as against the defendant constructively served . See G. S. ,
chap . 27 ; Love vs. Cummings , 10 Bush , 578 .
7. TESTIMONY OF PARTY AS TO ACCOUNT BOOKS.
a. An account book which shows on its face , that it does not contain the original
entries of the items contained in it , is incompetent . Lawhorn vs. Carter , II
Bush , 10.
b. "Mercantile books can only be admitted as affirmative evidence , and are never
admissible to establish a negative proposition ." Lawhorn vs. Carter , 11 Bush,
10.
616 to 621.
623.
625 .
SECURITY FOR COSTS .
a. Does [684] 616 refer to costs in the Court of Appeals ? Chrismas vs. Russell , 2
Met., 114.
b. This section does not apply to a non -resident defendant , prosecuting an appeal .
He may be required to give security for costs , under 744 ; but , if he should fail
to do so , his attorney would not be liable under 621. Chrismas vs. Russell , 2
Met., 113.
c. It was held under the General Statutes (ch . 26, 3) that the action must be dis-
missed , unless bond be given before its commencement (11 Bush , 47) ; but that
the motion to dismiss must be made before judgment . Id. , 305 .
MOTIONS .
a. A purchaser of land , under an order of sale , may , by motion , obtain an order for
rent due from a tenant. Cooper vs. Baker, 2 Bush, 244 .
b. A trustee appointed by the court , to invest funds, and the sureties on his bond ,
may be proceeded against by rule , with reference to the funds . Dickinson vs.
Trout , 8 Bush, 441 .
SERVICE OF NOTICE .
a. A sheriff's return , on a notice of a motion , that he had executed it by leaving a
true copy with the wife of the defendant , he not being at home , is prima facie
sufficient . Fleece vs. Goodrum , 1 Dûv . , 306 .

OFFER TO COMPROMISE .
a. If a defendant offer in his answer to pay part of a demand , and the plaintiff
refuse to accept it, the defendant should not be charged with any costs subse-
quently accruing , unless plaintiff's recovery exceed what defendant offered to
pay . Bull, & c., vs. Harragon , & c., 17 B. Mon. , 353 .
b. An attorney at law , under his general authority as such , independent of any
special authority conferred upon him by his client , has no power to compromise
the suit or to surrender the claim of his client to the defendants in the action,
and dismiss his petition . Smith vs. Dixon , 3 Met ., 438 .
The extent to which the authorities go is , that admissions made by an attorney of
record in court, in a case then pending , while conducting and managing it, and
preparatory to the trial or decision of the case , are binding upon the client . Ib.
SUBMITTING A CONTROVERSY .

section 637 of the Civil Code , unless there be an affidavit filed stating that " the

facts upon which the controversy depends (Canaday vs. Hopkins , 7 Bush , 108) ;
but , if the agreed case merely state the questions , leaving the facts to be proved ,
the court has jurisdiction , though the practice is irregular and inconvenient .

classification.title: Civil and Criminal Code 1883 - Sections 634-643
classification.summary: This section of the Civil Code of 1883 details procedures for offers of compromise in civil suits, the submission of agreed cases to court, and the requirements for indemnifying bonds before the levy of execution or distress warrants. It outlines conditions under which a defendant is not charged with costs after an offer to compromise, the limitations on an attorney's power to compromise, and the jurisdictional requirements for hearing agreed cases, including the necessity of an affidavit attesting to the reality of the controversy. It also specifies the conditions an officer must meet to be protected by an indemnifying bond, emphasizing strict compliance with statutory requirements regarding the bond's execution, return, and surety's solvency, and the timing of these actions relative to property seizure and sale.
classification.reasoning: The provided text is from the Civil Code of 1883 and discusses procedural aspects of civil litigation, specifically concerning offers of compromise, agreed cases, and indemnifying bonds for officers. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly target or discriminate against any racial group. The content is purely procedural and relates to the administration of justice in civil matters. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

e. The officer may , if he see proper, advertise a sale of the property , and await the
execution of the bond until the hour of sale has arrived ; and , if it be then exe-
cuted , he may proceed to sell , and return it to the proper office as soon there-
after as it can be reasonably returned ; and may rely upon its execution and return
as a bar to an action to recover the value of the property sold , and also as a bar
to a recovery for damages for the seizure and detention of the property , unless
the action was instituted before the bond was executed . Rudy vs. Johnson , II
Bush, 543.
f. If the officer doubt, upon reasonable grounds , whether personal property levied upon
is subject to levy or sale , the law gives him the right to demand an indemnifying
bond . This right is not confined to any particular class of cases ; and the exist-
ence of such a doubt is the sole condition upon which the right depends . Boara
vs. Helm , 5 Met ., 500.
The sheriff's return upon an execution showed that , in the action in which the
judgment was rendered , process had not been served on the party whose property
had been seized by him , but on another person of the same name , and that the
levy was made with the understanding that he was to be indemnified by the
plaintiff before selling . Upon motion for a rule against the sheriff to compel
him to make the sale-held, that he was justified in doubting whether the prop-
erty was subject to the execution , and in demanding of the plaintiff a bond of
indemnity before he would proceed to sell . Id. , 500.
2. Requisites and effectof indemnifying bond.
a. The action of a claimant against an officer is not barred unless the bond contain a
covenant to pay damages which any claimant may sustain . Jewell vs. Mills , 3
Bush, 62.
b. The bond embraces equitable as well as legal claimants .
141.
Watts vs. Cook, 2 Bush,
c. If the officer have taken the proper bond , and suit be brought against him by
the claimant, and the plaintiff in the execution be substituted for the officer , as
defendant in the action ; the effect of the bond , as a bar to the suit , is the same ,
in favor of the plaintiff , as it would have been in favor of the officer . Gunn vs.
Gudehus , 15 B. M. , 450 .
In such a case , the burden rests on the defendant to prove the sufficiency of the
surety in the bond when taken . Id.
d. The defendant may sue upon it for the value of property , sold by the sheriff,
which was exempt from execution . Dixon vs. Bacon , 3 Bush, 534.
e. If property not subject to execution be levied on , and an indemnifying bond be
given , and a sale be made for more than enough to satisfy the execution , the
obligors will be liable , not merely for the amount of the execution , but for all
damages caused by the seizure and sale . Secrets vs. Markwell , 11 Bush , 316 .
f. Sheriff failing to sell after indemnifying bond, measure of damages. Although it is
made the duty of the sheriff, by the express provisions of section [709] 641 of
the Civil Code , to proceed to subject the property to the execution where a bond
of indemnity is given , yet if he fails to do so , he is only liable for such damage
as the plaintiff actually sustained by his misconduct ; and may , therefore , in an
action on his official bond , show that the property was not subject to the execu-
tion , and that the title was in some one else , in mitigation of damages . Snoddy
& Bransford et al. vs. Foster , &c. , 1 Met., 160.
645 to 651. SUSPENDING SALES OF PERSONAL PROPERTY , UNDER EXECU-
TION .
a. To whom bond to be payable . The bond must be payable to the plaintiff ; and , if
made payable to the assignee of an execution , it is not valid as a statutory bond,

classification.summary: This section of the Civil Code from 1883 details the procedures and legal implications surrounding indemnifying bonds taken by officers when levying on and selling personal property under execution. It covers when an officer can demand such a bond, the requisites and effects of the bond, and the liabilities of the obligors and the officer in various scenarios, including cases where property is wrongly seized or not subject to execution. It also touches upon the sheriff's duty to proceed with sales after an indemnifying bond is provided and the measure of damages if they fail to do so.

and cannot be proceeded upon by motion . Watson vs. Gabby, 18 B. M. , 660 .
See 2 Littell, 125.
b. Effect of bond. If a sale of personal property , upon which an execution has been
levied , be suspended , by the execution of a bond by persons who are not defend- -
ants in the execution , they can show any equitable cause against judgment on
the bond , in whole or in part. Williams vs. Smith , 4 Bush, 540 ; overruling Wat-
son vs. Gabby , 18 B. M. , 663 .
An execution , in favor of A vs. B , having been levied on property of B , on which
his landlord had a lien for rent ; C , who was B's surety on a covenant to pay the
rent, executed bond pursuant to [713 ] 545 ; and afterward paid the rent , and
took from the landlord an assignment of the covenant . A, having moved for-
judgment on the bond , it was held that C was substituted to the landlord's lien
on the property ; and that , the property being of less value than the rent paid.
by C , A was not entitled to judgment . Smith vs. Wells , 4 Bush , 92.
An execution in favor of A against B, having been levied on property which B had
mortgaged to C for more than its value , C executed bond pursuant to [ 713] 645 :
held , on A's motion upon the bond , that he was not entitled to a judgment against
C. Hudson vs. Stone, decided in October, 1875.
In the case last cited , it was held that , though the claimant is not bound to give
bond to suspend the sale , until the property has been appraised by three
appraisers , and though the sheriff has no right to proceed with the levy before
causing appraisement to be made ; yet , if it be made by two appraisers , and
bond be given , reciting that fact , it will be valid as a statutory bond .
In Spark vs. Shropshire , 4 Bush , 550, it was held that an obligor in a bond given
pursuant to [ 713] 645 , was estopped from denying that the property belonged
to the defendant in the execution , by a forthcoming bond previously executed
by said obligor , in which it was stated that the property belonged to said defend-
ant .
The sheriff may take a replevin -bond after taking bond pursuant to [713] 645 ;
but this does not extinguish the liability on the last mentioned bond . The
plaintiff would still be entitled to ten per cent . thereon , and its costs ; and , if
the replevin -bond be invalid , because it is not signed by all the defendants , the
plaintiff may proceed on the other bond as is authorized by ? [716] 648 .
c. Proceedings on bond. The provision of ? [481 ] 446 , requiring notice " to state the
nature and grounds of the motion , " does not apply to proceedings under ? [716]
648. Smith vs. Wells ' adm'x , 8 Bush, 92. But the provision of ? [484 ] 449 , that
judgment shall be given " according to law and the rules of equity ," does apply
thereto . Williams vs. Smith , 4 Bush , 540 .

a. An action by an accommodation indorser of a bill of exchange , against a prior

DUTIES OF CLERKS .
a. The clerk's entry " shall be evidence of the service of the summons if it should
be lost " ( 670) ; and a statement in a judgment that the defendant was duly
summoned is not evidence of that fact. Robinson vs. Mobley , 1 Bush , 197.
b. It is the duty of the clerk of the court to which an appeal is about to be taken ,
to prepare the appeal -bond required to be executed by the party appealing.

EXECUTION OF NEW BOND FOR DEFECTIVE ONE .
If a party, desiring to appeal , attempt in good faith to execute a bond , and does
execute , with surety , such bond as is prepared for him by the clerk, and it be
defective , he should be allowed to execute a new bond, without prejudice to his
rights . Adams vs. Settles, 2 Duv . , 76 ; Walters vs. Patrick , ↳ Bush , 223 ; Manier

b. Conduct of commissioner in making sale .
If the commissioner fail to advertise the sale of land as directed by the judgment ,
and the land sell for less than its value , the sale must be set aside . Williams vs.
Woodruff, 1 Duv ., 257.
The fact that the commissioner designated which part of the tract of land should
be sold , instead of referring the matter to the court, after having ascertained that
less than the whole tract would satisfy the judgment , is not sufficient of itself to
quash the sale . But the discretion conferred upon the commissioner in this
respect , when abused , may be corrected . Ib .
The commissioner was directed to advertise the sale of land at the court-house
door , and two other public places in the county of Henderson . It was advertised
by posting notices at the court -house door , and at two other places in the city of
Henderson , which is in the county of Henderson . This , although a literal com-
pliance with the directions to the commissioner , is not a compliance with their
spirit and meaning . Ib.
c. Upon setting aside a chancery sale , the purchaser may be allowed a reasonable
compensation for his trouble and outlay . Gathwright vs. Hazard and wife , 17 B.
M. , 557 ; Egard vs. Chearnly et al., 1 Bush, 12.
d. Sales by commissioners under orders of court , must be confirmed by the court
[see 394 , subs . 2] ; and a sale for a flagrantly inadequate price should not be
confirmed . Egard vs. Chearnly et al. , 1 Bush , 12.
Stump-
But a decretal sale should not be rejected by the chancellor for inadequacy of price,
unless there has been such a sacrifice of the property as to import fraud .
vs. Martin , & c., 9 Bush, 285 .
e. Opening biddings . The chancellor should not order a re -sale , merely because there
is an offer of an advance of ten per cent . " There must be either fraud or mis-
conduct in some one connected with the sale ; some surprise or misapprehension
on the part of those interested or of the officer who conducts the sale ; or some
irregularity in the proceedings , or other cumstances attending it , conducing to
show unfairness , before the chancellor w efuse to confirm this act of his com--
missioner ." Stump vs. Martin , 9 Bush, 285 .
f. A bidder for improved real property was held liable for the price , though before-
he received possession , and before the sale was confirmed , the improvements were
accia..any destroyed by fire, Vince's adm'r vs. Foster & Ray , 9 Bush , 389 .
g. It is error to order a sale of property when the title is so defective as to prevent →
a sale for a fair price. Skillman vs. Hamilton , 1 Bush , 248.
h . The fact that a judgment unde ; which a sale of land is made by commissioner
was erroneous , is no ground for quashing the sale . Vanbussum vs. Maloney , 2-
Met., 550.
i. As to sales under mortgages and liens , see, further , 2 374 and notes thereto .

a It was held in Weatherford vs. Myers , 2 Duv ., 91 , that the circuit court had no
jurisdiction in an action in equity to subject land of the defendant upon the
return of nulla bona on executions issued by a justice of the peace : as the land
might have been subjected , by procuring an execution from the circuit court , as

APPEALS FROM INFERIOR COURTS .
a. Where court appealed from had no jurisdiction . If the quarterly court has no juris-
diction of the case , no recovery can be had upon an appeal to the circuit court,
although the circuit court would have had jurisdiction of the case had the
action been brought in it in the first instance . In such a case the circuit court
should make such orders as will render the proceedings in the quarterly court
inoperative . Wigginton vs. Moss , 2 Met., 38.
b. Same. What order should be made on the appeal . Upon an appeal from the judg
ment of a justice of the peace , if it appear that the justice had no jurisdiction ,
the court should dismiss the suit. Fleming vs. Limebaugh , 2 Met ., 265 .
c. Amount in controversy . The amount in controversy was over $ 50 ; and the magis-
trate dismissed the warrant ; the plaintiff appealed to the quarterly court , which
likewise dismissed him , and he appealed to the circuit court, which gave him
judgment . The action of the justice and quarterly court was correct , and the
circuit court should have dismissed the case for want of jurisdiction . Fidler vs.
Hall, 2 Met . , 461 .
d. Upon trial of appeal , cause of action cannot be changed. Upon the trial of an
appeal from a justice of the peace , the cause of action must be the same as that
set out in the warrant . The same action is to be tried anew ; but no new action
can be tried , neither cau the demand be increased by pleading or otherwise
beyond the jurisdiction of the ustice . Burbage vs. Squires , 3 Met., 77.
e. Costs. Section 727 [ 8501 authorizes the court only to adjudge to , or withhold
from the appellant his own costs , and not to adjudge against him the appellee's
Gentry vs. Doolin, 1 Bush , I.costs .

costs , but that plaintiff shall not have execution until further order (3 Met ., 309 ) ;
order, before cause was finally decided , that a party should pay a commissioner
for his services ( 3 B. M. , 385) ; for attempted general definitions of final orders
see 1 B. M. , 48 ; 4 Met ., 30.
2. The following have been held to be orders from which no appeal could be taken : the
decision of a judge , though in court , upon a writ of habeas corpus (15 B. M. ,
147) ; a commitment for contempt , unless the conditions of discharge are illegal
(15 B. M. , 607) ; order of county court refusing to qualify a person as deputy
sheriff (4 Met ., 237) ; order that administrator would be removed , unless he
should give counter -security at the next term (2 J. J. M. , 53) ; decree , that B
should pay A $ 532 by next term ; and that B had an equitable lien on C's land ,
for $ 500, which A could enforce for payment of his demand on B ; and unless
B should pay A, such further orders would be made as might be necessary (4 J.
J. M. , 38) ; decree " settling [deciding upon ] the rights of the parties " to land ,
and ordering surveys to ascertain , by metes and bounds, the part to be con-
veyed to the plaintiff (4 Mon. , 414 ) ; decree that A is equitably entitled to B's
interest in land ; that C , who purchased it from B , holds in trust for A , and
appointing commissioner to report as to the extent of said interest , and of A's
payments to B (9 B. M. , 9) ; decree deciding as to sums due to several parties '
validity of liens , and priorities among them ; but not ordering a sale of the prop
erty nor providing for payment of the demands out of it (17 B. M. , 149) ; decree
that defendant convey land to plaintiff , and acknowledge deed before clerk of
county court, to be produced at hearing of cause , &c. (6 Dana , 389) ; decree dis-
solving injunction , & c . (6 Dana , 389) ; decree , and suit to enforce a lien , fixing
the sum due by defendant , and ordering him to pay it on or before the first day
of the next term (3 B. M. , 455 ) ; order before final hearing sustaining or discharg-
ing an attachment (14 B. M. , 195 ; 4 Met ., 56 ; 108) ; or order overruling motion
to discharge an attachment (17 B. M. , 144 ; 14 B. M. , 200 ) ; order , in attach-
ment -case , that A is entitled to priority over B , and that B is entitled to prior
ity over C ; but not distributing the fund , nor giving any other relief to either
of the parties (4 Met . , 108) ; order requiring plaintiff to elect which of two
entries.citation: Civil and Criminal Codes of Practice of Kentucky, 1883, p. 431

classification.title: Civil and Criminal Code 1883 - Notes to Section 734
classification.summary: This text consists of notes to Section 734 of the Civil Code of 1883, detailing various types of court orders that are considered final and thus appealable, and those that are not. It lists examples of judgments, decrees, and orders related to jurisdiction, setting aside judgments, conveyance of land, appointment of executors, probate of wills, removal of nuisances, costs, sale of property, distribution of assets, and injunctions. It also lists orders from which no appeal could be taken, such as decisions on habeas corpus, commitments for contempt, refusal to qualify deputy sheriffs, and certain decrees regarding liens and property rights.

CIVIL CODE- NOTES TO SEC . 734. 107
causes of action he will prosecute ( 10 Bush , 649 ) ; order dissolving an injunction ,
and continuing the case (2 Met ., 325 ) ; order appointing a receiver ( 15 B. M.,
47 ) [but see new Code , 298 ] ; order remanding a case to a county from which it
had been moved by change of venue ( 18 B. M. , 826 ) ; order setting aside a
verdict and judgment (3 Litt ., 333 ) ; order that petition be taken for confessed
(2 Duv ., 402 ) [ but see 2 Bush , 153] ; order setting aside an order , made at the
same term , dismissing a petition (6 Bush , 681) ; order of county court requiring
executor to give security at next term until the order is enforced (7 Bush , 504) ;
order quashing an inquisition upon a writ of ad quod damnum , in mill case (3
Mon. , 7) ; orders confirming biennial reports of commissioner , concerning guard-
ian's accounts , in an equitable action brought by him , for a settlement of his
accounts from time to time. 10 Bush, 99 .
But erroneous orders , not final , are subject to revision after a final judgment . 14
B. M., 200.
3. A judgment of the circuit court , reversing a judgment of a justice of the peace, is a
nullity from which no appeal lies . Bennett vs. Thompson , 10 Bush, 365 .
4. Jurisdiction of Court of Appeals .
Sections 15 and 16 of the Code of 1854, gave jurisdiction to the Court of
Appeals , " where the action or proceeding is for the recovery of money or per-
sonal property and the matter in controversy does not exceed $ 100 in value ; or ,
in behalf of the defendant where the judgment of the inferior court is against
him for money or personal property , not exceeding in value $ 100." Those sec-
tions were so amended by an act of 1857, as to substitute $ 50 for $ 100.
a . It was held under the Code , that the Court of Appeals had jurisdiction , if the
debt and interest exceeded $ 100 at the commencement of the action ( Orth & Wal-
lace vs. Clutz's adm'r , 18 B. M. , 223 ; Bakewell vs. Howell, 2 Met ., 268) ; or if the
aggregate of several notes exceeded $ 100. Bakewell vs. Howell, 2 Met ., 268.
b. Under the act of 1857, it was held that the Court of Appeals had jurisdiction on
an appeal by the plaintiff , if the matter in controversy exceeded $ 50 ; though the
defendant could not appeal if the judgment against him was for less than $ 50
(Brandies vs. Stewart , 1 Met ., 395 ); unless it was reduced below that sum by a
set -off or counter claim . Tipton vs. Chambers , 1 Met . , 565.
c. If several persons be jointly suable , on one contract , for separate amounts , the
aggregate claimed against all determines the jurisdiction . Wilde vs. Haycraft, 2
Duv., 309 .
Smith
d. The defendant cannot appeal from a judgment against him for one cent and costs ,
in an action for false imprisonment . Revil et al. vs. Petit, 3 Met ., 321.
e. An appeal lies from a judgment for land without reference to its value .
vs. Moberly and wife , 15 B. M. , 70 ; Moore , & c. , vs. Boner, &c. , 7 Bush , 28.
And if an action for damages involves the title to land , an appeal lies without ref-
erence to the sum of money in controversy . Caskey vs. Lewis , 15 B. M. , 27 .
2. Who may appeal.
a. A garnishee , against whom an attaching creditor has obtained judgment , may
appeal . Forepaugh , & c., vs. Appold & Sons , 17 B. M. , 625 .
b. A purchaser of land , at a decretal sale , may except to the sale , and may appeal
from a judgment overruling his exceptions . Allen vs. Graves , 3 Bush, 491 .
c. In an action for mandamus , against the mayor and council of a city to compel
the performance of a municipal duty , the city may appeal , as it is in effect a pro-
ceeding against the city . City of Louisville vs. Kean , 18 B. M. , 13.
d. See 763 and notes thereto .

108 CIVIL CODE- NOTES TO SECS . 734 , 736 тo 738 .
3. Granting of appeals by clerk of the Court of Appeals .
a . It was held under the Code of 1854-
Ist . That " the mode of prosecuting an appeal to this court, when it is not granted
by the court rendering the judgment , is by application to the clerk of this court,
by filing the record with him , with the names of the parties appellants and
appellees , and as matter of right he must grant the appeal ."
2d . That the suing out of a summons against the appellee was not a requisite to
the granting of the appeal ; and that the appeal dated from the filing of the
record with the clerk . Jones ' ex'x vs. Finnell & Winston , 8 Bush, 25 .
This Code does not seem to have made any change upon those subjects , except
that it authorizes the taking of an appeal , upon filing a copy of the judgment from
which the appeal is taken . 734 .
4. Effect of appeal .
a. A cause cannot be in progress in the inferior and superior courts at the same
time . An appeal terminates the action of the inferior court in the case , until
the appeal is disposed of. Helm vs. Boone & Talbot , 6 J. J. Mar. , 353 .
It was held , however , in Irvine vs. Scobee, 5 Litt ., 70, that the sheriff's return upon
a summons might be amended , whilst a writ of error was pending to reverse the
judgment in the action .
5. Appeals by consent.
a. In Jameson vs. Mosely , 4 Mon. , 414 , the Court of Appeals entertained jurisdiction
of an appeal , by consent , from an interlocutory decree , " settling the rights of
the parties ," and directing the surveyor to go on the lands to ascertain , by metes-
and bounds , the part to be conveyed to the appellee according to the principles
of the decree . See acc.: Helm vs. Boone & Talbot , 6. J. J. M. , 351 ; Portwood ™
vs. Outon , I B. M. , 149,
But in Overby vs. Gay , 17 B. M. , 144, the court refused to entertain jurisdiction
of an appeal by consent from an order overruling a motion to discharge an attach-
ment issued upon an ordinary petition ; no judgment having been rendered upon
the demand in the petition ; no order of sale having been made ; and no final
action of the court , determining the rights of the parties to any extent , having
been taken .
736 to 738 . SUMMONS AND FILING TRANSCRIPT .
a. Section 878 of the Code of 1854 required a summons of the appellee , unless he
entered his appearance , or unless the appeal was granted by the court below ;
and section 879 required the appellant to file a transcript of the record in the
clerk's office of the Court of Appeals within 90 days after granting the appeal ;
unless that court should extend the time for filing it , not, however , beyond the
first day of the second term of the said court after the granting of the appeal .
In Cobb & Wood vs. Waggoner , 17 B. M. , 562 , it was held that , if an appeal be
prayed from a judgment of the circuit court when rendered , and the record is
filed on or before the first day of the second succeeding term of the Court of
Appeals , no process is necessary . The appellee is bound to take notice of the
filing of the record , though the ninety days may have elapsed and no supersedeas
bond was given .
b. In Bixler's adm'r vs. Parker , & c., 3 Bush , 166, it was held that a failure to make
the motion to dismiss the appeal , because the record was not filed within the
time prescribed by law , until after the case was submitted on final hearing , must
be regarded as a waiver of the right .
c. In Finnell vs. Jones' ex'x , &c., 7 Bush, 359, the appeal was submitted , and the
judgment appealed from was reversed , though there had been no summons actual :
or constructive , and no appearance by the appellees . At a subsequent term the

court held that the appeal was prematurely heard , and so far as the opinion and
judgment affected the rights of the appellees they were void .
The court further ordered that the appeal will be regarded as still pending , and
will be set for trial at the next term ; and as the appellees have appeared in the
case no summons against them will be necessary . Finnell , & c., vs. Jones' ex'x, 7
Bush, 359.
742, 743. CERTIORARI AND SUBPOENA DUCES TECUM , FROM COURT OF
APPEALS .
8 744 .
8 745 .
8 748 .
& 749.
755-
a. In Franklin Academy vs. Hall, 16 B. M.; 474 , it was held that the Court of
Appeals will of their own mere motion award a certiorari to the clerk of the
inferior court where there is a strong presumption from the facts appearing in
the record that the clerk has copied a paper erroneously .
COSTS IN THE COURT OF APPEALS .
a . Though the appellant be insolvent the clerk of the circuit court is bound to copy:
the record for the Court of Appeals without payment or security for the fee .
Collins vs. Cleveland , 17 B. M. , 459 .
b. An attorney , prosecuting an appeal for a non-resident defendant , is not liable
under 616 for his costs in the Court of Appeals . Christmas vs. Russell , 2 Met . ,
I12 .
LIMITATION TO APPEALS .
a. It was held under ? 884 , Code of 1854, that an infant could not appeal , more than..
three years after the rendition of a joint decree against him and adults ; and
that this doctrine applied where infant plaintiffs and adult defendants united in
obtaining a decree for the sale of land in which they had a common interest
(Helm vs. Bentley , 1 Met ., 510) ; nor could a wife appeal , after three years , from a
joint judgment against her and her husband . Farlee vs. Rodes, 11 Bush, 365 .
Observe, that with reference to limitation ( 745) , as upon most other subjects (see
index , title " Married Women " ) , married women are free from disability .
SUPERSEDEAS BOND .
a. The bond is obligatory as to any judgment ordered by the Court of Appeals ,
though the judgment appealed from may be reversed . Hobbs vs. King, 2 Met ., 349 .
b. A successful appellee may sue on a supersedeas bond without issuing execution
on affirmed judgment ; but if he cause an execution to be issued and release a
lien thereby acquired , the surety in the supersedeas bond is released . Dills vs.
Cecil , 4 Bush, 579 .
c. A obtained a temporary injunction against a judgment , which was dissolved , with
costs and damages . A appealed from that order and , with B as surety , executed
bond pursuant to 2 887, and the order was affirmed ; held, that the surety in the
bond was not liable thereon for the amount of the judgment enjoined . Steele
vs. Wilson, 9 Bush, 699 .
ISSUING OF SUPERSEDEAS .
a . It was held under 2 889 of the Code of 1854 that the clerk of the Court of
Appeals could not issue a supersedeas , until a copy of the record was filed in
his office ; because that section expressly so declared , and it thus changed the
old practice . Hunt vs. Berryman , 2 Met ., 239 .
Observe that ? 749 of the new Code does not so declare , and that 2 734 entitles a
party to an appeal upon filing a copy of the judgment .
CROSS -APPEALS .
a. A cross -appeal is only allowed in behalf of the appellee against the appellant , and :
not against a co -appellee . Smith vs. N. Bank , 1 Met ., 575 ; Gar vs. Louisville
Bank & Co. , II Bush , 180.
