(Section 349. )
COURT OF APPEALS OF KENTUCKY .
John Smith, Appellant ,
against
The Commonwealth of Kentucky , Appellee .
I, Jacob Swigert , clerk of the Court of Appeals of Kentucky , do certify that the appel-
lant , John Smith , has taken an appeal from a judgment of the Franklin circuit court ,
rendered at its October term , 1877, in favor of the Commonwealth of Kentucky against him
for a fine of one thousand dollars and costs, and for his imprisonment in the Franklin county
jail for six months , and has executed a supersedeas bond agreeably to law , a transcript of
which , and of the record , has been filed in my office . Therefore , the execution of said
judgment is suspended .
Given under my hand , this 1st November , 1877.
John Smith , Appellant ,
against
J. SWIGERT , Clerk C. A.

Appeal Bond.

Franklin county , against said appellant for a fine of one hundred dollars and costs, now
if said judgment be affirmed we covenant to pay the costs of the appeal .
Witness cur hands , this 20th day of September , 1877. THOMAS JONES ,
GEORGE SMITH .

against

FRANKLIN CIRCUIT COURT .
}The Commonwealth of Kentucky , Appellee .

The forms detail the process of taking a person into custody until a fine and costs are satisfied.

HABEAS CORPUS .
Writ of Habeas Corpus .
(Section 402. )
The Commonwealth of Kentucky to Lewis Jackson , of the County of Mason :
We command you , that you have the body of Willis Strother , detained in your custody ,
as it is said , together with the day and cause of his being taken , at the court -house of the
county in which this writ is served , before the circuit judge of said county , on Monday, the
4th day of October , 1877, at the hour of nine o'clock of the forenoon of said day ; and that
you then and there state in writing the cause of detaining the said Willis Strother , and
produce your authority for so doing ; and hereof you are not to fail , under the penalties
denounced by law against those who disobey this writ , and to submit to and receive all those
things which shall then and there be adjudged in this behalf .
Witness my hand , as police judge of the town of East Maysville , this 2d day of October ,
1877. ISRAEL STICKLEY, P. J. E. M.

COMMONWEALTH OF KENTUCKY , COUNTY OF Mason .
The within writ having been served this day , but at such time as renders it impossible for
Lewis Jackson to obey the same at the time therein specified , the time for said Jackson to
produce the body of Willis Strother , and make return to said writ , is changed from the 4th
day of October , 1877, to the 6th day of October , 1877, at 10 o'clock in the forenoon .
JOHN HARRIS , Sheriff of Mason county .
Notice of the Change of Time for Return to the Writ, to be Given to the Person on Whose
Application it is Issued .
(Section 408. )
Robert Calvert , Esq .:
You are hereby notified that the time for the return to the writ of habeas corpus, issued on
your application , in behalf of Willis Strother , against Lewis Jackson , has been changed from
the 4th day of October , 1877, to the 6th day of October , 1877, at 10 o'clock , A. M. , as ap
pears from the indorsement made by me upon said writ , of which the following is a copy ,
viz : [here copy the indorsement ] .
JOHN HARRIS , Sheriff M. C.
Bond that the Person Detained shall not Escape , and for Payment of Costs.
(Section 404. )
A writ of habeas corpus having been applied for in behalf of Willis Strother , said to be
unlawfully detained by Lewis Jackson , we , Thomas Smith and Samuel Johnson , hereby cove-
nant with the Commonwealth of Kentucky , in the penal sum of $ ————————, that said Willis
Strother shall not escape while he is being brought to the place at which he is commanded to

classification.title: Writ of Habeas Corpus Forms
classification.summary: This document provides forms and procedures for issuing and managing a writ of habeas corpus in Kentucky. It includes examples of the writ itself, an indorsement to change the return time, notice of such change, and a bond for the detained person not to escape. The examples involve a writ issued on behalf of Willis Strother against Lewis Jackson, with a judge and sheriff involved in the process.
classification.reasoning: The provided text details the legal process and forms for a writ of habeas corpus. A writ of habeas corpus is a legal order that requires a person under arrest to be brought before a judge or into court. This is a fundamental legal protection against unlawful detention and is not inherently a Jim Crow law. The text does not contain any explicit or implicit racial indicators, nor does it suggest any discriminatory application. Therefore, it is classified as 'no' for Jim Crow. The document type is 'codes' as it appears to be part of a compiled legal code from 1883, specifically the Civil and Criminal Code.

classification.summary: This index entry outlines the procedures for appeals in criminal cases to the Court of Appeals in Kentucky, covering both felonies and misdemeanors. It details requirements for bills of exceptions, the effect of a defendant's death on an appeal, conditions for remaining in the penitentiary pending appeal, and proceedings upon affirmance or reversal of judgments. It also covers appeals taken by the Commonwealth and general provisions regarding the precedence and trial of criminal appeals. Additionally, it touches upon appeals to Circuit Courts, including when a defendant may appeal, the inability of the Commonwealth to appeal to circuit courts, and how appeals are taken and tried anew. Finally, it includes brief entries on arraignment and arrest.

NOTES TO CRIMINAL CODE .
I to 3. REPEAL OF FORMER LAWS .
a . It was held , under the same provisions of the Code of 1854, that the provision
giving the Commonwealth the right of peremptory challenge , applied , as well to
prosecutions commenced before , as to those commenced after , that Code went
into effect ; and that this , affecting proceedings only , was not an ex post facto law .
Watson vs. Commonwealth , 16 B. M. , 15.
b. The Code repeals all former laws as to penal and criminal proceedings and , since
its adoption , no prosecutor is necessary to an indictment . Commonwealth vs. Pat-
9, 11.
terson , 2 Met., 374.
It was held , however , that the Code of 1854 did not authorize an appeal from a
judgment rendered in a criminal case before it took effect . Commonwealth vs.
Craig , 15 B. M. , 534.
HOW FINE RECOVERABLE .
a. Fines are recoverable by indictment or penal action ; and the right of the Com-
monwealth so to recover a fine for usurpation of office , is not affected by the pro-
visions of 483 of the Civil Code . Commonwealth vs. Adams , 3 Met . , 6.
A proceeding by warrant to recover a
provisions of Title 16, Civil Code .
13, 14.
46 .
49.
fine in a justice's court must conform to the
Wilson vs. Commonwealth , 7 Bush, 536.
JURISDICTION OF COURTS .
a . Where the indictment charged the maintenance of a nuisance upon a public
highway , for such a length of time as to aggregate more than ten dollars fine ,
the circuit court has jurisdiction . Commonwealth vs. Mills , 6 Bush, 296 .
b. The jurisdiction of the circuit court of Henderson county , under this section ,
was held not to be affected by a subsequent statute giving exclusive jurisdiction
to the Henderson city court in all cases of misdemeanor arising within said city .
This provision of the Code will not be deemed repealed by a special local law
unless such an intention on the part of the Legislature is manifest . Common-
wealth vs. Pointer, 5 Bush , 301 .
ARREST WITHOUT WARRANT .
a. A magistrate has no authority to take a bail -bond for a prisoner brought before
him for examination of a charge of felony committed in another county ; nor has
he any right to take temporary bail for the appearance of the accused before him .
Commonwealth vs. Salyar , 8 Bush, 461 .
COMMITTING MAGISTRATES .
a. Judges of the county courts , judges of city or police courts , mayors , chairmen of
the trustees of towns , and justices of the peace are magistrates (? 26) ; and any
one of them may commit or hold to bail , except that, in cases of felony , if the
committing magistrate be a justice of the peace he must associate with him
NOTES-II

255 .
another justice (871 ) . Commonwealth vs. Cummins , 18 B. M. , 26 ; Rice vs. Com
monwealth , 3 Bush , 14.
b. One justice of the peace cannot act as an examining court , and commit to jail or
hold to bail , a person charged with felony ; another justice must be associated
with him . Revill vs. Pettit , 3 Met . , 314 .
But a single justice may commit or hold to bail in misdemeanor cases . Tharp vs.
Commonwealth , Id. , 411 .
DISPOSITION OF ACCUSED DURING ADJOURNMENT .
a . An examining court has no right , during the progress of the examination , to
admit to bail or release from custody , a party charged with felony ; and a bail-
bond taken for his appearance during the examination is unauthorized and void .
Commonwealth vs. Moore , 3 Met ., 477.
b. Writs or bail -bonds issued or taken on Sunday are valid . Rice vs. Commonwealth
3 Bush , 16 ; Watts vs. Commonwealth , 5 Bush , 309 .
8 67, 68.
871.
TAKING BAIL AFTER COMMITMENT .
a. A statute providing that the city court of Lexington " as to committing criminal
offenders , and sending them on for trial shall have the powers of two justices of
the peace of Fayette county ," does not authorize the clerk of that court to take
a bail -bond . Dugan vs. Commonwealth , 6 Bush , 305 .
b. The court having at the first term after the commitment of the accused fixed his
bail , a bail -bond taken by the county judge after the adjournment of the court
was held to be void . It should have been taken by the clerk . Branham vs.
Commonwealth , 2 Bush, 3.
But the court having adjourned without fixing the amount of bail , it was held that
two justices , the prisoner having been brought before them on habeas corpus , had
power to fix the amount of bail and take bond (see ? 423) . Creekmore vs. Com-
monwealth , 5 Bush, 312.
c. But the clerk cannot take bail unless the court has previously made an order
allowing bail , and fixing the amount . Wellenweber vs. Commonwealth , 3 Bush, 69.
HOW JUSTICE TO PROCEED IN FELONY CASES .
a. See notes to ? 49 .
66b. The term " larceny " does not under the Code necessarily import felony " ( 5,
6, 7) , and where it appeared that a bail bond was taken by a single justice , it
will be presumed that the case was one of petty larceny , and that the bond was
valid . Tharp vs. Commonwealth , 3 Met., 411 .
c. A single justice has no power to require or receive bail from one brought before
him charged with a felony , and such a bond is not good for any purpose . Com-
monwealth vs. Fisher, 2 Duv . , 376 ; Murphy vs. Commonwealth , 11 Bush , 219 .
8 72, 73. OFFICERS AUTHORIZED TO TAKE BAIL .
a. Magistrates . See 2 49 and notes .
b. Sheriffs . 28, 83 and notes , 143, 144.
c. Coroner . 283 .
d. Taking bail after commitment . 67, 68 and notes .
76, 80. QUALIFICATIONS OF BAIL .
a . Although the statute requires the surety in a bail -bond to be a resident of the
State , yet , if a non-resident be accepted , he will be bound . Commonwealth vs.
Ramsey , 2 Duv ., 385.

CRIMINAL CODE- NOTES TO SECS . 76 , 80 , 82 , 83 , 85 . 151
282.
283 .
285 .
b. Under an act of 1872, of which 80 is substantially a copy , it was held that , if
any of the persons named therein were accepted as bail , they would be bound
as such . Holandsworth vs. Commonwealth , 11 Bush , 617.
UNDERTAKING OF BAIL .
a . If it appear that the defendant was legally in custody , that he was discharged by
the giving of the bond , and it can be ascertained from the bond , that the bail
undertook that the defendant should appear before a court for trial , such bond
will be valid though it does not strictly conform to the provisions of this section .
Commonwealth vs. Cummins , 18 B. M. , 26.
But a bond which does not state the charge which the defendant is required to
appear and answer is void. Henry vs. Commonwealth , 4 Bush, 427 .
b. An undertaking that the defendant “ will render himself obedient to the orders
and process of this court," is equivalent to an undertaking , that he shall " at all
times render himself amenable, " &c. Miller vs. Commonwealth , I Duv ., 14.
c. A bond omitting the stipulation , " if he fails to perform either of these condi-
tions , that he will pay to the Commonwealth of Kentucky the sum of
dollars , " is nevertheless valid . Ibid: Commonwealth vs. O'Daniel , 9 Bush , 551 .
d. Where the minutes of the examining court recited , only, that the defendant and
his sureties " were duly recognized in the sum of $200 , conditioned as the law
directs :" held, that the recognizance was insufficient . The conditions should have
been set out in the recognizance . Roberts vs. Commonwealth , 7 Bush , 431 .
e. An indictment against A having been abated by the Commonwealth's Attorney
under the mistaken impression that the defendant was dead and having been
afterwards reinstated and the defendant arrested and bailed : held, that the abate-
ment terminated the prosecution and the bond was void . Henry vs. Common-
wealth , 4 Bush , 429 .
f. Where bail -bond was conditioned for the appearance of the defendant at the
ensuing May term and no court was held in May : held , that the sureties in the
bond were bound for the appearance of their principal at the next ensuing term
of the court . Commonwealth vs. Branch, 1 Bush, 59.
g. For other irregularities see ? 85 and notes .
WHEN SHERIFF MAY TAKE BAIL .
The sheriff arresting a person by authority of his bail is not authorized to take new
bail ; he can only take bail when he has made the arrest under a warrant, or
other process , in which it shall appear that the person is to be admitted to bail in
a specified sum . Schneider vs. Commonwealth , 3 Met., 409 .
IRREGULARITIES AS TO BAIL - BONDS .
a. A joint recognizance for the defendant's appearance to answer to an indictment
for burglary , and an indictment for grand larceny , both charges relating to
the same acts of the defendant , was held to be valid .
As in such case , there could be but one conviction and one punishment , the two
indictments are , in effect , no more than two counts in the same indictment .
Baird & Price vs. Commonwealth , 2 Duv ., 78 .
b. A defendant who voluntarily appears in court on the return of an indictment , is
in legal custody of the court , and a recognizance entered into by him and his
sureties , whilst in such custody , is valid . Baird & Price vs. Commonwealth , 2
Duv ., 78.
c. Where a bail -bond bore this indorsement : " M. W. King signed this bond in my
presence , on the date above ; it was left at the grocery of Mr. J. R. Covington
37

152 CRIMINAL CODE- NOTES TO SECS . 85 , 86 , 89 TO 92 , 93 TO 98 .
886 .
to get security ; it was returned to me by Mr. Covington with the names of these
six persons subscribed as surety ." Held, that the indorsement showed that the
bond was taken by an unauthorized person and was void . Covington vs. Com-
monwealth , 3 Bush , 478.
d. Where a blank sheet of paper was signed and left with the committing magis-
trate , with authority to him to fill it up as a bail -bond , and it was afterwards
filled up , not by him , but by the county attorney , it was held to be void . Com-
monwealth vs. Ball, 6 Bush, 292 .
e. A bond taken by an unauthorized officer is void under the statute and at the com-
mon law . Schneider vs. Commonwealth , 3 Met., 409 ; Commonwealth vs. Moore , Id.,
477 ; Same vs. Roberts , 1 Duv ., 199 ; Same vs. Fisher, 2 Id. , 376 .
And a bond taken by officers authorized to take bail -bonds , but under circum-
stances which did not authorize the taking of a bail -bond , was held to be void ;
as , a bond taken by two justices during adjournment of examination . Common-
wealth vs. Moore , 3 Met . , 477 .
f. The fact that an indictment was defective and liable to be quashed , does not ren-
der a recognizance void . Commonwealth vs. Skeggs, 3 Bush , 19.
g . For decisions as to irregularity of form see note to 282.
SURRENDER OF DEFENDANT .
a. Before the forfeiture of their bond , the bail may surrender the defendant to the
jailer , whose duty it will be to detain him in custody " as upon a commitment ."
He may then be admitted to bail in the mode prescribed by ? 68 and 81 of the
Criminal Code . Schneider vs. Commonwealth , 3 Met., 409 .
? 89 to 92. DEPOSIT OF MONEY IN LIEU OF BAIL .
a. Where money was deposited with the committing magistrate in lieu of bail and
the defendant thereupon discharged , and the money , after some days , was paid by
the magistrate to the trustee of the jury fund : held, that in so doing he acted
as the agent of the party making the deposit , and that upon defendant's failure
to appear the money was properly forfeited to the Commonwealth . Dean vs.
Commonwealth , 1 Bush, 20.
93 to 98. FORFEITURE , PROCEEDINGS , DISCHARGE , AND REMISSION OF
BAIL .
1. Forfeiture .
2. Proceedings .
3. Discharge .
4. Remission .
1. FORFEITURE .
a. Where a defendant in a criminal case had appeared in discharge of each of sev-
eral recognizances , and another is executed , and he fails to appear in discharge
of it , an order reciting the failure of the defendant “ to appear in discharge of
his recognizance , " and declaring the same forfeited , sufficiently refers to the
recognizance last executed , it being the only one then in force or obligatory upon
the bail . Tenney , &c., vs. Commonwealth , 3 Met . , 415 .
b. Failure to forfeit the bond at the appearance term operates to extend the time
for surrender of the principal by the surety . Commonwealth vs. Branch, 1 Bush,
59 .
c. That the defendant was absent in the Federal army at the time he was bound by
a recognizance to appear in court to answer an indictment , and was unable to
procure a furlough to attend the court, was a valid defense against the recogni-

entries.ocr_text:
CRIMINAL CODE- NOTES TO SECS . 93 TO 98. 153
zance ; and , though the defendant is not a competent witness in such a case in
behalf of his sureties , his statement under oath might be heard upon the motion
to remit the forfeiture . Commonwealth vs. Terry , 2 Duv ., 383.
d. The omission in the order of forfeiture to name the sureties , or to give the date
of the recognizance , is not a fatal defect , where the recognizance is otherwise
sufficiently identified . 3 Met . 415 ; Miller vs. Commonwealth , 1 Duv . , 14.
2. PROCEEDINGS .
a. A summons which recites the date and amount of the recognizance , and , by ref-
erence to the recognizance , sufficiently identifies the offense , is sufficient . Baird
& Price vs. Commonwealth , 2 Duv . , 78.
b. The appearance of the parties to a motion to set aside a judgment of forfeiture ,
and their agreement to set the hearing of it for a specified day , and a trial and
judgment on the merits , is a waiver of previous irregularities . Commonwealth vs.
Marks, 2 Duv ., 387.
c. In a proceeding against bail upon a forfeited recognizance all pleadings on the part
of the Commonwealth are dispensed with . The office of the summons is simply
to notify the bail that there has been a forfeiture of the bond , and that the Com-
monwealth is proceeding to enforce it. The failure to state in the summons the
amount specified in the bond is immaterial . In this case the recognizance was
entered into in the circuit court. Tenney , &c., vs. Commonwealth , 3 Mct. , 415 .
d. In an action upon a forfeited recognizance the defendant must make a written
statement of the facts constituting his defense . Without this the mere exhibi-
tion to the court of the evidence relied on by him , either to defeat or suspend the
action , will be disregarded ; and in such case the production of the respite of
the Governor , not relied on by pleading , constitutes no defense to the action .
The court is not authorized to take any judicial notice of the paper presented
in that way. Brown vs. Commonwealth , 4 Met ., 221.
e. A defendant indicted for felony , whose recognizance had been forfeited , surren-
dered himself to the court before a judgment was entered against the bail , who
thereupon moved to dismiss the proceeding upon the forfeited bond ; this , though
not a formal , was a substantial application for remission , and the motion having
been sustained by the court, must be deemed to have been an exercise of the
discretion vested in the judge by ? 98 of this Code . Commonwealth vs. Coieman ,
2 Met., 382.
f. It is not a sufficient defense by bail that his principal is under arrest in another
State . Withrow vs. Commonwealth , I Bush , 18.
g. The Code does not authorize the defendant in an indictment to enter into a
bail -bond or recognizance ; and such a bond or recognizance cannot be enforced
against him by the statutory remedies . Commonwealth vs. Radford , 2 Duv . , 9 .
h . An answer by bail in the Bourbon circuit court that the principal " was necessa-
rily prevented from appearing in said court on said [appearance ] day because he
was then on said day in the custody and control of the law of this State , and
legally confined by legal process in the jail of Carroll county , Kentucky , upon a
charge of felony, where he yet is , " was held to be insufficient . Kirby vs. Com
monwealth , I Bush , 113 ; but see Withrow vs. Commonwealth , Id., 18.
3. DISCHARGE .
a. A surety in a bail -bond cannot be made responsible for the failure of his principal
to appear when such appearance was prevented by the arrest of the principal by
a United States provost marshal . Commonwealth vs. Webster , I Bush, 616.
b. Sureties in a bail -bond are discharged by the re-arrest of the accused and his com-
mitment to jail from which he made his escape during the pendency of a second
examining trial for the same offense . Medlin vs. Commonwealth , 11 Bush , 605 .

entries.ocr_text:
154 CRIMINAL CODE- NOTES TO SECS . 93 TO 98 .
c. A release of the principal upon a writ of habeas corpus by a judge having jurisdic-
tion , though improper, discharges the bail . Commonwealth vs. Bronson , 14 B...
M. , 361.
d. Where the record shows that the defendant to an indictment for felony " appeared
in discharge of a recognizance " and was put upon trial , during the progress of
which he escaped ; it was held that his bail was discharged . Askins vs. Common-
wealth , I Duv ., 275 ; overruling Commonwealth vs. Coleman , 2 Met ., 382 .
Aliter as to misdemeanor cases . 1 Duv ., 275, supra .
But it was held , in a bastardy case , that where the defendant appeared in court
"to answer the charge " but was not surrendered , nor taken into custody , his
bail was not discharged . Wintersoll vs. Commonwealth , 1 Duv ., 177.
e. Pending a proceeding on a forfeited recognizance , by summons in the circuit
court , on motion of the defendant , who was surety in the recognizance , the court
quashed the indictment , and forthwith rendered judgment for execution for the
amount of the adjudged forfeiture . By quashing the indictment , the pending
prosecution was closed ; but the recognizance did not thereby become functus
officio , and the judgment of forfeiture was not thereby stifled . The quashal of
the indictment did not bar a continuance or renewed prosecution for the same
offense ; nor did it deprive the surety in the bail -bond of his right to surrender
or arrest the accused , and put him in the custody of the court , subject to trial
under such prosecution , and thus bring himself within the 98th section of the
Criminal Code . Little vs. Commonwealth , 3 Bush, 22.
f. The accused being under bond in the sum of $ 500 for his appearance , and having
failed to appear , a bench warrant issued with an indorsement allowing bail in the
sum of $250 : held, that these facts did not release the sureties in the bail -bond .
Commonwealth vs. Branch, I Bush, 59.
g. For discharge of bail by failure to indict , see 2 117 and notes .
4. REMISSION .
a. 2 98 of the Criminal Code merely confers a judicial discretion and power upon
the court to render judgment in an action on a bail -bond for such part of the
penalty of the bond as may be just and proper, after hearing proof of defend-
ant's facts in defense - not a power to remit a fine or forfeiture in the sense in
which those terms are used in the Constitution — and is therefore no infringement
of the rights of the executive department , and is not unconstitutional . This
discretion is to be exercised judicially upon consideration of the facts relied upon
in the defense , and is not an arbitrary and uncontrollable discretion . Common-
wealth vs. Thornton , 1 Met ., 380 ; Commonwealth vs. Coleman , &c., 2 Met ., 384 ;
Same vs. Rowland, 4 Met ., 225 .
b. Under 98 of the Criminal Code the court has power to remit the penalty of a
bail -bond , although a judgment had been rendered upon the bond , which was
set aside on the same day , and the remission then made . Commonwealth vs. Run-
nion & Hay, 3 Met ., 2.
c. After judgment is entered against bail the court has no power to remit the for-
feiture . Commonwealth vs. Radford , 2 Duv . , 9 .
d. The fact that the defendant had either been surrendered or arrested must be
alleged and shown in the defense before the court can remit. Commonwealth vs.
Rowland, 4 Met ., 225.
e. The discretion of the inferior court in remitting the penalty of a bail -bond will
not be controlled by the Court of Appeals unless flagrantly abused . Common-
wealth vs. Davidson , 1 Bush , 133 ; Same vs. Coleman , 2 Met ., 382 ; Same vs. Row
land , 4 Met., 225.
entries.citation: Civil and Criminal Codes of Practice of Kentucky, 1883, p. 603

CRIMINAL CODE- NOTES TO SECS . 101 , 112 , 113 , 117 , 122 TO 137. 155
ΙΟΙ .
f. The Governor may remit the whole forfeiture , including the per centage of the
attorney for the Commonwealth, at any time before a valid judgment against
the bail . Commonwealth vs. Ramsay , 2 Duv . , 385 ; Same vs. Spraggins , 18 B. M. ,
512.
g. That the Governor remits , not the forfeiture , but the judgment itself except as
to " fees and costs ," furnished no ground for setting aside the judgment so in
part remitted . Brown vs. Commonwealth , Met., 221.
FORMATION OF GRAND JURY .
a. The summoning of bystanders to serve as grand jurors , when properly ordered ,
is a duty in the performance of which the accused in a criminal case is entitled
to the services of the sheriff, or coroner where there is no sheriff. If summoned
by one specially appointed by the court, the indictment may be quashed . It is
a substantial error. Commonwealth vs. Graddy , 4 Met . , 223 .
b. Section 193 of the Criminal Code , which authorizes the court , for sufficient cause ,
to designate some other officer or person to summons jurors , relates only to petit
jurors . Id.
c. Where the grand jury is composed of jurors selected by commissioners , as directed
by the Revised Statutes , the court say they incline to the opinion that the sum-
moning of them by a person other than the sheriff or coroner , would not be " a
substantial error." Id.
d. An order of the circuit court showing that the grand jury was empanneled , the
Court of Appeals will presume that it was regularly empanneled , though the
order did not show that the court appointed a foreman , or that the jurors were
sworn . Commonwealth vs. Pullan , 3 Bush, 47.
e. That a civil officer , to -wit , a trustee of the public schools , was a member of the
grand jury is no ground for setting aside an indictment . Commonwealth vs.
Pritchett , II Bush , 277.
112, 113.
117.
PROCEEDINGS TO BE KEPT SECRET .
The testimony of a grand juror is not admissible to explain or impeach their find-
ing . Commonwealth vs. Skeggs, 3 Bush, 19.
DISCHARGE OF DEFENDANT IF NO INDICTMENT FOUND .
a. The Code of 1854, 116, provided that if the grand jury failed to indict at the
term next after the submission of the charge to them , the defendant should be
discharged , " unless for cause shown " the court should otherwise direct . It was
held under this section that the cause and direction must appear upon the record
or bail would be exonerated . Rion vs. Commonwealth , I Duv . , 235 ; Common-
wealth vs. Roberts, 4 Met ., 219 ; but aliter under this Code (? 117, 115) as to the
necessity for showing in the order the cause of the action of the court .
b. Where the grand jury fails to indict , bail is exonerated though no order be
entered discharging the bond . Commonwealth vs. Roberts, 4 Met . , 219 ; Common-
wealth vs. Blincoe , 3 Bush , 12 ; Bryant vs. Commonwealth , 3 Bush , 9 .
122 to 137. FORM AND REQUISITES OF INDICTMENTS .
1. Form of indictments . Page 156.
2. Name of defendant . Page 156.
3. Joinder of offenses. Page 156.
4. Certainty in indictments . Page 156.
5. Averments under statutes containing provisos and exceptions. Page 158.
6. Unnecessary allegations in indictments . Page 158.

classification.title: CRIMINAL CODE - NOTES TO SECS. 101, 112, 113, 117, 122 TO 137.

156 CRIMINAL CODE- NOTES TO SECS . 122 TO 137 .
7. Averments in indictments for obtaining money, & c. , under false pretenses. Page 158.
8. Averments in indictments under statutes describing offenses. Page 159.
9. Averments in indictments generally . Page 161.
1. FORM OF INDICTMENTS .
a . Though an indictment be founded on a statute it is not necessary , since the adop-
tion of the Code of Practice , that it should conclude , " against the form of the
statute ." Under the Code an indictment is sufficient if it shows intelligibly the
offense intended to be charged and is not deemed insufficient by reason of any
defect which does not tend to the prejudice of the substantial rights of the
defendant on the merits . Commonwealth vs. Kennedy , 15 B. M. , 531 .
b. It is not necessary to the validity of an indictment that it should show upon its
face that it had been presented on oath by the grand jury , and , in cases of felony ,
that the word "feloniously " should be used in charging the intent with which
the offense was committed . Jane (a slave) vs. Commonwealth , 3 Met . , 18.
c. The clerk's indorsement , describing the character of the indictment , forms no
part of the indictment . Commonwealth vs. English , 6 Bush, 431 .
2. NAME OF DEFENDANT .
a. The omission to set out the first or Christian name of the defendant in the indict-
ment will not vitiate it. Commonwealth vs. Kelcher , 3 Met ., 484.
3. JOINDER Of offenses .
a. An indictment for murder contained three paragraphs . The first charged that
the murder was committed by five defendants jointly ; the second, that H com-
mitted the murder , and that the other defendants " did , with malice afore--
thought , counsel , advise , and persuade the said H to kill and murder " the-
deceased ; the third, that T committed the murder, and that the other defend-
ants in like manner counseled and advised the commission of the act . Held,
that there is no misjoinder of offenses ; that but one offense is set forth in the
indictment , though charged to have been committed in different modes . That ,
although the particular agency of each defendant is unnecessarily stated , it does
not vitiate the indictment . Thompson vs. Commonwealth , 1 Met ., 13.
b. It was held that an indictment , charging " that the defendant unlawfully and
without felonious intent destroyed , injured , and carried away timber from four
trees on the premises of John W. Wash , which said property belonged to the
said Wash , and was taken without his consent ," was sufficient ; that the indict-
ment charged but one offense , that of unlawfully taking and carrying away the
timber of four trees belonging to Wash ; the charge of carrying away necessarily
involving the taking of the timber ; the words " destroyed " and " injured , ” as
used in the indictment , if not appropriately descriptive of that offense , being
too general and indefinite to constitute with sufficient certainty either of the
other offenses intended to be embraced by 7, art . 25, chap . 28, of the Revised .
Statutes . Commonwealth vs. Powell , 8 Bush, 6 .
c. See 168 as to misjoinder .
4. CERTAINTY IN INDICTMENTS .
a. An indictment , found in May , 1865, averred the commission of an offense in
July , 1865, but also averred that the offense was committed before the finding of
the indictment : Held sufficient . Commonwealth vs. Jones , Bush, 34.
b. An indictment for burglary is sufficient in describing the house entered as that
of A B. It is not necessary to state the street and number . Olive vs. Common-
wealth , 5 Bush, 377.
c. A warrant under an ordinance prohibiting the sale of liquor on Sunday, is
sufficient , without averring the quantity sold , on the place where it was drunk .
Megowan vs. Commonwealth , 2 Met ., 3.

entries.ocr_text:
CRIMINAL CODE- NOTES TO SECS . 122 TO 137 . 157
d. In an indictment for murder the person murdered was described as " Barbara
Kriel , the wife of the defendant , " and upon the trial it appeared that her name
was Margaret Kriel . Held to be an error of description which did not vitiate
the indictment . Kriel vs. Commonwealth , 5 Bush , 375 .
e. An indictment must set forth the offense with such certainty as to apprise the
defendant of the nature of the accusation upon which he is to be tried , and to
constitute a bar to any subsequent proceeding for the same offense .
An indictment which charges that the defendant suffered certain named persons
"to play in a house or on premises in the county aforesaid , then in the occupa-
tion and under the control of the said P, a game of cards , at which game of
cards , played as aforesaid , money or property was won and lost ," was held insuffi-
cient upon demurrer because in the alternative . Commonwealth vs. Perrigo , 3
Met., 5.
But an indictment charging that the defendant carried concealed deadly weap-
ons , to -wit : a bowie -knife , and also a dirk or dagger , is sufficient , and it is not
necessary , in order to conviction , to prove that he carried both a bowie -knife
and a dirk or dagger . Commonwealth vs. Howard, 3 Met., 407 .
f. An indictment charging that the defendant “ did unlawfully sell whisky and brandy ,
and other spirituous liquors , &c. ," lacks the ordinary certainty required by the
Criminal Code, to give the defendant notice of the particular charge constituting
his offense . Commonwealth vs. White , 18 B. M. , 492 .
g. An indictment charging, in substance , that the defendant did " peddle and sell "
in the county of M. , buggies , pleasure carriages , &c ., not having a license to
peddle the same , and said articles not being the product or manufacture of the
State of Kentucky , is insufficient for uncertainty . Commonwealth vs. Dudley, 3
Met., 222.
h . An indictment charging that defendant " did feloniously tender in payment to
Mrs. Margaret Boyle an altered bank bill of the Dayton Bank " is bad for uncer-
tainty . The bill and the alteration should have been described with such cer-
tainty as to enable the defendant to plead the indictment and judgment in bar
of a subsequent prosecution . Mount vs. Commonwealth , ▾ Duv . , 90 .
-
. An indictment charging that " the said William White heretofore , to -wit : on the
day of April , 1871, in the Commonwealth and county aforesaid , did unlaw-
fully , willfully , maliciously , feloniously , and of his malice aforethought kill and
murder his wife , one Nancy White , against the peace and dignity of the Com-
monwealth of Kentucky ," was held to be insufficient , because it wholly failed to
state the particular facts which were necessary to show the manner in which the
crime may have been committed , or the means of its perpetration . White vs.
Commonwealth , 9 Bush, 178.
j. 131 of the Code of 1854, which was omitted in this Code , provided that , if the
indictment contained no statement of the place in which the offense was com-
mitted , it should be considered as charged in the indictment that it was commit-
ted within the local jurisdiction of the court. Under that section it was held
that an indictment charging the renting of a house to be kept as a bawdy -house
was sufficient without describing its locality . Harlow vs. Commonwealth , 11 Bush,
610 .
k. It was held , under the Code of 1854, that an indictment , charging the defendant
with having stolen bank notes , was insufficient , because it did not describe their
denomination , number , or value . Rhodus vs. Commonwealth , 2 Duv ., 159. But
see 135 of this Code.
7. It was held , under the Code of 1854, that an indictment for larceny must aver
the ownership of the stolen property , or an excuse for the omission , as that the
owner is to the grand jurors unknown . Reed vs. Commonwealth , 7 Bush , 642 .

158 CRIMINAL CODE- NOTES TO SECS . 122 TO 137 .
Also that an averment that property stolen was " the property of the Tennessee
River Packet Company , D. W. Swan , Little Brothers , and others ," was suffi
ciently certain . Reed vs. Commonwealth , 7 Bush, 643 .
Also that a conviction on an indictment for stealing Stephen Daniel's hog , upon
proof that the defendant stole Philip Daniel's hog, was erroneous . Hensley vs.
Commonwealth , 1 Bush , 11.
Also that a mistake in spelling , in the indictment , the name of the person robbed ,
was immaterial . Olive vs. Commonwealth , 5 Bush, 379.
128 of this Code , extending the provisions of 127 of that Code , declares that
an erroneous allegation as to the ownership of the property taken shall not be
material, provided the offense be otherwise described with sufficient certainty .
5. AVERMENTS UNDER STATUTES CONTAINING PROVISOS AND EXCEPTIONS .
a . It is well settled that where provisos and exceptions are contained in distinct
clauses of a statute defining an offense , it is not necessary to aver in the indict-
ment that the defendant does not come within the exceptions , or to negative the
provisos it contains . Nor is it necessary to allege that he is not within such
provisos , even though the purview should expressly notice them ; as by saying
that none shall do the act prohibited , except in the cases thereinafter excepted .
These are properly matters of defense . Commonwealth vs. McClanahan , 2 Met ., 8.
b. An indictment charging that the defendant " having a wife then living did unlaw-
fully marry Laurena Farley " is sufficient . It is not necessary to aver that he
was not within any of the saving clauses of the statute . Commonwealth vs.
Whaley , 6 Bush, 266.
6. UNNECESSARY ALLEGATIONS IN INDICTMENTS .
a. In a warrant , for sale of liquor on Sunday , it was averred that the defendant sold
liquor " on Sunday, the 10th day of February , 1859. " Held, that the averment
of the day of the month was surplusage ; and that proof of sale , on any Sunday
previous to the issuing of the warrant , was sufficient to authorize conviction .
Megowan vs. Commonwealth , 2 Met ., 3 .
b. Where a person or thing necessary to be mentioned in an indictment , is described
with circumstances of greater particularity than is requisite , yet those circum-
stances must be proved , otherwise it would not appear that the person or thing is
the same described in the indictment . Clark vs. Commonwealth , 16 B. M. , 213 ;
Commonwealth vs. Magowan , I Met., 368 .
But the decisions in the cases of Clark vs. Commonwealth and Commonwealth vs.
Magowan , and the authorities relied on in support of them , go no farther than
to establish the doctrine that " no allegation , whether necessary or unnecessary ,
which is descriptive of the identity of that which is legally essential to the charge
in the indictment , can ever be rejected as surplusage ;" and allegations of value
in an indictment are in no proper sense descriptive of identity , and need not be
proved as laid . They serve , in general , only to distinguish between different
degrees of the offense of larceny . Commonwealth vs. Garland, 3 Met ., 478 .
7. AVERMENTS IN INDICTMENTS FOR OBTAINING MONEY , &C. , UNDER FALSE PRE-
TENSES .
a . In prosecutions for obtaining money or goods under false pretenses , the indict-
ment must contain all the material facts and circumstances necessary to be proved
upon the trial in order to convict . It will not do to charge that the defendant
obtained the goods or money by false pretenses , without stating what the false
pretenses were . And some care must be observed in setting forth the pretenses ,
because they must be proved as laid . Glacken , etc., vs. Commonwealth , 3 Met. , 232 .
b. It is essential to a conviction for obtaining property or money under false pre-
tenses to allege and prove that the pretense , whereby the money or property was

CRIMINAL CODE- NOTES TO SECS . 122 TO 137. 159
obtained , was a statement of some pretended past occurrence or existing fact,
made for the purpose of inducing the party injured to part with his property .
No statement or representation of anything to take place in future is a pretense
within the meaning of the statute , whether it be in the form of a promise or not .
Ib., 232.
c. Where the false pretenses charged were , that , if the owner would permit the
defendants to take her cow and calf to market and sell the same , they would
account for and pay over to the owner, or her daughter for her , the money
received therefor ; and that they , the defendants , would at their own costs and
expense remove the owner from the house in which she then resided , and pro-
vide her with another house free of rent ; the indictment was held insufficient ,
the promises being to do future acts . Ib. , 232 .
d. The offense of " feloniously obtaining money by false pretenses " consists in
obtaining the money, with a fraudulent intent ; the false pretense employed is
only the means by which the offense is perpetrated . Therefore , where V sold
and delivered to J a negro man , in the State of Ohio , falsely representing him to
be a slave , and received the purchase and executed the bill of sale in Kentucky ,
and the negro turned out to be a free man , the court in Kentucky had jurisdic-
tion to try V for the offense of feloniously obtaining money by false pretenses , as
the offense of so obtaining the money was committed in Kentucky . Commonwealth
vs. Van Tuyl, 1 Met., I.
e. An indictment for obtaining a note by false pretenses , charged , in substance , that
the defendant falsely represented to his creditor , that he had a specified quantity
of tobacco , worth 14 cents per pound ; that it was of a particular description and
quality , according to a sample which he exhibited ; and that , by these false pre-
tenses and representations , with intent to cheat and defraud the creditor , the
defendant obtained , and the creditor was induced to deliver him , a note which
he held upon the defendant , upon which there was a balance due of $ 122 64,
and to credit said note for said sum ; when in truth said lot of tobacco was not a
good article of tobacco , and not of the quality and description of the sample ,
etc. , and was of no value , which the defendant knew . Held, that the facts
charged did not amount to a false pretense within the meaning of the statute .
Commonwealth vs. Haughey , 3 Met ., 223.
8. AVERMENTS IN INDICTMENTS UNDER STATUTES DESCRIBING OFFENSES .
a . An indictment for robbery , which followed the language of the statute (22 , art.
5, chap . 28, R. S. ) was held to be sufficient , although it did not describe the
property alleged to have been taken , nor aver that the assault was made against
the will of the person robbed . Commonwealth vs. Tanner, 5 Bush , 316 .
b. An indictment charging that the defendant " did , with force and arms , feloni-
ously assault , beat , shake , and hold , and restrain Susan L. Rogers , with his
hands and arms ; and , with a large knife and weapon , putting her , the said
Susan , in great fear of some serious and immediate injury to her life and limb ,
with the felonious intent to rob her, the said Rogers , contrary , " & c. , was held to
be sufficient ; though the offense was not charged in the precise words of the
statute . Taylor vs. Commonwealth , 3 Bush , 508 .
c. Under the statute providing that " if any person shall maliciously shoot at and
wound another , with an intention to kill him , so that he does not die thereby ,
with a gun or other instrument , loaded with a leaden bullet , or other hard sub-
stance ," it was held , that an indictment which charged that the defendant ,
" with a certain pistol , which he then and there had and held , feloniously and
maliciously shot and wounded one John Bailey , then and there being , with the
intent then and there to kill and murder the said John Bailey ;" but failed to

allege that the person wounded did not die thereby , and that the pistol was
loaded with a leaden bullet or other hard substance , was sufficient , either upon
demurrer or upon motion to arrest the judgment . Burns vs. Commonwealth , 3
Met., 13.
99
d. An indictment charging defendant with permitting " a game of chance called
pigeon-hole " to be played for " greenbacks was held to be sufficient on demur-
rer , the demurrer admitting it to be a game of chance . Commonwealth vs. Bran-
ham , 3 Bush , I.
e. An indictment charging " malicious mischief, in the shooting and killing by
appellee of the sheep of Allen Collins , willfully and unlawfully , without the
owner's consent , " was held to be sufficient under 7, art . 25, ch . 28, R. S. ,
which prohibited the " unlawful " injury of personal property . Commonwealth
vs. Smith , 6 Bush , 263 .
j. An indictment charging the defendant with being the owner of a ten-pin alley ,
at which games were played , and money bet, won and lost , and that he and
others played at such games , was held to be sufficient without an averment that
defendant knew of such betting . Commonwealth vs. Fraize , 5 Bush, 325 .
g. An act of 1864 ( Myers ' Sup . , 247) declared that if any person , being the owner
of a billiard -table , " where money or any thing of value is bet ," shall suffer a
minor , without the permission of his parent, & c., to play a game thereon , he
shall be fined , & c. In indictment under this statute charging the defendant with
permitting a minor to play on defendant's billiard -table , " kept for hire and
profit , " was held to be sufficient ; the court saying that a reversal was sought
" only because the indictment does not explicitly charge that money was bet or
lost ' on the game played by the minor . " Green vs. Commonwealth , 5 Bush, 328 .
Quere : An averment of betting by the minor was clearly unnecessary ; but it
seems equally clear that the indictment was defective in not averring that the
table was one " where money or any thing of value is bet ."
h. Under 19, ch . 42 , R. S. , which made it a penal offense to keep any billiard -table ;
and an amendment of 1858, which permitted private tables " not kept or used
for hire or profit ; " it was held that an indictment , for keeping a billiard -table ,
must aver that it was kept for hire or profit . Hoit vs. Commonwealth , 2 Bush, 33 .
And see Ritte vs. Commonwealth , 18 B. M. , 35 .
i. When the words of the statute are descriptive of the offense , the indictment
should follow the language of the statute : thus , a statute having made it an
offense to kill any horse " willfully ," an indictment , charging the defendant with
having killed a horse " unlawfully , " was held to be defective . Commonwealth vs.
Turner , 8 Bush , 2.
j. An indictment , charging a sale of liquor to a white minor " without the written
consent and request of the father, " & c., is defective under the statute forbidding
such sale without the written consent or request , " & c. Commonwealth vs. Hod-
craft, 6 Bush , 91 .
k. A statute having imposed a penalty for selling liquor to a white person under
twenty -one years of age , an indictment , charging the defendant with having sold
liquor to a person under twenty -one years of age , was , on demurrer held to be
defective , because it did not state that the said person was white . Commonwealth
vs. Ewing , 7 Bush, 105.
7. Under the statute prohibiting the counterfeiting of the notes " of any bank or
company incorporated by law in any part of the United States ," it was held that
an indictment , charging the counterfeiting of notes of " the Merchants ' Bank of
Massachusetts ," was defective , because it did not aver that the bank was incor-
porated. Kennedy vs. Commonwealth , 2 Met ., 36.

classification.summary: This section of the Criminal Code provides notes and case law interpretations for sections 122 to 137, focusing on the sufficiency of indictments for various criminal offenses. It covers cases related to assault, gambling, malicious mischief, and the sale of liquor. Notably, it discusses the requirement for indictments to precisely follow statutory language, including specifying the race of individuals in certain liquor sale offenses.

CRIMINAL CODE- NOTES TO SECS . 122 TO 137 . 16г
# it being a con-m. An indictment charging the keeping of a " keno -table ,
trivance for betting , at which and whereby money and property was bet, won
and lost ," was held insufficent under chap . 16, 6, R. S. , because the statute did
not denounce keno -tables by name , and the indictment failed to charge that they
were contrivances ordinarily used for purposes of gaming . Commonwealth vs.
Monarch , 6 Bush , 268. Acc . , Ritte vs. Commonwealth , 18 B. M. , 35.
But , an indictment charging the keeping of a " faro-bank, " was held to be suffi
cient, without an averment that it was ordinarily used for purposes of gaming ;
because faro -banks were expressly denounced by the statute . Commonwealth vs.
Monarch , 6 Bush, 301.
n . Under 21 , art . 17, chap . 29, of the General Statutes , providing that any person
who should , in sudden heat and passion , " cut , thrust , or stab any other person
with a knife , dirk , sword , or other deadly weapon , " it was held that an indict-
ment charging that the defendant did " strike and wound George Gregory with a
pair of blacksmith's tongs , which were then and there a deadly weapon ," was
defective . Commonwealth vs. Hawkins , II Bush , 603 .
Under the statute making it penal to " maliciously shoot at and wound another," it
was held that an indictment , charging that the defendant " willfully and with
malice aforethought shot at Asa Oliver , with intent to kill him , and , missing his
aim , wounded W. T. Buntin ," was insufficient . Commonwealth vs. Morgan, 11
Bush, 601.
An allegation that the defendant aided another to escape " beyond the custody of
the jailer of Scott county " is not equivalent to an allegation that defendant
aided him to escape from said custody ; and is insufficient under ? 5, art . 16,
chap . 29, General Statutes . Tully vs. Commonwealth , 11 Bush , 161.
9. AVERMENTS IN INDICTMENTS GENERALLY .
a. Murder . An indictment , found by a grand jury charged in substance , that the
said Jane , the slave of Ed . Smith , in the county aforesaid , on the 24th day of
December , 1857, did willfully and maliciously , and with malice aforethought,
kill and murder one Jane Porter , by administering to her a deadly and corrosive
and destructive poison , called strychnine , from the effects of which the said Jane
Porter died, &c.: held, upon motion in arrest of judgment , that the indictment
was sufficient ; though it did not charge that death occurred within a year and a
day after the poison was administered , or that the prisoner knew that strychnine
was poisonous ; and though it attributed the malice to the killing and not to the
administration of the poison . Jane vs. Commonwealth , 3 Met ., 18.
b. Burgiary . In an indictment for burglary , it is necessary to aver a breaking with
intent to rob, but where the indictment charges a breaking and robbery, that is a
sufficient averment of intent . Olive vs. Commonwealth , 5 Bush, 376 .
It is not necessary in such an indictment to charge that the breaking was done
'burglariously ." Ibid.66
c. Robbery. An indictment charging the defendant with having taken a mare , the
property of R, " in his presence , and against his will, by putting him in fear of
some immediate injury to his person ," contains facts sufficient to constitute rob-
bery at the common law . Commonwealth vs. Brooks , 1 Duv ., 150.
Assault with intent to rob. An indictment charging an assault with intent to rob is
good without averring any fact showing such intent . Dickerson vs. Commonwealth ,
2 Bush, I.
d. Duelling . An indictment against one for challenging another to fight in single
combat with deadly weapons , is sufficient , although it does not aver that the
paper therein copied and averred to have been meant and intended by the former

162 CRIMINAL CODE- NOTES TO SECS . 122 TO 137 .
as a challenge , was so understood by the parties . Such an averment was unnec
essary . Moody vs. Commonwealth , 4 Met . , 1 ; Heffren vs. Commonwealth , 4 Met., 5 .
e. Bribery. A charge in an indictment for bribery at an election that defendant
" bribed " another to vote is sufficient without further description of the act , the
meaning of the word " bribe " being defined in the statute . Commonwealth vs.
Stephenson, 3 Met . , 226.
That an election was held at the time and place stated in an indictment for bribery ,
is sufficiently alleged by the averment that the person charged to have been
bribed did , for said bribe , vote for the several persons and for the several officers
therein named . Nor is it necessary to allege that the persons voted for were can-
didates for the offices . Ibid.
f. Receiving illegal vote. An indictment charging that the defendant , as one of the
judges of an election , " knowingly and unlawfully " received the vote of one not
entitled to vote , is sufficient , without showing whether the sheriff or other judge
was in favor of, or opposed to , allowing the vote to be cast . Commonwealth vs.
Gray, 2 Duv ., 373 .
g. Betting on elections. It is necessary , in an indictment for betting on an election ,
that a certain individual would or would not be elected , to aver that the individ-
ual was a candidate , or was proposed or voted for , for office . Commonwealth vs.
Shouse, 16 B. M. , 329 .
h. Disorderly house. An indictment charging that defendant " did suffer and permit
an indecent and disorderly house to be kept on his plantation or premises ," was
held insufficient, because it does not charge directly or inferentially that defend-
ant kept the house , or had leased it to another , knowing the purposes for which
it was to be used , or that the house was in the occupancy or control of the defend-
Taylor vs. Commonwealth , 1 Duv ., 160 .ant.
i. Extortion . In an indictment against a jailer , for extorting money from a person
in his custody , it is not necessary to aver that such person was lawfully impris
oned . Commonwealth vs. Mitchell , 3 Bush , 25.
j. Escape . In an indictment against a jailer for voluntarily and willfully permitting
the escape of a prisoner , allegations that a peace officer , who had the prisoner in
his custody , delivered him to the appellee , a jailer , with the order of commit-
ment , made out in writing , signed by the magistrate who rendered the judgment ,
in the manner prescribed by ? 67 , Criminal Code , was held to be sufficient ; and
whether the offense , with which the prisoner was charged , was a felony or a
misdemeanor , it was , nevertheless , the duty of the jailer to take him into his
custody , and keep him safely until he was lawfully discharged . Commonwealth
vs. Mitchell, 3 Bush, 30 .
1. Exhibiting theatrical performance .
k. Keeping unclean jail. A jailer cannot be proceeded against by indictment for
failing to keep the jail clean , & c. Commonwealth vs. Mitchell , 3 Bush , 39.
An indictment charging that the defendant
" unlawfully exhibited a theatrical performance " is not good . It should aver
the facts showing it to be unlawful . Pike vs. Commonwealth , 2 Duv ., 89.
m. Selling by sample . An indictment charging the defendant with unlawfully selling
by sample , was held to be insufficient , because it did not charge that the sale was
without license . Mork vs. Commonwealth , 6 Bush , 397 .
n. Tippling -house. An indictment charging the defendant with "keeping a tip-
pling -house " was held to be sufficient , such an averment necessarily importing
the absence of license . Commonwealth vs. Campbell , 5 Bush, 311. Acc ., Com-
monwealth vs. Harvey , 16 B. M. , 1.
0. Accessory. An indictment against an accessory must charge by proper averments
the guilt of the principal . Tully vs. Commonwealth , 11 Bush , 154.

CRIMINAL CODE — NOTES TO SECS . 140 , 141 , 151 , 157 , 158 , 159 , 168. 163
140, 141.
8151 .
PROCESS AGAINST DEFENDANT .
a . Upon an indictment being found , if the defendant is not in custody or on bail ,
the court should forthwith order process , either a bench warrant or a summons ;
but until such order is made , the clerk has no authority to issue process ; and if
he does , it will confer on the sheriff no authority to arrest the defendant or to
take bail . A motion for judgment on a bail -bond taken in such case should be
dismissed . Shaw , &c., vs. Commonwealth , 1 Duv ., 1.
CONTRADICTION BY PARTY OF HIS OWN WITNESS .
It was held in the case of Champ vs. Commonwealth , 2 Met . , 22 , without citing any
provision of the Criminal Code , that 660 of the Civil Code of 1854, which is
the same as 596 of the Civil Code of 1876, relating to the contradiction by a
party of his own witness , applied to criminal cases . For decisions on that sub-
ject see notes to 596 of the Civil Code .
2157. DEFENDANT MUST PLEAD , OR MOVE TO SET ASIDE INDICTMENT .
a. Upon an indictment for a misdemeanor , a judgment may be rendered against the
defendant by default . Commonwealth vs. Cheek, 1 Duv ., 26 .
2158 .
159.
168.
b. On an indictment for misdemeanor , the defendant may be tried in his absence ,
and may , by counsel , put in any plea, save that of guilty . This right cannot be
taken from him by requiring bail for his appearance . The object of bail in mis-
demeanor is simply to secure compliance with the judgment when rendered .
Johnson vs. Commonwealth , I Duv ., 244.
c. The right to make a motion to set aside an indictment is waived by the defend-
ant if not made at the proper time and in the appropriate mode (C. C. , ? 158) .
Commonwealth vs. Conner et al., 10 Bush, 476 .
Whenever an indictment for misdemeanor is called for trial the defendant ought
either to move to set it aside or plead thereto ; and by failing to do either the
defendant loses his opportunity to plead as matter of right , and should thereafter
be refused leave to plead any other than a meritorious plea in bar . Ib .
d. Objection to the indictment because one of the grand jurors was not a house-
keeper , not having been made until the second term after the defendants were
before the court on process , came too late , and ought not to have been enter-
tained . Ib.
GROUNDS FOR SETTING ASIDE INDICTMENT .
a . An order of court, showing the presentment of an indictment for " sheep -steal-
ing ," is a sufficient identification of an indictment for grand larceny “ by taking ,
carrying away , and converting to his own use seven sheep , " which was filed on
the day on which the order was made , as appeared from the clerk's indorsement
on the indictment . Commonwealth vs. English , 6 Bush , 431 .
PROCEEDINGS WHEN INDICTMENT SET ASIDE .
a. An order of court setting aside an indictment , and directing that the defendant
stand upon his bond , pending a re -submission to another grand jury , did not
release the bail . Brewer vs. Commonwealth , 3 Bush , 550.
b. See 279 and notes thereto .
MISJOINDER OF OFFENSES .
a. See note 3 to sections 122 to 137, page 155.

164 CRIMINAL CODE — NOTES TO SECS . 170 , 176 , 177 , 188 , 189 , 203 , 204.
170. RE -SUBMISSION OF CASE TO GRAND JURY AFTER SUSTAINING
DEMURRER TO INDICTMENT .
a. An erroneous judgment for the defendant , sustaining a demurrer to an indict-
ment upon the ground that it contains a misjoinder of offenses , does not operate
as a bar to a future prosecution , and may be reversed by the Court of Appeals .
Commonwealth vs. Anthony , 2 Met. , 399.
176, 177. ACQUITTAL OR CONVICTION BARS ANOTHER PROSECUTION .
a. By the same act , and with the same intent , F took a horse , wagon , and harness ,
the property of H. Two indictments were found against F-one for stealing
the horse , the other for stealing the wagon and harness . Held that an acquittal
on one indictment was a bar to proceedings on the other. Fisher vs. Common-
wealth , I Bush, 211.
184.
b. A fine for a breach of the peace , assessed under the statute for suppressing riots ,
routs , unlawful assemblies , and breaches of the peace , is a legal bar to a subse-
quent indictment against the same party for an assault and battery . Common-
wealth vs. Foster , 3 Met., 1.
c. M was indicted , tried , convicted , and sentenced to the penitentiary ; the judg
ment was appealed from , the indictment held defective , and a subsequent
indictment having been found against him , he pleaded the former conviction .
Held, that the plea was not good . Mount vs. Commonwealth , 2 Duv ., 93.
The action of the court in withdrawing a juror , and substituting another in his
place , against the objections of the defendant , is erroneous ; and a verdict and
judgment , though erroneous , and set aside , bar further proceedings against
the defendant . O'Brian vs. Commonwealth , 9 Bush, 333 ; overruling the same
case in 6 Bush , 563 .
But the dismissal of an indictment , even after the jury was sworn , does not bar
a second indictment . Wilson vs. Commonwealth , 3 Bush, 105.
d. Where a licensed tavern -keeper has been presented and fined $300 for a breach
of his obligation for the performance of his duties as tavern -keeper , his surety
not being a party , and not notified of the proceeding , the latter , in a subse-
quent motion for a judgment against him , is not affected by the judgment
against his principal , but may contest the breach of the obligation ; and he is
entitled to ten days ' notice , and to a trial of the issue by a jury . Magorly vs.
Commonwealth , 3 Met ., 405 .
e. A plea of pardon or remission by the Governor , after indictment , is a good plea
in bar . Commonwealth vs. Bush, 2 Duv ., 264 .
TRIAL OF MISDEMEANORS IN DEFENDANT'S ABSENCE .
a. See notes to 157.
188, 189. POSTPONEMENT OF TRIAL .
a. An affidavit of a defendant for a continuance being admitted to be true , he has
a right to have it read as evidence to the jury (Young vs. Commonwealth , 8
Bush, 366 ); and the Commonwealth cannot afterward introduce evidence tending
to disprove the facts thus admitted . Nichols vs. Commonwealth , 8 Bush , 366 .
203, 204. PEREMPTORY CHALLENGES .
a . It was held , under the Code of 1854, that these provisions , affecting the proceed-
ings only , were not ex post facto laws ; and that they appiied to prosecutions
pending when the Code took effect . Walston vs. Commonwealth , 16 B. M. , 36 .

CRIMINAL CODE- NOTES TO SECS . 210 , 221 , 223 . 165
210 . CHALLENGE FOR IMPLIED BIAS .
a . Waiver of right of challenge . A prisoner may waive objection to a juror on account
of bias. Thus , during the trial of an indictment for murder, a juror announced
that he recollected that he had been one of a grand jury that found the indict-
ment ; and the court, sua sponte, and against the objection of the prisoner , ordered
the discharge of the juror and the selection of another in his place , which was
done ; and the trial resulted in the conviction and sentence of the accused . Upon
his appeal , the court held that the circuit court should have discharged the jury ;
and reversed the judgment and remanded the case for a new trial . O'Brian vs.
Commonwealth , 6 Bush, 563. Upon a second trial the accused was again con-
victed ; and , upon his appeal the court, overruling its former decision , held that
the accused had a right to waive , and did waive , objection to the juror on account
of bias ; that the court erred in discharging the juror and in causing another to
be placed in the panel ; and that the first trial was a bar to further proceedings
against the prisoner ; and reversed the judgment , and remanded the case , with
directions to discharge him . O'Brian vs. Commonwealth , 9 Bush, 333 .
EVIDENCE .221 , 223.
1. Testimony of defendants for or against each other. Page 165.
2. Testimony of defendant's wife for co-defendant . Page 165.
3. Testimony given on former trial . Page 166.
4. Res gesta. Page 166 .
5. Confessions and conversations . Page 166.
6. Statements of co-defendants . Page 167.
7. Statements of persons in combination . Page 167.
8. Guilly knowledge . Page 168.
9. Dying declarations . Page 168.
10. Opinions of experts and other witnesses. Page 168.
11. Secondary and hearsay evidence. Page 169.
12. Character of the accused. Page 169.
13. Character of the deceased. Page 169.
14. Character of witness . Page 169.
15. Contradiction of witness by party introducing him . Page 170.
16. Threats . Page 170.
17. Circumstantial evidence. Page 170.
18. Impeaching witness . Page 170.
I. TESTIMONY OF DEFENDANTS FOR OR AGAINST EACH OTHER .
a. According to the common law one of several joint defendants was incompetent
to testify for or against a co -defendant though tried separately , under the rule of
evidence forbidding parties to testify . That rule was recognized by ? 232 of the
Code of 1854, providing , that where two or more persons were included in the
same indictment , and the court is of opinion that the evidence as to one is not
sufficient to put him on his defense , it must , on motion of either party desiring
to use him as a witness , order him to be discharged from the indictment , and
permit him to be examined by the party so moving . Adwell vs. Commonwealth ,
17 B. M. , 318 ; Thompson vs. Same, I Met . , 16 ; Chandler vs. Same, I Bush, 41 .
Edgerton vs. Same, 7 Bush , 142. 232 of the Code of 1854 has been substituted
by 234 of this Code , providing that in such case each shall be a competent wit-
ness for the others unless the indictment charge a conspiracy .
2. TESTIMONY OF DEFENDANT'S WIFE FOR CO-DEFENDANT .
a. Upon the separate trial of one defendant , under a joint indictment against him
and several others , charging them with murder , the wives of the latter are com-

166 CRIMINAL CODE- NOTES TO SECS . 221 , 223 .
petent witnesses to prove an alibi for the former .
I Met., 13.
Thompson vs. Commonwealth ,
There are three states of case in which a wife cannot be a witness for a co -defend-
ant with her husband : First , where her testimony, as in case of conspiracy ,
would tend directly to the acquittal of her husband ; secondly , where , as in the
case of an assault , the interests of all the defendants are inseparable ; and ,
thirdly , where the rights of the husband , though not a party to the suit or pros-
ecution , would be concluded by any verdict therein . Cornelius vs. Commonwealth ,
3 Met. , 481 .
3. TESTIMONY GIVEN ON FORMER TRIAL .
a . Upon a trial of the accused in the circuit court, the substance of the testimony
of a deceased witness , before the examining court, may be proved by persons
who were present at that trial , and heard the statements of the witness , if they
can remember and state the substance of all his testimony . O'Brian vs. Common-
wealth , 6 Bush , 563 ; Kean vs. Commonwealth , 10 Bush, 190.
But , in a criminal case , the testimony of a deceased witness , upon a former trial ,
cannot be proved by a bill of exceptions ; as this would be a violation of the con
stitutional right of the accused to meet the witness face to face . Kean vs. Com-
monwealth , supra .
4. RES GESTÆ .
a. Contemporaneous expressions of the assailant or of his coadjutors , or of the
deceased in cases of homicide , may be proved for the purposé of illustrating the
character or quality of the act . Sherley , & c., vs. Billings , 8 Bush , 147 ; Bradshaw
vs. Commonwealth , 10 Bush , 576.
But cries and exclamations of bystanders in no way acting in concert with either
of the parties to the transaction do not constitute part of the res gesta . Brad-
shaw vs. Commonwealth , supra .
b. In Tipper vs. Commonweaith , 1 Met ., 6, the court said , without deciding the ques-
tion : " It was held that a statement made by a person , not then suspected of
theft , and before any search made , accounting for his possession of property
which he is afterwards charged with having stolen , is admissible in his favor
(Rex vs. Abraham , 2 Karrington & Kerwin , 550) ; and we are inclined to favor
the reception of declarations made under such circumstances , as tending to
explain the manner in which the possession was acquired ."
c. Declarations of the accused in his own favor are not admissible except as part of
the res gesta . Tipper vs. Commonwealth , 1 Met ., 11.
5. CONFESSIONS AND CONVERSATIONS .
a. In Becker vs. Crow , 7 Bush , 198, the court said : " Judge Trimble , in Myers vs.
Baker, & c., Hard . , 544, states the rule correctly , with the reasons therefor , when
he says : Proof of confessions of a party in the presence of the witness only , or
of him and the adverse party , although certainly competent testimony, ought to
be weighed with caution , because it is impossible for the party to counteract it
by other testimony , because the expressions used are easily misunderstood or per-
verted , either through mistake or design , and because not the whole conversation
but only parts of it are generally detailed by the witness . It is the most danger-
ous species of testimony held competent by the law ; and unless the story told is
probable in its nature , or is corroborated by circumstances , very little weight is
due to it.""
But , threats on the part of the deceased having been proved by the concurrent
testimony of several witnesses , the court held that the following instruction was
properly refused : " The statements of witnesses as to threats and declarations of
Becker against Glass , in the absence of all persons except the deceased and the

entries.ocr_text:
CRIMINAL CODE- NOTES TO SECS . 221 , 223 167
witnesses so testifying , is the weakest of all evidence known to the law , and
should be received and weighed by the jury with great caution ." Becker , &c.,
vs. Crow, supra .
b. "If the witness called to prove the confessions of the prisoner says he does not
remember all the conversation , and that a great many things were said in the
conversation which he did not remember , and is still permitted to testify without
even stating that he remembers the substance of all that he said at the time on
the subject , " his testimony should be excluded . Berry vs. Commonwealth , 10
Bush, 15.
And , if a witness for the Commonwealth should testify as to part of the conversa-
tion of the accused , he has a right to prove the entire conversation by the testi-
mony of other witnesses ; but a judgment of conviction will not be reversed on
account of the exclusion of such testimony , unless the bill of exceptions show
that the witness was introduced by the Commonwealth . Berry vs. Commonwealth ,
10 Bush, 15.
c. Where a witness for the prosecution remembered a part only of the declaration
of the accused relative to the homicide , the accused had the right to introduce
other witnesses , who heard the entire conversation , to prove the whole , unless he
himself drew forth the statement from the witness as to the declaration . Coff-
man vs. Commonwealth , 10 Bush, 495 .
d. Besides the proof of any confession a defendant may have made of his guilt ,
unless made in open court, there must , to warrant a conviction , be other evi-
dence conducing to prove him guilty of the offense alleged ; or , in other words ,
to show that such an offense had been committed , and not inconsistent with his
guilt , and not merely some " other testimony " which might have no tendency
whatever to establish the charge . Cunningham vs. Commonwealth , 9 Bush, 149 ;
Criminal Code, & 240 .
e. Proof of confessions of guilt , or of exculpatory statements , made by a prisoner,
under the influence of hope or fear , are inadmissible against him . Jane vs. Com
monwealth , 2 Met ., 30 ; Rutherford vs. Commonwealth , 2 Met., 387 ; Young vs.
Commonwealth , 8 Bush , 366 ; Hudson vs. Same, 2 Duv ., 531 ; Butler vs. Same , 2
Duv ., 435.
But proof of a fact, the discovery of which is made by means of an extorted con-
fession , is admissible against the prisoner ; as , the discovery of a hidden vial
which had contained poison , charged to have been administered by the accused
to the deceased . Jane vs. Commonwealth , supra .
f. Whether , in view of all the facts and circumstances attending a confession , it was
voluntary or induced by duress , is a question of law for the court. Henderson
vs. Commonwealth , 2 Duv . , 532.
6. STATEMENTS OF CO-DEFENDANTS .
a . No act or declaration of one of several defendants jointly indicted , made or
committed subsequently to the commission of the offense charged , is competent
evidence against the other defendants . Thompson vs. Commonwealth , I Met ., 13 ;
Hudson vs. Same, 2 Duv ., 531 ; Jones vs. Same, 2 Duv . , 554 .
7. STATEMENTS OF PERSONS IN COMBINATION .
a. Statements of persons who had agreed to act with the accused , in any hostile
meeting between him and the deceased , are competent evidence for the Com-
monwealth ; and , if any such statement be brought out by the prosecution , the
whole of the conversation at the time is competent evidence for the accused ;
but not statements made at other times , though during the same day . Cornelius
vs. Commonwealth , 15 B. M. , 539 .
38

168 CRIMINAL CODE- NOTES TO SECS . 221 , 223 .
b. Upon trial of an indictment of shooting with intent to kill , it was held that the
court erred in refusing to permit the accused to prove that a son of the person
wounded , who was in the store when his father and the accused entered it , and
who immediately ran up stairs , and returned with a pistol which he snapped at
the accused , had loaded it a few days before , then making a contingent threat to
shoot the accused ; of which the accused was notified before the shooting . Rapp
vs. Commonwealth , 14 B. M. , 614 .
8. GUILTY KNOWLEDGE .
a. Upon the trial of an indictment for receiving certain goods , knowing them to
have been stolen , evidence that other goods were found in the possession of the
defendant is admissible for the purpose of showing guilty knowledge on the
part of the accused that the goods , for receiving which he is indicted , were
stolen . Devoto vs. Commonwealth , 3 Met. , 417 .
And the fact that defendant had , about the same time , tendered another altered
bill, similar in the character of the alteration to the bill for the tendering of
which he is prosecuted , is competent evidence to prove guilty knowledge . Mount
vs. Commonwealth , I Duv ., 90 .
9. DYING DECLARATIONS .
a . The admission of proof of dying declarations is not an infringement of the con-
stitutional right of the accused to confront the witnesses against him face to face .
Walston vs. Commonwealth , 16 B. M. , 15.
b. To be admissible the declaration must be made in extremis and under a solemn
sense of impending dissolution . Ibid.
66c. Where a wounded man stated that he was a ruined and a dead man unless he
got speedy relief, " and died in about twenty minutes , the court inclined to the
opinion that he was not under such a sense of impending dissolution as to make
evidence of his declarations admissible . Adwell vs. Commonwealth , 17 B. M. ,
316 .
d. The deceased , after being shot , told a witness that the accused had shot him ,
and stated the circumstances which attended the shooting ; but said he did not
believe that he would die . About an hour afterward , he re-affirmed said state-
ments , about the shooting, to another witness (his father), and told him that he
had made said statements to the first named witness , and had told him that he
did not believe that he would die ; but that he then believed and still believed
that he would die ; and had said that he did not believe it , because a man in the
neighborhood , after saying that he believed he would die , had got well and been
disgraced ; and he was afraid it would be a reproach to him if he should get
well : held , that his statements were admissible as dying declarations . Young
vs. Commonwealth , 6 Bush , 312 .
e . Dying declarations are only admissible in evidence where the death of the
deceased is the subject of the charge , and the circumstances of the death the
subject of the dying declaration . Walston vs. Commonwealth , 16 B. M. , 34 .
Evidence of dying declarations should be restricted to the act of killing and the
circumstances immediately attending it and forming a part of the res gesta . The
circuit court erred by admitting evidence which purported to disclose former and
distinct transactions not relating to the particular facts constituting the subject-
matter of the charge or the identification of the defendant , from which the jury
might have inferred the existence of malice on the part of the defendant toward
the deceased . Leiber vs. Commonwealth , 9 Bush, 11.
10. OPINIONS OF EXPERTS AND OTHER WITNESSES .
a. On a trial for murder , evidence of previous threats of the accused was given , the
witness stating that the name of the deceased was not called , but that witness

CRIMINAL CODE- NOTES TO SECS . 221 , 223 . 169
took it that accused was talking about deceased . Held, error to admit this testi-
mony against defendant's objection . Johnson vs. Commonwealth , 9 Bush, 224 .
b. The opinions of experts upon questions of art or science , to be admissible as
evidence , must always be predicated upon and relate to the facts established by
the proof in the case . Mere professional opinions upon abstract questions of
science , having no proper relation to the facts upon which the jury are to pass ,
tend to lead their minds away from the true and real points of inquiry , and
should therefore always be excluded . Champ vs. Commonwealth , 2 Met., 17.
II . SECONDARY AND HEARSAY EVIDENCE .
a. The statements of a witness as to the rules of the code duello , in relation to send-
ing and accepting challenges , upon an indictment against a party for sending a
challenge , are inadmissible . The code itself should have been produced . Moody
vs. Commonwealth , 4 Met., I.
b. See , in the case last cited , a statement of circumstances under which a copy of the
correspondence between duelists was held to be competent evidence .
c. Hearsay evidence ; as, proof of exclamations of bystanders witnessing a homicide
(Bradshaw vs. Commonwealth , 10 Bush , 576) ; or proof of the statements of a
witness before an examining court, it not being shown that his personal attend-
ance could not be procured , is not admissible . Dye vs. Commonwealth , 3 Bush, 3.
An error in admitting such testimony was not cured by instructing the jury to
disregard such hearsay evidence , " except so far as it harmonized withfacts after-
wards developed." Dye vs. Commonwealth , supra .
12. CHARACTER OF THE ACCUSED .
a. Evidence of the good character of the prisoner is admissible ; on the ground
that an individual , who has maintained an unblemished character , up to the
time when he is charged with crime , may avail him self of that fact as some evi-
dence that he has not committed the crime with which he is accused . Young vs.
Commonwealth , 6 Bush, 312.
" But the prosecution is not allowed to call witnesses to the general bad character
of the prisoner, unless to rebut the evidence of good character already adduced
by him ; and even this has recently been denied in England . And the evidence ,
when admissible , must be restricted to the trait of character which is in issue , or
ought to bear some analogy and reference to the nature of the charge ." Young
vs. Commonwealth , supra .
13. CHARACTER OF THE DECEASED .
a. In Payne
